Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus

[!info]
Heading hub for Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus — covers 12 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Epidemiology and classification/Type 1 diabetes mellitus.md|Type 1 diabetes mellitus]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Epidemiology and classification/Type 2 diabetes mellitus.md|Type 2 diabetes mellitus]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Epidemiology and classification/Monogenic diabetes (MODY, neonatal).md|Monogenic diabetes (MODY, neonatal)]]
  • [[Secondary diabetes]]
  • [[Diabetes in Special Populations/Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM).md|Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Diagnostic criteria/Fasting plasma glucose.md|Fasting plasma glucose]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Diagnostic criteria/Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT).md|Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Diagnostic criteria/HbA1c diagnosis.md|HbA1c diagnosis]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Diagnostic criteria/Random glucose with symptoms.md|Random glucose with symptoms]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Prediabetic states/Impaired fasting glucose (IFG).md|Impaired fasting glucose (IFG)]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Prediabetic states/Impaired glucose tolerance (IGT).md|Impaired glucose tolerance (IGT)]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/Prediabetic states/Increased HbA1c risk category.md|Increased HbA1c risk category]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
Diagnostic criteria (ADA/WHO)FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L · 2h-OGTT ≥11.1 mmol/L · HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) · Random ≥11.1 + symptoms → confirm with repeat unless unequivocal
PrediabetesIFG: FPG 6.1-6.9 · IGT: 2h-OGTT 7.8-11.0 · HbA1c 39-47 mmol/mol (5.7-6.4%)
Type 1 vs Type 2Age, BMI, ketosis, autoantibodies, C-peptide, family history
Monogenic red flagsAge <25, non-obese, strong FH (3 generations), negative autoantibodies, non-insulin requiring
GDM diagnosisIADPSG: 75g OGTT → FBG ≥5.1, 1h ≥10.0, 2h ≥8.5 mmol/L · NICE: risk factors + OGTT

  • [[NPDR]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
Retinopathy screeningType 1: 5y post-dx · Type 2: at dx · Annual if no DR · ETDRS grading (10-12 no DR, 20-35 mild/mod NPDR, 43-53 severe NPDR, 61+ PDR)
DMOCentre-involving macular oedema · Anti-VEGF 1st line (ranibizumab/aflibercept) · Laser if non-centre
DKD stagingKDIGO: GFR G1-G5 × ACR A1-A3 (A1<3, A2 3-30, A3>30 mg/mmol) · Heat map for prognosis
DKD managementACEi/ARB max tolerated · SGLT2i (eGFR ≥20) · Finerenone (eGFR ≥25, K+ ≤4.8) · GLP-1 RA
DSPNStocking-glove sensory loss · Painful (10-20%) · Duloxetine/pregabalin/gabapentin/amitriptyline
Autonomic neuropathyCV: resting tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, silent MI · GI: gastroparesis · GU: ED, bladder
Foot risk (IWGDF)0: no LOPS/PAD · 1: LOPS · 2: LOPS+PAD/deformity · 3: hx ulcer/amputation
DFU classificationWagner 0-5 (depth) · UT Grade 0-3 × Stage A-D (infection/ischaemia)
DFU managementTCC gold standard offloading · PEDIS infection grading · Revascularisation if PAD · MDT
CharcotEichenholtz 0-3 · Hot swollen foot, preserved pulses · Immobilisation (TCC) · Bisphosphonates?

Monogenic and Atypical Diabetes

[!info]
Heading hub for Monogenic and Atypical Diabetes — covers 9 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[HNF1A-MODY]]
  • [[GCK-MODY]]
  • [[HNF4A-MODY]]
  • [[Monogenic and Atypical Diabetes/Maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY)/Other MODY subtypes.md|Other MODY subtypes]]
  • [[MODY testing indications]]
  • [[KCNJ11/ABCC8]]
  • [[Transient vs permanent]]
  • [[Monogenic and Atypical Diabetes/Neonatal diabetes/Sulfonylurea responsiveness.md|Sulfonylurea responsiveness]]
  • [[m.3243A>G]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
MODY red flagsAge <25, non-obese, autosomal dominant (3 generations), negative autoantibodies, C-peptide preserved
GCK-MODY (MODY2)Glucokinase defect · Mild fasting hyperglycaemia (5.5-8.0) · No treatment needed · No complications
HNF1A-MODY (MODY3)Progressive β-cell defect · Sulfonylurea responsive (excellent response) · Low renal threshold
HNF4A-MODY (MODY1)Similar to HNF1A · Macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia · Sulfonylurea responsive
MODY testingCost-effective if probability >6% (calculator: MODY probability calculator) · Panel vs targeted
Neonatal diabetes<6 months age · KCNJ11 (Kir6.2), ABCC8 (SUR1) = 40-50% · Sulfonylurea responsive (90% switch)
Transient vs permanentTransient: remission by 18mo, relapse later · Permanent: lifelong · 6q24 imprinting = transient
Mitochondrial diabetesm.3243A>G (MELAS/MIDD) · Maternal inheritance · Sensorineural hearing loss, CPEO, cardiomyopathy
Wolfram syndromeWFS1 gene · DIABETES INSIPIDUS, DIABETES MELLITUS, OPTIC ATROPHY, DEAFNESS (DIDMOAD)
LipodystrophyGeneralised (CGL) or partial (FPLD) · Severe insulin resistance · Leptin replacement (metreleptin)

Pathophysiology of Diabetes

[!info]
Heading hub for Pathophysiology of Diabetes — covers 9 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[Pathophysiology of Diabetes/Type 1 diabetes pathogenesis/Autoimmune beta-cell destruction.md|Autoimmune beta-cell destruction]]
  • [[Genetic susceptibility]]
  • [[Pathophysiology of Diabetes/Type 1 diabetes pathogenesis/Environmental triggers.md|Environmental triggers]]
  • [[Stages of type 1 diabetes]]
  • [[Pathophysiology of Diabetes/Type 2 diabetes pathogenesis/Insulin resistance.md|Insulin resistance]]
  • [[Pathophysiology of Diabetes/Type 2 diabetes pathogenesis/Beta-cell dysfunction and failure.md|Beta-cell dysfunction and failure]]
  • [[Incretin defect]]
  • [[Pathophysiology of Diabetes/Type 2 diabetes pathogenesis/Lipotoxicity and glucotoxicity.md|Lipotoxicity and glucotoxicity]]
  • [[Genetics of type 2 diabetes]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
T1D autoimmunityCD4+/CD8+ T-cell mediated β-cell destruction · Insulitis · GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA autoantibodies
T1D geneticsHLA-DR3/DR4-DQ2/DQ8 (40-50% risk) · INS VNTR · PTPN22 · CTLA4 · >50 loci
T1D stagesStage 1: ≥2 autoantibodies, normoglycaemia · Stage 2: ≥2 autoantibodies, dysglycaemia · Stage 3: symptomatic
T2D insulin resistanceImpaired PI3K-Akt signalling in muscle/liver/adipose · ↑FFA, inflammation (TNF-α, IL-6), adipokines
T2D β-cell failure↑Proinsulin/insulin ratio · Amyloid (IAPP) deposition · Glucolipotoxicity · Dedifferentiation
T2D incretin defect↓GLP-1 secretion · ↓GIP effect on β-cell · ↑Glucagon → ↑HGP
T2D geneticsTCF7L2 (strongest) · KCNJ11 · PPARG · FTO · >400 loci (polygenic risk score)

Secondary Diabetes

[!info]
Heading hub for Secondary Diabetes — covers 14 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[Chronic pancreatitis]]
  • [[Pancreatic cancer]]
  • [[Pancreatectomy/CF]]
  • [[Cushing syndrome]]
  • [[Acromegaly]]
  • [[Thyrotoxicosis]]
  • [[Phaeochromocytoma]]
  • [[Glucagonoma]]

  • [[Calcineurin inhibitors]]
  • [[Immune checkpoint inhibitors]]
  • [[Protease inhibitors]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
Type 3c diabetesPancreatic exocrine insufficiency + diabetes · Low faecal elastase · No autoantibodies · Often insulin requiring
Chronic pancreatitisAlcohol, smoking, genetic (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR) · Pain, malabsorption, diabetes late
Pancreatic cancerNew-onset diabetes (>50y, weight loss) as presentation · ↑CA19-9 · CT pancreas
Cushing syndromeCortisol excess → insulin resistance · Dexamethasone suppression test · Midnight cortisol
AcromegalyGH/IGF-1 excess → insulin resistance · OGTT for GH suppression · Pituitary MRI
Thyrotoxicosis↑HGP, ↓insulin clearance → hyperglycaemia · Resolves with euthyroidism
PhaeochromocytomaCatecholamines → ↑glycogenolysis, ↓insulin secretion · Plasma metanephrines · MIBG
GlucagonomaGlucagon excess → hyperglycaemia, NME rash, weight loss, DVT · Plasma glucagon
Steroid diabetesPost-prandial hyperglycaemia prominent · Morning dose → afternoon hyperglycaemia · NPH insulin or basal-bolus
AntipsychoticsOlanzapine, clozapine > risperidone > aripiprazole → weight gain, insulin resistance
Calcineurin inhibitorsTacrolimus > ciclosporin → β-cell toxicity, insulin resistance · Monitor post-transplant
ICI diabetesAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 → fulminant type 1, DKA at presentation · Check autoantibodies
Thiazides/β-blockersThiazide: hypokalaemia → ↓insulin secretion · β-blocker: ↓insulin secretion, mask hypoglycaemia

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
Classic presentationPolyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue → DKA in 25-30% at diagnosis
LADAAge >30, non-obese, GAD65+, slow progression, initial non-insulin response
AutoantibodiesGAD65 (70-80%), IA-2 (50-70%), ZnT8 (60-80%), IAA (children >adults) · ≥2 = high risk
Basal-bolusBasal 40-50% TDD (glargine U-100/U-300, degludec) · Bolus 50-60% TDD (rapid analogues)
CSIIIndications: HbA1c >58 on MDI, severe hypoglycaemia, Dawn phenomenon, lifestyle
CGM metricsTIR 70-180mg/dL >70% · TBR <54mg/dL <4% · TAR >180mg/dL <25% · CV <36%
DKASee Diabetic Emergencies hub
Hypoglycaemia unawarenessImpaired counter-regulation → avoid <3.9 for 2-3 weeks to restore awareness

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
ScreeningADA: age ≥35 or BMI ≥25 + risk factor · NICE: risk assessment + HbA1c/FPG · USPSTF: 35-70 BMI ≥25
Metformin1st line · Start 500mg OD → 2g/day · Contraindicated eGFR <30 · B12 monitoring · Lactic acidosis rare
Sulfonylureas↑Insulin secretion · Hypoglycaemia (esp. elderly, CKD) · Weight gain · 2nd/3rd line
DPP-4iWeight neutral · Low hypoglycaemia · Linagliptin no renal dose adjust · Neutral CV
SGLT2i↓Glucose reabsorption · CV/renal benefit · Genital mycotic infection · Euglycaemic DKA risk · eGFR thresholds
TZDsPioglitazone · ↑Insulin sensitivity · Fluid retention, HF, fracture, bladder cancer · 2nd/3rd line
GLP-1 RASemaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide · CV benefit · Weight loss · Nausea, pancreatitis risk
TirzepatideDual GIP/GLP-1 · Superior HbA1c/weight vs semaglutide · SURPASS/SURMOUNT trials
ADA/EASD 2023ASCVD/HF/CKD → SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA 1st line · Obesity → GLP-1 RA/tirzepatide · Cost/access considered
NICE NG28Metformin → dual → triple → insulin · SGLT2i if ASCVD/HF/CKD · GLP-1 RA if BMI ≥35
Bariatric surgeryBMI ≥35 (or ≥30 with comorbidities) · Remission rates 60-80% (RYGB/SG)

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
GDM screeningIADPSG: universal 75g OGTT 24-28wk (FBG≥5.1, 1h≥10.0, 2h≥8.5) · NICE: risk factors then OGTT · ADA: universal or risk-based
GDM targetsFBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L · HbA1c not recommended for monitoring
GDM managementDiet 1st → Metformin (if fasting >5.5 or postprandial >7.0) → Insulin · Glyburide alternative
Pre-existing DM pregnancyPre-conception HbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%) · Folic acid 5mg OD to 12wk · Stop ACEi/ARB/statin/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
PostpartumOGTT 6-13 weeks · Annual HbA1c · Lifetime T2DM risk 50%
Elderly HbA1c targetsFit: <53mmol/mol (7.0%) · Mild frailty: <58mmol/mol (7.5%) · Moderate/severe: <64mmol/mol (8.0%)
DeintensificationStop sulfonylurea/insulin if hypoglycaemia · Simplify regimens
CKD HbA1cFalsely low in CKD/ESRD (↓RBC lifespan) · Use fructosamine or glycated albumin
CKD drug dosingMetformin: eGFR≥45 full, 30-45 half dose, <30 contraindicated · SGLT2i: initiate eGFR≥45/30 · Insulin: ↓dose 25-50%
DialysisGlucose-free dialysate → hypoglycaemia risk · Insulin requirements variable · Monitor pre/post dialysis
HIV/ARTProtease inhibitors, integrase inhibitors → insulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, lipodystrophy

Diabetes MOC

[!info]
Davidson alignment: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes (Diabetes Mellitus section)
Related: [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]] · [[../00_Index/Davidson Chapter Roadmap|Davidson Chapter Roadmap]] · [[../00_Index/Davidson Chapter Progress Tracker|Davidson Chapter Progress Tracker]] · [[../Endocrinology/Endocrinology MOC|Endocrinology MOC]]

Template

  • [[../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

Chapter Hierarchy

  • [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Study Plan / Topic Queue

Core High-Yield Topics (FCPS/MRCP Priority)

  • [ ] Classification & Diagnostic Criteria — ADA/WHO/EASD criteria, HbA1c vs glucose, prediabetes categories, monogenic/secondary diabetes red flags
  • [ ] Type 1 Diabetes Pathophysiology — Autoimmune destruction, staging (1-3), autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA), LADA, genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR3/DR4)
  • [ ] Type 2 Diabetes Pathophysiology — Insulin resistance, beta-cell failure, incretin defect, lipotoxicity/glucotoxicity, TCF7L2 and polygenic risk
  • [ ] Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) — Diagnostic criteria (glucose, ketones, pH, bicarbonate), severity grading, management protocol (fluids, insulin, K+, monitoring), cerebral oedema, euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2i)
  • [ ] Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS) — Diagnostic criteria, HHS vs DKA differentiation, management (lower insulin, higher fluids), mortality predictors
  • [ ] Hypoglycaemia — Classification (level 1/2/3), glucose counter-regulation failure, hypoglycaemia unawareness, glucagon (IM/IN/auto-injector), hospital insulin errors
  • [ ] Type 1 Insulin Therapy — Basal-bolus principles, insulin analogues (rapid: lispro/aspart/glulisine, long: glargine U-100/U-300, degludec, ultra-long), CSII/pump therapy, carbohydrate counting, CGM-guided dosing
  • [ ] Type 2 Treatment Algorithm — ADA/EASD 2023+ consensus, NICE NG28, CVOT-guided (SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA), CKD/HF-guided therapy, metabolic surgery indications
  • [ ] Oral Glucose-Lowering Agents — Metformin (contraindications, B12, lactic acidosis), sulfonylureas (hypoglycaemia, weight), DPP-4i (neutral), SGLT2i (CV/renal benefit, genital infections, euglycaemic DKA), TZDs (fluid retention, fracture, bladder cancer), alpha-glucosidase, meglitinides
  • [ ] Injectable Non-Insulin Therapy — GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide, CV benefit, weight loss, pancreatitis risk), dual GIP/GLP-1 (tirzepatide), amylin analogues
  • [ ] Insulin in Type 2 Diabetes — Basal initiation, basal-bolus intensification, pre-mixed, concentrated insulins (U-200/U-300/U-500), degludec vs glargine U-300
  • [ ] Diabetic Retinopathy — NPDR vs PDR, ETDRS grading, diabetic macular oedema, screening intervals, treatment (laser, anti-VEGF: ranibizumab/aflibercept, vitrectomy)
  • [ ] Diabetic Kidney Disease (DKD) — KDIGO staging (GFR + albuminuria), ACR categories, GFR categories, management (ACEi/ARB max dose, SGLT2i, finerenone, GLP-1 RA), proteinuria reduction targets
  • [ ] Diabetic Neuropathy — DSPN (stocking-glove), autonomic (cardiovascular: HRV, orthostatic; GI: gastroparesis; GU: ED, bladder), screening (10g monofilament, vibration, ANNS), neuropathic pain (duloxetine, pregabalin, gabapentin, amitriptyline)
  • [ ] Diabetic Foot Disease — IWGDF/Scottish risk classification (low/moderate/high/active), Wagner/University of Texas DFU classification, offloading (TCC gold standard), infection (PEDIS), revascularisation, Charcot neuroarthropathy (Eichenholtz), MDT amputation prevention
  • [ ] Cardiovascular Disease in Diabetes — Diabetes as CV risk equivalent, QRISK3/ASCVD Pooled Cohort, statin intensity (high-intensity atorvastatin 40-80mg), BP targets (<130/80 ADA/ESC, <140/90 NICE), aspirin primary prevention (10-year risk >10%), SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for CV risk reduction
  • [ ] Heart Failure in Diabetes — HFrEF vs HFpEF, SGLT2i (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced/Preserved, DELIVER), GLP-1 RA (FIGHT, STEP-HFpEF), diagnostic challenges (diastolic dysfunction)
  • [ ] Diabetes in Pregnancy — GDM screening (IADPSG 75g OGTT, NICE risk factors + OGTT), targets (FBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L), management (diet → metformin → insulin), pre-existing DM (pre-conception HbA1c <48mmol/mol, folic acid 5mg), postpartum OGTT 6-13 weeks
  • [ ] Diabetes in Elderly — Frailty-guided HbA1c targets (Fit: <53, Mild frailty: <58, Moderate/severe: <64), deintensification/deprescribing, hypoglycaemia avoidance, care home management
  • [ ] Diabetes in CKD/Dialysis — HbA1c pitfalls (falsely low in CKD/ESRD, use fructosamine/GA), drug dosing adjustments (metformin eGFR cut-offs, SGLT2i eGFR thresholds, GLP-1 RA safe, insulin dose reduction), dialysis glucose management
  • [ ] Monogenic Diabetes — MODY (HNF1A MODY3: sulfonylurea responsive, GCK MODY2: no treatment, HNF4A MODY1), neonatal diabetes (KCNJ11/ABCC8: sulfonylurea responsive), mitochondrial diabetes (m.3243A>G), genetic testing indications
  • [ ] Secondary Diabetes — Type 3c (pancreatic: chronic pancreatitis, cancer, post-pancreatectomy, CF), endocrine (Cushing, acromegaly, thyrotoxicosis, phaeochromocytoma, glucagonoma), drug-induced (steroids, antipsychotics, calcineurin inhibitors, ICI, protease inhibitors, thiazides)
  • [ ] Type 2 Diabetes Prevention & Remission — DPP (lifestyle 58%, metformin 31%), Da Qing, Finnish DPS, DiRECT trial (46% remission at 1 year, 36% at 2 years), remission criteria (HbA1c <48mmol/mol off glucose-lowering meds x3 months), maintenance
  • [ ] Diabetes Technology — rtCGM vs isCGM, CGM metrics (TIR >70%, TBR <4%, TAR <25%, GMI, CV <36%), hybrid closed-loop (670G, 780G, CamAPS FX, Loop), connected pens, digital platforms
  • [ ] Monitoring & Quality of Care — Individualised HbA1c targets, annual review (retinopathy, nephropathy ACR/eGFR, neuropathy/foot, CV risk), structured education (DAFNE type 1, DESMOND/X-PERT type 2), psychosocial (diabetes distress, depression, eating disorders/diabulimia)

Key Algorithms & Scoring Systems (Memorize for Viva)

Algorithm/ScoreUse CaseKey Cut-offs
ADA/WHO Diagnostic CriteriaDiabetes diagnosisFPG ≥7.0, 2h-OGTT ≥11.1, HbA1c ≥48mmol/mol (6.5%), random ≥11.1 + symptoms
Prediabetes CriteriaRisk stratificationIFG 6.1-6.9, IGT 7.8-11.0, HbA1c 39-47mmol/mol (5.7-6.4%)
DKA SeverityDKA triageMild: pH 7.25-7.30, K+ >3.5; Moderate: pH 7.0-7.24, K+ 3.0-3.5; Severe: pH <7.0, K+ <3.0
HHS CriteriaHHS diagnosisGlucose >30mmol/L, osmolality >320mOsm/kg, pH >7.3, bicarbonate >18, no significant ketonaemia
KDIGO DKD StagingDKD prognosisG1-G5 (GFR) × A1-A3 (ACR); e.g., G3aA2 = moderate risk
ETDRS Retinopathy SeverityRetinopathy grading10-12: no DR; 20-35: mild/mod NPDR; 43-53: severe NPDR; 61+: PDR
IWGDF Foot RiskFoot risk stratification0: no LOPS/PAD; 1: LOPS; 2: LOPS + PAD/deformity; 3: history ulcer/amputation
Wagner DFU ClassificationUlcer depth0: pre-ulcer; 1: superficial; 2: deep to tendon; 3: bone/abscess; 4: forefoot gangrene; 5: whole foot gangrene
University of Texas DFUUlcer depth + infection + ischaemiaGrade 0-3 × Stage A-D (A=clean, B=infected, C=ischaemic, D=infected+ischaemic)
CHA₂DS₂-VAScAF stroke risk (often asked in DM)CHF, HTN, Age≥75, DM, Stroke/TIA, Vascular, Age 65-74, Sex
ADA/EASD 2023 AlgorithmT2DM treatment selectionASCVD/HF/CKD → SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; no CV → metformin + lifestyle → add based on comorbidities
NICE NG28 T2DM PathwayUK T2DM algorithmMetformin → dual → triple → insulin; SGLT2i if ASCVD/HF/CKD
CGM Targets (ATTD)CGM interpretationTIR 70-180mg/dL >70%; TBR <54mg/dL <4%; TAR >180mg/dL <25%; CV <36%
Diabetes Remission CriteriaRemission definitionHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%) off all glucose-lowering meds ≥3 months
Folic Acid in Pre-existing DM PregnancyNeural tube defect prevention5mg daily from pre-conception to 12 weeks gestation

High-Yield Viva Topics (FCPS/MRCP)

Must-Know Definitions

  • [ ] Diabetes mellitus (ADA/WHO) vs impaired glucose regulation
  • [ ] DKA vs HHS vs mixed DKA-HHS
  • [ ] DKA severity grading (mild/moderate/severe)
  • [ ] DKD staging (KDIGO G1-G5 × A1-A3)
  • [ ] Retinopathy: NPDR vs PDR vs DMO
  • [ ] Foot risk: IWGDF 0-3 vs Wagner 0-5 vs UT classification
  • [ ] Hypoglycaemia levels (Level 1: <3.9, Level 2: <3.0, Level 3: severe requiring assistance)
  • [ ] Diabetes remission (ADA/Diabetes UK consensus)
  • [ ] LADA vs classic type 1 vs type 2
  • [ ] MODY vs neonatal diabetes vs type 1 vs type 2

Must-Know Doses/Durations

  • [ ] DKA: 0.9% NaCl 1L/hr initially then adjusted; fixed-rate insulin infusion 0.1U/kg/hr; K+ replacement (40mmol/L if K+ 3.5-5.5, 60mmol/L if <3.5, hold if >5.5); bicarbonate if pH <7.0
  • [ ] HHS: 0.9% NaCl 1L/hr (lower insulin: 0.05U/kg/hr); monitor osmolality drop <3mOsm/kg/hr
  • [ ] Hypoglycaemia: Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg/child; nasal glucagon 3mg; oral carbs 15-20g if conscious
  • [ ] Metformin: Start 500mg OD, titrate to 2g/day max; hold if eGFR <30, caution 30-45
  • [ ] SGLT2i: Empagliflozin 10/25mg, dapagliflozin 10mg, canagliflozin 100/300mg; eGFR cut-offs (initiate ≥45/30, continue ≥20/15)
  • [ ] GLP-1 RA: Semaglutide SC 0.25→0.5→1mg weekly; oral 3→7→14mg daily; liraglutide 0.6→1.2→1.8mg daily; dulaglutide 0.75/1.5mg weekly
  • [ ] Tirzepatide: 2.5→5→7.5→10→12.5→15mg weekly
  • [ ] Insulin analogues: Rapid (lispro/aspart/glulisine) 0-15min pre-meal; Long (glargine U-100/U-300, degludec) once daily; Pre-mixed BD
  • [ ] Folic acid 5mg OD pre-conception to 12 weeks in pre-existing DM pregnancy
  • [ ] Aspirin 75mg OD primary prevention if 10-year ASCVD risk >10% (ADA) or age >50 with risk factor (ESC)
  • [ ] Statin: Atorvastatin 40-80mg (high-intensity) for primary prevention age 40-75 with DM

Quick Reference Cards (One-Page)

  • [ ] Diabetes Diagnosis Algorithm — FPG/OGTT/HbA1c/Random, confirmatory testing
  • [ ] DKA Management Protocol — Fluids, insulin, K+, bicarbonate, monitoring, cerebral oedema
  • [ ] HHS Management Protocol — Fluids, low-dose insulin, osmolality monitoring
  • [ ] Hypoglycaemia Algorithm — Conscious vs unconscious, glucagon, IV dextrose, prevention
  • [ ] T2DM Treatment Algorithm (ADA/EASD) — CVOT-guided, CKD/HF-guided, stepwise
  • [ ] SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA Indication Matrix — ASCVD, HF, CKD, obesity, hypoglycaemia risk
  • [ ] DKD Staging & Management — KDIGO heat map, ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, finerenone, GLP-1 RA
  • [ ] Retinopathy Screening & Referral — ETDRS grading, referral thresholds
  • [ ] Diabetic Foot Risk & Referral — IWGDF classification, urgent vs routine referral
  • [ ] GDM Diagnosis & Management — IADPSG vs NICE, targets, metformin vs insulin, postpartum
  • [ ] Monogenic Diabetes Red Flags — Age <25, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies, GCK vs HNF1A features
  • [ ] Diabetes in Elderly HbA1c Targets — Frailty categories, deintensification triggers
  • [ ] Diabetes in CKD Drug Dosing — Metformin eGFR, SGLT2i eGFR, GLP-1 RA, insulin reduction
  • [ ] CGM Metrics & Interpretation — TIR/TBR/TAR/GMI/CV, AGP report key features
  • [ ] Diabetes Remission Pathway — DiRECT criteria, low-calorie diet, maintenance

Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care

[!info]
Heading hub for Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care — covers 12 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[HbA1c targets]]
  • [[TIR targets]]
  • [[SMBG frequency]]
  • [[Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care/Annual review and complication screening/Retinopathy screening.md|Retinopathy screening]]
  • [[Nephropathy screening]]
  • [[Neuropathy/foot screening]]
  • [[Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care/Annual review and complication screening/CV risk assessment.md|CV risk assessment]]
  • [[DAFNE]]
  • [[DESMOND/X-PERT]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
HbA1c targets (individualised)Standard: <53mmol/mol (7.0%) · Tighter: <48mmol/mol (6.5%) if achievable without hypo · Looser: <58-64mmol/mol (7.5-8.0%) if elderly/frail/comorbidities/hypo risk
TIR targets>70% time 70-180mg/dL (3.9-10.0mmol/L) · <4% <54mg/dL (3.0mmol/L) · <1% <54mg/dL · CV <36%
SMBG frequencyType 1/insulin: pre-meal, bedtime, pre-driving, suspected hypo (4-10x/day) · Type 2 non-insulin: individualised
Annual review (9 care processes)1) HbA1c 2) BP 3) Cholesterol 4) Retinopathy screening 5) Foot check 6) ACR 7) eGFR 8) BMI 9) Smoking
Retinopathy screeningDigital photography · Annual if no DR · Refer if R2/R3 or M1 (maculopathy)
Nephropathy screeningACR (spot urine) + eGFR (CKD-EPI) · ACR <3 (A1), 3-30 (A2), >30 (A3) mg/mmol
Neuropathy/foot screening10g monofilament, vibration (128Hz tuning fork), ANNS · IWGDF risk classification
CV risk assessmentQRISK3 (UK) / ASCVD Pooled Cohort (US) · Statin if 10-year risk >10% or age 40-75 with DM
DAFNEDose Adjustment For Normal Eating · 5-day structured education for type 1 · Carb counting, insulin adjustment
DESMOND/X-PERTStructured education for type 2 · Self-management, lifestyle, medication understanding
Diabetes distressPAID scale, DDS · Not depression · Address with education, support, shared decision-making
Depression in DM2x risk · PHQ-9 screening · SSRIs 1st line · Collaborative care model
Eating disorders (diabulimia)Insulin omission for weight loss · DKA recurrent · High mortality · Multidisciplinary (psych, diabetes, dietitian)

Diabetes Prevention and Remission

[!info]
Heading hub for Diabetes Prevention and Remission — covers 10 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[Teplizumab]]
  • [[Diabetes Prevention and Remission/Type 1 diabetes prevention/Immunotherapy trials.md|Immunotherapy trials]]
  • [[DPP]]
  • [[Diabetes Prevention and Remission/Type 2 diabetes prevention/Da Qing study.md|Da Qing study]]
  • [[Finnish DPS]]
  • [[Metformin prevention]]
  • [[Diabetes Prevention and Remission/Type 2 diabetes remission/DiRECT trial.md|DiRECT trial]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Facts
TeplizumabAnti-CD3 monoclonal antibody · Delays stage 3 T1D by ~2 years in stage 2 (≥2 autoantibodies + dysglycaemia) · FDA approved
T1D immunotherapyAbatacept, rituximab, alefacept, verapamil, IL-2, ATG/G-CSF · Mostly delay, not prevent
DPP (US)Lifestyle 58% reduction · Metformin 31% reduction · Weight loss 7% + 150min/wk activity
Da Qing (China)Diet, exercise, diet+exercise · 43% reduction at 20y · CVD/mortality benefit
Finnish DPSIntensive lifestyle · 58% reduction · Weight loss, fibre, fat reduction, exercise
Metformin preventionBMI ≥35, age <60, women with GDM history · Less effective than lifestyle
DiRECT trialLow-calorie diet (825-853 kcal/d) 12-20wk → food reintroduction → maintenance · 46% remission at 1y, 36% at 2y
Remission criteriaHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%) off all glucose-lowering meds ≥3 months · ADA/Diabetes UK consensus
Low-calorie dietFormula meal replacements (soups/shakes) · 15kg mean weight loss → remission
MaintenanceContinued weight maintenance, physical activity, regular monitoring · Relapse if weight regain

TopicKey Facts
DKA diagnosisGlucose >11.1 · Ketonaemia >3 mmol/L or significant ketonuria · pH <7.3 · Bicarbonate <15
DKA severityMild: pH 7.25-7.30, K+ >3.5 · Moderate: pH 7.0-7.24, K+ 3.0-3.5 · Severe: pH <7.0, K+ <3.0
DKA management1) Fluids: 0.9% NaCl 1L/hr ×1-2h then adjust · 2) Insulin: FRII 0.1U/kg/hr · 3) K+: replace per level · 4) Bicarb if pH <7.0 · 5) Monitor: glucose q1h, ketones q2-4h, VBG q2-4h
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9 mmol/L · On SGLT2i · Same ketone/pH criteria · Stop SGLT2i
HHS diagnosisGlucose >30 mmol/L · Osmolality >320 mOsm/kg · pH >7.3 · Bicarb >18 · Minimal ketones
HHS vs DKAHHS: older, type 2, higher glucose, higher osmolality, no acidosis, higher mortality (10-20% vs 1-5%)
HHS managementFluids priority (lower insulin 0.05U/kg/hr) · Monitor osmolality drop <3 mOsm/kg/hr
Hypoglycaemia levelsLevel 1: <3.9 mmol/L · Level 2: <3.0 mmol/L · Level 3: severe (requires assistance)
Glucagon1mg IM/SC adult · 0.5mg <25kg child · Nasal 3mg · IV dextrose 10-20% if IV access

topics.title: Chapter Moc
topics.html:
Document Type: MOC (Map of Content) (Chapter-Level Overview)
Davidson Alignment: Chapter 21: Diabetes Mellitus
Chapter Number: 21
Target Exams: FCPS, MRCP Part 1, MRCP Part 2, PACES
Style: Sea Knowledge comprehensive integrated note with MCQs/SBAs/Flashcards/Tracker
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep
This is the Master Overview for Chapter 21: Diabetes Mellitus. It provides the big-picture roadmap — study priorities, key algorithms, viva topics, cross-references to all atomic topics, and progress tracking. Read this FIRST, then drill into atomic topics using the hierarchy file.

1. Study Plan & Prioritisation

This chapter contains 68 atomic topics organised into hubs. Below is the recommended study priority for Diabetes Mellitus.

Tier 1 — Core High-Yield (Master first — covers ~80% of exam marks)

  • Definitions, classifications, and severity criteria
  • First-line investigation algorithms
  • First-line management and emergency protocols
  • Common clinical syndromes and bedside recognition

Tier 2 — High-Probability (Solidify second — ~15% of exam marks)

  • Scoring systems and risk stratification
  • Differential diagnoses and mimics
  • Pharmacology: indications, contraindications, doses
  • Device and procedural indications

Tier 3 — Differentiators (Polishing — ~5% of exam marks)

  • Rare conditions and eponymous syndromes
  • Pathophysiology deep-dive
  • Latest trial evidence and guidelines
  • Genetic and molecular mechanisms

Method: Master Tier 1 → Read all atomic topics in order → Drill MCQs/SBAs → Use spaced repetition tracker below.

2. Original Medicine Note Content (Preserved)

The following is preserved from the Medicine source Diabetes MOC.md. It contains the author-curated study plans, scoring systems, viva topics, and progress trackers from the original source.

Template

  • [[../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

Chapter Hierarchy

  • [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Study Plan / Topic Queue

Core High-Yield Topics (FCPS/MRCP Priority)

  • [ ] Classification & Diagnostic Criteria — ADA/WHO/EASD criteria, HbA1c vs glucose, prediabetes categories, monogenic/secondary diabetes red flags
  • [ ] Type 1 Diabetes Pathophysiology — Autoimmune destruction, staging (1-3), autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA), LADA, genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR3/DR4)
  • [ ] Type 2 Diabetes Pathophysiology — Insulin resistance, beta-cell failure, incretin defect, lipotoxicity/glucotoxicity, TCF7L2 and polygenic risk
  • [ ] Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) — Diagnostic criteria (glucose, ketones, pH, bicarbonate), severity grading, management protocol (fluids, insulin, K+, monitoring), cerebral oedema, euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2i)
  • [ ] Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS) — Diagnostic criteria, HHS vs DKA differentiation, management (lower insulin, higher fluids), mortality predictors
  • [ ] Hypoglycaemia — Classification (level 1/2/3), glucose counter-regulation failure, hypoglycaemia unawareness, glucagon (IM/IN/auto-injector), hospital insulin errors
  • [ ] Type 1 Insulin Therapy — Basal-bolus principles, insulin analogues (rapid: lispro/aspart/glulisine, long: glargine U-100/U-300, degludec, ultra-long), CSII/pump therapy, carbohydrate counting, CGM-guided dosing
  • [ ] Type 2 Treatment Algorithm — ADA/EASD 2023+ consensus, NICE NG28, CVOT-guided (SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA), CKD/HF-guided therapy, metabolic surgery indications
  • [ ] Oral Glucose-Lowering Agents — Metformin (contraindications, B12, lactic acidosis), sulfonylureas (hypoglycaemia, weight), DPP-4i (neutral), SGLT2i (CV/renal benefit, genital infections, euglycaemic DKA), TZDs (fluid retention, fracture, bladder cancer), alpha-glucosidase, meglitinides
  • [ ] Injectable Non-Insulin Therapy — GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide, CV benefit, weight loss, pancreatitis risk), dual GIP/GLP-1 (tirzepatide), amylin analogues
  • [ ] Insulin in Type 2 Diabetes — Basal initiation, basal-bolus intensification, pre-mixed, concentrated insulins (U-200/U-300/U-500), degludec vs glargine U-300
  • [ ] Diabetic Retinopathy — NPDR vs PDR, ETDRS grading, diabetic macular oedema, screening intervals, treatment (laser, anti-VEGF: ranibizumab/aflibercept, vitrectomy)
  • [ ] Diabetic Kidney Disease (DKD) — KDIGO staging (GFR + albuminuria), ACR categories, GFR categories, management (ACEi/ARB max dose, SGLT2i, finerenone, GLP-1 RA), proteinuria reduction targets
  • [ ] Diabetic Neuropathy — DSPN (stocking-glove), autonomic (cardiovascular: HRV, orthostatic; GI: gastroparesis; GU: ED, bladder), screening (10g monofilament, vibration, ANNS), neuropathic pain (duloxetine, pregabalin, gabapentin, amitriptyline)
  • [ ] Diabetic Foot Disease — IWGDF/Scottish risk classification (low/moderate/high/active), Wagner/University of Texas DFU classification, offloading (TCC gold standard), infection (PEDIS), revascularisation, Charcot neuroarthropathy (Eichenholtz), MDT amputation prevention
  • [ ] Cardiovascular Disease in Diabetes — Diabetes as CV risk equivalent, QRISK3/ASCVD Pooled Cohort, statin intensity (high-intensity atorvastatin 40-80mg), BP targets (<130/80 ADA/ESC, <140/90 NICE), aspirin primary prevention (10-year risk >10%), SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for CV risk reduction
  • [ ] Heart Failure in Diabetes — HFrEF vs HFpEF, SGLT2i (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced/Preserved, DELIVER), GLP-1 RA (FIGHT, STEP-HFpEF), diagnostic challenges (diastolic dysfunction)
  • [ ] Diabetes in Pregnancy — GDM screening (IADPSG 75g OGTT, NICE risk factors + OGTT), targets (FBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L), management (diet → metformin → insulin), pre-existing DM (pre-conception HbA1c <48mmol/mol, folic acid 5mg), postpartum OGTT 6-13 weeks
  • [ ] Diabetes in Elderly — Frailty-guided HbA1c targets (Fit: <53, Mild frailty: <58, Moderate/severe: <64), deintensification/deprescribing, hypoglycaemia avoidance, care home management
  • [ ] Diabetes in CKD/Dialysis — HbA1c pitfalls (falsely low in CKD/ESRD, use fructosamine/GA), drug dosing adjustments (metformin eGFR cut-offs, SGLT2i eGFR thresholds, GLP-1 RA safe, insulin dose reduction), dialysis glucose management
  • [ ] Monogenic Diabetes — MODY (HNF1A MODY3: sulfonylurea responsive, GCK MODY2: no treatment, HNF4A MODY1), neonatal diabetes (KCNJ11/ABCC8: sulfonylurea responsive), mitochondrial diabetes (m.3243A>G), genetic testing indications
  • [ ] Secondary Diabetes — Type 3c (pancreatic: chronic pancreatitis, cancer, post-pancreatectomy, CF), endocrine (Cushing, acromegaly, thyrotoxicosis, phaeochromocytoma, glucagonoma), drug-induced (steroids, antipsychotics, calcineurin inhibitors, ICI, protease inhibitors, thiazides)
  • [ ] Type 2 Diabetes Prevention & Remission — DPP (lifestyle 58%, metformin 31%), Da Qing, Finnish DPS, DiRECT trial (46% remission at 1 year, 36% at 2 years), remission criteria (HbA1c <48mmol/mol off glucose-lowering meds x3 months), maintenance
  • [ ] Diabetes Technology — rtCGM vs isCGM, CGM metrics (TIR >70%, TBR <4%, TAR <25%, GMI, CV <36%), hybrid closed-loop (670G, 780G, CamAPS FX, Loop), connected pens, digital platforms
  • [ ] Monitoring & Quality of Care — Individualised HbA1c targets, annual review (retinopathy, nephropathy ACR/eGFR, neuropathy/foot, CV risk), structured education (DAFNE type 1, DESMOND/X-PERT type 2), psychosocial (diabetes distress, depression, eating disorders/diabulimia)

Key Algorithms & Scoring Systems (Memorize for Viva)

Algorithm/ScoreUse CaseKey Cut-offs
ADA/WHO Diagnostic CriteriaDiabetes diagnosisFPG ≥7.0, 2h-OGTT ≥11.1, HbA1c ≥48mmol/mol (6.5%), random ≥11.1 + symptoms
Prediabetes CriteriaRisk stratificationIFG 6.1-6.9, IGT 7.8-11.0, HbA1c 39-47mmol/mol (5.7-6.4%)
DKA SeverityDKA triageMild: pH 7.25-7.30, K+ >3.5; Moderate: pH 7.0-7.24, K+ 3.0-3.5; Severe: pH <7.0, K+ <3.0
HHS CriteriaHHS diagnosisGlucose >30mmol/L, osmolality >320mOsm/kg, pH >7.3, bicarbonate >18, no significant ketonaemia
KDIGO DKD StagingDKD prognosisG1-G5 (GFR) × A1-A3 (ACR); e.g., G3aA2 = moderate risk
ETDRS Retinopathy SeverityRetinopathy grading10-12: no DR; 20-35: mild/mod NPDR; 43-53: severe NPDR; 61+: PDR
IWGDF Foot RiskFoot risk stratification0: no LOPS/PAD; 1: LOPS; 2: LOPS + PAD/deformity; 3: history ulcer/amputation
Wagner DFU ClassificationUlcer depth0: pre-ulcer; 1: superficial; 2: deep to tendon; 3: bone/abscess; 4: forefoot gangrene; 5: whole foot gangrene
University of Texas DFUUlcer depth + infection + ischaemiaGrade 0-3 × Stage A-D (A=clean, B=infected, C=ischaemic, D=infected+ischaemic)
CHA₂DS₂-VAScAF stroke risk (often asked in DM)CHF, HTN, Age≥75, DM, Stroke/TIA, Vascular, Age 65-74, Sex
ADA/EASD 2023 AlgorithmT2DM treatment selectionASCVD/HF/CKD → SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; no CV → metformin + lifestyle → add based on comorbidities
NICE NG28 T2DM PathwayUK T2DM algorithmMetformin → dual → triple → insulin; SGLT2i if ASCVD/HF/CKD
CGM Targets (ATTD)CGM interpretationTIR 70-180mg/dL >70%; TBR <54mg/dL <4%; TAR >180mg/dL <25%; CV <36%
Diabetes Remission CriteriaRemission definitionHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%) off all glucose-lowering meds ≥3 months
Folic Acid in Pre-existing DM PregnancyNeural tube defect prevention5mg daily from pre-conception to 12 weeks gestation

High-Yield Viva Topics (FCPS/MRCP)

Must-Know Definitions

  • [ ] Diabetes mellitus (ADA/WHO) vs impaired glucose regulation
  • [ ] DKA vs HHS vs mixed DKA-HHS
  • [ ] DKA severity grading (mild/moderate/severe)
  • [ ] DKD staging (KDIGO G1-G5 × A1-A3)
  • [ ] Retinopathy: NPDR vs PDR vs DMO
  • [ ] Foot risk: IWGDF 0-3 vs Wagner 0-5 vs UT classification
  • [ ] Hypoglycaemia levels (Level 1: <3.9, Level 2: <3.0, Level 3: severe requiring assistance)
  • [ ] Diabetes remission (ADA/Diabetes UK consensus)
  • [ ] LADA vs classic type 1 vs type 2
  • [ ] MODY vs neonatal diabetes vs type 1 vs type 2

Must-Know Doses/Durations

  • [ ] DKA: 0.9% NaCl 1L/hr initially then adjusted; fixed-rate insulin infusion 0.1U/kg/hr; K+ replacement (40mmol/L if K+ 3.5-5.5, 60mmol/L if <3.5, hold if >5.5); bicarbonate if pH <7.0
  • [ ] HHS: 0.9% NaCl 1L/hr (lower insulin: 0.05U/kg/hr); monitor osmolality drop <3mOsm/kg/hr
  • [ ] Hypoglycaemia: Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg/child; nasal glucagon 3mg; oral carbs 15-20g if conscious
  • [ ] Metformin: Start 500mg OD, titrate to 2g/day max; hold if eGFR <30, caution 30-45
  • [ ] SGLT2i: Empagliflozin 10/25mg, dapagliflozin 10mg, canagliflozin 100/300mg; eGFR cut-offs (initiate ≥45/30, continue ≥20/15)
  • [ ] GLP-1 RA: Semaglutide SC 0.25→0.5→1mg weekly; oral 3→7→14mg daily; liraglutide 0.6→1.2→1.8mg daily; dulaglutide 0.75/1.5mg weekly
  • [ ] Tirzepatide: 2.5→5→7.5→10→12.5→15mg weekly
  • [ ] Insulin analogues: Rapid (lispro/aspart/glulisine) 0-15min pre-meal; Long (glargine U-100/U-300, degludec) once daily; Pre-mixed BD
  • [ ] Folic acid 5mg OD pre-conception to 12 weeks in pre-existing DM pregnancy
  • [ ] Aspirin 75mg OD primary prevention if 10-year ASCVD risk >10% (ADA) or age >50 with risk factor (ESC)
  • [ ] Statin: Atorvastatin 40-80mg (high-intensity) for primary prevention age 40-75 with DM

Quick Reference Cards (One-Page)

  • [ ] DKA Management Protocol — Fluids, insulin, K+, bicarbonate, monitoring, cerebral oedema
  • [ ] HHS Management Protocol — Fluids, low-dose insulin, osmolality monitoring
  • [ ] Hypoglycaemia Algorithm — Conscious vs unconscious, glucagon, IV dextrose, prevention
  • [ ] T2DM Treatment Algorithm (ADA/EASD) — CVOT-guided, CKD/HF-guided, stepwise
  • [ ] SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA Indication Matrix — ASCVD, HF, CKD, obesity, hypoglycaemia risk
  • [ ] DKD Staging & Management — KDIGO heat map, ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, finerenone, GLP-1 RA
  • [ ] Retinopathy Screening & Referral — ETDRS grading, referral thresholds
  • [ ] Diabetic Foot Risk & Referral — IWGDF classification, urgent vs routine referral
  • [ ] GDM Diagnosis & Management — IADPSG vs NICE, targets, metformin vs insulin, postpartum
  • [ ] Monogenic Diabetes Red Flags — Age <25, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies, GCK vs HNF1A features
  • [ ] Diabetes in Elderly HbA1c Targets — Frailty categories, deintensification triggers
  • [ ] Diabetes in CKD Drug Dosing — Metformin eGFR, SGLT2i eGFR, GLP-1 RA, insulin reduction
  • [ ] CGM Metrics & Interpretation — TIR/TBR/TAR/GMI/CV, AGP report key features
  • [ ] Diabetes Remission Pathway — DiRECT criteria, low-calorie diet, maintenance

6. Chapter-Level SBAs (Single Best Answer)

These SBAs test application of Diabetes Mellitus-specific scoring systems and clinical decision algorithms.

SBA1. A patient with diabetes diagnosis requires risk stratification using ADA/WHO Diagnostic Criteria. What is the clinical significance of the threshold value?

A. ADA/WHO Diagnostic Criteria < threshold = no clinical action needed

B. ADA/WHO Diagnostic Criteria ≥ threshold = FPG ≥7.0, 2h-OGTT ≥11.1, HbA1c ≥48mmol/mol (6.5%), random ≥11.1 + symptoms

C. ADA/WHO Diagnostic Criteria score is purely prognostic without therapeutic implications

D. ADA/WHO Diagnostic Criteria should only be calculated by specialists

E. ADA/WHO Diagnostic Criteria replaces clinical judgement entirely

Answer: B
Rationale: ADA/WHO Diagnostic Criteria thresholds guide clinical decisions: FPG ≥7.0, 2h-OGTT ≥11.1, HbA1c ≥48mmol/mol (6.5%), random ≥11.1 + symptoms.

SBA2. A patient with risk stratification requires risk stratification using Prediabetes Criteria. What is the clinical significance of the threshold value?

A. Prediabetes Criteria < threshold = no clinical action needed

B. Prediabetes Criteria ≥ threshold = IFG 6.1-6.9, IGT 7.8-11.0, HbA1c 39-47mmol/mol (5.7-6.4%)

C. Prediabetes Criteria score is purely prognostic without therapeutic implications

D. Prediabetes Criteria should only be calculated by specialists

E. Prediabetes Criteria replaces clinical judgement entirely

Answer: B
Rationale: Prediabetes Criteria thresholds guide clinical decisions: IFG 6.1-6.9, IGT 7.8-11.0, HbA1c 39-47mmol/mol (5.7-6.4%).

SBA3. A patient with dka triage requires risk stratification using DKA Severity. What is the clinical significance of the threshold value?

A. DKA Severity < threshold = no clinical action needed

B. DKA Severity ≥ threshold = Mild: pH 7.25-7.30, K+ >3.5; Moderate: pH 7.0-7.24, K+ 3.0-3.5; Severe: pH <7.0, K+ <3.0

C. DKA Severity score is purely prognostic without therapeutic implications

D. DKA Severity should only be calculated by specialists

E. DKA Severity replaces clinical judgement entirely

Answer: B
Rationale: DKA Severity thresholds guide clinical decisions: Mild: pH 7.25-7.30, K+ >3.5; Moderate: pH 7.0-7.24, K+ 3.0-3.5; Severe: pH <7.0, K+ <3.0.

7. Flashcards — Active Recall (Chapter-Specific)

Cover the answer, try to recall, then reveal. These flashcards cover the most frequently tested Diabetes Mellitus facts.

Card 1. ADA/WHO Diagnostic Criteria — what does it score and what are the key thresholds?

Answer: Diabetes diagnosis. FPG ≥7.0, 2h-OGTT ≥11.1, HbA1c ≥48mmol/mol (6.5%), random ≥11.1 + symptoms.

Card 2. Prediabetes Criteria — what does it score and what are the key thresholds?

Answer: Risk stratification. IFG 6.1-6.9, IGT 7.8-11.0, HbA1c 39-47mmol/mol (5.7-6.4%).

Card 3. DKA Severity — what does it score and what are the key thresholds?

Answer: DKA triage. Mild: pH 7.25-7.30, K+ >3.5; Moderate: pH 7.0-7.24, K+ 3.0-3.5; Severe: pH <7.0, K+ <3.0.

Card 4. HHS Criteria — what does it score and what are the key thresholds?

Answer: HHS diagnosis. Glucose >30mmol/L, osmolality >320mOsm/kg, pH >7.3, bicarbonate >18, no significant ketonaemia.

Card 5. KDIGO DKD Staging — what does it score and what are the key thresholds?

Answer: DKD prognosis. G1-G5 (GFR) × A1-A3 (ACR); e.g., G3aA2 = moderate risk.

topics.hub_name: Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus
topics.title: Diagnostic Criteria
topics.html:
[!info]
FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) = Diabetes — primary diagnostic test per ADA/WHO. Requires confirmatory repeat unless unequivocal hyperglycaemia with acute metabolic decompensation.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State FPG diagnostic cut-offs for diabetes and prediabetes
  • [ ] Apply confirmatory testing rules (repeat on separate day)
  • [ ] Interpret FPG in context of symptoms and other tests
  • [ ] Recognise pre-analytical factors affecting FPG accuracy
  • [ ] Counsel on screening intervals and populations

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionFasting plasma glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) after ≥8h caloric restriction
Prediabetes (IFG)FPG 6.1–6.9 mmol/L (110–125 mg/dL)
NormalFPG <6.1 mmol/L (<110 mg/dL)
Global Prevalence537M adults (2021) → 783M by 2045 (IDF); ~90% type 2
UK Prevalence~4.9M diagnosed (2023); ~1M undiagnosed
Peak AgeType 2: >40y; Type 1: bimodal (4–7y, 10–14y)
Sex RatioSlight male predominance in type 2
Risk FactorsObesity, family history, ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean), gestational DM, PCOS, antipsychotics, steroids
WHO 2006 / ADA 2024 alignment: Identical FPG cut-offs. HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) equally valid.

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
Asymptomatic~50% at diagnosisIncidental finding on screening/health check
Classic osmotic symptomsCommon if marked hyperglycaemiaPolyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, weight loss, fatigue, blurred vision
InfectionsCommonRecurrent candidiasis, UTIs, skin infections
Acute metabolic decompensationType 1: 25–30% at DxDKA (T1DM), HHS (T2DM elderly)
Red Flags: Ketosis at presentation → think Type 1 / LADA; rapid weight loss + osmotic symptoms in adult → LADA; pancreatic cancer (new-onset DM >50y + weight loss).

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SystemCategoriesKey Features / Prognosis
ADA/WHO Diagnostic CriteriaNormal: FPG <6.1 mmol/LLow risk
IFG (Prediabetes): FPG 6.1–6.9 mmol/L5–10%/yr progression to T2DM
Diabetes: FPG ≥7.0 mmol/LConfirm on separate day unless symptomatic + random ≥11.1
Aetiological ClassificationType 1Autoimmune β-cell destruction
Type 2Insulin resistance + β-cell failure
Hybrid (LADA)Adult-onset, GAD65+, slow progression
Monogenic (MODY)Autosomal dominant, <25y, non-obese
Secondary (Type 3c, endocrine, drug)Pancreatic, Cushing, steroids, etc.
GDMHyperglycaemia first detected in pregnancy
Diagnostic Algorithm: Symptomatic → Random glucose ≥11.1 = DM (no repeat needed). Asymptomatic → Two abnormal tests required (same test on different day, or two different tests e.g., FPG + HbA1c).

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details / Findings
FPGPrimary diagnostic / screening≥7.0 mmol/L = DM; 6.1–6.9 = IFG; <6.1 = normal. Pre-analytical: True fasting ≥8h; avoid stress/illness; sample in fluoride tube (grey top); process ≤1h or centrifuge promptly (glycolysis ↓glucose 5–7%/hr at room temp).
HbA1cAlternative diagnostic / monitoring≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) = DM; 39–47 = prediabetes. NGSP/DCCT standardised. Limitations: Haemoglobinopathies, anaemia, CKD, pregnancy, recent transfusion.
OGTT (75g)Gold standard (research/GDM)2h ≥11.1 mmol/L = DM; 7.8–11.0 = IGT. Rarely used for routine T2DM diagnosis.
Random glucose + symptomsAcute presentation≥11.1 mmol/L with polyuria/polydipsia = DM (no confirm needed).
Autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)Aetiological classification≥1 positive = autoimmune (Type 1/LADA).
C-peptideResidual β-cell functionLow/undetectable = Type 1; preserved = Type 2/MODY.
⬇ Clinical Pathway
Clinical Suspicion / Screening
↓
Asymptomatic?
↓
FPG ≥7.0 OR HbA1c ≥48
Random glucose ≥11.1 + symptoms
↓
Confirmatory test same/different day
Diagnosis: Diabetes
↓
Repeat in 3-6 months
Aetiological classification: autoantibodies, C-peptide, clinical context

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness, MI, stroke, surgery → resolves; no prior dysglycaemia; HbA1c normal
Steroid-induced hyperglycaemiaKnown steroid use; post-prandial predominance; resolves with taper
Pancreatic diabetes (Type 3c)Exocrine insufficiency (low faecal elastase), chronic pancreatitis/CT changes, no autoantibodies
MODYAge <25, non-obese, strong FH (3 generations), negative autoantibodies, C-peptide preserved
Gestational diabetesFirst detected in pregnancy; OGTT diagnostic; resolves postpartum (but 50% lifetime T2DM risk)
Renal glycosuriaNormal FPG, glycosuria at low glucose threshold (SGLT2 mutation) — benign

7. Management

Acute / Inpatient Management

StepInterventionDose / Details
1If symptomatic + random ≥11.1 → diagnose immediatelyNo confirmatory test needed; start treatment per type
2If DKA/HHS → emergency protocolSee DKA/HHS notes
3New diagnosis without ketosis → outpatient workupFPG/HbA1c confirm, autoantibodies, C-peptide, education

Chronic / Outpatient Management

SettingInterventionDose / Monitoring
Prediabetes (IFG/IGT)Intensive lifestyle (7% wt loss, 150min/wk)FPG/HbA1c annually; metformin if BMI ≥35, age <60, GDM hx
Type 2 DMMetformin + lifestyle 1st lineHbA1c 3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
Type 1 DMBasal-bolus insulin + carb countingHbA1c 3-monthly; CGM if available
ScreeningADA: age ≥35 or BMI ≥25 + risk factorRepeat q3y if normal
Screening Algorithm: ADA: ≥35y OR BMI ≥25 (23 Asian) + risk factor → FPG/HbA1c/OGTT. NICE: Risk score (age, ethnicity, BMI, FH) → if high risk → HbA1c/FPG.

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
FPG cut-offsNormal <6.1; IFG 6.1–6.9; DM ≥7.0 mmol/L
Confirmatory ruleAsymptomatic: two abnormal tests (same test different day, or two different tests). Symptomatic + random ≥11.1: single test sufficient.
Pre-analyticalFluoride tube, ≥8h fast, process ≤1h (glycolysis artefact)
HbA1c equivalent≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) = DM; 39–47 = prediabetes
OGTT2h ≥11.1 = DM; 7.8–11.0 = IGT (rarely used for T2DM dx)
Screening ageADA: ≥35y; NICE: risk score → test if high
IFG vs IGTIFG = hepatic IR; IGT = muscle IR; both = higher risk

9. Viva Questions (MRCP PACES / FCPS)

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the diagnostic FPG for diabetes?≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) after ≥8h fast
What is impaired fasting glucose (IFG)?FPG 6.1–6.9 mmol/L (110–125 mg/dL)
When do you need a confirmatory test?Asymptomatic patient — require two abnormal results (same test on different day, or FPG + HbA1c). Symptomatic + random ≥11.1 = single test sufficient.
What are pre-analytical requirements for FPG?True fast ≥8h (water only), fluoride-oxalate tube (grey top), process ≤1h or centrifuge immediately (glycolysis lowers glucose 5–7%/hr at room temp).
Diagnostic criteria for diabetes (ADA/WHO)?1) FPG ≥7.0, 2) 2h-OGTT ≥11.1, 3) HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%), 4) Random ≥11.1 + symptoms. Any one confirmed = DM.
How does IFG differ from IGT pathophysiologically?IFG = hepatic insulin resistance (excess HGP); IGT = peripheral/muscle insulin resistance; combined IFG+IGT = highest progression risk (~15–20%/yr).
Screening recommendations for asymptomatic adults?ADA: age ≥35 OR BMI ≥25 (23 Asian) + ≥1 risk factor. NICE: validated risk score (e.g., Leicester, QDiabetes) → if high risk, test HbA1c/FPG. Repeat q3y if normal.
Red flags for secondary/monogenic diabetes?Age <25 + non-obese + strong FH + negative autoantibodies → MODY; new-onset >50y + weight loss → pancreatic cancer; steroid use → drug-induced; Cushing/acromegaly features → endocrine.

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
FPG vs HbA1c for diagnosisBoth valid; HbA1c = convenience (no fast), but invalid in anaemia/haemoglobinopathy/CKD/pregnancy. FPG = gold standard in those conditions.
One test vs two testsSymptomatic = one test. Asymptomatic = two tests (per ADA/WHO).
IFG vs IGTIFG = fasting hepatic IR; IGT = post-load muscle IR. Different pathophysiology, same prediabetes category.

Mnemonic: FASTING

  • Fasting ≥8h (water only)
  • Analyte: Plasma glucose (not whole blood)
  • Sample: Fluoride-oxalate (grey top)
  • Timing: Process ≤1h (glycolysis)
  • Interpret: <6.1 normal, 6.1–6.9 IFG, ≥7.0 DM
  • Needs confirm if asymptomatic
  • Glucose random + symptoms = single test dx

11. Mind Map

Fasting Plasma Glucose
Definition
FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L = DM
IFG 6.1–6.9
Classification
Normal / Prediabetes / DM
Aetiology: T1/T2/LADA/MODY/Secondary/GDM
Clinical

DKA/HHS at presentation
Diagnosis
FPG / HbA1c / OGTT / Random+symptoms
Confirmatory rule (2 tests if asymptomatic)
Pre-analytical: fluoride tube, fast, process fast
DDx
Stress / Steroid / Type 3c / MODY / GDM
Management
Lifestyle + metformin (IFG)

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionFPG ≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) after ≥8h fast = Diabetes
Key TestFPG (fluoride tube, true fast, process ≤1h)
ClassificationNormal <6.1; IFG 6.1–6.9; DM ≥7.0 mmol/L
Acute MgmtSymptomatic + random ≥11.1 → diagnose & treat immediately
Chronic MgmtIFG: lifestyle ± metformin; DM: type-specific algorithm
Key ScoreADA screening: age ≥35 or BMI ≥25 + risk factor
ComplicationsMissed diagnosis → DKA/HHS; overdiagnosis (stress hyperglycaemia) → unnecessary treatment
PrognosisIFG → 5–10%/yr progression to T2DM; lifestyle halves risk

Diagnosis: Diabetes
↓
Repeat in 3-6 months
Aetiological classification: autoantibodies, C-peptide, clinical context

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness, MI, stroke, surgery → resolves; no prior dysglycaemia; HbA1c normal
Steroid-induced hyperglycaemiaKnown steroid use; post-prandial predominance; resolves with taper
Pancreatic diabetes (Type 3c)Exocrine insufficiency (low faecal elastase), chronic pancreatitis/CT changes, no autoantibodies
MODYAge <25, non-obese, strong FH (3 generations), negative autoantibodies, C-peptide preserved
Gestational diabetesFirst detected in pregnancy; OGTT diagnostic; resolves postpartum (but 50% lifetime T2DM risk)
Renal glycosuriaNormal FPG, glycosuria at low glucose threshold (SGLT2 mutation) — benign

7. Management

Acute / Inpatient Management

StepInterventionDose / Details
1If symptomatic + random ≥11.1 → diagnose immediatelyNo confirmatory test needed; start treatment per type
2If DKA/HHS → emergency protocolSee DKA/HHS notes
3New diagnosis without ketosis → outpatient workupFPG/HbA1c confirm, autoantibodies, C-peptide, education

Chronic / Outpatient Management

SettingInterventionDose / Monitoring
Prediabetes (IFG/IGT)Intensive lifestyle (7% wt loss, 150min/wk)FPG/HbA1c annually; metformin if BMI ≥35, age <60, GDM hx
Type 2 DMMetformin + lifestyle 1st lineHbA1c 3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
Type 1 DMBasal-bolus insulin + carb countingHbA1c 3-monthly; CGM if available
ScreeningADA: age ≥35 or BMI ≥25 + risk factorRepeat q3y if normal
Screening Algorithm: ADA: ≥35y OR BMI ≥25 (23 Asian) + risk factor → FPG/HbA1c/OGTT. NICE: Risk score (age, ethnicity, BMI, FH) → if high risk → HbA1c/FPG.

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
FPG cut-offsNormal <6.1; IFG 6.1–6.9; DM ≥7.0 mmol/L
Confirmatory ruleAsymptomatic: two abnormal tests (same test different day, or two different tests). Symptomatic + random ≥11.1: single test sufficient.
Pre-analyticalFluoride tube, ≥8h fast, process ≤1h (glycolysis artefact)
HbA1c equivalent≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) = DM; 39–47 = prediabetes
OGTT2h ≥11.1 = DM; 7.8–11.0 = IGT (rarely used for T2DM dx)
Screening ageADA: ≥35y; NICE: risk score → test if high
IFG vs IGTIFG = hepatic IR; IGT = muscle IR; both = higher risk

9. Viva Questions (MRCP PACES / FCPS)

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the diagnostic FPG for diabetes?≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) after ≥8h fast
What is impaired fasting glucose (IFG)?FPG 6.1–6.9 mmol/L (110–125 mg/dL)
When do you need a confirmatory test?Asymptomatic patient — require two abnormal results (same test on different day, or FPG + HbA1c). Symptomatic + random ≥11.1 = single test sufficient.
What are pre-analytical requirements for FPG?True fast ≥8h (water only), fluoride-oxalate tube (grey top), process ≤1h or centrifuge immediately (glycolysis lowers glucose 5–7%/hr at room temp).
Diagnostic criteria for diabetes (ADA/WHO)?1) FPG ≥7.0, 2) 2h-OGTT ≥11.1, 3) HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%), 4) Random ≥11.1 + symptoms. Any one confirmed = DM.
How does IFG differ from IGT pathophysiologically?IFG = hepatic insulin resistance (excess HGP); IGT = peripheral/muscle insulin resistance; combined IFG+IGT = highest progression risk (~15–20%/yr).
Screening recommendations for asymptomatic adults?ADA: age ≥35 OR BMI ≥25 (23 Asian) + ≥1 risk factor. NICE: validated risk score (e.g., Leicester, QDiabetes) → if high risk, test HbA1c/FPG. Repeat q3y if normal.
Red flags for secondary/monogenic diabetes?Age <25 + non-obese + strong FH + negative autoantibodies → MODY; new-onset >50y + weight loss → pancreatic cancer; steroid use → drug-induced; Cushing/acromegaly features → endocrine.

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
FPG vs HbA1c for diagnosisBoth valid; HbA1c = convenience (no fast), but invalid in anaemia/haemoglobinopathy/CKD/pregnancy. FPG = gold standard in those conditions.
One test vs two testsSymptomatic = one test. Asymptomatic = two tests (per ADA/WHO).
IFG vs IGTIFG = fasting hepatic IR; IGT = post-load muscle IR. Different pathophysiology, same prediabetes category.

Mnemonic: FASTING

  • Fasting ≥8h (water only)
  • Analyte: Plasma glucose (not whole blood)
  • Sample: Fluoride-oxalate (grey top)
  • Timing: Process ≤1h (glycolysis)
  • Interpret: <6.1 normal, 6.1–6.9 IFG, ≥7.0 DM
  • Needs confirm if asymptomatic
  • Glucose random + symptoms = single test dx

11. Mind Map

Fasting Plasma Glucose
Definition
FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L = DM
IFG 6.1–6.9
Classification
Normal / Prediabetes / DM
Aetiology: T1/T2/LADA/MODY/Secondary/GDM
Clinical
Asymptomatic 50%
Osmotic symptoms
DKA/HHS at presentation
Diagnosis
FPG / HbA1c / OGTT / Random+symptoms
Confirmatory rule (2 tests if asymptomatic)
Pre-analytical: fluoride tube, fast, process fast
DDx
Stress / Steroid / Type 3c / MODY / GDM
Management
Lifestyle + metformin (IFG)
Type-specific Rx
Screening

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionFPG ≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) after ≥8h fast = Diabetes
Key TestFPG (fluoride tube, true fast, process ≤1h)
ClassificationNormal <6.1; IFG 6.1–6.9; DM ≥7.0 mmol/L
Acute MgmtSymptomatic + random ≥11.1 → diagnose & treat immediately
Chronic MgmtIFG: lifestyle ± metformin; DM: type-specific algorithm
Key ScoreADA screening: age ≥35 or BMI ≥25 + risk factor
ComplicationsMissed diagnosis → DKA/HHS; overdiagnosis (stress hyperglycaemia) → unnecessary treatment
PrognosisIFG → 5–10%/yr progression to T2DM; lifestyle halves risk

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt

topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, oral-glucose-tolerance-test-(ogtt), fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State OGTT protocol (75g anhydrous glucose, fasting, 2h sample)
  • [ ] Interpret OGTT results for diabetes, IGT, IFG
  • [ ] Know indications for OGTT (GDM, Discordant FPG/HbA1c, Research)
  • [ ] Recognise limitations (variability, poor reproducibility)
  • [ ] Apply IADPSG/NICE/ADA GDM diagnostic criteria

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Definition75g OGTT: Fasting + 2h post-load glucose; Gold standard for glucose tolerance
Protocol≥8h fast → baseline FPG → 75g anhydrous glucose in 250–300ml water over 5min → 2h sample (patient seated, no smoking)
Diagnostic Cut-offs (WHO/ADA)2h ≥11.1 mmol/L = Diabetes; 2h 7.8–11.0 = IGT; FPG 6.1–6.9 = IFG
GDM Criteria (IADPSG)FBG ≥5.1, 1h ≥10.0, 2h ≥8.5 mmol/L (one abnormal = GDM)
GDM Criteria (NICE)FBG ≥5.6 OR 2h ≥7.8 mmol/L (if risk factors present)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
Screening T2DMRareOnly if FPG/HbA1c discordant
GDM screeningUniversal (IADPSG) / Risk-based (NICE)24–28 weeks gestation
Research/epidemiologyStandardWHO criteria

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SystemCategoriesKey Features
WHO/ADA 2h GlucoseNormal: <7.8 mmol/LLow risk
IGT: 7.8–11.0 mmol/L↑T2DM risk (5–10%/yr); CVD risk
Diabetes: ≥11.1 mmol/LConfirm if asymptomatic
Combined IFG/IGTIsolated IFGHepatic IR
Isolated IGTMuscle IR
IFG + IGTHighest progression risk (15–20%/yr)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
FPGPart of OGTT / alternative dx≥7.0 = DM; 6.1–6.9 = IFG
2h GlucosePrimary OGTT outcome≥11.1 = DM; 7.8–11.0 = IGT
HbA1cAlternative dx≥48 mmol/mol = DM; 39–47 = prediabetes
GDM OGTT75g, 3 timepointsIADPSG: FBG≥5.1, 1h≥10.0, 2h≥8.5 (one value)
⬇ Clinical Pathway
75g OGTT
↓
2h Glucose?
↓
Diabetes
IGT
Normal
↓
Asymptomatic?
Lifestyle intervention ± metformin
↓
Confirm repeat OGTT or FPG/HbA1c
Diagnosis confirmed

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Physiological glucose intolerance of pregnancyNormal OGTT outside pregnancy; resolves postpartum
Dumping syndromeEarly peak (30–60min), then reactive hypoglycaemia; post-gastrectomy
AcromegalyParadoxical GH rise on OGTT; IGF-1 elevated
PhaeochromocytomaCatecholamine-induced hyperglycaemia; plasma metanephrines
Renal glycosuriaNormal FPG/2h, glycosuria at low threshold (SGLT2 mutation)

7. Management

SettingInterventionDetails
IGTIntensive lifestyle (DPP: 7% wt loss, 150min/wk) ± metformin (if BMI≥35, age<60, GDM hx)Annual OGTT/FPG/HbA1c
GDMDiet → Metformin (if FBG>5.5 or 1h>7.0) → Insulin; Targets: FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4Postpartum OGTT 6–13 weeks
Discordant FPG/HbA1cOGTT as tie-breakerUse if HbA1c unreliable

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
OGTT protocol75g anhydrous glucose, fasting ≥8h, 2h sample, patient seated
Cut-offs2h ≥11.1 = DM; 7.8–11.0 = IGT; <7.8 = normal
GDM (IADPSG)FBG≥5.1, 1h≥10.0, 2h≥8.5 mmol/L — ONE abnormal = GDM
GDM (NICE)Risk factors → OGTT; FBG≥5.6 OR 2h≥7.8
When to use OGTTGDM; discordant FPG/HbA1c; research; equivocal HbA1c
ReproducibilityPoor (CV ~15%) — less reproducible than FPG/HbA1c

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
OGTT protocol for diabetes diagnosis?75g anhydrous glucose in 250–300ml water over 5min after ≥8h fast; measure FPG and 2h glucose; patient seated, no smoking
2h glucose cut-offs?Normal <7.8; IGT 7.8–11.0; DM ≥11.1 mmol/L
GDM diagnostic criteria (IADPSG)?FBG ≥5.1, 1h ≥10.0, 2h ≥8.5 mmol/L — ONE abnormal value sufficient
GDM diagnostic criteria (NICE)?Risk factors (BMI>30, prev GDM, FH, ethnicity) → OGTT; FBG ≥5.6 OR 2h ≥7.8
When is OGTT preferred over FPG/HbA1c?GDM; discordant FPG/HbA1c; HbA1c unreliable (haemoglobinopathy, anaemia, CKD, pregnancy)
Difference between IFG and IGT?IFG = hepatic IR (↑HGP); IGT = peripheral/muscle IR; IFG+IGT = highest risk
What is the reproducibility of OGTT?Poor — CV ~15%; less reproducible than FPG or HbA1c

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
OGTT vs FPG for T2DM dxFPG/HbA1c preferred (convenience, reproducibility); OGTT reserved for GDM, discordance, research
IADPSG vs NICE GDMIADPSG: universal, 1 abnormal value, lower thresholds, more diagnoses; NICE: risk-based, 2 thresholds
IGT vs IFGDifferent pathophysiology: IGT = muscle IR; IFG = hepatic IR

Mnemonic: OGTT-2HR

  • Oral 75g anhydrous glucose
  • Glucose measured at fasting and 2h
  • Two-hour ≥11.1 = DM
  • Two-hour 7.8–11.0 = IGT
  • 2h sample critical (not 1h for non-GDM)
  • HbA1c/FPG preferred for routine T2DM
  • Reserved for GDM, discordance, research

topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, random-glucose, diagnosis, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Diagnostic criterion for symptomatic hyperglycaemia; no repeat needed if classic symptoms + random glucose ≥11.1"

see_also: ["Fasting plasma glucose", "HbA1c diagnosis", "Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)", "Diagnostic criteria"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Random Glucose with Symptoms

[!info]
Random plasma glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) + classic hyperglycaemia symptoms = Diabetes — No confirmatory repeat required per ADA/WHO.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State diagnostic threshold and required symptoms
  • [ ] Define "classic symptoms" of hyperglycaemia
  • [ ] Explain when confirmatory testing is NOT needed
  • [ ] Distinguish from stress hyperglycaemia
  • [ ] Apply in acute presentation (DKA/HHS)

2. Diagnostic Criteria

ComponentRequirement
Random plasma glucose≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) — any time of day, regardless of last meal
SymptomsClassic hyperglycaemia triad: Polyuria + Polydipsia + Weight loss (± polyphagia, blurred vision, fatigue)
Confirmatory repeatNOT REQUIRED if both criteria met unequivocally

ADA 2024 / WHO 2006: "In a patient with classic symptoms of hyperglycaemia or hyperglycaemic crisis, a random plasma glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) confirms diabetes."

3. Classic Symptoms (Must Have)

SymptomMechanism
PolyuriaOsmotic diuresis from glycosuria (> renal threshold ~10 mmol/L)
PolydipsiaThirst from hyperosmolality + volume depletion
Weight lossCatabolism (lipolysis/proteolysis) from insulin deficiency ± glycosuria calorie loss
PolyphagiaCellular starvation despite hyperglycaemia
Blurred visionOsmotic lens swelling
FatigueImpaired glucose utilisation

All three (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) = classic triad. Two of three often sufficient.

4. When Repeat Confirmation IS Still Needed

ScenarioAction
No symptoms (asymptomatic screening)Repeat on separate day (FPG, HbA1c, or OGTT)
Equivocal symptoms (e.g. only fatigue)Confirm with second test
Random glucose 11.1–13.8 mmol/L (mild)Consider confirmatory FPG/HbA1c next day
Stress hyperglycaemia (acute illness, MI, stroke, steroids)Do not diagnose — repeat when stable
ChildrenOften require confirmatory test per ISPAD

5. Stress Hyperglycaemia — Key Differential

FeatureStress HyperglycaemiaNew-Onset Diabetes
ContextAcute illness, trauma, surgery, steroidsOften subacute, outpatient
GlucoseUsually <15 mmol/LOften >15, can be >30
KetonesUsually negative/traceOften positive (if T1DM)
HbA1cNormal or mildly elevated (reflects chronic)Usually elevated (>48 mmol/mol)
ResolutionResolves with illness recoveryPersists
Insulin needTransient (sliding scale)Ongoing

Rule: Never diagnose diabetes based on glucose during acute illness without follow-up.

6. Application in Acute Presentations

DKA: Random glucose usually >14 mmol/L (often 20–40) + ketosis + acidosis → diagnosis clear

HHS: Random glucose >30 mmol/L (often 40–60) + severe dehydration + no significant ketosis

New T1DM in child: Polyuria/polydipsia/weight loss + random glucose ≥11.1 → diagnostic

Steroid-induced: May meet criteria during treatment; classify as drug-induced diabetes if persistent

7. Comparison of Diagnostic Criteria

TestThresholdSymptoms Needed?Repeat Needed?
Random + symptoms≥11.1 mmol/LYes (classic)No
FPG≥7.0 mmol/LNoYes (separate day)
HbA1c≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%)NoYes (separate day)
OGTT 2-hr≥11.1 mmol/LNoYes (separate day)

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Random glucose diagnostic threshold≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL)
Symptoms requiredPolyuria + Polydipsia + Weight loss (classic triad)
No repeat needed ifClassic symptoms + random glucose ≥11.1
Stress hyperglycaemiaDo NOT diagnose diabetes — repeat when well
HbA1c in new presentationUsually elevated (>48) if chronic; may be normal in acute T1DM/DKA
SteroidsClassify as drug-induced if persistent after taper

9. Management After Diagnosis

  1. Type clarification: Autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA) if T1DM suspected
  2. Ketone check: If T1DM or glucose >15 — check β-hydroxybutyrate
  3. Baseline: HbA1c, renal function, lipids, LFTs, urine ACR
  4. Education: Sick day rules, hypoglycaemia awareness, DVLA notification (if on insulin)
  5. Referral: DSN, dietitian, structured education (DAFNE/DESMOND)

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, secondary-diabetes, pancreatic, endocrine, drug-induced, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Secondary diabetes often missed; pancreatic (type 3c) common after pancreatitis; steroid-induced very common; endocrine causes need specific treatment"

see_also: ["Pancreatic diabetes (type 3c)", "Endocrine diabetes", "Drug-induced diabetes", "Corticosteroid-induced diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Secondary Diabetes (Pancreatic, Endocrine, Drug-Induced) — Epidemiology & Classification

[!info]
Secondary diabetes = Diabetes due to identifiable cause — 1–5% of diabetes; always consider in atypical presentation (lean, rapid onset, associated features).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify secondary diabetes by aetiology
  • [ ] Recognise clinical clues for pancreatic vs endocrine vs drug-induced
  • [ ] State key causes in each category
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic approach
  • [ ] Explain management differences from T1/T2

2. Classification (WHO/ADA)

CategoryKey ExamplesProportion
Pancreatic (Type 3c)Chronic pancreatitis, pancreatectomy, CF, pancreatic cancer, haemochromatosis~1–2% (under-diagnosed)
EndocrineCushing's, acromegaly, phaeochromocytoma, glucagonoma, thyrotoxicosis, somatostatinomaRare (<1%)
Drug/Chemical inducedGlucocorticoids, antipsychotics, calcineurin inhibitors, protease inhibitors, thiazides, β-blockers, immune checkpoint inhibitorsCommon (iatrogenic)
Genetic syndromesMODY, mitochondrial, Wolfram, lipodystrophySee monogenic
InfectionsCMV, Coxsackie B (rare)Very rare
AutoimmuneStiff person syndrome, anti-insulin receptor antibodiesVery rare

3. Pancreatic Diabetes (Type 3c) — Most Common Secondary

Aetiology & Prevalence

Cause% of Type 3cDiabetes Prevalence in Condition
Chronic pancreatitis~80%30–50% (↑ with duration/severity)
Pancreatectomy10–15%100% total; 20–40% partial
Cystic fibrosis (CFRD)5%50% by age 30
Pancreatic cancer<5%50–80% (often new-onset clue to cancer)
Haemochromatosis<5%50–60% (bronze diabetes)
Acute pancreatitisRareTransient or permanent

Diagnostic Criteria (Ewald et al. 2012)

All 3 required:

  1. Pancreatic pathology (imaging/histology) OR pancreatectomy
  2. Absence of T1DM autoantibodies (GAD, IA-2, ZnT8)
  3. At least 2 of: (a) Exocrine insufficiency (low faecal elastase), (b) Impaired incretin secretion (low GLP-1), (c) No insulin resistance (HOMA-IR normal), (d) Pancreatic atrophy on imaging

4. Endocrine Diabetes

ConditionMechanismDiabetes PrevalenceKey Features
Cushing's syndromeCortisol → IR + ↑ gluconeogenesis20–50%Central obesity, striae, HTN, hypokalaemia
AcromegalyGH → IR + ↑ hepatic glucose output20–40%Acral growth, sweating, macroglossia, HTN
PhaeochromocytomaCatecholamines → ↑ glycogenolysis, ↓ insulin10–20%Paroxysmal HTN, headaches, sweating
GlucagonomaGlucagon → ↑ gluconeogenesis50–80%Necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, DVT
ThyrotoxicosisT3 → ↑ absorption, ↑ gluconeogenesis, IR2–5%Tachycardia, tremor, weight loss, goitre
SomatostatinomaSomatostatin → ↓ insulin, ↓ glucagon, ↓ incretins~100%Gallstones, steatorrhoea, achlorhydria

5. Drug-Induced Diabetes — Very Common

Glucocorticoids (Most Common Iatrogenic Cause)

AspectDetails
Prevalence20–50% on chronic steroids develop hyperglycaemia
PatternPost-prandial predominant (↑ hepatic output, ↓ peripheral uptake); fasting often normal initially
Dose relationship>7.5mg prednisolone/day ↑ risk; cumulative dose matters
ReversibilityOften improves on taper; may unmask underlying T2DM

Antipsychotics (2nd Generation)

DrugRiskMechanism
Clozapine, OlanzapineHigh (↑ weight, direct IR)H1 antagonism, weight gain, direct β-cell toxicity
Quetiapine, RisperidoneModerateWeight gain
Aripiprazole, Lurasidone, ZiprasidoneLowWeight neutral

Calcineurin Inhibitors (Transplant)

DrugDiabetes IncidenceMechanism
Tacrolimus15–30% (higher than ciclosporin)↓ Insulin transcription, β-cell toxicity
Ciclosporin10–20%Similar

Other Agents

Drug/ClassRiskNotes
Protease inhibitors (HIV)ModerateLipodystrophy, IR
Thiazides (high dose)Low-moderateHypokalaemia → ↓ insulin secretion
β-blockers (non-selective)Low↓ Insulin secretion, weight gain
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1/PD-L1)EmergingAutoimmune T1DM-like (fulminant)
StatinsSmall ↑ riskDose-dependent; CVD benefit outweighs

6. Clinical Clues Suggesting Secondary Diabetes

PresentationThink Of
Lean, rapid onset, abdominal painPancreatitis, pancreatic cancer, type 3c
Weight gain, moon face, striae, HTN, hypokalaemiaCushing's (or steroid use)
Acral enlargement, sweating, macroglossiaAcromegaly
Paroxysmal HTN, palpitations, sweatingPhaeochromocytoma
Rash (necrolytic migratory erythema), DVT, weight lossGlucagonoma
New diabetes on antipsychotics/steroids/tacrolimusDrug-induced
Post-transplant new diabetes (PTDM)Tacrolimus, steroids
Bronze skin, hepatomegaly, cardiomyopathyHaemochromatosis
Diabetes + pancreatic exocrine insufficiencyType 3c (faecal elastase low)

7. Management Principles

CategoryApproach
Pancreatic (Type 3c)Insulin often needed (loss of insulin + glucagon + incretins); PERT for exocrine insufficiency; avoid sulfonylureas (hypoglycaemia risk from glucagon deficiency)
EndocrineTreat underlying cause (surgery for Cushing's/acromegaly/phaeo); diabetes often resolves/improves
Drug-inducedMinimise dose, switch agents if possible; steroids: basal insulin + QDS correction; antipsychotics: switch to low-risk agent; transplant: consider conversion from tacrolimus

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Type 3c = pancreatic diabetesChronic pancreatitis (80%), CF, pancreatectomy, cancer, haemochromatosis
Type 3c diagnosisPancreatic pathology + Ab negative + 2/4 criteria (low elastase, low GLP-1, no IR, atrophy)
Steroid diabetes patternPost-prandial predominant; fasting normal initially
Tacrolimus > ciclosporin for diabetes risk post-transplant
Antipsychotics: Clozapine/olanzapine highest risk
Cushing's: 20–50% get diabetes; treat cause
Acromegaly: 20–40% diabetes; GH → IR
Phaeochromocytoma: Catecholamines → glycogenolysis; 10–20% diabetes
Glucagonoma: Rash (necrolytic migratory erythema) + diabetes + DVT
ICI-induced diabetes: Anti-PD-1 → fulminant T1DM (low C-peptide, +ve Abs)
Treatment Type 3c: Insulin + PERT; avoid sulfonylureas (no glucagon counter-regulation)
