topics.hub_name: Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus
topics.title: Epidemiology And Classification
topics.html:
[!info]
Secondary diabetes = Diabetes due to identifiable cause — 1–5% of diabetes; always consider in atypical presentation (lean, rapid onset, associated features).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify secondary diabetes by aetiology
  • [ ] Recognise clinical clues for pancreatic vs endocrine vs drug-induced
  • [ ] State key causes in each category
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic approach
  • [ ] Explain management differences from T1/T2

2. Classification (WHO/ADA)

CategoryKey ExamplesProportion
Pancreatic (Type 3c)Chronic pancreatitis, pancreatectomy, CF, pancreatic cancer, haemochromatosis~1–2% (under-diagnosed)
EndocrineCushing's, acromegaly, phaeochromocytoma, glucagonoma, thyrotoxicosis, somatostatinomaRare (<1%)
Drug/Chemical inducedGlucocorticoids, antipsychotics, calcineurin inhibitors, protease inhibitors, thiazides, β-blockers, immune checkpoint inhibitorsCommon (iatrogenic)
Genetic syndromesMODY, mitochondrial, Wolfram, lipodystrophySee monogenic
InfectionsCMV, Coxsackie B (rare)Very rare
AutoimmuneStiff person syndrome, anti-insulin receptor antibodiesVery rare

3. Pancreatic Diabetes (Type 3c) — Most Common Secondary

Aetiology & Prevalence

Cause% of Type 3cDiabetes Prevalence in Condition
Chronic pancreatitis~80%30–50% (↑ with duration/severity)
Pancreatectomy10–15%100% total; 20–40% partial
Cystic fibrosis (CFRD)5%50% by age 30
Pancreatic cancer<5%50–80% (often new-onset clue to cancer)
Haemochromatosis<5%50–60% (bronze diabetes)
Acute pancreatitisRareTransient or permanent

Diagnostic Criteria (Ewald et al. 2012)

All 3 required:

  1. Pancreatic pathology (imaging/histology) OR pancreatectomy
  2. Absence of T1DM autoantibodies (GAD, IA-2, ZnT8)
  3. At least 2 of: (a) Exocrine insufficiency (low faecal elastase), (b) Impaired incretin secretion (low GLP-1), (c) No insulin resistance (HOMA-IR normal), (d) Pancreatic atrophy on imaging

4. Endocrine Diabetes

ConditionMechanismDiabetes PrevalenceKey Features
Cushing's syndromeCortisol → IR + ↑ gluconeogenesis20–50%Central obesity, striae, HTN, hypokalaemia
AcromegalyGH → IR + ↑ hepatic glucose output20–40%Acral growth, sweating, macroglossia, HTN
PhaeochromocytomaCatecholamines → ↑ glycogenolysis, ↓ insulin10–20%Paroxysmal HTN, headaches, sweating
GlucagonomaGlucagon → ↑ gluconeogenesis50–80%Necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, DVT
ThyrotoxicosisT3 → ↑ absorption, ↑ gluconeogenesis, IR2–5%Tachycardia, tremor, weight loss, goitre
SomatostatinomaSomatostatin → ↓ insulin, ↓ glucagon, ↓ incretins~100%Gallstones, steatorrhoea, achlorhydria

5. Drug-Induced Diabetes — Very Common

Glucocorticoids (Most Common Iatrogenic Cause)

AspectDetails
Prevalence20–50% on chronic steroids develop hyperglycaemia
PatternPost-prandial predominant (↑ hepatic output, ↓ peripheral uptake); fasting often normal initially
Dose relationship>7.5mg prednisolone/day ↑ risk; cumulative dose matters
ReversibilityOften improves on taper; may unmask underlying T2DM

Antipsychotics (2nd Generation)

DrugRiskMechanism
Clozapine, OlanzapineHigh (↑ weight, direct IR)H1 antagonism, weight gain, direct β-cell toxicity
Quetiapine, RisperidoneModerateWeight gain
Aripiprazole, Lurasidone, ZiprasidoneLowWeight neutral

Calcineurin Inhibitors (Transplant)

DrugDiabetes IncidenceMechanism
Tacrolimus15–30% (higher than ciclosporin)↓ Insulin transcription, β-cell toxicity
Ciclosporin10–20%Similar

Other Agents

Drug/ClassRiskNotes
Protease inhibitors (HIV)ModerateLipodystrophy, IR
Thiazides (high dose)Low-moderateHypokalaemia → ↓ insulin secretion
β-blockers (non-selective)Low↓ Insulin secretion, weight gain
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1/PD-L1)EmergingAutoimmune T1DM-like (fulminant)
StatinsSmall ↑ riskDose-dependent; CVD benefit outweighs

6. Clinical Clues Suggesting Secondary Diabetes

PresentationThink Of
Lean, rapid onset, abdominal painPancreatitis, pancreatic cancer, type 3c
Weight gain, moon face, striae, HTN, hypokalaemiaCushing's (or steroid use)
Acral enlargement, sweating, macroglossiaAcromegaly
Paroxysmal HTN, palpitations, sweatingPhaeochromocytoma
Rash (necrolytic migratory erythema), DVT, weight lossGlucagonoma
New diabetes on antipsychotics/steroids/tacrolimusDrug-induced
Post-transplant new diabetes (PTDM)Tacrolimus, steroids
Bronze skin, hepatomegaly, cardiomyopathyHaemochromatosis
Diabetes + pancreatic exocrine insufficiencyType 3c (faecal elastase low)

7. Management Principles

CategoryApproach
Pancreatic (Type 3c)Insulin often needed (loss of insulin + glucagon + incretins); PERT for exocrine insufficiency; avoid sulfonylureas (hypoglycaemia risk from glucagon deficiency)
EndocrineTreat underlying cause (surgery for Cushing's/acromegaly/phaeo); diabetes often resolves/improves
Drug-inducedMinimise dose, switch agents if possible; steroids: basal insulin + QDS correction; antipsychotics: switch to low-risk agent; transplant: consider conversion from tacrolimus

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Type 3c = pancreatic diabetesChronic pancreatitis (80%), CF, pancreatectomy, cancer, haemochromatosis
Type 3c diagnosisPancreatic pathology + Ab negative + 2/4 criteria (low elastase, low GLP-1, no IR, atrophy)
Steroid diabetes patternPost-prandial predominant; fasting normal initially
Tacrolimus > ciclosporin for diabetes risk post-transplant
Antipsychotics: Clozapine/olanzapine highest risk
Cushing's: 20–50% get diabetes; treat cause
Acromegaly: 20–40% diabetes; GH → IR
Phaeochromocytoma: Catecholamines → glycogenolysis; 10–20% diabetes
Glucagonoma: Rash (necrolytic migratory erythema) + diabetes + DVT
ICI-induced diabetes: Anti-PD-1 → fulminant T1DM (low C-peptide, +ve Abs)
Treatment Type 3c: Insulin + PERT; avoid sulfonylureas (no glucagon counter-regulation)

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["HbA1c diagnosis"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

HbA1c diagnosis

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State HbA1c diagnostic cut-offs for diabetes and prediabetes
  • [ ] Identify conditions where HbA1c is unreliable (haemoglobinopathies, anaemia, CKD, pregnancy)
  • [ ] Choose alternative tests when HbA1c invalid
  • [ ] Interpret HbA1c vs FPG/OGTT discordance
  • [ ] Apply screening and monitoring intervals

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionHbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) = Diabetes; 39–47 mmol/mol (5.7–6.4%) = Prediabetes
PrincipleNon-enzymatic glycation of haemoglobin β-chain; reflects mean glucose over ~120 days (RBC lifespan)
UnitsIFCC: mmol/mol; NGSP/DCCT: % (DCCT = IFCC × 0.0915 + 2.15)
Global StandardisationNGSP certified, traceable to DCCT

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
Asymptomatic screeningCommonIncidental finding
Symptomatic hyperglycaemiaIf markedOsmotic symptoms, weight loss
Falsely elevatedIn conditions belowAnaemia (iron/B12/folate deficiency), asplenia, haemoglobinopathies (HbF, HbS trait)
Falsely loweredIn conditions belowHaemolytic anaemia, blood loss, CKD/ESRD (↓RBC lifespan), ESA therapy, pregnancy (2nd/3rd trim), recent transfusion

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SystemCategoriesKey Features
ADA/WHO DiagnosticNormal: <39 mmol/mol (<5.7%)Low risk
Prediabetes: 39–47 mmol/mol (5.7–6.4%)5–10%/yr progression
Diabetes: ≥48 mmol/mol (≥6.5%)Confirm on separate day unless symptomatic
Monitoring TargetsStandard: <53 mmol/mol (7.0%)Most adults
Tighter: <48 mmol/mol (6.5%)If achievable without hypo, short duration
Looser: <58–64 mmol/mol (7.5–8.0%)Elderly, frail, comorbidities, hypo unawareness
⬇ Clinical Pathway
HbA1c Result
↓
Diagnostic Range?
↓
Diabetes - confirm if asymptomatic
Prediabetes - lifestyle intervention
Normal - repeat per screening interval
↓
Conditions affecting accuracy?
↓
Use FPG/OGTT instead
Diagnosis confirmed

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
HbA1cPrimary dm dx / monitoring≥48 mmol/mol = DM; 39-47 = prediabetes. No fasting needed. Standardised (NGSP/DCCT).
FPGAlternative dx / when HbA1c invalid≥7.0 mmol/L = DM. Use if haemoglobinopathy, anaemia, CKD, pregnancy, recent transfusion.
OGTTGold standard / GDM75g, 2h ≥11.1 = DM. Use if HbA1c/FPG discordant.
Fructosamine / Glycated AlbuminAlternative in CKD/haemoglobinopathyReflects 2–3 week glucose; no DCCT standardisation.

6. Differential Diagnosis (Conditions Affecting HbA1c)

ConditionEffect on HbA1cMechanism
Iron/B12/folate deficiency anaemia↑ Falsely high↓Erythropoiesis → older RBCs → more glycation
Haemolytic anaemia / blood loss↓ Falsely low↓RBC lifespan → less time for glycation
CKD / ESRD / ESA therapy↓ Falsely low↓RBC lifespan, ESA stimulates young RBCs
Pregnancy (2nd/3rd tri)↓ Falsely low↑Erythropoiesis, ↓RBC lifespan
Haemoglobinopathies (HbS, HbC, HbF, HbE)VariableAltered glycation kinetics, assay interference
Recent transfusion↓ Falsely lowDonor RBCs with normal glucose exposure
Vitamin E / C high dose↓ Falsely lowAntioxidant inhibits glycation
Opioids / Salicylates↑ Falsely highChemical modification of Hb

7. Management

SettingInterventionDetails
Diabetes dxHbA1c ≥48 on two occasions (or one if symptomatic)Use FPG/OGTT if HbA1c unreliable
PrediabetesIntensive lifestyle (DPP protocol) ± metforminRepeat HbA1c annually
Monitoring established DMIndividualised target (see table)3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
CKD/haemoglobinopathyUse FPG + fructosamine/GAHbA1c not recommended

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Diagnostic cut-offsDM ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%); Prediabetes 39–47 (5.7–6.4%)
When HbA1c unreliableAnaemia, haemoglobinopathy, CKD/ESRD, pregnancy, recent transfusion, haemolysis, ESA
Alternative in CKDFructosamine (2–3 wk avg) or glycated albumin (3 wk); FPG/OGTT
ConversionIFCC (mmol/mol) = (NGSP% - 2.15) × 10.93; NGSP% = IFCC × 0.0915 + 2.15
Monitoring frequency3-monthly if changing therapy/not at target; 6-monthly if stable
Individualised targetsStandard <53 (7.0%); Tight <48 (6.5%); Loose <58–64 (7.5–8.0%)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
HbA1c diagnostic threshold for diabetes?≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) per ADA/WHO
When is HbA1c unreliable for diagnosis?Haemoglobinopathies, anaemia (iron/B12/folate), CKD/ESRD, pregnancy 2nd/3rd tri, recent transfusion, haemolysis, ESA therapy
What alternative tests in CKD?FPG, OGTT, fructosamine, glycated albumin
What does HbA1c reflect?Mean glucose over ~120 days (RBC lifespan); weighted to recent 30 days (50%)
Why falsely low in CKD?↓RBC lifespan + ESA → younger RBC population → less glycation time
Difference between IFCC and NGSP units?NGSP% = DCCT aligned; IFCC mmol/mol = SI unit; Conversion: NGSP = IFCC × 0.0915 + 2.15

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HbA1c vs FPG discordanceIf both available and discordant → repeat abnormal test; if still discordant → 3rd test (OGTT)
HbA1c in pregnancyNot recommended for diagnosis/monitoring; use OGTT (GDM) or FPG/CGM (pre-existing DM)
Fructosamine vs HbA1cFructosamine = 2–3 week avg; no DCCT standardisation; use when HbA1c invalid

Mnemonic: HBA1C FAILS

  • Haemoglobinopathies (HbS, HbC, HbF, HbE)
  • Blood loss / haemolysis
  • Anaemia (iron, B12, folate deficiency) → falsely HIGH
  • 1 (CKD/ESRD/ESA) → falsely LOW
  • CKD / pregnancy / transfusion → falsely LOW
  • Fructosamine/GA alternative
  • Assay interference (vit E/C, opioids, salicylates)
  • Iron deficiency → falsely HIGH
  • Low RBC lifespan → falsely LOW
  • Screening: not in pregnancy

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, impaired-fasting-glucose, ifg, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Common prediabetes category; screening and progression risk to T2DM"

see_also: ["Impaired glucose tolerance (IGT)", "Increased HbA1c risk category", "Type 2 diabetes mellitus", "Type 2 diabetes prevention"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Impaired Fasting Glucose (IFG)

[!info]
FPG 6.1–6.9 mmol/L (110–125 mg/dL) — Isolated IFG reflects hepatic insulin resistance; ~5–10%/year progression to T2DM.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State diagnostic criteria for IFG (ADA vs WHO differences)
  • [ ] Explain pathophysiology: hepatic insulin resistance vs muscle
  • [ ] Quantify progression risk to T2DM
  • [ ] Apply screening intervals and lifestyle intervention evidence
  • [ ] Recognise when to do OGTT (IFG + risk factors)

2. Diagnostic Criteria

GuidelineFPG RangeNotes
ADA 20245.6–6.9 mmol/L (100–125 mg/dL)Lower cut-off captures more at-risk
WHO 1999/20066.1–6.9 mmol/L (110–125 mg/dL)Higher cut-off, more specific
NICE CG126.1–6.9 mmol/LAligns with WHO

Key difference: ADA uses 5.6 mmol/L (100 mg/dL) lower threshold; WHO/NICE use 6.1 mmol/L (110 mg/dL). Both require confirmatory repeat test unless symptomatic hyperglycaemia.

3. Pathophysiology

Isolated IFG = Hepatic insulin resistance predominates:

  • Fasting hyperglycaemia from ↑ hepatic glucose output (HGO)
  • Normal muscle glucose disposal → normal 2-hr OGTT
  • Early β-cell compensation maintains post-prandial glucose

Isolated IGT = Muscle insulin resistance predominates:

  • Normal fasting (hepatic insulin sensitivity preserved)
  • Impaired post-prandial disposal → ↑ 2-hr glucose

Combined IFG+IGT = Highest progression risk (dual defect).

4. Progression Risk

CategoryAnnual Progression to T2DM5-Year Risk
Normal (FPG <5.6, 2h <7.8)<1%<5%
IFG alone (WHO)4–6%15–25%
IGT alone5–8%20–35%
IFG + IGT10–15%40–60%
HbA1c 39–47 mmol/mol (5.7–6.4%)~5–8%25–35%

Risk modifiers: BMI ≥30, family history, GDM history, PCOS, ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean), hypertension, dyslipidaemia.

5. Screening & Diagnosis

Who to screen (ADA/NICE):

  • Age ≥45 years (or ≥25 if South Asian/other high-risk ethnicity)
  • BMI ≥25 (≥23 Asian) + ≥1 risk factor
  • GDM history
  • PCOS, acanthosis nigricans, HIV, antipsychotics

Tests:

TestCut-offAdvantagesLimitations
FPG5.6–6.9 (ADA) / 6.1–6.9 (WHO)Cheap, convenient, reproducibleMisses isolated IGT; stress hyperglycaemia
HbA1c39–47 mmol/mol (5.7–6.4%)No fasting, stableMisses IFG/IGT; unreliable in anaemia, Hb variants, CKD
OGTT2-hr 7.8–11.0 mmol/LGold standard for IGTInconvenient, poor reproducibility (CV 15%)

Diagnostic algorithm:

Screen FPG or HbA1c
  ├─ Normal → Repeat 3-yearly
  ├─ IFG (5.6–6.9) → Do OGTT to exclude IGT/diabetes
  ├─ HbA1c 39–47 → Confirm with FPG/OGTT
  └─ Diabetes range → Confirm second test

6. Management — Lifestyle First

Evidence: DPP, Finnish DPS, Da Qing all show ~58% risk reduction with intensive lifestyle.

TargetIntervention
Weight5–7% body weight loss
DietReduced calorie, low fat, high fibre (Mediterranean/DASH)
Activity≥150 min/week moderate (e.g. brisk walking)
BehaviourStructured programme (DPP curriculum, DESMOND)

Metformin consideration (ADA):

  • Age 25–59, BMI ≥30, FPG ≥6.1, HbA1c ≥42 mmol/mol (6.0%)
  • Women with prior GDM
  • Reduces progression ~31% (vs 58% lifestyle)

7. Follow-up Intervals

FindingRepeat Testing
Normal3 years
IFG/IGT/HbA1c 39–47Annually
Previous GDM6–12 weeks postpartum, then 1–3 yearly

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ADA vs WHO IFG cut-offADA 5.6 (100), WHO 6.1 (110) mmol/L — know both
IFG vs IGT pathophysiologyIFG = hepatic IR; IGT = muscle IR
Progression riskCombined IFG+IGT > IGT alone > IFG alone
Metformin indicationAge 25–59, BMI ≥30, high-risk IFG
Screening interval IFGAnnual (not 3-yearly)
OGTT indicationIFG on FPG — to rule out IGT/diabetes

9. Differential Diagnosis

  • Stress hyperglycaemia (acute illness, steroids) — repeat when well
  • Early T2DM — HbA1c ≥48 or 2-hr ≥11.1
  • MODY (GCK) — Mild fasting hyperglycaemia 5.5–8.0, stable, family history, non-progressive
  • Type 1 diabetes (early) — Weight loss, ketosis, autoantibodies+

10. Complications if Untreated

  • Micro/macrovascular risk starts in prediabetes (DECODE, DECODA studies)
  • Retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy prevalence ↑ even at IFG/IGT
  • CVD risk ~1.5–2× normal

topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, impaired-glucose-tolerance, igt, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Higher progression risk than IFG; requires OGTT for diagnosis; key prevention target"

see_also: ["Impaired fasting glucose (IFG)", "Increased HbA1c risk category", "Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)", "Type 2 diabetes prevention"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT)

[!info]
2-hr OGTT 7.8–11.0 mmol/L (140–199 mg/dL) — Reflects muscle insulin resistance; higher T2DM progression risk than IFG alone.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State OGTT diagnostic criteria for IGT
  • [ ] Contrast pathophysiology vs IFG (muscle vs hepatic IR)
  • [ ] Quantify progression risk and CVD association
  • [ ] Apply lifestyle/metformin prevention evidence
  • [ ] Identify when IGT coexists with IFG (highest risk)

2. Diagnostic Criteria

TestIGT RangeNotes
2-hr OGTT (75g)7.8–11.0 mmol/L (140–199 mg/dL)FPG <7.0 mmol/L required
HbA1cNot diagnostic for IGTHbA1c 39–47 mmol/mol correlates but not diagnostic

Requirements: Standard 75g OGTT after ≥3 days normal diet, overnight fast. WHO and ADA criteria identical for IGT.

3. Pathophysiology

IGT = Skeletal muscle insulin resistance (primary defect):

  • Impaired insulin-stimulated glucose disposal in muscle
  • Hepatic insulin sensitivity relatively preserved → normal fasting glucose
  • β-cell compensation initially maintains fasting glucose
  • Post-prandial hyperglycaemia from failed muscle uptake

Contrast with IFG:

FeatureIFGIGT
Primary siteLiverMuscle
Fasting glucose↑ (6.1–6.9)Normal (<6.1)
2-hr glucoseNormal (<7.8)↑ (7.8–11.0)
Insulin secretion↓ Early phase↓ Late phase
FFA suppressionImpairedRelatively preserved

4. Progression Risk (Higher than IFG)

CategoryAnnual Progression10-Year Risk
IGT alone5–8%30–50%
IFG alone4–6%20–35%
IFG + IGT10–15%50–70%
IGT + HbA1c 42–47~12%>60%

DECODE study: IGT carries higher CVD mortality than IFG.

5. Clinical Significance

CVD risk: IGT independently predicts CVD (HR 1.5–2.0), similar to diabetes in some cohorts.

  • Post-challenge hyperglycaemia → oxidative stress, endothelial dysfunction
  • Associated with: hypertension, dyslipidaemia (high TG, low HDL), prothrombotic state

Microvascular risk: Retinopathy/nephropathy prevalence ↑ at IGT stage.

6. Screening & Diagnosis

When to do OGTT (NICE/ADA):

  • FPG 5.6–6.9 mmol/L (ADA IFG) or 6.1–6.9 (WHO IFG)
  • HbA1c 39–47 mmol/mol (5.7–6.4%)
  • High clinical suspicion despite normal FPG/HbA1c
  • GDM follow-up (6–12 weeks postpartum)
  • PCOS, antipsychotic use, post-transplant

OGTT procedure:

  1. 3 days normal carbohydrate intake (≥150g/day)
  2. 8–14 hr fast
  3. 75g anhydrous glucose in 250–300mL water over 5 min
  4. Sample at 0 and 120 min (some protocols 30, 60, 90, 120)

7. Management — Lifestyle Intervention

Evidence base (IGT populations):

TrialPopulationInterventionRisk Reduction
Da Qing (China)IGTDiet ± exercise ± both31–46% at 6 yr
Finnish DPSIGT + BMI>25Intensive lifestyle58% at 3.2 yr
DPP (USA)IGT + FPG 5.3–6.9Lifestyle vs metformin58% vs 31%

Targets (same as IFG):

  • Weight: 5–7% loss
  • Activity: ≥150 min/week moderate
  • Diet: Reduced fat (<30% kcal), high fibre, Mediterranean pattern

8. Pharmacological Prevention

Metformin (ADA indication for IGT):

  • BMI ≥30 (or ≥25 Asian), age 25–59
  • FPG ≥6.1 or HbA1c ≥42 mmol/mol (6.0%)
  • Prior GDM
  • Not NICE-recommended for prevention in UK (lifestyle only)

Other agents (evidence in IGT):

  • Acarbose (STOP-NIDDM): 25% reduction, GI side effects limit use
  • Pioglitazone (ACT NOW): 72% reduction but weight gain, HF risk, fractures
  • GLP-1 RA / SGLT2i: Emerging data, not licensed for prevention

9. Follow-up

StatusInterval
IGT confirmedAnnual OGTT or HbA1c+FPG
Post-GDM6–12 weeks (OGTT), then 1–3 yearly
High risk (IFG+IGT)6–12 monthly

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
IGT diagnosisOnly by OGTT — FPG/HbA1c cannot diagnose IGT
IGT vs IFG progressionIGT > IFG for T2DM progression
IGT CVD riskIndependent predictor, similar to diabetes
Metformin for preventionADA yes (high-risk IGT); NICE no
Da Qing / DPS / DPPAll in IGT populations — know risk reductions
OGTT prep3 days normal carbs, overnight fast

11. Differential Diagnosis

  • Early T2DM: 2-hr ≥11.1 mmol/L
  • Renal glycosuria: Low renal threshold → false high 2-hr
  • Dumping syndrome: Post-gastrectomy → rapid glucose peak then hypoglycaemia
  • Acromegaly/Cushing: Secondary IGT from hormone excess

12. Complications if Untreated

  • T2DM: 30–50% at 10 years
  • CVD: MI, stroke risk 1.5–2×
  • Microvascular: Early retinopathy, microalbuminuria
  • NAFLD/NASH progression

topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, hba1c-risk, prediabetes, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - HbA1c-based prediabetes category; screening standard; conditions affecting reliability"

see_also: ["Impaired fasting glucose (IFG)", "Impaired glucose tolerance (IGT)", "HbA1c diagnosis", "Conditions affecting HbA1c reliability"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Increased HbA1c Risk Category (Prediabetes by HbA1c)

[!info]
HbA1c 39–47 mmol/mol (5.7–6.4%) — ADA prediabetes category; convenient but misses isolated IFG/IGT; unreliable in haemoglobinopathies, anaemia, CKD, pregnancy.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State HbA1c prediabetes cut-offs (ADA vs NICE/WHO)
  • [ ] List conditions where HbA1c is falsely low/high
  • [ ] Choose confirmatory test when HbA1c discordant
  • [ ] Apply screening intervals for HbA1c-defined prediabetes
  • [ ] Recognise ethnic differences in HbA1c

2. Diagnostic Cut-offs

OrganisationDiabetesPrediabetes / High RiskNormal
ADA 2024≥48 mmol/mol (≥6.5%)39–47 mmol/mol (5.7–6.4%)<39 (<5.7%)
NICE NG28≥48 mmol/mol (≥6.5%)42–47 mmol/mol (6.0–6.4%)<42 (<6.0%)
WHO≥48 mmol/molNot endorsed for diagnosis—
Diabetes UK≥48 mmol/mol42–47 mmol/mol (high risk)<42

Key difference: ADA uses 39 mmol/mol (5.7%) lower threshold; NICE/Diabetes UK use 42 mmol/mol (6.0%).

3. Conditions Affecting HbA1c Reliability

ConditionEffect on HbA1cMechanismAlternative Test
Haemoglobinopathies (HbS, HbC, HbE, thalassaemia)Falsely LOWReduced RBC lifespan / variant HbFPG, OGTT, fructosamine
Iron deficiency anaemiaFalsely HIGH↑ RBC lifespan, altered glycationFPG, OGTT (treat iron first)
Haemolytic anaemiaFalsely LOW↓ RBC lifespanFPG, OGTT
Recent blood loss/transfusionFalsely LOW↓ Mean RBC ageFPG, OGTT (wait 3 months)
CKD (esp. dialysis)Falsely LOW↓ RBC lifespan, ESA useFPG, OGTT, fructosamine
Pregnancy (2nd/3rd trimester)Falsely LOW↑ RBC turnover, haemodilutionOGTT (diagnostic standard)
High-dose vitamin C/EFalsely LOWAntioxidant effect on glycationFPG, OGTT
Opioids, high-dose aspirinFalsely HIGHChemical modificationFPG, OGTT
SplenectomyFalsely HIGH↑ RBC lifespanFPG, OGTT

4. Ethnic Differences

  • African-Caribbean / Black: HbA1c ~0.3–0.4% (3–4 mmol/mol) higher at same mean glucose
  • South Asian: Slightly higher at same glucose
  • Implication: Same HbA1c cut-off may over-diagnose prediabetes in Black populations; consider FPG/OGTT confirmation

5. HbA1c vs FPG/OGTT Discordance

ScenarioInterpretationAction
HbA1c 39–47, FPG normalIsolated HbA1c elevationRepeat HbA1c; check for falsely high causes (IDA, splenectomy); do OGTT
HbA1c normal, FPG 5.6–6.9Isolated IFGDo OGTT to exclude IGT
HbA1c normal, 2-hr 7.8–11.0Isolated IGTConfirm with repeat OGTT
All three discordantAnalytical/pre-analytical issueRepeat all; check RBC indices, haemoglobinopathy screen

Concordance: Only ~50–70% agreement between HbA1c and FPG/OGTT for prediabetes.

6. Progression Risk

HbA1c Range5-Year T2DM RiskCVD Risk
<39 (<5.7%)<5%Baseline
39–41 (5.7–5.9%)9–15%1.2×
42–47 (6.0–6.4%)25–50%1.5–2.0×
≥48 (≥6.5%)Diabetes2–4×

7. Screening & Follow-up

Who to screen: Same as IFG/IGT (age ≥45, BMI ≥25 + risk factors, etc.)

Intervals:

ResultRepeat Interval
Normal (<39 ADA, <42 NICE)3 years
39–47 (ADA) / 42–47 (NICE)Annually
Previous GDM1–3 yearly

8. Management

Lifestyle (same evidence as IFG/IGT):

  • DPP, DPS, Da Qing all included HbA1c-defined prediabetes subsets
  • Weight loss 5–7%, 150 min/week activity, Mediterranean/DASH diet

Metformin (ADA):

  • HbA1c ≥42 mmol/mol (6.0%), BMI ≥30, age 25–59
  • Prior GDM
  • Not NICE-recommended in UK

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ADA vs NICE HbA1c prediabetesADA 39–47 (5.7–6.4%), NICE 42–47 (6.0–6.4%)
Falsely LOW HbA1cHaemoglobinopathies, haemolysis, CKD, pregnancy, recent transfusion
Falsely HIGH HbA1cIron deficiency, splenectomy, opioids
PregnancyOGTT only — HbA1c not diagnostic
AnaemiaTreat iron deficiency first, then recheck HbA1c
EthnicityBlack African-Caribbean: HbA1c reads higher at same glucose

10. When HbA1c Cannot Be Used (Must Use FPG/OGTT)

  1. Pregnancy (all trimesters for diagnosis; 1st trimester screening only)
  2. Haemoglobinopathies (HbSS, HbSC, thalassaemia major)
  3. Significant anaemia (Hb <100 g/L) — treat first
  4. CKD stage 4–5 / dialysis
  5. Recent blood transfusion / haemorrhage (≤3 months)
  6. Children and adolescents (limited validation)
  7. Acute illness / stress hyperglycaemia

11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, monogenic-diabetes, mody, neonatal, epidemiology, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - MODY 1-5% of diabetes; misdiagnosed as T1/T2; genetic testing indications; sulfonylurea responsiveness in HNF1A/HNF4A/GCK/ABCC8/KCNJ11"

see_also: ["Maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY)", "Neonatal diabetes", "MODY genetic testing indications", "GCK-MODY (MODY 2)", "HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3)", "HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Monogenic Diabetes (MODY, Neonatal) — Epidemiology & Classification

[!info]
Monogenic diabetes = Single gene defect causing diabetes — 1–5% of diabetes; often misdiagnosed as T1DM/T2DM; correct diagnosis changes management (sulfonylureas, no treatment).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State prevalence of monogenic diabetes
  • [ ] Classify MODY subtypes (GCK, HNF1A, HNF4A, HNF1B, etc.)
  • [ ] Distinguish neonatal diabetes (transient vs permanent)
  • [ ] Apply clinical criteria for genetic testing
  • [ ] Explain management implications per subtype

2. Prevalence & Proportion

CategoryProportion of DiabetesPrevalence
All monogenic1–5% (up to 6% in some cohorts)~80/100,000
MODY1–3% of diabetes~1/10,000
Neonatal diabetes<0.1%1/100,000–200,000 births
MitochondrialRare<1% of monogenic

Misdiagnosis rate: ~80% initially diagnosed as T1DM or T2DM.

3. MODY Classification (Top Subtypes)

MODYGeneProtein% of MODYKey FeaturesTreatment
MODY 2GCKGlucokinase30–50%Mild fasting hyperglycaemia (5.5–8.0), stable, non-progressive, no complicationsNone (diet only)
MODY 3HNF1ATranscription factor30–50%Progressive, glycosuria at low threshold, sulphurea-sensitive, ↑ CVD riskSulfonylureas (1st line)
MODY 1HNF4ATranscription factor5–10%Similar to HNF1A, macrocephaly, neonatal hypoglycaemiaSulfonylureas
MODY 5HNF1BTranscription factor5%Renal cysts, GU anomalies, pancreatic atrophy, gout, ↓ HDLInsulin often needed
MODY 4PDX1Transcription factor<1%Pancreatic agenesis (homozygous)Variable
MODY 6NEUROD1Transcription factor<1%RareVariable
MODY 7-14KLF11, CEL, PAX6, etc.VariousVery rare——

4. Clinical Features Suggesting MODY

FeatureMODYT1DMT2DM
Age at diagnosis<25 (often childhood/teens)<30>40 (usually)
Family historyAutosomal dominant (3 generations)Negative/sporadicOften positive but not Mendelian
AutoantibodiesNegativePositive (GAD, IA-2, ZnT8)Negative
C-peptidePreserved (low in HNF1A late)Low/absentNormal/high
BMINormal (except HNF1A later)Low/normalHigh
Insulin requirementNot initially (except HNF1B)ImmediateVariable/late
Sulfonylurea responseExcellent (HNF1A/4A)PoorModest
ComplicationsRare in GCK; typical in HNF1ATypicalTypical

5. Neonatal Diabetes (Diagnosis <6 Months)

TypeGeneProportionFeaturesTreatment
Transient (TNDM)6q24 (PLAGL1/HYMAI)~50%Remits by 18m, recurs adolescenceInsulin initially; may need later
Permanent (PNDM)KCNJ11 (Kir6.2)~30%Sulfonylurea-responsive (90%)High-dose sulfonylurea
ABCC8 (SUR1)~15%Sulfonylurea-responsiveHigh-dose sulfonylurea
INS~10%Not sulfonylurea-responsiveInsulin
GCK (homozygous)RareSevere hyperglycaemiaInsulin

Key: ALL neonatal diabetes <6 months should have genetic testing — changes management.

6. Mitochondrial Diabetes

FeatureDetails
Common mutationm.3243A>G (MELAS/MIDD)
InheritanceMaternal
Diabetes phenotypeUsually adult-onset, insulin-deficient, sensorineural deafness
Other featuresShort stature, cardiomyopathy, renal, GI dysmotility
TreatmentAvoid metformin (lactic acidosis risk); insulin often needed

7. Genetic Testing Indications

Test for MODY if: (NICE/ADA)

  • Diagnosed <25 years + any of:
  • Strong FHx (autosomal dominant pattern, 2+ generations)
  • Negative autoantibodies
  • Preserved C-peptide (>200 pmol/L at 3+ years)
  • No features of insulin resistance (normal BMI, no acanthosis)
  • Features of specific subtype (renal cysts → HNF1B; deafness → mitochondrial)

Test for neonatal diabetes if:

  • Diagnosis <6 months (mandatory)
  • Diagnosis 6–12 months + negative antibodies

Testing approach: Targeted panel (GCK, HNF1A, HNF4A, HNF1B, KCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, 6q24) → WES if negative.

8. Management Implications by Subtype

SubtypeTreatmentMonitoring
GCK-MODYNo pharmacotherapy; diet onlyAnnual HbA1c; pregnancy: consider insulin if 2-hr post-prandial >7.8
HNF1A/4A-MODYLow-dose sulfonylurea (gliclazide 20–80mg, glipizide)Excellent response; monitor hypoglycaemia; CVD risk ↑
HNF1B-MODYOften insulin (pancreatic atrophy)Renal US, LFTs, gout screen, Mg²⁺, lipids
KCNJ11/ABCC8 neonatalHigh-dose sulfonylurea (glibenclamide up to 1mg/kg/day)Neurodevelopmental monitoring; transition from insulin
Mito m.3243A>GAvoid metformin; insulin as neededAudiology, cardiac, renal screening

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Monogenic proportion1–5% of diabetes
Most common MODYGCK (MODY 2) — 30–50% = mild fasting hyperglycaemia, no treatment
Most common symptomatic MODYHNF1A (MODY 3) — 30–50% = sulfonylurea-responsive
HNF1B (MODY 5)Renal cysts + diabetes + genital anomalies + gout
Neonatal diabetes <6mMandatory genetic testing — KCNJ11/ABCC8 = sulfonylurea-responsive
Transient neonatal6q24 (PLAGL1) — remits by 18mo, recurs later
Mitochondrial m.3243A>GMELAS/MIDD; maternal inheritance; deafness; avoid metformin
Genetic testing criteriaDx <25y + FHx + Ab negative + preserved C-peptide
GCK in pregnancyNo treatment unless 2-hr >7.8; insulin if needed

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

topics.title: Autoimmune Beta Cell Destruction
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pathophysiology, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core pathophysiology topic"

see_also: ["Autoimmune beta-cell destruction", "Genetic susceptibility (HLA, INS, PTPN22)", "Environmental triggers", "Stages of type 1 diabetes (pre-symptomatic, symptomatic)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Autoimmune beta-cell destruction

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe the autoimmune mechanism of beta-cell destruction in T1DM
  • [ ] Identify the roles of CD4+/CD8+ T-cells, B-cells, and autoantibodies
  • [ ] Explain the concept of insulitis
  • [ ] Stage T1DM (pre-symptomatic to clinical)
  • [ ] Recognise LADA as slow-progressing autoimmune diabetes

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
ProcessT-cell mediated autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta-cells
TimelineMonths to years from autoimmunity onset to clinical diabetes
IncidenceRising 3-5% annually; peak age 10-14 years
GeneticsHLA-DR3/DR4-DQ2/DQ8 (40-50% heritability); 50+ non-HLA loci

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - pathophysiology)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Stages of T1DM (ADA/JDRF/Endocrine Society)

StageDefinitionGlucoseAutoantibodiesRisk of Clinical T1DM
Stage 1Pre-symptomatic autoimmunityNormal2+~100% lifetime
Stage 2Dysglycaemia (pre-diabetes)IFG/IGT2+~75% at 5 years
Stage 3Clinical T1DMHyperglycaemia2+100%

Autoantibodies

AntibodyTargetSensitivity (T1DM)Clinical Use
GAD65Glutamic acid decarboxylase 6570-80%Most common; LADA marker
IA-2Insulinoma-associated antigen 250-70%Predicts rapid progression
ZnT8Zinc transporter 860-80%Complements GAD65/IA-2
IAAInsulin autoantibodies40-60% (children)More common in young children

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRole
Autoantibodies1+ positive = autoimmune; 2+ = high risk
C-peptideLow/undetectable = T1DM; preserved = T2DM/MODY
HLA typingResearch/family screening; not routine

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Type 2 DMInsulin resistance, not autoimmune; autoantibodies negative
MODYMonogenic, autosomal dominant, non-insulin requiring, negative autoantibodies
Secondary DMPancreatic, endocrine, drug-induced; context-dependent

7. Management Implications

StageIntervention
Stage 1Monitoring (autoantibodies, glucose); clinical trial enrolment (teplizumab)
Stage 2Teplizumab (FDA approved): delays Stage 3 by ~2 years; glucose monitoring
Stage 3Insulin therapy initiated; DKA prevention; education

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
AutoimmunityCD4+/CD8+ T-cell mediated beta-cell destruction; insulitis
AutoantibodiesGAD65 (70-80%), IA-2 (50-70%), ZnT8 (60-80%), IAA (children)
StagingStage 1: 2+ Ab + normal glucose; Stage 2: 2+ Ab + IFG/IGT; Stage 3: clinical
TeplizumabAnti-CD3 mAb; delays Stage 3 by ~2 years in Stage 2
LADAAdult-onset, GAD65+, slow progression, initial non-insulin response

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of beta-cell destruction in T1DM?T-cell mediated autoimmune destruction (CD4+/CD8+); insulitis
What are the stages of T1DM?Stage 1: 2+ Ab, normoglycaemia; Stage 2: 2+ Ab, dysglycaemia; Stage 3: clinical
Which autoantibodies are tested in T1DM?GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA; 2+ = high risk
What is teplizumab?Anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody; FDA approved for Stage 2 T1DM; delays onset by ~2 years

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
T1DM = only children?NO - can occur at any age; LADA = adult-onset T1DM
Autoantibodies = diagnosis?Support diagnosis; clinical context essential

Mnemonic: T1DM-AUTO

  • T1DM: T-cell mediated beta-cell destruction
  • 1mmune: autoimmune (not metabolic like T2DM)
  • Destruction: insulitis -> beta-cell loss
  • Markers: GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA
  • Antibodies: 2+ = high risk
  • Unstoppable: Stage 1-2-3 progression
  • Teplizumab: delays Stage 3 in Stage 2
  • Over time: months to years from autoimmunity to clinical

11. Mind Map

Autoimmune Beta-cell Destruction
Mechanism
CD4+/CD8+ T-cells
Insulitis
B-cells/autoantibodies
Autoantibodies
GAD65 (70-80%)
IA-2 (50-70%)
ZnT8 (60-80%)
IAA (children)
Staging
Stage 1: normal glucose
Stage 2: IFG/IGT
Stage 3: clinical
Therapy
Teplizumab (Stage 2)
Insulin (Stage 3)
LADA
Adult onset
GAD65+
Slow progression

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionT-cell mediated autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta-cells
Key TestAutoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA); 2+ = high risk
ClassificationStage 1: normal glucose; Stage 2: IFG/IGT; Stage 3: clinical
Acute MgmtTeplizumab (Stage 2): delays Stage 3 by ~2 years
Chronic MgmtInsulin therapy (Stage 3); DKA prevention; education
Key Score2+ autoantibodies = high risk; C-peptide low
ComplicationsDKA at presentation (25-30%); long-term micro/macrovascular
PrognosisLifelong insulin; microvascular complications if poorly controlled

topics.title: Environmental Triggers
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pathophysiology, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core pathophysiology topic"

see_also: ["Autoimmune beta-cell destruction", "Genetic susceptibility (HLA, INS, PTPN22)", "Stages of type 1 diabetes (pre-symptomatic, symptomatic)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Environmental triggers

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify proposed environmental triggers for T1DM
  • [ ] Explain the hygiene hypothesis and viral trigger theories
  • [ ] Evaluate evidence for dietary factors (cow's milk, gluten, vitamin D)
  • [ ] Understand microbiome role in autoimmunity

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Trigger HypothesisEnvironmental factors initiate autoimmunity in genetically susceptible
EvidenceMigrant studies (risk follows destination); seasonal variation; twin discordance

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Proposed Environmental Triggers

CategoryFactorEvidence
ViralEnterovirus (Coxsackie B), Rotavirus, Rubella, CMV, EBVMolecular mimicry; seasonal peaks; viral RNA in pancreas
Dietary (early life)Cow's milk protein (BSA), gluten, early cereal introductionTRIGR study: hydrolysed formula no benefit; mixed evidence
Vitamin DLow 25(OH)D, low sunlight exposureEcological: latitude gradient; supplementation trials mixed
MicrobiomeDysbiosis, low diversity, antibiotic useAnimal models strong; human: correlational
Hygiene hypothesisReduced infections, caesarean section, urban livingInverse association with infections/helminths
ToxinsNitrosamines (cured meat), heavy metalsWeak/conflicting evidence

Timing of Exposure

PeriodHypothesis
PrenatalMaternal infections, diet, vitamin D
PerinatalMode of delivery, breastfeeding, early antibiotics
Early childhoodDiet introduction, infections, microbiome establishment

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management / Prevention

StrategyEvidence
Vitamin D supplementationMixed trials; no clear prevention benefit
Hydrolysed formulaTRIGR: no reduction in T1DM autoantibodies
Avoidance of early glutenNo consistent benefit
Probiotics/prebioticsExperimental; microbiome modulation
Enterovirus vaccinesIn development (Coxsackie B)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Viral triggersEnterovirus (Coxsackie B) strongest; molecular mimicry; seasonal peaks
DietaryCow's milk (BSA) controversial; TRIGR negative; gluten timing unclear
Vitamin DLatitude gradient; low 25(OH)D association; supplementation not proven preventive
MicrobiomeDysbiosis, low diversity; antibiotic use risk factor
Hygiene hypothesisReduced early infections -> increased autoimmunity

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the strongest viral trigger for T1DM?Enterovirus (Coxsackie B) - molecular mimicry; seasonal variation
Does cow's milk cause T1DM?TRIGR trial: hydrolysed formula no benefit; evidence inconclusive
What is the hygiene hypothesis in T1DM?Reduced early-life infections -> immune dysregulation -> increased autoimmunity
Is vitamin D supplementation preventive?No RCT evidence for prevention; observational association only

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Triggers = cause?Triggers initiate in genetically susceptible; not sufficient alone
One trigger for all?Heterogeneous; multiple pathways to autoimmunity

Mnemonic: T1DM-TRIGGERS

  • T1DM: environmental triggers in susceptible
  • 1 Viral: enterovirus (Coxsackie B) strongest
  • Diet: cow's milk (BSA), gluten - inconclusive
  • Microbiome: dysbiosis, antibiotics risk
  • Timing: prenatal, perinatal, early childhood
  • Regional: latitude gradient (vitamin D)
  • Inhygiene hypothesis: less infections -> more autoimmunity
  • Gluten: timing unclear
  • Gene-environment interaction essential
  • Epidemiology: migrant studies prove environmental role
  • Rotavirus, rubella, CMV also implicated
  • Seasonal peaks: winter onset common

11. Mind Map

Environmental Triggers
Viral
Enterovirus/Coxsackie B
Seasonal peaks
Molecular mimicry
Dietary
Cow's milk (BSA)
Gluten timing
TRIGR negative
Vitamin D
Latitude gradient
Supplementation inconclusive
Microbiome
Dysbiosis
Antibiotics risk
Hygiene hypothesis
Less infections = more autoimmunity

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionEnvironmental factors initiate autoimmunity in genetically susceptible
Key TestN/A (epidemiological)
ClassificationViral, dietary, microbiome, hygiene, vitamin D
Acute MgmtN/A
Chronic MgmtVitamin D optimization; breastfeeding; avoid early cow's milk? (inconclusive)
Key ScoreMigrant studies prove environmental role
ComplicationsN/A
PrognosisTriggers initiate but not sufficient alone

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pathophysiology, fcps, mrcp]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Genetic susceptibility (HLA, INS, PTPN22)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State HLA associations with T1DM (DR3/DR4-DQ2/DQ8)
  • [ ] Explain non-HLA genetic susceptibility loci (INS, PTPN22, CTLA4, IL2RA)
  • [ ] Calculate genetic risk scores for T1DM
  • [ ] Apply genetic testing indications in clinical practice

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Heritability40-50% (concordance: MZ twins 30-50%, DZ twins 6-10%)
HLA Contribution~40% of familial risk
Non-HLA Loci50+ identified (GWAS); each small effect (OR 1.1-1.3)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

HLA Associations

HaplotypeRiskPopulation FrequencyNotes
DR3-DQ2OR ~3-4~15-20%DRB103:01-DQA105:01-DQB1*02:01
DR4-DQ8OR ~3-4~20-25%DRB104:01/04/05-DQA103:01-DQB1*03:02
DR3/DR4 heterozygousOR ~15-20Highest risk~30-40% of T1DM patients
DR15-DQ6 (06:02)Protective~10-15%Dominant protection

Key Non-HLA Genes

GeneFunctionOdds RatioClinical Relevance
INSInsulin gene (VNTR)1.5-2.0Class I VNTR = risk; Class III = protective
PTPN22Lymphoid phosphatase1.8-2.0R620W variant; autoimmunity risk
CTLA4T-cell regulation1.2-1.5Immune checkpoint
IL2RACD25 (IL-2 receptor alpha)1.2-1.5Treg function
IFIH1Viral RNA sensor (MDA5)1.1-1.3Viral trigger link

Polygenic Risk Score (PRS)

ApplicationStatus
Population screeningResearch only; not routine
Newborn screeningPilot studies (e.g., TEDDY, Fr1da)
Family risk stratificationLimited clinical use currently

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestIndication
HLA typingResearch; family studies; not routine diagnosis
AutoantibodiesClinical diagnosis (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)
PRSResearch stratification

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

ContextAction
Family screeningAutoantibodies (not HLA); TrialNet, Fr1da, TEDDY
Genetic counsellingEmpiric recurrence risk: 5% (sibling), 3% (offspring if father), 1% (offspring if mother)
Prevention trialsTeplizumab (Stage 2); abatacept, verapamil, IL-2, ATG/G-CSF trials

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HLADR3/DR4-DQ2/DQ8 = 40-50% risk; DR3/DR4 het = highest; DR15-DQ6 = protective
Non-HLAINS VNTR, PTPN22, CTLA4, IL2RA, IFIH1; 50+ loci total
Heritability40-50%; MZ twin concordance 30-50%
Recurrence riskSibling 5%; Offspring: father 3%, mother 1%
PRSResearch only; not clinical

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the primary HLA association with T1DM?HLA-DR3/DR4-DQ2/DQ8; DR3/DR4 heterozygous = highest risk (OR 15-20)
Which HLA haplotype is protective?DR15-DQ6 (DQB1*06:02)
What is the recurrence risk for siblings of T1DM patients?~5%
Name key non-HLA genes in T1DM.INS (VNTR), PTPN22, CTLA4, IL2RA, IFIH1

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HLA = diagnostic?NO - risk stratification only; autoantibodies for diagnosis
All DR4 = risk?Specific alleles: DRB104:01, 04:02, 04:05 = risk; 04:03, *04:07 = neutral/protective

Mnemonic: T1DM-GENES

  • T1DM: 40-50% heritable
  • 1 HLA: DR3/DR4-DQ2/DQ8 (40% risk)
  • DR3/DR4 het: highest risk
  • MHC Class II: DQ/DR
  • Genes non-HLA: INS, PTPN22, CTLA4, IL2RA
  • Empiric risk: sib 5%, offsp father 3%, mother 1%
  • No routine HLA typing
  • Exclusion: DR15-DQ6 protective
  • Score: PRS research only

11. Mind Map

Genetic Susceptibility T1DM
HLA
DR3-DQ2 (OR 3-4)
DR4-DQ8 (OR 3-4)
DR3/DR4 het (OR 15-20)
DR15-DQ6 protective
Non-HLA
INS VNTR Class I risk
PTPN22 R620W
CTLA4, IL2RA, IFIH1
Heritability
40-50%
MZ twin 30-50%
Recurrence
Sibling 5%
Offspring: father 3%, mother 1%

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionGenetic susceptibility to T1DM: HLA + non-HLA loci
Key TestAutoantibodies (clinical); HLA typing (research only)
ClassificationHLA Class II (DR/DQ); non-HLA loci (INS, PTPN22, etc.)
Acute MgmtN/A
Chronic MgmtFamily screening (autoantibodies); prevention trials
Key ScoreEmpiric recurrence: sib 5%, offspring: father 3%, mother 1%
ComplicationsN/A
Prognosis40-50% heritable; prevention trials targeting high-risk

topics.title: Genetics Of Type 2 Diabetes Polygenic Risk
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pathophysiology, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core pathophysiology topic"

see_also: ["Insulin resistance", "Beta-cell dysfunction and failure", "Incretin defect and glucagon dysregulation", "Lipotoxicity and glucotoxicity"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Genetics of type 2 diabetes (polygenic risk)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State the heritability and genetic architecture of T2DM
  • [ ] Identify key susceptibility genes (TCF7L2, KCNJ11, PPARG, FTO)
  • [ ] Explain polygenic risk scores and their clinical utility
  • [ ] Contrast monogenic (MODY) vs polygenic (T2DM) genetics

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Heritability30-70% (family studies); higher than T1DM
ArchitecturePolygenic: 400+ loci identified (GWAS); each small effect (OR 1.05-1.3)
TCF7L2Strongest signal (OR ~1.4); Wnt signalling, proinsulin processing

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Major T2DM Susceptibility Genes

GeneFunctionOdds RatioMechanism
TCF7L2Transcription factor (Wnt)1.4down Proinsulin processing, down GLP-1 secretion
KCNJ11Kir6.2 (KATP channel)1.15up Insulin secretion (activating variants cause neonatal DM)
PPARGPPAR-gamma (adipogenesis)1.14Pro12Ala variant protective; regulates adipocyte differentiation
FTOFat mass/obesity1.3 (via BMI)up Appetite, up food intake; obesity-mediated
KCNQ1Potassium channel1.1-1.2Beta-cell function
CDKAL1tRNA modification1.1-1.2Beta-cell function
IGF2BP2mRNA binding1.1-1.2Beta-cell development
SLC30A8ZnT8 (zinc transporter)1.1-1.2Insulin crystallisation/secretion

Genetic Risk Score (GRS / PRS)

TypeCompositionUtility
UnweightedCount of risk allelesSimple; research
Weightedsum beta x allele countBetter prediction; research
Clinical GRS~100 SNPsExplains ~10-15% variance; not diagnostic

Monogenic vs Polygenic

FeatureMODY (Monogenic)T2DM (Polygenic)
InheritanceAutosomal dominantComplex
GenesSingle (HNF1A, GCK, HNF4A, etc.)400+ loci
Effect sizeHigh (penetrance >90%)Small (OR 1.05-1.4)
Age of onsetUsually <25 yearsUsually >40 years
Genetic testingDiagnostic (causal)Risk stratification only

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
PRSResearch stratification; not clinical
Family historyBest clinical predictor (empiric)
Monogenic gene panelIf MODY suspected

6. Differential Diagnosis

FeatureMODYT2DM
Family history3 generations, autosomal dominantPolygenic clustering
Age<25>40 (usually)
BMINon-obeseObese
AutoantibodiesNegativeNegative
C-peptidePreservedVariable

7. Management Implications

ContextAction
Family riskEmpiric: 40% lifetime if both parents; 20-30% if one parent
PRS screeningNot recommended for general population
PharmacogenomicsTCF7L2: sulfonylurea response?; PPARG: TZD response?

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Heritability30-70%; 400+ loci (GWAS)
Strongest geneTCF7L2 (OR 1.4) -> Wnt signalling, proinsulin processing
Obesity geneFTO (OR 1.3 via BMI)
PPARGPro12Ala protective; TZD target
KCNJ11Kir6.2; activating -> neonatal DM; common variant -> T2DM risk
GRS/PRSExplains ~10-15% variance; not diagnostic; research only
Monogenic vs polygenicMODY: single gene, dominant, high penetrance; T2DM: 400+ loci, small effects

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the strongest genetic risk factor for T2DM?TCF7L2 (OR 1.4); transcription factor in Wnt signalling
How does FTO contribute to T2DM?Via obesity (OR 1.3); up appetite, up food intake
What is a polygenic risk score?Weighted sum of risk alleles across ~100-400 SNPs; explains ~10-15% variance
How does TCF7L2 affect diabetes risk?down Proinsulin processing, down GLP-1 secretion -> impaired insulin secretion

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Genetics = destiny?NO - lifestyle modifies risk; DPP showed lifestyle > metformin even in high genetic risk
PRS = diagnostic?NO - risk stratification only; explains small fraction of variance

Mnemonic: T2DM-GENES

  • T2DM: polygenic, 400+ loci
  • 2 (heritability) 30-70%
  • DM genes: TCF7L2 (strongest), FTO, PPARG, KCNJ11
  • Monogenic vs polygenic: MODY single gene; T2DM 400+ SNPs
  • GRS: explains 10-15% variance
  • Empiric risk: 40% if both parents, 20-30% if one
  • No clinical PRS use yet
  • Effect sizes small: OR 1.05-1.4
  • SNP variants: common, not rare mutations
  • TCF7L2: Wnt, proinsulin processing
  • You: lifestyle modifies genetic risk (DPP)

11. Mind Map

Genetics of T2DM
Heritability
30-70%
400+ loci
Key Genes
TCF7L2 (OR 1.4)
FTO (OR 1.3 via BMI)
PPARG (Pro12Ala protective)
KCNJ11, KCNQ1, CDKAL1, SLC30A8
Architecture
Polygenic
>400 loci
OR 1.05-1.4
PRS
~10-15% variance
Research only
MODY vs T2DM
Single gene vs 400+ loci
High penetrance vs small effect

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionPolygenic architecture: 400+ loci, small effects; TCF7L2 strongest
**Key Test"Family history (empiric); PRS research only
**Classification"Polygenic (T2DM) vs Monogenic (MODY)
**Acute Mgmt"N/A
**Chronic Mgmt"Lifestyle modifies genetic risk (DPP)
**Key Score"PRS explains ~10-15% variance; not diagnostic
**Complications"N/A
**Prognosis"Lifestyle > genetics for prevention (DPP)

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Incretin defect and glucagon dysregulation

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define the incretin effect and its loss in T2DM
  • [ ] Contrast GIP and GLP-1 physiology
  • [ ] Explain the role of DPP-4 in incretin degradation
  • [ ] Apply this to rationale for GLP-1 RA and DPP-4i therapy

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incretin EffectOral glucose -> 50-70% greater insulin vs IV glucose
Loss in T2DMDecreased 50-70% of incretin effect; GIP action severely impaired
DPP-4Degrades GLP-1/GIP (t-half ~2 min)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Incretin Hormones

HormoneSourcePrimary ActionT2DM Defect
GLP-1L-cells (distal ileum/colon)up Insulin, down glucagon, down gastric emptying, up satietyReduced secretion; effect relatively preserved
GIPK-cells (duodenum/jejunum)up Insulin (glucose-dependent), up adipocyte lipid storageSeverely impaired insulinotropic effect
DPP-4Ubiquitous (endothelium, T-cells)Degrades GLP-1/GIPup Activity in T2DM?

Incretin Effect Quantification

MeasureNormalT2DM
Incretin Effect (%)50-70%down 20-30%
GLP-1 AUCNormal/lowdown 30-50%
GIP AUCNormalNormal/low but action lost

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
IVGTT vs OGTTIncretin effect = (AUCinsulin_OGTT - AUCinsulin_IVGTT) / AUCinsulin_OGTT
GLP-1/GIP assaysResearch; not routine
DPP-4 activityNot clinically measured

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Therapeutic Implications

Drug ClassMechanismEffect on Incretin Axis
GLP-1 RAReplaces/augments GLP-1Bypasses DPP-4; supraphysiological
DPP-4iInhibits DPP-4up Endogenous GLP-1/GIP 2-3x
TirzepatideDual GIP/GLP-1 RARestores GIP action + GLP-1

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Incretin effectOral > IV glucose insulin response (50-70%); lost in T2DM
GLP-1Reduced secretion; effect relatively preserved
GIPSevere insulinotropic defect in T2DM (despite normal levels)
DPP-4Degrades both (t-half 2 min); target for DPP-4i
TherapeuticsGLP-1 RA (replace), DPP-4i (preserve), Tirzepatide (dual)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the incretin effect?Oral glucose elicits 50-70% greater insulin response than IV glucose at same glycaemia
How does the incretin effect differ in T2DM?Reduced to 20-30%; GIP insulinotropic effect severely impaired; GLP-1 secretion reduced
Why does GIP lose effect in T2DM?Post-receptor defect in beta-cells; GIP receptor signalling impaired; mechanism unclear
How do GLP-1 RA and DPP-4i address the incretin defect?GLP-1 RA: supraphysiological GLP-1R agonism; DPP-4i: up endogenous GLP-1/GIP 2-3x

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
GIP = no role in T2DM?GIP levels normal but action lost; tirzepatide restores GIP action
GLP-1 secretion = normal in T2DM?Reduced by 30-50%; GLP-1 RA provides supraphysiological levels

Mnemonic: INCRETIN-LOSS

  • Incretin effect: oral > IV glucose insulin (50-70%)
  • Normal: 50-70% augmentation
  • Creases: T2DM -> 20-30%
  • Reason: GIP action lost, GLP-1 reduced
  • Enttin: GLP-1 + GIP
  • T2DM: GIP action severely impaired
  • Insulinotropic: GIP > GLP-1 normally
  • Neutral: DPP-4 degrades both (t-half 2 min)
  • Loss: GIP > GLP-1 in T2DM
  • Oral vs IV: incretin effect quantified
  • SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA: bypass/restore
  • Story: tirzepatide dual agonist

11. Mind Map

Incretin Defect
Normal
Oral > IV insulin
50-70% augmentation
GLP-1 + GIP
T2DM
Incretin effect 20-30%
GIP action LOST
GLP-1 secretion down
Hormones
GLP-1: L-cells, insulin, glucagon, gastric emptying
GIP: K-cells, insulin, adipocyte lipid storage
DPP-4
Degrades both (t1/2 2 min)
Therapeutics
GLP-1 RA: replace
DPP-4i: augment
Tirzepatide: dual

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionIncretin effect (oral > IV insulin) reduced from 50-70% to 20-30% in T2DM
**Key Test"IVGTT vs OGTT comparison; not routine clinically
**Classification"GLP-1 (reduced secretion, preserved effect) vs GIP (normal levels, LOST effect)
**Acute Mgmt"N/A
**Chronic Mgmt"GLP-1 RA (replace), DPP-4i (augment), Tirzepatide (dual restore)
**Key Score"Incretin effect: normal 50-70%, T2DM 20-30%
**Complications"Beta-cell workload increased; post-prandial hyperglycaemia
**Prognosis"Incretin defect contributes to beta-cell exhaustion; target for therapy

90 days

topics.title: Lipotoxicity And Glucotoxicity
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pathophysiology, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core pathophysiology topic"

see_also: ["Insulin resistance", "Beta-cell dysfunction and failure", "Incretin defect and glucagon dysregulation", "Genetics of type 2 diabetes (polygenic risk)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Lipotoxicity and glucotoxicity

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define glucotoxicity and lipotoxicity in beta-cell failure
  • [ ] Explain molecular mechanisms: oxidative stress, ER stress, mitochondrial dysfunction
  • [ ] Apply concepts to therapeutic targets (SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, early insulin)
  • [ ] Contrast acute vs chronic lipid/glucose toxicity

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GlucotoxicityChronic hyperglycaemia -> beta-cell dysfunction
LipotoxicityChronic FFA excess -> beta-cell dysfunction
GlucolipotoxicitySynergistic; both required for full beta-cell failure

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Glucotoxicity Mechanisms

MechanismDetails
Oxidative stressMitochondrial ROS -> DNA damage, protein/lipid oxidation
ER stressMisfolded proinsulin -> UPR -> CHOP -> apoptosis
PKC activationDAG -> PKC -> NF-kappaB -> inflammation
AGE formationNon-enzymatic glycation -> RAGE -> inflammation
Hexosamine pathwayGFAT -> O-GlcNAc modification of proteins

Lipotoxicity Mechanisms

MechanismDetails
CeramidesDe novo synthesis -> down Akt, up PP2A -> apoptosis
DAG/PKCPKC-theta -> IRS-1 serine phosphorylation
Mitochondrial dysfunctionIncomplete fatty acid oxidation -> ROS
ER stressSaturated FFA -> UPR -> CHOP
InflammationFFA -> TLR4 -> NF-kappaB -> cytokines

Synergistic Glucolipotoxicity

FeatureEffect
Glucose + FFAup Ceramide synthesis (glucose provides substrate)
Glucose + FFAup ER stress, up oxidative stress beyond either alone
Beta-cell specificityHigh glucokinase -> glucose metabolism; low FFA oxidation -> lipid accumulation

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

MarkerSignificance
Proinsulin/Insulinup = ER stress/glucolipotoxicity
CeramidesPlasma ceramides predict T2DM
Oxidative stress markers8-OHdG, MDA, F2-isoprostanes
CHOPER stress apoptosis marker

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Therapeutic Targets

InterventionTargets
SGLT2idown Glucotoxicity (glycosuria), weight loss -> down lipotoxicity
GLP-1 RAWeight loss -> down lipotoxicity; direct anti-apoptotic
Early insulin"Beta-cell rest" -> down glucotoxicity
TZDsup Adiponectin, down FFA, down ectopic fat
AntioxidantsExperimental (ALA, NAC)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
GlucotoxicityChronic hyperglycaemia -> oxidative stress, ER stress, AGE, PKC
LipotoxicityFFA/ceramides/DAG -> PKC, mitochondrial dysfunction, ER stress
GlucolipotoxicitySynergistic: glucose + FFA > either alone; ceramides need glucose substrate
CeramidesKey lipotoxic mediator; down Akt, up PP2A, apoptosis
ReversibilityEarly intervention can reverse; chronic -> irreversible loss

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is glucolipotoxicity?Synergistic beta-cell toxicity from chronic hyperglycaemia + elevated FFA; both required for full effect
How do ceramides cause beta-cell apoptosis?Ceramides -> down Akt, up PP2A -> dephosphorylate Bad -> apoptosis; also down PDX-1
What is the role of ER stress in beta-cell failure?Misfolded proinsulin -> UPR -> CHOP -> apoptosis; both glucose and FFA induce ER stress
Can glucolipotoxicity be reversed?Early intervention (weight loss, GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i, insulin) can reverse; chronic -> irreversible loss

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Glucotoxicity = lipotoxicity?Distinct but synergistic; both use overlapping pathways (ER stress, oxidative stress)
All FFA toxic?Saturated FFA (palmitate) toxic; unsaturated (oleate) protective

Mnemonic: GLUCO-LIPO-TOXIC

  • Glucotoxicity: chronic hyperglycaemia -> ROS, ER stress, AGEs, PKC
  • Lipotoxicity: FFA -> ceramides, DAG, ER stress, mitochondrial dysfunction
  • Unitary: synergistic - glucose + FFA > either alone
  • Ceramides: key mediator -> down Akt, apoptosis
  • Oxidative stress: mitochondria, NADPH oxidase
  • Low-grade inflammation: NF-kappaB, cytokines
  • Insulin resistance worsened: vicious cycle
  • Prevention: early weight loss, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, insulin
  • Treatment: reverse if early; chronic -> irreversible
  • Overlap: ER stress, oxidative stress, inflammation common
  • XAGEs: AGE-RAGE axis
  • Insulin rest: early intensive control
  • CHOPS: ER stress apoptosis

11. Mind Map

Glucolipotoxicity
Glucotoxicity
Oxidative stress
ER stress -> CHOP
AGE formation
PKC activation
Lipotoxicity
Ceramides -> down Akt
DAG -> PKC
Mitochondrial dysfunction
ER stress
Synergy
Glucose + FFA > either alone
Ceramides need glucose substrate
Therapy
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Early insulin
Weight loss

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionSynergistic beta-cell toxicity from chronic hyperglycaemia + elevated FFA
**Key Test"Proinsulin/Insulin ratio (up = ER stress); plasma ceramides
**Classification"Glucotoxicity + Lipotoxicity -> Glucolipotoxicity (synergistic)
**Acute Mgmt"Early insulin -> beta-cell rest
**Chronic Mgmt"SGLT2i (glycosuria), GLP-1 RA (weight loss), weight loss
**Key Score"Proinsulin/Insulin ratio up; plasma ceramides
**Complications"Irreversible beta-cell loss; progression to insulin requirement
**Prognosis"Early reversible; chronic -> permanent loss

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pathophysiology, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core pathophysiology topic"

see_also: ["Autoimmune beta-cell destruction", "Genetic susceptibility (HLA, INS, PTPN22)", "Environmental triggers"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Stages of type 1 diabetes (pre-symptomatic, symptomatic)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define the 3 stages of T1DM (ADA/JDRF/Endocrine Society)
  • [ ] Apply staging to screening and monitoring protocols
  • [ ] Identify criteria for teplizumab therapy (Stage 2)
  • [ ] Counsel patients on progression risks

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Staging SystemADA/JDRF/Endocrine Society 2015 (updated 2021)
PurposeStandardise pre-clinical phases; enable prevention trials

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Three Stages of T1DM

StageAutoantibodiesGlucose StatusClinical FeaturesProgression Risk
Stage 12+Normal (FPG <6.1, 2h-OGTT <7.8, HbA1c <39)Asymptomatic~100% lifetime
Stage 22+Dysglycaemia (IFG 6.1-6.9, IGT 7.8-11.0, HbA1c 39-47)Asymptomatic~75% at 5 years
Stage 32+ (usually)Hyperglycaemia (FPG >=7.0, 2h-OGTT >=11.1, HbA1c >=48)Symptomatic (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss)Clinical T1DM

Autoantibody Requirements

RequirementDetail
Number2+ of 4 (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)
PersistenceConfirmed on 2 occasions
AgeAny (peak childhood/adolescence)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestStage 1Stage 2Stage 3
Autoantibodies2+ positive2+ positiveUsually 2+
FPG<6.16.1-6.9 (IFG)>=7.0
2h-OGTT<7.87.8-11.0 (IGT)>=11.1
HbA1c<3939-47>=48

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
LADAAdult-onset, single autoantibody often, slower progression
MODYMonogenic, autosomal dominant, negative autoantibodies
Stress hyperglycaemiaTransient, no autoantibodies

7. Management by Stage

StageInterventionEvidence
Stage 1Monitoring q6-12mo (autoantibodies, OGTT/HbA1c); trial enrolmentNatural history studies
Stage 2Teplizumab (FDA 2022): 14-day IV; delays Stage 3 by ~2 years (median 48 vs 24 mo)TN-10 Trial
Stage 3Insulin therapy; DKA prevention; education; CSII/CGM considerationStandard care

Teplizumab Protocol

AspectDetail
IndicationStage 2 T1DM, age >=8 years
DoseWeight-based IV daily x14 days
EffectMedian delay to Stage 3: 48.4 vs 24.4 months (HR 0.48)
AdverseLymphopenia (transient), rash, cytokine release

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Stage 12+ Ab + normal glucose; monitoring q6-12mo
Stage 22+ Ab + IFG/IGT; Teplizumab indicated (age >=8)
Stage 3Clinical T1DM; insulin required
TeplizumabAnti-CD3 mAb; 14-day IV; delays Stage 3 by ~2 years
LADAOften Stage 2 equivalent but adult-onset, slower

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the 3 stages of T1DM?Stage 1: 2+ Ab + normoglycaemia; Stage 2: 2+ Ab + dysglycaemia; Stage 3: clinical hyperglycaemia
Who is eligible for teplizumab?Stage 2 T1DM (2+ Ab + IFG/IGT), age >=8 years
What is the effect of teplizumab?Delays Stage 3 by median 2 years (HR 0.48; 48 vs 24 months)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Stages = linear?Not all progress at same rate; Stage 2 -> 3 ~75% at 5 years
Teplizumab = cure?NO - delays onset; not prevention

Mnemonic: T1DM-STAGES

  • T1DM staging: 1, 2, 3
  • 1 Stage 1: 2+ Ab + normal glucose
  • 2 Stage 2: 2+ Ab + IFG/IGT -> Teplizumab
  • 3 Stage 3: Clinical T1DM -> Insulin
  • Teplizumab: Anti-CD3, delays Stage 3 by 2 years
  • Eligibility: Stage 2, age >=8
  • Autoantibodies: 2+ of 4 (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)
  • Glucose: Stage 1 normal, Stage 2 IFG/IGT, Stage 3 hyperglycaemia
  • Endpoint: Stage 3 = insulin requirement
  • Screening: TrialNet, Fr1da, general population pilots

11. Mind Map

Stages of T1DM
Stage 1
2+ Ab + normal glucose
Monitor q6-12mo
Stage 2
2+ Ab + IFG/IGT
Teplizumab (age >=8)
Stage 3
Clinical T1DM
Insulin required
Teplizumab
Anti-CD3 mAb
Delays Stage 3 by ~2 yrs
Screening
TrialNet, Fr1da
Autoantibodies 2+

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
Definition3 stages: 1=autoimmunity+normoglycaemia; 2=+dysglycaemia; 3=clinical
Key TestAutoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA): 2+ required
ClassificationStage 1: normal glucose; Stage 2: IFG/IGT; Stage 3: clinical
Acute MgmtTeplizumab (Stage 2): 14-day IV anti-CD3; delays Stage 3
Chronic MgmtInsulin (Stage 3); DKA prevention; education; CSII/CGM
Key Score2+ autoantibodies; Stage 2 = teplizumab eligible
ComplicationsDKA at presentation if Stage 3 missed
PrognosisStage 2 -> 3 ~75% at 5 years without intervention

90 days

see_also: ["Cushing syndrome diabetes", "Thyrotoxicosis diabetes", "Phaeochromocytoma diabetes", "Glucagonoma diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Acromegaly diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise acromegaly as cause of secondary diabetes
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (OGTT GH suppression, IGF-1)
  • [ ] Manage diabetes in acromegaly
  • [ ] Apply definitive treatment (surgery, medical therapy)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGH/IGF-1 excess causing insulin resistance and diabetes
Prevalence50% impaired glucose tolerance; 25% diabetes
MechanismGH/IGF-1 -> insulin resistance, ↓insulin signalling, ↑lipolysis

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGH/IGF-1 excess causing insulin resistance and diabetes
Prevalence50% impaired glucose tolerance; 25% diabetes
MechanismGH/IGF-1 -> insulin resistance, ↓insulin signalling, ↑lipolysis

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePresentation
DiabetesOften mild-moderate; post-prandial hyperglycaemia
Acromegaly featuresCoarse features, macroglossia, sweating, prognathism, large hands/feet
CardiovascularHypertension, cardiomyopathy, premature atherosclerosis
RespiratorySleep apnoea (macroglossia, soft tissue hypertrophy)
MetabolicInsulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureDetail
MechanismGH/IGF-1 -> ↓insulin receptor signalling, ↑lipolysis, ↓GLUT4
ReversibilityImproves with biochemical control (surgery/medical)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRoleInterpretation
IGF-1ScreeningAge-matched >ULN = positive
OGTT with GHGold standardGH not suppressed <1 ug/L (or <0.4 modern assays)
Random GHScreening>1 ug/L suggests acromegaly
MRI pituitaryLocalisationMicroadenoma vs macroadenoma
HbA1c/glucoseMonitor DMMonitor response to treatment

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRoleInterpretation
IGF-1ScreeningAge-matched >ULN = positive
OGTT with GHGold standardGH not suppressed <1 ug/L (or <0.4 modern assays)
Random GHScreening>1 ug/L suggests acromegaly
MRI pituitaryLocalisationMicroadenoma vs macroadenoma
HbA1c/glucoseMonitor DMMonitor response to treatment

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
GigantismEpiphyseal closure not yet fused
PseudoacromegalySevere insulin resistance + acanthosis; normal GH/IGF-1
Pituitary macroadenoma (non-functioning)No GH excess; mass effect only
Type 2DM + obesityNo acromegaly features; normal GH/IGF-1

7. Management

Diabetes Management (Pre-cure)

ApproachDetail
LifestyleWeight loss; exercise; low GI diet
Metformin1st line; improves insulin sensitivity
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RAIf CVD/CKD; weight loss benefit
SulfonylureasAvoid (weight gain)
InsulinIf severe hyperglycaemia

Definitive Treatment

AetiologyTreatment
Pituitary adenomaTranssphenoidal surgery (first-line)
Medical (if surgery contraindicated/recurrent)Somatostatin analogues (octreotide, lanreotide); Pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist); Dopamine agonists (cabergoline)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismGH/IGF-1 -> insulin resistance (↓IRS-1, ↑lipolysis)
Prevalence25% DM; 50% IGT
DiagnosisOGTT: GH not suppressed <1 ug/L; IGF-1 >ULN
DM managementMetformin 1st line; improves with biochemical control
Definitive cureTranssphenoidal surgery -> often resolves DM

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of diabetes in acromegaly?GH/IGF-1 -> insulin resistance (↓insulin signalling, ↑lipolysis)
What is the diagnostic test for acromegaly?OGTT: GH not suppressed <1 ug/L; IGF-1 > age-matched ULN
Does diabetes resolve after acromegaly cure?Often improves/resolves with biochemical remission
What medical therapy for acromegaly?Somatostatin analogues (octreotide/lanreotide), pegvisomant, cabergoline
What is the GH suppression cutoff on OGTT?<1 ug/L (modern assays <0.4 ug/L)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Acromegaly DM = T1DM?NO - insulin resistance, not autoimmune
GH directly causes hyperglycaemia?GH -> insulin resistance -> compensatory hyperinsulinaemia -> beta-cell exhaustion
All acromegaly = diabetes?NO - 25% DM, 50% IGT; depends on duration/severity

Mnemonic: ACROMEGALY-DM

  • Acroghaly: GH/IGF-1 excess
  • Cause of DM: insulin resistance (GH -> ↓IRS-1, ↑lipolysis)
  • Resolution: surgery -> DM often resolves
  • OGTT: GH not suppressed <1 ug/L (gold standard)
  • Mediastinum: not relevant (pituitary)
  • EIGF-1: screening (age-matched ULN)
  • Growth hormone: pituitary adenoma
  • Adenoma: micro vs macro
  • Lanreotide/octreotide: somatostatin analogues
  • Yield: transsphenoidal surgery 1st line
  • Diabetes: 25% DM, 50% IGT
  • Medical: pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist)
  • Insulin resistance: GH -> ↓GLUT4, ↓insulin signalling
  • Acromegaly features: coarse features, macroglossia, sleep apnoea**

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Immune checkpoint inhibitor diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise ICI-induced autoimmune diabetes (fulminant T1DM)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (autoantibodies, acute onset, DKA)
  • [ ] Manage acute presentation (DKA, insulin)
  • [ ] Apply long-term management

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence0.5-1.5% on anti-PD-1/PD-L1; higher with combination
OnsetMedian 6-12 weeks; can occur anytime
MechanismAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 -> loss of peripheral tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
PhenotypeFulminant T1DM; DKA at presentation common (60-80%)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence0.5-1.5% on anti-PD-1/PD-L1; higher with combination
OnsetMedian 6-12 weeks; can occur anytime
MechanismAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 -> loss of peripheral tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
PhenotypeFulminant T1DM; DKA at presentation common (60-80%)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureICI-DiabetesClassic T1DM
OnsetAcute (days-weeks)Subacute (weeks-months)
DKA at presentation60-80%25-30%
AutoantibodiesOften negative initiallyPositive (2+)
C-peptideVery low/undetectableLow
AgeOlder (median 60s)Bimodal (childhood/adolescence)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureICI-DiabetesClassic T1DM
OnsetAcute (days-weeks)Subacute (weeks-months)
DKA at presentation60-80%25-30%
AutoantibodiesOften negative initiallyPositive (2+)
C-peptideVery low/undetectableLow
AgeOlder (median 60s)Bimodal

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureICI-Diabetes
MechanismImmune checkpoint blockade -> loss of tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
AgentsAnti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab), Anti-PD-L1 (atezolizumab, durvalumab, avelumab)
OnsetWeeks-months after ICI initiation
DKACommon at presentation (60-80%)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
AutoantibodiesOften negative initially; may become positive
C-peptideVery low/undetectable
HbA1cOften normal at onset (acute)
KetonesElevated (DKA)
Pancreatic imagingDiffuse enlargement (autoimmune pancreatitis-like)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
AutoantibodiesOften negative initially; may become positive
C-peptideVery low/undetectable
HbA1cOften normal at onset (acute)
KetonesElevated (DKA)
Pancreatic imagingDiffuse enlargement (autoimmune pancreatitis-like)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Classic T1DMSlower onset; autoantibodies positive; younger age
Type 2DMNo DKA; autoantibodies negative
Acute pancreatitisAbdominal pain; amylase/lipase elevated; no autoantibodies
Pancreatic cancer DMWeight loss; imaging mass; older

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Classic T1DMSlower onset; autoantibodies positive; younger age
Type 2DMNo DKA; autoantibodies negative
Acute pancreatitisAbdominal pain; amylase/lipase elevated; no autoantibodies
Pancreatic cancer DMWeight loss; imaging mass; older

7. Management

Acute DKA

StepAction
1Standard DKA protocol (fluids, FRII, K+, monitor)
2Continue ICI controversy (generally hold; oncologist decision)
3High-dose steroids? (Not routinely; may worsen hyperglycaemia)

Long-term Management

AspectManagement
InsulinLifelong (fulminant beta-cell destruction)
RegimenBasal-bolus or CSII
ICI continuationMultidisciplinary decision (oncologist + endocrinologist)
SteroidsGenerally avoided (worsens hyperglycaemia)
MonitoringStandard T1DM follow-up

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Incidence0.5-1.5% on anti-PD-1/PD-L1
MechanismImmune checkpoint blockade -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
PresentationAcute; DKA in 60-80%; autoantibodies often negative initially
MechanismLoss of peripheral tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
ManagementStandard DKA protocol; lifelong insulin; ICI continuation debated
ICI agentsAnti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab); anti-PD-L1 (atezolizumab, durvalumab, avelumab)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the incidence of diabetes with immune checkpoint inhibitors?0.5-1.5% (higher with combination therapy)
What is the mechanism of ICI-induced diabetes?Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 -> loss of peripheral tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
What is the typical presentation?Acute onset; DKA in 60-80%; autoantibodies often negative initially
How does it differ from classic T1DM?Older age, acute onset, DKA common, autoantibodies often negative initially
How do you manage ICI-induced diabetes?Standard DKA protocol; lifelong insulin; ICI continuation multidisciplinary decision

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
ICI diabetes = T1DM?Similar but: older age, acute onset, DKA common, autoantibodies often negative
Continue ICI after diabetes?Multidisciplinary decision; often held during DKA; may continue if cancer benefit > risk
Steroids for ICI diabetes?Generally avoided (worsens hyperglycaemia); different from ICI-colitis/hepatitis

Mnemonic: ICI-DIABETES-ICI

  • Imune checkpoint: PD-1/PD-L1 blockade
  • Causes: autoimmune beta-cell destruction (fulminant T1DM)
  • Immune: loss of peripheral tolerance
  • Diabetes: fulminant T1DM
  • Incidence: 0.5-1.5% (higher combo)
  • Anti-PD-1: pembrolizumab, nivolumab
  • Beta-cell: destroyed (autoimmune)
  • Early: 6-12 weeks median
  • Treatment: DKA protocol + insulin
  • Early DKA: 60-80% at presentation
  • Steroids: avoid (worsen hyperglycaemia)
  • Autoantibodies: often negative initially
  • C-peptide: very low
  • Insulin: lifelong
  • Negative Abs initially
  • Hold ICI? MDT decision
  • Immunotherapy: continued if benefit > risk
  • C-peptide: undetectable
  • Type 1: fulminant
  • Elderly: median age 60s
  • Shared decision: oncologist + endocrinologist**
