topics.title: Diabetes On Dialysis
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Manage glycaemic control in haemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis
  • [ ] Apply insulin dosing adjustments for dialysis
  • [ ] Use alternative glycaemic markers (GA, fructosamine, CGM)
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia risk in dialysis patients

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Manage glycaemic control in haemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis
  • [ ] Apply insulin dosing adjustments for dialysis
  • [ ] Use alternative glycaemic markers (GA, fructosamine, CGM)
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia risk in dialysis patients

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Prevalence20-30% dialysis patients have diabetes; leading cause of ESRD
MortalityDM on dialysis: 2x mortality vs non-DM; CVD main cause
HbA1cUnreliable in dialysis (falsely low); use GA/fructosamine/CGM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Prevalence20-30% dialysis patients have diabetes
MortalityDM on dialysis: 2x mortality vs non-DM; CVD main cause
HbA1cUnreliable in dialysis (falsely low); use GA/fructosamine/CGM

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureHaemodialysis (HD)Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)
Glucose variabilityHigh (pre/post dialysis swings)More stable; continuous glucose exposure
Hypoglycaemia riskHigh (post-dialysis); glucose-free dialysateLower; but glucose absorption from dialysate
Insulin requirementVariable; often ↓ post-HDOften higher (glucose absorption from dialysate)
HbA1c reliabilityUnreliable in bothUnreliable; GA/fructosamine essential

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureHaemodialysis (HD)Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)
Glucose variabilityHigh (pre/post dialysis swings)More stable; continuous glucose exposure
Hypoglycaemia riskHigh (post-dialysis); glucose-free dialysateLower; but glucose absorption from dialysate
Insulin requirementVariable; often ↓ post-HDOften higher (glucose absorption from dialysate)
HbA1c reliabilityUnreliable in bothUnreliable; GA/fructosamine essential

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Glycaemic Monitoring in Dialysis

MarkerHDPDNotes
HbA1cUnreliableUnreliableFalsely low (↓RBC lifespan, ESA)
Glycated Albumin (GA)PreferredPreferred2-3 wk mean; GA <20% target
FructosamineAlternativeAlternative2-3 wk; <320 umol/L target
CGMEmergingEmergingReal-time; avoid hypo

Glycaemic Targets (Dialysis)

PopulationGA TargetFructosamine TargetPre-dialysis Glucose
HD<20-21%<320 umol/L7-10 mmol/L
PD<20-21%<320 umol/L7-10 mmol/L

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Glycaemic Monitoring in Dialysis

MarkerHDPDNotes
HbA1cUnreliableUnreliableFalsely low (↓RBC lifespan, ESA)
Glycated Albumin (GA)PreferredPreferred2-3 wk mean; GA <20% target
FructosamineAlternativeAlternative2-3 wk; <320 umol/L target
CGMEmergingEmergingReal-time; avoid hypo

Glycaemic Targets (Dialysis)

PopulationGA TargetFructosamine TargetPre-dialysis Glucose
HD<20-21%<320 umol/L7-10 mmol/L
PD<20-21%<320 umol/L7-10 mmol/L

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Insulin Requirements by Modality

ModalityPatternInsulin Adjustment
HDPre-HD glucose high; post-HD rapid drop↓ dose post-HD; pre-HD correction
PDContinuous glucose absorption from dialysateHigher total daily dose; consistent
BothGlucose-free dialysate -> hypo riskMonitor pre/post; adjust basal

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HypoglycaemiaPost-HD (glucose-free dialysate); post-prandial (PD)
HyperglycaemiaPre-HD (catabolic); pre-dialysis (PD glucose absorption)
Glucose-free dialysate effectRapid glucose drop during HD

7. Management

Insulin Management on Dialysis

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Diabetes on Dialysis
↓
Modality?
↓
Insulin variable; ↓ post-HD
Insulin higher; glucose absorption from dialysate
↓
Pre-HD: correction if >10
Post-HD: ↓ basal 25-50%; monitor 1-2h post
Consistent higher dose; glucose absorption
↓
Monitor pre-HD, 1h post, bedtime
↓
GA/Fructosamine q3mo; CGM if available

Insulin Dosing Principles

PrincipleHDPD
Basal insulinDegludec/U-300 preferred (stable)Standard; consistent
Bolus insulinPre-meal; skip if pre-HD glucose <7Standard; match dialysate glucose
Dose reduction50-75% vs non-dialysis25-50%
U-100 vs U-300/U-200U-100 preferred (predictable)U-100 preferred

Hypoglycaemia Prevention

StrategyHDPD
Pre-dialysis glucoseTarget 7-10 mmol/LTarget 7-10 mmol/L
Post-dialysisMonitor 1-2h; ↓ basal if <5-
Dialysis day↓ basal 25-50% on HD daysConsistent
CGMReal-time; predictive alertsReal-time; predictive alerts
Glucose-free dialysateHypo risk -> monitor 1-2h post-

Medication Adjustments (Dialysis)

DrugHDPDNotes
SGLT2iContinue (dapa/empa)ContinueInitiate >=20; continue on dialysis
MetforminCONTRAINDICATED (eGFR<15)CONTRAINDICATEDStop
SUAvoid (hypo risk)AvoidGliclazide MR if essential
DPP-4iLinagliptin (no adjust)LinagliptinOthers dose reduce
GLP-1 RANo adjustNo adjustLimited data

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HbA1cUnreliable in dialysis; use GA <20% / fructosamine <320
InsulinReduce 50-75%; U-100 preferred; variable needs
HD vs PDHD: variable, post-HD hypo; PD: stable, ↑dose (dialysate glucose)
MonitoringGA <20% / Fructosamine <320; CGM emerging
Pre/post HDPre-HD high; post-HD rapid drop -> monitor 1-2h post
MedicationsSGLT2i continue; metformin STOP; SU avoid; linagliptin preferred DPP-4i

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
Why is HbA1c unreliable in dialysis?↓RBC lifespan (uraemia, ESA, blood loss); carbamylation; falsely low
What alternative markers do you use in dialysis?Glycated Albumin (GA) <20%, Fructosamine <320 umol/L, CGM
How does insulin requirement differ between HD and PD?HD: variable, ↓post-HD; PD: stable, ↑dose (glucose absorption from dialysate)
What is the insulin dose reduction on dialysis?50-75% reduction vs non-dialysis
Why is hypoglycaemia common post-haemodialysis?Glucose-free dialysate -> rapid glucose drop; ↓hepatic glucose production
Which DPP-4i is preferred in dialysis?Linagliptin (no renal dose adjustment)
Can SGLT2i be used on dialysis?Yes - dapagliflozin/empagliflozin continue on dialysis (initiated >=20)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HbA1c useful in dialysis?NO - falsely low; use GA/Fructosamine/CGM
Insulin same HD vs PD?NO - HD variable/post-dialysis drop; PD stable higher dose
Metformin on dialysis?CONTRAINDICATED (eGFR <15)
SGLT2i in dialysis?Dapa/Empa: continue on dialysis if initiated >=20

Mnemonic: DIALYSIS-DM-INSULIN

  • Dialysis: HbA1c unreliable; use GA <20%, Fructosamine <320
  • Insulin: reduce 50-75% (U-100 preferred)
  • Alternatives: GA <20%, Fructosamine <320 umol/L, CGM
  • Low glucose post-HD: glucose-free dialysate -> hypo risk
  • You monitor: pre-HD, 1-2h post-HD, bedtime
  • SGLT2i: dapa/empa continue dialysis (if >=20)
  • Insulin dose: reduce 50-75% (U-100)
  • Stable PD: glucose absorption -> higher insulin needs
  • Dialysis types: HD variable; PD stable higher
  • Insulin U-100: preferred (predictable vs U-300/U-200)
  • Alternatives: GA <20%, Fructosamine <320, CGM
  • Low glucose post-HD: monitor 1-2h post
  • You use GA/Fructosamine: HbA1c unreliable
  • Stop metformin (eGFR<15); stop SU
  • Unique PD: dialysate glucose -> insulin needs
  • Stop SU: hypo risk; glic MR if essential**

topics.title: Hypoglycaemia Risk In Elderly
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Quantify hypoglycaemia risk in elderly diabetes
  • [ ] Identify risk factors: renal impairment, polypharmacy, cognitive impairment
  • [ ] Apply preventive strategies: CGM, relaxed targets, simplified regimens
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia in cognitively impaired elderly

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Quantify hypoglycaemia risk in elderly diabetes
  • [ ] Identify risk factors: renal impairment, polypharmacy, cognitive impairment
  • [ ] Apply preventive strategies: CGM, relaxed targets, simplified regimens
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia in cognitively impaired elderly

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGlucose < 3.9 mmol/L (Level 1); < 3.0 mmol/L (Level 2); severe = requires assistance (Level 3)
IncidenceElderly T2DM: 2-3x higher than younger; insulin/SU = highest risk
Risk5x higher in frail vs fit; 2x in >75y vs <65y
MortalityHypoglycaemia = 2x CVD mortality; "dead in bed" in T1DM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGlucose < 3.9 mmol/L (Level 1); < 3.0 mmol/L (Level 2); severe = requires assistance (Level 3)
IncidenceElderly T2DM: 2-3x higher than younger; insulin/SU = highest risk
Risk5x higher in frail vs fit; 2x in >75y vs <65y
MortalityHypoglycaemia = 2x CVD mortality; "dead in bed" in T1DM

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureElderly Specifics
Atypical presentationConfusion, falls, agitation, drowsiness (not classic autonomic)
NeuroglycopenicPredominant: confusion, drowsiness, speech difficulty
Autonomic bluntedReduced sweating, tremor, palpitations
NocturnalCommon; "dead in bed" syndrome (T1DM)
AsymptomaticHypoglycaemia unawareness; cognitive impairment masks symptoms

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureElderly Specifics
Atypical presentationConfusion, falls, agitation, drowsiness (not classic autonomic)
NeuroglycopenicPredominant: confusion, drowsiness, speech difficulty
Autonomic bluntedReduced sweating, tremor, palpitations
NocturnalCommon; "dead in bed" syndrome (T1DM)
AsymptomaticHypoglycaemia unawareness; cognitive impairment masks symptoms

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Risk Factors in Elderly

CategoryRisk Factors
RenaleGFR < 45 (↓ insulin clearance); dialysis (glucose-free dialysate)
MedicationsInsulin, sulfonylureas, beta-blockers (mask symptoms), ACEi/ARB
CognitiveDementia, MCI (impaired recognition/reporting)
Physiological↓ counter-regulation (glucagon/epinephrine); ↓ renal gluconeogenesis
SocialLiving alone; erratic meals; carer-dependent; alcohol
ComorbiditiesCKD, HF, liver disease; malnutrition; polypharmacy

Severity Grading (ADA)

LevelGlucoseClinical
1 (Alert)< 3.9 mmol/LSelf-treat
2 (Clinical)< 3.0 mmol/LImpaired judgement; self-treat possible
3 (Severe)Any (requires help)Unconscious, seizure, needs 3rd party

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

ToolRole
CGMGold standard detection (nocturnal, asymptomatic); predictive alerts
SMBG4-6x/day if insulin/SU; pre-meal, bedtime, 3am
HbA1cFalsely LOW in CKD/ESRD (↓RBC lifespan); use fructosamine/GA
Renal functioneGFR; adjust insulin/SU doses
Cognitive screenMoCA/MoCA; identify impaired recognition

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
FallsHypoglycaemia = common cause; check glucose
Confusion/deliriumCheck glucose first (reversible cause)
Stroke/TIAFocal neurology; glucose may be high/low
SepsisFever, tachycardia; glucose variable
Medication toxicityDigoxin, opioids, benzodiazepines

7. Management

Prevention Strategies

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Elderly DM
↓
Assess hypo risk factors
↓
On SU/insulin?
↓
Review doses; STOP SU; reduce insulin
Review other meds (beta-blockers, ACEi)
↓
Simplify regimen: once-daily basal; stop SU
↓
Individualised HbA1c target per frailty
↓
CGM with predictive alerts
↓
Carer education: hypo recognition, glucagon
↓
Regular review: 3-monthly HbA1c; CGM review

Prevention Strategies by Domain

DomainIntervention
MedicationSTOP sulfonylureas; reduce insulin 20-50%; once-daily basal; stop bolus
TargetsRelax HbA1c per frailty (fit 53, mild 58, mod 64, sev 69 mmol/mol)
TechnologyCGM with predictive alerts (low glucose suspend if pump); SMBG 4x/day if no CGM
RenalInsulin dose ↓ 25-50% if eGFR < 45; stop SU if eGFR < 45
CognitiveCarer-administered insulin; CGM with remote monitoring; simplified regimen
SocialRegular meals; bedtime snack; alcohol moderation; glucagon available

Nocturnal Hypoglycaemia Prevention

StrategyDetail
Bedtime snack15-20g complex carb if bedtime glucose < 7 mmol/L
Basal insulinReduce evening dose; switch to degludec/U-300 (stable)
CGM alertsLow glucose suspend (pump); predictive alerts (CGM)
Bedtime glucoseTarget 7-10 mmol/L at bedtime

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Risk5x higher in frail; 2x in >75y; renal/polypharmacy/cognitive = multipliers
PresentationAtypical: confusion, falls, agitation (not tremor/sweat)
PreventionSTOP SU; reduce insulin; relax targets; CGM alerts; carer education
NocturnalBedtime glucose 7-10; basal reduction; CGM predictive alerts
RenalInsulin ↓ 25-50% if eGFR < 45; stop SU if eGFR < 45
CognitiveCarer-administered insulin; CGM remote monitoring; simplified regimen

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
Why is hypoglycaemia risk higher in elderly?Renal impairment (↓ insulin clearance), polypharmacy, cognitive impairment, blunted counter-regulation, erratic meals
How does hypoglycaemia present differently in elderly?Atypical: confusion, falls, agitation, drowsiness (not classic tremor/sweating); neuroglycopenic predominant
What medications increase hypoglycaemia risk in elderly?Sulfonylureas (highest), insulin, beta-blockers (mask symptoms), ACEi/ARB
How do you prevent nocturnal hypoglycaemia?Bedtime glucose 7-10; reduce evening basal; bedtime snack if <7; CGM predictive alerts
How do you adjust insulin in CKD?Reduce 25-50% if eGFR < 45; stop sulfonylureas if eGFR < 45; monitor glucose closely
What is hypoglycaemia unawareness in elderly?Blunted autonomic symptoms (autonomic neuropathy + cognitive impairment); sudden neuroglycopenia

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Elderly = same hypo symptoms?NO - autonomic blunted; neuroglycopenic (confusion, falls) predominant
HbA1c reliable in elderly CKD?NO - falsely LOW (↓ RBC lifespan, ESA); use fructosamine/GA
Glucagon in elderly?Works if glycogen present; ineffective in starvation/alcohol/liver failure

Mnemonic: HYPO-ELDERLY-RISK

  • Hypoglycaemia: 5x risk in frail; 2x in >75y
  • Yield: atypical presentation (confusion, falls, not tremor)
  • Polypharmacy: SU + insulin + beta-blockers = highest risk
  • Old age: blunted counter-regulation (glucagon/epinephrine down)
  • Elderly CKD: insulin clearance down; reduce dose 25-50%
  • Living alone: no one to recognize/help
  • Dementia: cognitive impairment = can't recognize/report
  • Elderly frail: Rockwood >=5 = 5x hypo risk
  • Renal: eGFR<45 -> insulin reduce 25-50%; stop SU
  • Low glucose at night: bedtime 7-10; CGM alerts; snack
  • Year: annual hypoglycaemia review
  • Renal: fructosamine/GA if HbA1c unreliable
  • Insulin: reduce 25-50% in CKD; stop bolus; simplify basal
  • Stop sulfonylureas FIRST (highest risk)
  • Know: glucagon ineffective if glycogen depleted (starvation/alcohol)

topics.title: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Gdm
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply IADPSG (universal) and NICE (risk-based) GDM diagnostic criteria
  • [ ] State GDM glycaemic targets and monitoring frequency
  • [ ] Execute stepwise management: diet -> metformin -> insulin
  • [ ] Manage pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy (pre-conception, targets, teratogenicity)
  • [ ] Counsel on postpartum screening and lifetime T2DM risk

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGlucose intolerance with onset or first recognition in pregnancy (excluding pre-existing diabetes)
Incidence2-14% pregnancies (varies by population, criteria); IADPSG criteria -> up diagnosis 2-3x vs older criteria
Risk FactorsBMI >=30, previous GDM, previous macrosomia (>4.5kg), family history T2DM, ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean, Middle Eastern), age >40, PCOS, corticosteroid use
PathophysiologyPlacental hormones (hPL, progesterone, cortisol, oestrogen) -> insulin resistance up 2-3x by 3rd trimester; pancreatic beta-cells fail to compensate
ComplicationsFetal: macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, hyperbilirubinaemia, RDS, stillbirth; Maternal: pre-eclampsia, C-section, future T2DM (50% at 10y), future GDM (recurrence 30-50%)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
AsymptomaticMostDetected on screening OGTT
Osmotic symptomsIf marked hyperglycaemiaPolyuria, polydipsia (often masked by pregnancy)
Recurrent infectionsCommonCandidiasis, UTIs
Macrosomia (clinical)Late pregnancySFH >dates, large for dates on USS
Red Flags: Pre-existing diabetes (known before pregnancy); fasting glucose >=7.0 at booking -> likely pre-existing DM not GDM.

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

GDM Diagnostic Criteria (24-28 weeks)

CriteriaApproach75g OGTT Cut-offs (mmol/L)Diagnosis

| IADPSG / WHO 2013 / ADA | Universal (all pregnant) | FBG >=5.1

1h >=10.0

2h >=8.5 | >=1 abnormal value = GDM |

NICE (UK)Risk-based (if risk factors)FBG >=5.6 OR 2h >=7.8Either abnormal = GDM
ADA (alternative)Universal or risk-basedSame as IADPSG>=1 abnormal = GDM
Carpenter-Coustan (older)Universal100g OGTT: FBG>=5.3, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.6, 3h>=7.8>=2 abnormal = GDM
IADPSG preferred internationally; NICE used in UK -- IADPSG diagnoses more women, down adverse outcomes.

Pre-existing Diabetes in Pregnancy

TypePre-conception TargetsPregnancy Targets
T1DMHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%)FBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L
T2DMHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%)Same as T1DM

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationTimingKey Details
Booking HbA1c/FPG<10 weeksExclude pre-existing DM (HbA1c >=48 or FPG >=7.0 = pre-existing)
75g OGTT24-28 weeksIADPSG: FBG>=5.1, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.5 (1 abnormal = GDM); NICE: risk factors first
SMBGAfter diagnosisFBG + 1h post-prandial x4/day minimum; target FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4
HbA1cNot for monitoring in GDMUse in pre-existing DM (target <48mmol/mol); falsely low in 2nd/3rd tri
USS (fetal)28, 32, 36 weeksMacrosomia (EFW >90th centile), polyhydramnios, growth
Renal functionBaselineCKD affects metformin/insulin dosing

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Pre-existing diabetesKnown before pregnancy OR HbA1c >=48 / FPG >=7.0 at booking; requires pre-conception care
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness, steroids; resolves; no prior dysglycaemia
Type 1 diabetes presenting in pregnancyKetosis, weight loss, young, lean; check autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2), C-peptide
MODY (GCK)Mild fasting hyperglycaemia, strong FH, non-progressive; genetic testing if suspected
Renal glycosuriaNormal glucose, glycosuria; benign

7. Management

GDM Stepwise Management

StepInterventionThreshold to Escalate
1. Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT)Individualised diet (1800-2000 kcal, 40-45% carb, 20% protein, 35% fat); 3 meals + 2-3 snacks; dietitian reviewFBG >5.5 OR 1h post-prandial >7.0 mmol/L on 2+ occasions in 1-2 weeks
2. Metformin500mg OD -> 500mg BD -> 1000mg BD (max 2g/day); crosses placenta but safe (MiG trial); stop at deliveryFBG >6.0 OR 1h >7.8 OR failure to achieve targets in 1-2 weeks
3. InsulinBasal-bolus (rapid analogue pre-meal + basal) or Basal only if fasting hyperglycaemia only; TDD 0.7 U/kg (higher in 3rd tri); stop at deliveryIf metformin fails or contraindicated (eGFR<30, hepatic, patient choice)

Glycaemic Targets (ADA/NICE/IADPSG aligned)

MeasureTarget
Fasting<5.3 mmol/L (95 mg/dL)
1-hour post-prandial<7.8 mmol/L (140 mg/dL)
2-hour post-prandial<6.4 mmol/L (115 mg/dL)
HbA1cNot recommended for monitoring in GDM

Pre-existing Diabetes in Pregnancy

PhaseKey Actions
Pre-conceptionHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%); Folic acid 5mg OD (start >=3mo pre-conception -> 12wks); Stop teratogens: ACEi/ARB, statins, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, sulfonylureas; baseline retinal/renal/CV assessment
1st TrimesterIntensive monitoring (SMBG 7x/day); insulin dose down then up; screen for complications
2nd/3rd TrimesterInsulin requirements up 2-3x (placental insulin resistance); basal-bolus; tight targets; USS surveillance
Delivery38-39 weeks if well-controlled; 37-38 if complications; insulin infusion intrapartum (target 4-7 mmol/L)
PostpartumInsulin requirements drop dramatically; reduce to pre-pregnancy doses; breastfeeding -> adjust insulin/calories; OGTT 6-13 weeks postpartum

Medication Safety in Pregnancy

DrugSafetyNotes
InsulinSafe (does not cross placenta)Gold standard; human insulin/analogues
MetforminSafe (MiG trial)Crosses placenta but no teratogenicity; 1st line oral
GlibenclamideAlternativeCrosses placenta; up neonatal hypoglycaemia; 2nd line if metformin failed
SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA / DPP-4i / TZD / SU (except glibenclamide)ContraindicatedTeratogenicity / limited safety data

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
IADPSG GDMUniversal 75g OGTT 24-28wk; FBG>=5.1, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.5; 1 abnormal = GDM
NICE GDMRisk factors (BMI>=30, prev GDM, macrosomia, FH, ethnicity, age>40) -> OGTT; FBG>=5.6 OR 2h>=7.8
GDM TargetsFBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L; SMBG 4x/day (FBG + 1h post-meal)
ManagementDiet -> Metformin (if FBG>5.5 or 1h>7.0) -> Insulin (basal-bolus/basal)
Pre-existing DMPre-conception HbA1c <48; Folic acid 5mg OD -> 12wks; Stop ACEi/ARB/statin/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA/SU
IntrapartumInsulin IV infusion; target 4-7 mmol/L; reduce postpartum
PostpartumOGTT 6-13 weeks; annual HbA1c; lifetime T2DM risk 50%
Metformin in GDMMiG trial: safe, crosses placenta but no teratogenicity; 1st line oral; max 2g/day
Glibenclamide2nd line if metformin fails; crosses placenta; up neonatal hypoglycaemia
Drugs to STOPACEi/ARB, statins, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, sulfonylureas (except glibenclamide), TZDs

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the IADPSG diagnostic criteria for GDM?75g OGTT at 24-28 weeks: FBG >=5.1, 1h >=10.0, 2h >=8.5 mmol/L -- one or more abnormal values = GDM
How does NICE GDM screening differ from IADPSG?NICE: risk-based (BMI>=30, prev GDM, macrosomia, FH, ethnicity, age>40) -> OGTT if risk factors; thresholds: FBG>=5.6 OR 2h>=7.8; IADPSG: universal, lower thresholds
What are the glycaemic targets in GDM?FBG <5.3 mmol/L; 1h post-prandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2h post-prandial <6.4 mmol/L
What is the stepwise management of GDM?1) MNT + SMBG; 2) Metformin if FBG>5.5 or 1h>7.0 on 2+ occasions; 3) Insulin (basal-bolus or basal only) if metformin fails
What is the pre-conception care for pre-existing diabetes?HbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%); Folic acid 5mg OD (>=3mo pre-conception -> 12wks); Stop ACEi/ARB, statins, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, sulfonylureas; baseline retinal/renal/CV screen
Why is metformin preferred over glibenclamide in GDM?MiG trial: metformin safe, no teratogenicity; glibenclamide crosses placenta -> up neonatal hypoglycaemia; metformin 1st line
What happens to insulin requirements postpartum?Dramatic drop (placental insulin resistance removed); reduce to pre-pregnancy doses; monitor for hypoglycaemia; breastfeeding -> may need further reduction
When do you do postpartum OGTT after GDM?6-13 weeks postpartum (75g OGTT); annual HbA1c thereafter; lifetime T2DM risk ~50%

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
GDM = pre-existing diabetes?NO -- GDM = first detected in pregnancy; if HbA1c>=48 or FPG>=7.0 at booking -> pre-existing
HbA1c for GDM monitoring?NO -- falsely low in 2nd/3rd trimester (up RBC turnover); use SMBG
Metformin teratogenic?NO -- MiG trial: crosses placenta but no teratogenicity; 1st line oral
Insulin crosses placenta?NO -- large molecule; safe for fetus
Postpartum OGTT timing?6-13 weeks; not at 6 weeks (still placental hormones), not immediate

Mnemonic: GDM-IADPSG

  • GDM: hyperglycaemia 1st detected in pregnancy
  • Diagnosis: IADPSG universal 75g OGTT 24-28wk
  • Means: FBG>=5.1, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.5 (1 abnormal = GDM)
  • IADPSG vs NICE: universal vs risk-based
  • ADA: same as IADPSG
  • Diet 1st (MNT), SMBG 4x/day
  • Post-prandial targets: FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4
  • Stepped: Diet -> Metformin (FBG>5.5/1h>7.0) -> Insulin
  • Glibenclamide 2nd line (metformin fail)
  • Pre-existing: HbA1c>=48 at booking -> pre-conception care
  • Reconception: HbA1c<48, Folic 5mg, STOP teratogens (ACEi/ARB/statin/SGLT2i/GLP-1/SU)
  • OGTT postpartum: 6-13wk
  • Screening postpartum: annual HbA1c, lifetime T2DM risk 50%
  • Gestational: resolves delivery but recurrence 30-50%

11. Mind Map

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus
Diagnosis
IADPSG: universal 75g OGTT
FBG>=5.1, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.5
NICE: risk-based
Targets
FBG <5.3
1h <7.8
2h <6.4
SMBG 4x/day
Management
Diet (MNT)
Metformin 1st line oral
Insulin (basal-bolus)
Pre-existing DM
Pre-conception HbA1c<48
Folic 5mg OD
Stop teratogens
Postpartum
OGTT 6-13wk
Annual HbA1c
T2DM risk 50%
Contraindicated
SGLT2i, GLP-1, DPP-4i
SU (except glibenclamide)
ACEi/ARB, statins

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionHyperglycaemia first detected in pregnancy (excl. pre-existing)
**Key Test"75g OGTT 24-28wk (IADPSG universal); SMBG 4x/day; not HbA1c
**Classification"GDM vs pre-existing (HbA1c>=48/FPG>=7.0 at booking)
**Acute Mgmt"Intrapartum: IV insulin infusion target 4-7 mmol/L
**Chronic Mgmt"Diet -> Metformin (FBG>5.5/1h>7.0) -> Insulin; Pre-existing: folic 5mg, stop teratogens
**Key Score"IADPSG: 1 abnormal = GDM; Targets FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4
**Complications"Macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypo, pre-eclampsia, C-section
**Prognosis"Resolves delivery; postpartum OGTT 6-13wk; T2DM risk 50% lifetime

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt
Definition & Epidemiology
Classification & Staging
Diagnosis & Investigations
Management (Acute)
Management (Chronic)
Complications
Viva Questions
DDx Distinctions
Mnemonics/Algorithms

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply eGFR-based dosing for all diabetes medications
  • [ ] Identify contraindicated medications in CKD
  • [ ] Apply renal adjustment for SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, DPP-4i, insulin
  • [ ] Manage medication changes during dialysis

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply eGFR-based dosing for all diabetes medications
  • [ ] Identify contraindicated medications in CKD
  • [ ] Apply renal adjustment for SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, DPP-4i, insulin
  • [ ] Manage medication changes during dialysis

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail

CKD StagesG1 >=90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15
Drug clearance↓ renal excretion; ↑ half-life; ↑ hypoglycaemia risk

2. Definition & Epidemiology

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

eGFR-Based Dosing Table

BiguanideMetforminFull doseHalf max doseCONTRAINDICATEDCONTRAINDICATED

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
eGFR (CKD-EPI)Dosing guide; baseline, 3-6 monthly if <60
HbA1cTarget per CKD stage (falsely low in G4-5)
K+Monitor with SGLT2i, ACEi/ARB, K+-sparing diuretics
GlucoseSMBG/CGM; adjust insulin doses

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
eGFR (CKD-EPI)Dosing guide; baseline, 3-6 monthly if <60
HbA1cTarget per CKD stage (falsely low in G4-5)
K+Monitor with SGLT2i, ACEi/ARB, K+-sparing diuretics
GlucoseSMBG/CGM; adjust insulin doses

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Key Principles

⬇ Clinical Pathway
New DM drug in CKD
↓
Check eGFR
↓
Contraindicated?
↓
Choose alternative
Dose adjust needed?
↓
Adjust per eGFR table
Standard dose
↓
Monitor: glucose, eGFR 3-6mo, K+, HbA1c

Insulin Dose Reduction in CKD

eGFRReductionNotes
G3a (45-59)10-20%Monitor for hypo
G3b (30-44)20-30%↑ hypo risk
G4 (15-29)25-50%↑↑ hypo risk; simplify regimen
G5 (<15)50-75%↑↑↑ hypo risk; U-100 insulin; frequent monitoring
Dialysis50-75%Variable needs; pre/post dialysis glucose

Medications to AVOID in CKD

DrugReason
MetformineGFR < 30
GlibenclamideAll CKD (prolonged hypo)
SaxagliptinHF risk (avoid if HF)
PioglitazoneeGFR < 30 (HF, fracture)
CanagliflozineGFR < 30 (amputation risk)
Exenatide XReGFR < 30 (limited data)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MetformineGFR >=45 full; 30-44 half; <30 CONTRAINDICATED
SGLT2iDapa/Empa: initiate >=20, continue to dialysis; Cana >=30
LinagliptinNO renal adjustment (biliary excretion)
Sitagliptin50mg (30-44), 25mg (<30)
SulfonylureasGliclazide MR safe; glimepiride caution; glibenclamide AVOID
GLP-1 RANo dose adjustment (all stages)
InsulinReduce 25-50% in G4-5; 50-75% in dialysis

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MetformineGFR >=45 full; 30-44 half; <30 CONTRAINDICATED
SGLT2iDapa/Empa: initiate >=20, continue to dialysis; Cana >=30
LinagliptinNO renal adjustment (biliary excretion)
Sitagliptin50mg (30-44), 25mg (<30)
SulfonylureasGliclazide MR safe; glimepiride caution; glibenclamide AVOID
GLP-1 RANo dose adjustment (all stages)
InsulinReduce 25-50% in G4-5; 50-75% in dialysis

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How do you dose metformin in CKD?eGFR >=45: full; 30-44: half max; <30: CONTRAINDICATED
Which SGLT2i can be used in eGFR < 30?Dapagliflozin/empagliflozin: initiate >=20, continue to dialysis; canagliflozin >=30
Which DPP-4i requires NO renal dose adjustment?Linagliptin (biliary excretion)
Which sulfonylurea is preferred in CKD?Gliclazide MR (no active metabolites); glimepiride caution; glibenclamide AVOID
How do you adjust insulin in dialysis?Reduce 50-75%; variable needs; monitor pre/post dialysis glucose; U-100 insulin
Which GLP-1 RA needs dose adjustment in CKD?None - no dose adjustment for any GLP-1 RA
What about saxagliptin in CKD with HF?Avoid (SAVOR: ↑HF hospitalisation)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How do you dose metformin in CKD?eGFR >=45: full; 30-44: half max; <30: CONTRAINDICATED
Which SGLT2i can be used in eGFR < 30?Dapagliflozin/empagliflozin: initiate >=20, continue to dialysis; canagliflozin >=30
Which DPP-4i requires NO renal dose adjustment?Linagliptin (biliary excretion)
Which sulfonylurea is preferred in CKD?Gliclazide MR (no active metabolites); glimepiride caution; glibenclamide AVOID
How do you adjust insulin in dialysis?Reduce 50-75%; variable needs; monitor pre/post dialysis glucose; U-100 insulin
Which GLP-1 RA needs dose adjustment in CKD?None - no dose adjustment for any GLP-1 RA
What about saxagliptin in CKD with HF?Avoid (SAVOR: ↑HF hospitalisation)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All SGLT2i same in CKD?NO - dapa/empa continue to dialysis; cana stops <30
All DPP-4i same renal dosing?NO - linagliptin unique (no adjust); others need reduction
Metformin in all CKD?NO - only <30 contraindicated; 30-44 = half dose
GLP-1 RA in dialysis?Safe; no dose adjust; limited data but used

Mnemonic: CKD-DOSING-DM

  • CKD: eGFR stages G1-5
  • Key: eGFR drives dosing
  • Dose reductions: metformin (30-44 half, <30 stop)
  • Oral agents: linagliptin NO adjust; sitagliptin 50/25
  • SGLT2i: dapa/empa >=20 to dialysis; cana >=30
  • Insulin: G4-5 reduce 25-50%; dialysis 50-75%
  • No adjust: linagliptin, GLP-1 RA
  • Glibenclamide: AVOID all CKD
  • DPP-4i: linagliptin unique (no adjust)
  • Oral SU: glic MR safe; glime caution; gliben AVOID
  • SGLT2i: dapa/empa to dialysis; cana >=30
  • Insulin: reduce 25-50% (G4), 50-75% (dialysis)
  • No adjust: GLP-1 RA (all stages)
  • Metformin: <30 CONTRAINDICATED
  • Ezetimibe: no dose adjust
  • Pioglitazone: avoid if eGFR<30 (HF)
  • Renal: eGFR CKD-EPI
  • Impairment: dose per stage
  • Not all same: SGLT2i diff; DPP-4i diff; SU diff
  • GFR: CKD-EPI equation**

topics.title: Cgm Guided Insulin Adjustment
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain why HbA1c is falsely low in CKD/ESRD
  • [ ] Apply alternative glycaemic markers: fructosamine, glycated albumin
  • [ ] Set appropriate glycaemic targets in CKD
  • [ ] Interpret HbA1c in context of ESA use, blood transfusion

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain why HbA1c is falsely low in CKD/ESRD
  • [ ] Apply alternative glycaemic markers: fructosamine, glycated albumin
  • [ ] Set appropriate glycaemic targets in CKD
  • [ ] Interpret HbA1c in context of ESA use, blood transfusion

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
ProblemHbA1c underestimates true glycaemia in CKD/ESRD
Mechanism↓RBC lifespan (uraemia, ESA, blood loss); ↑carbamylation of Hb
MagnitudeHbA1c 5-15% lower than true mean glucose in ESRD
ESA effectESA -> reticulocytosis -> younger RBCs -> ↓HbA1c

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
ProblemHbA1c underestimates true glycaemia in CKD/ESRD
Mechanism↓RBC lifespan (uraemia, ESA, blood loss); ↑carbamylation of Hb
MagnitudeHbA1c 5-15% lower than true mean glucose in ESRD
ESA effectESA -> reticulocytosis -> younger RBCs -> ↓HbA1c

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

HbA1c Bias by CKD Stage

CKD StageeGFRHbA1c BiasAlternative Preferred
G1-G2>=60MinimalHbA1c reliable
G3a45-59Mild (5-10% low)HbA1c + fructosamine
G3b30-44Moderate (10-15% low)Fructosamine/GA preferred
G415-29Significant (15-20% low)Fructosamine/GA essential
G5/ESRD<15 / dialysisSevere (15-25% low)GA/Fructosamine only

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

HbA1c Bias by CKD Stage

CKD StageeGFRHbA1c BiasAlternative Preferred
G1-G2>=60MinimalHbA1c reliable
G3a45-59Mild (5-10% low)HbA1c + fructosamine
G3b30-44Moderate (10-15% low)Fructosamine/GA preferred
G415-29Significant (15-20% low)Fructosamine/GA essential
G5/ESRD<15 / dialysisSevere (15-25% low)GA/Fructosamine only

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Alternative Glycaemic Markers

MarkerReflectsAdvantagesLimitations
FructosamineMean glucose 2-3 weeksUnaffected by RBC lifespan; cheap↓ in hypoproteinaemia; no DCCT standardisation
Glycated Albumin (GA)Mean glucose 2-3 weeksUnaffected by RBC lifespan; standardised↓ in nephrotic syndrome (albumin loss)
Continuous Glucose MonitoringReal-timeDirect glucose measurementCost; calibration; not universally available

HbA1c Interpretation Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Diabetes + CKD
↓
eGFR >= 45?
↓
HbA1c reliable; target per guidelines
eGFR 30-44?
↓
HbA1c + Fructosamine; interpret with caution
Fructosamine/GA essential; HbA1c unreliable
↓
Target GA: <17-18% (equiv HbA1c ~53)
Target Fructosamine: ~230-280 umol/L

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Iron deficiency anaemiaHbA1c falsely HIGH (prolonged RBC survival)
Haemolysis/blood lossHbA1c falsely LOW (shortened RBC survival)
ESA therapyHbA1c falsely LOW (reticulocytosis)
Recent transfusionHbA1c falsely LOW (donor RBCs)
Nephrotic syndromeGA falsely LOW (albumin loss); fructosamine preferred

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Iron deficiency anaemiaHbA1c falsely HIGH (prolonged RBC survival)
Haemolysis/blood lossHbA1c falsely LOW (shortened RBC survival)
ESA therapyHbA1c falsely LOW (reticulocytosis)
Recent transfusionHbA1c falsely LOW (donor RBCs)
Nephrotic syndromeGA falsely LOW (albumin loss); fructosamine preferred

7. Management

Glycaemic Targets in CKD

CKD StageHbA1c Target (if reliable)Alternative Target (GA/Fructosamine)
G1-G3a< 53 mmol/mol (7.0%)GA < 18%; Fructosamine < 280 umol/L
G3b-G4< 58 mmol/mol (7.5%)GA < 19-20%; Fructosamine < 300 umol/L
G5 / ESRD< 64 mmol/mol (8.0%)GA < 20-21%; Fructosamine < 320 umol/L
Dialysis< 64 mmol/mol (8.0%)GA < 20-21%; Fructosamine < 320 umol/L

Monitoring Frequency

MarkerFrequency
HbA1c6-monthly (G1-G3); 3-monthly (G4-5)
Fructosamine/GA3-monthly (G3b-G5)
CGMContinuous (if available)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HbA1c in CKDFalsely LOW (↓RBC lifespan, ESA); bias 5-25% by stage
Iron deficiencyHbA1c falsely HIGH (prolonged RBC survival)
AlternativesFructosamine (2-3 wk); Glycated Albumin (2-3 wk); CGM
GA target<18% (equiv HbA1c ~7.0%); <20% if high risk
Fructosamine target<280 umol/L (equiv HbA1c ~7.0%)
DialysisHbA1c unreliable; use GA/Fructosamine/CGM

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
Why is HbA1c falsely low in CKD?↓RBC lifespan (uraemia, ESA, blood loss); carbamylation of Hb
When is HbA1c unreliable in CKD?eGFR < 45 (G3b+); ESRD/dialysis = completely unreliable
What are alternative glycaemic markers in CKD?Fructosamine (2-3 wk), Glycated Albumin (2-3 wk), CGM
What is the HbA1c target in dialysis?<64 mmol/mol (8.0%) but unreliable; use GA <20% or Fructosamine <320
How does iron deficiency affect HbA1c?Falsely HIGH (prolonged RBC survival)
How does ESA affect HbA1c?Falsely LOW (reticulocytosis -> younger RBCs)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HbA1c = gold standard always?NO - unreliable in CKD G3b+; use GA/Fructosamine/CGM
GA vs Fructosamine?GA standardised; fructosamine cheaper; both 2-3 wk mean
Nephrotic syndrome -> GA?GA falsely LOW (albumin loss); use fructosamine instead

Mnemonic: HBA1C-CKD-ALTERNATIVE

  • HbA1c in CKD: falsely LOW (RBC lifespan down)
  • Bias: 5-25% by stage (G3b+ unreliable)
  • Anaemia: iron deficiency -> HbA1c HIGH
  • 1 (ESA): reticulocytosis -> HbA1c LOW
  • CKD stage: G3b+ = HbA1c unreliable
  • KDIGO: use GA/Fructosamine in G3b+
  • Dialysis: HbA1c unreliable; GA/Fructosamine/CGM
  • Alternatives: Fructosamine (2-3wk), GA (2-3wk), CGM
  • Low albumin: nephrotic -> GA LOW; use fructosamine
  • Targets: GA <18% (HbA1c ~53); Fructosamine <280
  • ESA use: -> HbA1c LOW
  • Reflection: always check CBC/iron/ESA when interpreting HbA1c in CKD
  • Nephrotic: albumin loss -> GA unreliable
  • Alternative: CGM gold standard if available
  • Transfusion: recent -> HbA1c LOW
  • Iron: deficiency -> HbA1c HIGH
  • Validation: no DCCT standard for GA/Fructosamine
  • Extreme: ESRD = HbA1c useless**

topics.title: Insulin Resistance In Hiv
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain mechanisms of insulin resistance in HIV
  • [ ] Identify HIV-specific contributors: chronic inflammation, ART, lipodystrophy
  • [ ] Apply screening and management of insulin resistance in HIV
  • [ ] Differentiate HIV-related IR from traditional metabolic syndrome

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain mechanisms of insulin resistance in HIV
  • [ ] Identify HIV-specific contributors: chronic inflammation, ART, lipodystrophy
  • [ ] Apply screening and management of insulin resistance in HIV
  • [ ] Differentiate HIV-related IR from traditional metabolic syndrome

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PrevalenceIR in 40-60% PLWH; DM in 10-15% (vs 8-10% general)
MechanismsChronic inflammation, mitochondrial toxicity, ART, lipodystrophy
Risk FactorsART (PI, INSTI), lipodystrophy, HCV co-infection, visceral adiposity

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PrevalenceIR in 40-60% PLWH; DM in 10-15% (vs 8-10% general)
MechanismsChronic inflammation, mitochondrial toxicity, ART, lipodystrophy
Risk FactorsART (PI, INSTI), lipodystrophy, HCV co-infection, visceral adiposity

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureHIV-Specific IRTraditional Metabolic Syndrome
InflammationChronic (↑IL-6, TNF-α, CRP)Acute phase reactants
Mitochondrial toxicityNRTI (older), PI, INSTIAbsent
LipodystrophyLipoatrophy + lipohypertrophyCentral obesity only
ART contributionPI, INSTI (DTG/BIC), older NRTIAbsent
HCV co-infectionCommon; ↑IRUnrelated

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureHIV-Specific IRTraditional Metabolic Syndrome
InflammationChronic (↑IL-6, TNF-α, CRP)Acute phase reactants
Mitochondrial toxicityNRTI (older), PI, INSTIAbsent
LipodystrophyLipoatrophy + lipohypertrophyCentral obesity only
ART contributionPI, INSTI (DTG/BIC), older NRTIAbsent
HCV co-infectionCommon; ↑IRUnrelated

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Mechanisms of Insulin Resistance in HIV

MechanismDetails
Chronic inflammation↑IL-6, TNF-α, CRP → ↓IRS-1 signalling
Mitochondrial toxicityNRTI (stavudine, zidovudine) → mtDNA depletion
ART effectsPI: ↓GLUT4, ↑lipogenesis; INSTI (DTG/BIC): weight gain, ↑adiposity
LipodystrophyLipoatrophy → ↓adiponectin; lipohypertrophy → visceral fat
HCV co-infectionHCV core protein → IRS-1 serine phosphorylation

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
HOMA-IR(Fasting insulin × fasting glucose) / 22.5; >2.5 = IR
Fasting glucose/HbA1cScreen for diabetes (HbA1c falsely low in CKD)
Lipid profileTG, HDL, LDL, apoB
Adiponectin/leptinLow adiponectin = IR marker
Visceral fatCT/MRI (gold standard); waist circumference surrogate
HCV RNAScreen all PLWH; treat if positive

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
HOMA-IR(Fasting insulin × fasting glucose) / 22.5; >2.5 = IR
Fasting glucose/HbA1cScreen for diabetes (HbA1c falsely low in CKD)
Lipid profileTG, HDL, LDL, apoB
Adiponectin/leptinLow adiponectin = IR marker
Visceral fatCT/MRI (gold standard); waist circumference surrogate
HCV RNAScreen all PLWH; treat if positive

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Traditional metabolic syndromeObesity, HTN, dyslipidaemia; no ART, no HIV
Cushing syndromeCortisol excess; specific features
AcromegalyGH/IGF-1 excess
Lipodystrophy syndromesGenetic (AGPAT2, BSCL2, LMNA)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Traditional metabolic syndromeObesity, HTN, dyslipidaemia; no ART, no HIV
Cushing syndromeCortisol excess; specific features
AcromegalyGH/IGF-1 excess
Lipodystrophy syndromesGenetic (AGPAT2, BSCL2, LMNA)

7. Management

HIV-Specific IR Management

⬇ Clinical Pathway
HIV + Insulin Resistance
↓
Screen: HOMA-IR, lipids, HCV, visceral fat
↓
On ART?
↓
ART review: PI -> INSTI if suppressed
Start ART (DTG/BIC preferred; minimise weight gain)
↓
Metabolic Rx
↓
Metformin 1st line IR
GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i if T2DM
Statin + fibrate if dyslipidaemia
Weight loss if obese
HCV treatment if co-infected

Management Strategies

DomainIntervention
ART optimisationSwitch PI -> INSTI (DTG/BIC) if suppressed; avoid older NRTI
Metformin1st line for IR (if eGFR >=30); weight neutral
GLP-1 RA / SGLT2iIf T2DM + obesity/CV/CKD; weight loss benefit
StatinAtorvastatin 20-40mg (CV risk reduction)
Weight loss5-7% → ↓IR 30-50%; lifestyle + GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i
HCV treatmentDAA cure → ↓IR (SVR12)

HCV Co-infection

AspectManagement
ScreenAll PLWH: HCV Ab then RNA
TreatDAA (SOF/VEL, GLE/PIB); 8-12 weeks
BenefitSVR12 → ↓IR, ↓diabetes risk, ↓liver complications
TimingTreat early; before cirrhosis

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismsChronic inflammation, ART (PI/INSTI), mitochondrial toxicity, lipodystrophy, HCV
PrevalenceIR 40-60%; DM 10-15% (vs 8-10% general)
ARTPI (lopinavir) > INSTI (DTG/BIC) > NNRTI for IR
LipodystrophyLipoatrophy (face/limbs) + lipohypertrophy (abdomen) → IR
HCVCo-infection common; DAA cure → ↓IR
ScreeningAnnual fasting glucose/HbA1c; HOMA-IR if available
ManagementART switch (PI->INSTI); metformin; GLP-1/SGLT2i if T2DM; HCV cure

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the mechanisms of insulin resistance in HIV?Chronic inflammation (IL-6, TNF-α), ART (PI/INSTI), mitochondrial toxicity (NRTI), lipodystrophy, HCV
How does chronic inflammation cause IR in HIV?↑IL-6, TNF-α, CRP → serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 → ↓insulin signalling
Which ART drugs cause insulin resistance?PI (lopinavir/r > atazanavir/r), INSTI (dolutegravir, bictegravir), older NRTI (stavudine, zidovudine)
How does lipodystrophy contribute to IR?Lipoatrophy -> ↓adiponectin; lipohypertrophy -> visceral adiposity -> inflammation
Does HCV co-infection worsen IR?YES - HCV core protein → IRS-1 serine phosphorylation; DAA cure improves IR
What is the screening for diabetes in HIV?Annual fasting glucose/HbA1c; HOMA-IR if available; CV risk assessment

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HIV IR = traditional metabolic syndrome?NO - HIV IR has unique drivers (ART, chronic inflammation, lipodystrophy, HCV)
All ART = IR?PI > INSTI (DTG/BIC) > NNRTI; raltegravir/doravirine neutral
HCV cure = IR cure?DAA cure (SVR12) improves IR but may not fully normalise
Lipodystrophy = just cosmetic?NO - metabolic consequences: IR, dyslipidaemia, diabetes risk

Mnemonic: HIV-IR-MECHANISMS

  • HIV IR: 40-60% prevalence
  • Inflammation: chronic IL-6, TNF-a, CRP -> IRS-1 serine phosphorylation
  • Viral: HIV itself + HCV co-infection
  • INSULIN resistance: HOMA-IR >2.5
  • RAV: lopinavir/r > atazanavir/r > INSTI DTG/BIC
  • Toxicity: mitochondrial (NRTI stavudine/ZDV)
  • Lipodystrophy: lipoatrophy + lipohypertrophy
  • INVITIS: chronic inflammation (IL-6, TNF-a, CRP)
  • ART optimisation: PI -> INSTI (DTG/BIC) if suppressed
  • Nephropathy: not main driver
  • Screening: annual glucose/HbA1c; HOMA-IR
  • Metformin 1st line IR; GLP-1/SGLT2i if T2DM
  • EHC cure: DAA -> SVR12 -> IR improvement
  • Chronic inflammation: the core driver
  • HIV-specific: unique vs traditional MetS
  • Adiponectin low: lipoatrophy marker
  • NRTI: older (stavudine/ZDV) worst mitochondrial toxicity
  • IDA cure: DAA -> SVR12 -> IR improvement
  • CV risk: statin + metabolic control
  • ART switch: PI -> INSTI if suppressed
  • Lipodystrophy: lipoatrophy + lipohypertrophy = IR
  • SGLT2i/GLP-1: if T2DM + obesity/CKD/CV
  • You screen: annual glucose/HbA1c**

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Pump types differ in tubing, form factor, integration; tethered vs patch vs closed-loop; NICE criteria for pump access"

see_also: ["Insulin pump therapy (CSII)", "Hybrid closed-loop (670G, 780G, CamAPS, Loop)", "Automated insulin delivery (AID) systems", "CGM metrics (TIR, TBR, TAR, GMI, CV)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Insulin Pump Types (Tethered, Patch, Closed-Loop)

[!info]
CSII = Continuous Subcutaneous Insulin Infusion — Tethered (tubed), Patch (tubeless), and Closed-Loop (AID) systems; only rapid-acting insulin used; NICE criteria for funding.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify pump types: tethered, patch/tubeless, closed-loop
  • [ ] Compare features, pros/cons of each system
  • [ ] State NICE criteria for pump access
  • [ ] Explain pump mechanics: basal rates, bolus types, temporary basal
  • [ ] Understand infusion set issues and troubleshooting

2. Pump Classification

CategoryTypeExamplesKey Feature
Tethered (Tubed)TraditionalMedtronic 780G, Tandem t:slim X2, Ypsomed mylife YpsoPumpTubing connects pump to infusion set
Patch (Tubeless)Wearable/podInsulet Omnipod 5, Omnipod DASH, CeQur Simplicity, V-GoPod adheres to body; no tubing; controlled by PDA/phone
Closed-Loop / AIDHybrid (HCL)Medtronic 780G, Tandem Control-IQ, Omnipod 5, CamAPS FX, Loop (DIY)Automates basal ± some correction boluses
Full (experimental)Beta Bionics iLet, DIY bicornealAutomates basal + bolus

3. Tethered Pumps (Traditional)

PumpCGM IntegrationAID AlgorithmKey Features
Medtronic 780GGuardian 3/4PID (SmartGuard)Meal detection, auto-correction bolus, 70–180 target adjustable
Tandem t:slim X2Dexcom G6/G7MPC (Control-IQ)Predictive 30 min ahead, sleep/exercise modes, remote monitoring
Ypsomed mylife YpsoPumpDexcom G6/G7 / FreeStyle Libre 3CamAPS FX (on phone)Small, customisable, CamAPS FX app-based AID

Pros: Large insulin reservoir (300u), proven reliability, multiple AID options, screen on pump

Cons: Tubing (snagging, dislodgement), visible device, infusion set changes q2–3d

4. Patch Pumps (Tubeless)

PumpCGM IntegrationAID AlgorithmKey Features
Insulet Omnipod 5Dexcom G6/G7MPC (SmartAdjust)Pod holds 200u; controller = phone; waterproof (IP28); no tubing
Omnipod DASHDexcom G6/G7Manual (no AID)Phone controller; no AID
CeQur SimplicityNone (standalone)None3-day wearable; 200u; mechanical bolus button
V-GoNoneNoneDisposable 24h; basal + on-demand bolus (mechanical)

Pros: No tubing, discreet, waterproof, easy to wear, no infusion set tubing

Cons: Smaller reservoir (200u), pod change q3d (vs q2–3d set), adhesive issues, cost higher, limited AID (Omnipod 5 only)

5. NICE Criteria for CSII Access (TA151 / NG17/NG18)

Adults (T1DM)

All must be met:

  1. T1DM (or insulin-deficient T3c/pancreatectomy)
  2. HbA1c ≥69 mmol/mol (8.5%) on MDI OR disabling hypoglycaemia (recurrent severe/unpredictable) OR HbA1c target not met despite optimal MDI + education + CGM
  3. Structured education completed (DAFNE or equivalent)
  4. Competent in carb counting, SMBG/CGM, sick day rules
  5. Commitment to follow-up (pump team)

Children/Young People (T1DM)

  • All children <12y: CSII first-line if MDI fails (NG18)
  • Age ≥12y: Same as adults

Pregnancy

  • CSII recommended if MDI fails to achieve targets (NICE NG3)

T2DM

  • Not routinely funded; exceptional case-by-case (severe insulin resistance, high TDD)

6. Pump Mechanics — Core Concepts

ConceptDescriptionTypical Range
Basal rateContinuous insulin delivery (units/hour)0.05–2.0 u/hr; 24+ programmable rates
Basal profile24h pattern (often 3–6 segments)Dawn phenomenon: ↑ 2–8 AM
Temporary basal% of programmed basal (duration 30min–24h)Exercise: 50–80%; Illness: 120–150%
Standard bolusImmediate delivery for mealsPer ICR
Extended / Square bolusDelivered over 30min–8hHigh fat/protein meals, gastroparesis
Dual / Multi-wave bolusImmediate + extended portionPizza, curry, restaurant meals
Correction bolusBased on ISF + targetPer ISF
Active Insulin / IOBInsulin still acting from previous bolusesTracks 3–5h; prevents stacking

7. Infusion Sets & Sites

Set TypeAngleLengthBest For
Straight (90°)90°6–9mmMost adults, abdomen/thigh
Angled (30–45°)30–45°13–17mmLean, muscular, children
Steel needle90°6–8mmAllergy to Teflon; short-term
Soft cannula (Teflon)90° or angled6–17mmStandard

Site rotation: Abdomen (avoid 2" around umbilicus), thighs, buttocks, upper arms. Change every 2–3 days (72h max).

8. Common Problems & Troubleshooting

ProblemCauseSolution
Unexplained hyperglycaemiaOcclusion, dislodgement, insulin degradation, site lipohypertrophyCheck site; change set; rotate sites; new insulin vial
Nocturnal hypoglycaemiaBasal too high; exercise effect; alcohol↓ Basal 10–20% overnight; temp basal; review AGP
Dawn phenomenonGrowth hormone/cortisol surge 3–8 AM↑ Basal 2–3 AM; or AID handles automatically
Pump alarm: OcclusionKinked cannula, lipohypertrophy, insulin precipitationChange set + site; check for lumps
Adhesive failureSweat, oil, allergySkin prep (IV prep, Mastisol); different adhesive; patch pump

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
NICE CSII criteria T1DMHbA1c ≥69 mmol/mol (8.5%) OR disabling hypos + DAFNE + carb counting
Tethered pumpsMedtronic 780G, Tandem t:slim X2, Ypsomed — tubing
Patch pumpsOmnipod 5 (AID), Omnipod DASH — tubeless, pod on body
HCL systems780G (PID), Control-IQ (MPC), Omnipod 5 (MPC), CamAPS FX (MPC)
Basal rateUnits/hour; 24+ programmable segments
Temp basal% of programmed; exercise 50–80%, illness 120–150%
Bolus typesStandard, extended/square, dual/multi-wave
IOBActive insulin tracking 3–5h; prevents stacking
Infusion set changeEvery 2–3 days (72h max); rotate sites
LipohypertrophyCauses erratic absorption — must rotate sites

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - rtCGM vs isCGM differences critical for device selection; NICE/ADA criteria; cost-effectiveness; alerts vs scanning; data completeness"

see_also: ["Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)", "CGM metrics (TIR, TBR, TAR, GMI, CV)", "CGM-guided insulin adjustment", "Insulin pump therapy (CSII)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

rtCGM vs isCGM (Real-Time vs Intermittently-Scanned CGM)

[!info]
rtCGM = Real-time alerts + automatic upload (Dexcom G6/G7, Guardian, FreeStyle Libre 3); isCGM = Scan-dependent (FreeStyle Libre 1/2). Both factory-calibrated; rtCGM superior for hypoglycaemia prevention.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Compare rtCGM and isCGM technology, features, evidence
  • [ ] Apply NICE/ADA criteria for each
  • [ ] Explain cost-effectiveness considerations
  • [ ] Guide patient choice based on clinical needs
  • [ ] Understand data completeness implications

2. Definitions & Technology

FeaturertCGM (Real-Time CGM)isCGM (Intermittently-Scanned / Flash)
ExamplesDexcom G6/G7, Medtronic Guardian 3/4, FreeStyle Libre 3, EversenseFreeStyle Libre 1, FreeStyle Libre 2 (optional alarms)
Data transmissionAutomatic (Bluetooth/BLE to phone/receiver)Manual scan (NFC) every ≤8 hours
Real-time alertsYes (predictive low/high, rate-of-change)Libre 1: No; Libre 2: Optional (Bluetooth alarms only)
Data completenessHigh (≥95% auto)User-dependent (scan ≥8-hourly for 100%)
CalibrationFactory (no fingerstick)Factory (no fingerstick)
Sensor life10 days (G6/G7, Guardian); 14 days (Libre 3); 180 days (Eversense)14 days (Libre 1/2)
Warm-up2h (G6/G7); 2h (Guardian); 1h (Libre 3)1h (Libre 1/2)
Integration with pumpsFull (HCL: Tandem, Omnipod 5, CamAPS, 780G)Libre 2/3 with some (Omnipod 5, CamAPS FX)

3. Key Clinical Differences

Hypoglycaemia Prevention

AspectrtCGMisCGM
Nocturnal hypoglycaemia detectionAutomatic alerts (wakes patient/carer)Missed unless scanned; Libre 2 alarms if enabled
Predictive low alertsYes (20–30 min before <3.9)Libre 2 only
Rate-of-change alertsYes (↑↑/↓↓)No
Evidence for hypo reductionStrong (multiple RCTs)Moderate (Libre 2 approaching rtCGM)

HbA1c Reduction

PopulationrtCGMisCGM
T1DM on MDI−0.4 to −0.6%−0.3 to −0.5%
T1DM on pump−0.3 to −0.5%—
T2DM on insulin−0.3 to −0.5%−0.3 to −0.4%
T2DM not on insulin—−0.3% (modest)

Time in Range (TIR)

  • Both improve TIR; rtCGM advantage for TBR reduction (alerts prevent severe hypos)

4. NICE Guidance (NG17, NG18, TA updates)

T1DM — rtCGM Recommended (NG17 2022)

  • All adults with T1DM should be offered rtCGM
  • If rtCGM not suitable/tolerated → consider isCGM (Libre 2)
  • Criteria: HbA1c ≥58 mmol/mol (7.5%) OR recurrent hypos OR impaired awareness OR pregnancy planning

T2DM on Insulin — isCGM First-Line (NG28)

  • Offer isCGM (Libre 2) to adults on insulin (basal ± bolus) with:
  • HbA1c ≥58 mmol/mol (7.5%) OR
  • Recurrent/severe hypos OR
  • Impaired hypo awareness OR
  • Need for intensive monitoring (pregnancy, renal impairment)
  • rtCGM if isCGM not suitable or specific need (e.g., pump integration, carer alerts)

Pregnancy

  • rtCGM preferred for T1DM (CONCEPTT trial)
  • isCGM acceptable if rtCGM unavailable

5. ADA Standards of Care 2024

PopulationRecommendation
T1DM (all ages)rtCGM preferred (A); isCGM if rtCGM not available
T2DM on insulinrtCGM or isCGM (A)
T2DM on basal onlyisCGM reasonable (B)
T2DM not on insulinisCGM may be beneficial (C) — modest HbA1c benefit
Pregnancy T1DMrtCGM strongly recommended (A)
Pregnancy T2DM/GDMCGM may be considered (B)

6. Cost-Effectiveness (UK/NHS Context)

DeviceAnnual Cost (approx)ICER vs SMBGNotes
Dexcom G6/G7£1,500–2,000Cost-effective for T1DM, T2DM on insulinPump integration
Libre 2£800–1,000Highly cost-effective for T2DM on insulinLower cost, good for MDI
Libre 3£1,200–1,500rtCGM pricingLibre 3 is rtCGM
Eversense E3£2,000+ (incl. insertion)180-day sensor; fewer changesSurgical insertion

NICE: Libre 2 first-line for T2DM on insulin; rtCGM for T1DM.

7. Patient Selection Guide

Patient ProfilePreferredRationale
T1DM, frequent hypos, impaired awarenessrtCGM (Dexcom G7, Guardian, Libre 3)Predictive alerts prevent severe hypos
T1DM on pump / wanting HCLrtCGM (pump-compatible)Required for AID
T2DM on basal-bolus, cost-consciousisCGM (Libre 2)Lower cost, good HbA1c benefit
T2DM on basal onlyisCGM (Libre 2)NICE first-line
Pregnancy T1DMrtCGMCONCEPTT evidence
Paediatric / carer remote monitoringrtCGM (Dexcom Share, Guardian Connect)Remote alerts to carer
Needle-phobic / minimal burdenLibre 3 (small, 1h warm-up, no scanner)rtCGM convenience
Longest sensor lifeEversense E3 (180 days)Fewer insertions

8. Data Completeness — Critical for Valid Metrics

MetricrtCGMisCGM (Libre 1/2)isCGM (Libre 3)
Automatic uploadYes (Bluetooth)No (requires scan)Yes (Bluetooth)
Typical wear time>95%80–95% (scan-dependent)>95%
Gaps affectRareCommon (overnight, busy periods)Rare
TIR validityHighOnly if scans ≥8-hourlyHigh

Clinical tip: Check "data completeness" on AGP report before trusting TIR/GMI.

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
rtCGM vs isCGM main differencertCGM = automatic transmission + real-time alerts; isCGM = scan-dependent
Libre 1 vs Libre 2 vs Libre 3Libre 1: no alarms; Libre 2: optional Bluetooth alarms; Libre 3: rtCGM (continuous + alarms)
NICE T1DMrtCGM for all (first-line)
NICE T2DM on insulinisCGM (Libre 2) first-line; rtCGM if not suitable
CONCEPTT trialrtCGM in pregnancy T1DM → ↓ LGA, ↓ NICU, ↓ hypertensive disorders
CostLibre 2 cheaper (~£800–1000/yr) vs Dexcom G7 (~£1500–2000/yr)
Data completenessrtCGM auto; isCGM needs 8-hourly scans for valid AGP
Pump integrationrtCGM required for HCL (Dexcom→Tandem/Omnipod 5/CamAPS; Guardian→780G)

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

topics.title: Deintensification And Deprescribing
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply structured deprescribing algorithm for elderly diabetes
  • [ ] Identify medications to stop first (sulfonylureas, insulin)
  • [ ] Simplify regimens (once-daily dosing, stop bolus insulin)
  • [ ] Monitor for hypoglycaemia after deprescribing

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply structured deprescribing algorithm for elderly diabetes
  • [ ] Identify medications to stop first (sulfonylureas, insulin)
  • [ ] Simplify regimens (once-daily dosing, stop bolus insulin)
  • [ ] Monitor for hypoglycaemia after deprescribing

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Polypharmacy>=5 medications; common in elderly DM
DeprescribingPlanned reduction/stopping of inappropriate medications
GoalReduce hypoglycaemia, falls, hospitalisation; improve QoL

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Polypharmacy>=5 medications; common in elderly DM
DeprescribingPlanned reduction/stopping of inappropriate medications
GoalReduce hypoglycaemia, falls, hospitalisation; improve QoL

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Deprescribing Priority Order

PriorityDrug ClassReason
1 (STOP FIRST)SulfonylureasHighest hypoglycaemia risk; long half-life; renal accumulation
2Insulin (bolus/prandial)Complex; hypo risk; cognitive burden
3Insulin (basal)Reduce dose; simplify to once daily
4GLP-1 RAIf weight loss/frailty worsening; GI intolerance
4SGLT2iIf eGFR <20; volume depletion risk
5 (KEEP)MetforminIf eGFR >=30; cardiorenal benefit
5 (KEEP)DPP-4iWeight neutral; low hypo; renal adjust (except linagliptin)
5 (KEEP)SGLT2iIf eGFR >=20; cardiorenal benefit
5 (KEEP)ACEi/ARB/StatinCardiovascular/renal protection

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Deprescribing Priority Order

PriorityDrug ClassReason
1 (STOP FIRST)SulfonylureasHighest hypoglycaemia risk; long half-life; renal accumulation
2Insulin (bolus/prandial)Complex; hypo risk; cognitive burden
3Insulin (basal)Reduce dose; simplify to once daily
4GLP-1 RAIf weight loss/frailty worsening; GI intolerance
4SGLT2iIf eGFR < 20; volume depletion risk
5 (KEEP)MetforminIf eGFR >= 30; cardiorenal benefit
5 (KEEP)DPP-4iWeight neutral; low hypo; renal adjust (except linagliptin)
5 (KEEP)SGLT2iIf eGFR >= 20; cardiorenal benefit
5 (KEEP)ACEi/ARB/StatinCardiovascular/renal protection

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Deprescribing Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Elderly DM on multiple agents
↓
Assess: Frailty (Rockwood), HbA1c, Hypo history, Cognitive function
↓
HbA1c < target for frailty?
↓
Deintensify
Optimise current; consider intensification if fit
↓
STOP Sulfonylureas 1st
↓
Hypo resolved / HbA1c safe?
↓
Reduce/stop bolus insulin; simplify basal
Monitor 3-monthly
↓
Hypo resolved?
Monitor 3-monthly; CGM if available
↓
Reduce basal insulin; consider once-daily

Stepwise Deintensification

StepActionMonitoring
1Stop sulfonylureas (gliclazide, glimepiride, glibenclamide)SMBG 4x/day x 2 weeks; watch for hypo resolution
2Stop prandial/bolus insulinSimplify to basal only; check pre-meal glucose
3Reduce basal insulin (20-50% dose reduction)Target higher fasting glucose per frailty target
4Simplify to once-daily basal (glargine/degludec)Reduce injection burden; carer-administered if needed
5Review GLP-1 RA/SGLT2iStop if weight loss excessive, GI intolerance, eGFR < 20

Simplification Strategies

StrategyExample
Once-daily basalGlargine U-300 / Degludec OD
Prefilled pensReduce dosing errors
Carer administrationIf cognitive impairment
Blister packsWeekly organiser
CGM with alertsReplace frequent SMBG

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
STOP FIRSTSulfonylureas (highest hypo risk)
ThenBolus insulin -> reduce basal insulin -> simplify regimen
KeepMetformin (eGFR>=30), SGLT2i (eGFR>=20), DPP-4i, ACEi/ARB, Statin
SimplifyOnce-daily basal; prefilled pens; carer administration; CGM alerts
MonitorSMBG/CGM 2 weeks post-change; target frailty-appropriate HbA1c

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
STOP FIRSTSulfonylureas (highest hypo risk)
ThenBolus insulin -> reduce basal insulin -> simplify regimen
KeepMetformin (eGFR>=30), SGLT2i (eGFR>=20), DPP-4i, ACEi/ARB, Statin
SimplifyOnce-daily basal; prefilled pens; carer administration; CGM alerts
MonitorSMBG/CGM 2 weeks post-change; target frailty-appropriate HbA1c

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the first medication to stop in elderly diabetes?Sulfonylureas (highest hypoglycaemia risk)
How do you simplify insulin in frail elderly?Stop bolus insulin; reduce basal 20-50%; switch to once-daily basal (glargine U-300/degludec)
Which medications should be continued in frailty?Metformin (eGFR>=30), SGLT2i (eGFR>=20), DPP-4i, ACEi/ARB, statin
When do you stop GLP-1 RA / SGLT2i in elderly?Excessive weight loss, GI intolerance, eGFR < 20, frailty worsening
What is the HbA1c target for moderately frail elderly?< 64 mmol/mol (8.0%)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Stop all diabetes meds in frail?NO - keep cardiorenal protective (SGLT2i, ACEi/ARB, statin); stop SU/bolus insulin
Deintensification = worse control?NO - targets relaxed per frailty; prevents hypo without harming outcomes
Stop insulin completely?NO - reduce dose/simplify; basal insulin often still needed for glucose control

Mnemonic: DEINTENSIFY-DM

  • Deintensify: STOP sulfonylureas FIRST (hypo risk)
  • Eliminate: bolus insulin (complex, hypo risk)
  • Insulin basal: reduce 20-50%; simplify to OD
  • No more complex regimens: once-daily basal
  • Targets: relax HbA1c per frailty (fit 53, mild 58, mod 64, sev 69)
  • Eliminate: GLP-1/SGLT2i if weight loss/frailty/eGFR<20
  • No sulfonylureas: glibenclamide/glimepiride highest risk
  • Simplify: once-daily basal; prefilled pens; carer admin
  • ICGM: predictive alerts replace frequent SMBG
  • Frailty-based targets: fit 53, mild 58, mod 64, sev 69
  • Yield: monitor 2wk post-change; HbA1c 3-monthly
  • Don't stop: metformin (eGFR>=30), SGLT2i (eGFR>=20), DPP-4i
  • Metformin: half dose if eGFR 30-45
  • Insulin: reduce not stop (basal still needed)
  • Zero bolus: stop prandial insulin first**

topics.title: Dka Complications Cerebral Oedema Hypokalaemia
topics.html:
[!info]
Cerebral oedema = rare (0.5–1%) but devastating (20–25% mortality, neurological sequelae); children > adults, new-onset, severe acidosis, rapid fluid/insulin correction; hypokalaemia = lethal arrhythmia risk — intensive K+ replacement essential.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify risk factors for cerebral oedema in DKA
  • [ ] Recognise early clinical signs of cerebral oedema
  • [ ] Apply prevention strategies (fluids, insulin, bicarbonate)
  • [ ] Manage hypokalaemia during DKA treatment
  • [ ] Understand other DKA complications (AKI, thrombosis, rhabdomyolysis)

2. Cerebral Oedema

Epidemiology & Risk

PopulationIncidenceMortalitySequelae
Children0.5–1%20–25%20–25% neuro deficit
Adults<0.1%HighSimilar

Risk Factors

StrongestSignificant
Age <5y / new-onset T1DMSevere acidosis (pH <7.0)
Low initial pCO2 (excessive hyperventilation)High BUN (dehydration severity)
Rapid fluid correction (>IVF rate)Rapid glucose fall (>5 mmol/L/h)
Bicarbonate administrationLow initial serum Na+ (pseudohyponatraemia)
Delayed insulin startHigh initial glucose (>55 mmol/L)

Pathophysiology

TheoryMechanism
VasogenicBlood-brain barrier disruption → fluid extravasation
CytotoxicIschaemia-reperfusion → cellular oedema
OsmoticRapid glucose/osmolality fall → water shifts into brain
InflammatoryCytokine storm (IL-6, TNF-α) → BBB breakdown

Clinical Presentation (Early Recognition Critical)

Early (Subtle)Late (Ominous)
Headache (often severe, persistent)Decreased consciousness (GCS drop)
Irritability, restlessnessCushing's triad: Bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respiration
Vomiting(not just DKA nausea)Pupillary changes (dilated, fixed, asymmetric)
Inappropriate bradycardiaDecerebrate/decorticate posturing
Specific neurological signs (cranial nerve palsies)Respiratory arrest

Diagnosis: Clinical — CT head if suspected (shows oedema, loss of grey-white differentiation, herniation).

Management (Emergency)

StepAction
1. Recognise earlyAny neuro change in DKA → assume cerebral oedema
2. Reduce fluidsLimit IVF to maintenance + insensible losses (stop boluses)
3. Mannitol0.5–1g/kg IV over 15–20 min (first-line)
4. Hypertonic saline3% NaCl 2.5–5mL/kg over 10–15 min (alternative/addition)
5. Elevate head30 degrees
6. IntubateIf GCS ≤8 or respiratory compromise (avoid hyperventilation)
7. ICU transferNeurosurgical consultation; ICP monitoring

Do NOT: Hyperventilate (↓ pCO2 → cerebral vasoconstriction → ischaemia); give bicarbonate (↑ risk).

Prevention (Key)

StrategyEvidence
Avoid rapid glucose fallMax 3–5 mmol/L/h; if faster → increase dextrose concentration
Avoid fluid overloadIsotonic fluids (0.9% NaCl) initially; no hypotonic; monitor fluid balance
Avoid bicarbonateContraindicated in most DKA (↑ cerebral oedema risk, paradoxical CSF acidosis)
Start insulin after initial fluids1–2h after fluid resuscitation (not simultaneously with bolus)
Monitor neuro statusHourly GCS in children; q2h adults

3. Hypokalaemia — The Lethal Complication

Physiology

  • Total body K+ depleted (osmotic diuresis, aldosterone, vomiting)
  • Initial serum K+ often NORMAL/HIGH (acidosis shifts K+ out of cells)
  • Insulin + pH correction → massive K+ shift INTO cells → severe hypokalaemia

K+ Replacement Protocol (DKA)

Initial K+Action
>5.5 mmol/LHold K+; monitor 1h; restart when <5.5
3.5–5.5 mmol/LAdd 40mmol K+ per litre IVF (20mmol KCl + 20mmol KPO4)
<3.5 mmol/LHold insulin; give K+ 40mmol/h (cardiac monitor) until ≥3.5; then start insulin with K+ in IVF

Monitoring: K+ every 1–2h first 6h; then 2–4h; continuous cardiac monitoring.

Complications of Hypokalaemia

  • Arrhythmias: VT/VF, asystole (QT prolongation, U waves)
  • Ileus, respiratory muscle weakness, rhabdomyolysis
  • Cardiac arrest — leading cause of death in treated DKA

4. Other DKA Complications

ComplicationMechanismManagement
Acute Kidney InjuryHypovolaemia, rhabdomyolysis, contrastIVF resuscitation; avoid nephrotoxins; monitor Cr
RhabdomyolysisDehydration, acidosis, hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemiaAggressive IVF (target UO 200–300mL/h); bicarbonate if CK >5000
Thrombosis (DVT/PE, cerebral venous)Dehydration, hypercoagulability, inflammationProphylactic LMWH (all DKA admissions); therapeutic if confirmed
HypophosphataemiaOsmotic diuresis, insulin-driven cellular uptakeReplace if <0.5 mmol/L or symptomatic (rhabdo, weakness)
HypomagnesaemiaCommon; worsens hypokalaemiaReplace if <0.7 mmol/L
Aspiration pneumoniaAltered consciousness, vomitingNG tube if GCS <9; elevate head
Line sepsisCVC for monitoring/pressorsAseptic technique; review daily

5. Bicarbonate Therapy — Controversial

PositionGuideline
ADA / NICE / JBDSNot routinely recommended
Indications (rare)pH <6.9 (life-threatening acidosis); severe hyperkalaemia; cardiac arrest
Dose50–100 mmol in 200mL 5% dextrose over 1h
RisksParadoxical CSF acidosis; ↑ cerebral oedema; hypokalaemia; ↓ ionised Ca2+; tissue hypoxia (left shift O2 curve)

Consensus: Bicarbonate harms outweigh benefits in most DKA.

6. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Cerebral oedema incidenceChildren 0.5–1%; adults <0.1%
Mortality cerebral oedema20–25%
Risk factorsChildren, new-onset, severe acidosis, rapid correction, bicarbonate
Early signsHeadache, irritability, vomiting, bradycardia
Late signs↓ GCS, Cushing's triad, fixed pupils, posturing
Treatment cerebral oedemaMannitol 0.5–1g/kg OR 3% saline 2.5–5mL/kg; restrict fluids; ICU
Hypokalaemia riskInsulin drives K+ into cells; initial K+ normal/high → rapid drop
K+ protocol<3.5: hold insulin, replace K+ 40mmol/h; 3.5–5.5: 40mmol/L IVF; >5.5: hold
Monitor K+Every 1–2h first 6h
BicarbonateNot routine; only pH <6.9; ↑ cerebral oedema risk
AKI/rhabdo/thrombosisOther major complications; prophylactic LMWH for all

8. Viva Questions (MRCP PACES / FCPS)

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the incidence of cerebral oedema in DKA?Children 0.5–1%; adults <0.1%
What are the main risk factors for cerebral oedema?Age <5y, new-onset T1DM, severe acidosis (pH <7.0), rapid fluid/insulin correction, bicarbonate use
What are the early clinical signs of cerebral oedema?Headache, irritability, vomiting, inappropriate bradycardia
What is the first-line treatment for cerebral oedema?Mannitol 0.5–1g/kg IV over 15–20 min OR 3% saline 2.5–5mL/kg over 10–15 min
Why is bicarbonate contraindicated in most DKA?Paradoxical CSF acidosis; ↑ cerebral oedema risk; hypokalaemia; tissue hypoxia
What is the K+ replacement protocol in DKA?K+ >5.5: hold; 3.5–5.5: 40mmol/L IVF; <3.5: hold insulin, give 40mmol/h K+ until ≥3.5
How often should K+ be monitored in DKA?Every 1–2h for first 6h, then 2–4h
What are the other major DKA complications?AKI, rhabdomyolysis, thrombosis (DVT/PE/cerebral venous), hypoglycaemia, aspiration pneumonia
Why is prophylactic LMWH given in all DKA admissions?Hypercoagulable state from dehydration, inflammation, immobility

9. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Cerebral oedema vs HHSCerebral oedema is primarily a DKA complication (esp. children); HHS causes osmotic brain injury but not classic cerebral oedema
Initial K+ high = no replacement neededWRONG — initial K+ is falsely high due to acidosis; total body K+ is depleted; replacement mandatory as pH corrects
Bicarbonate in severe acidosisOnly if pH <6.9; harms outweigh benefits in most DKA (↑ cerebral oedema, paradoxical CSF acidosis)
Mannitol vs Hypertonic salineBoth effective; mannitol traditional first-line; 3% saline equally effective, preferred if hypovolaemic

Mnemonic: DKA-COED (Cerebral Oedema)

  • Children (<5y, new-onset)
  • Over-rapid correction (glucose >5 mmol/L/h, fluids)
  • Excess bicarbonate
  • Delayed insulin start / severe acidosis (pH <7.0)

10. Mind Map

DKA Complications
Cerebral Oedema
Incidence: Children 0.5-1%, Adults <0.1%
Mortality: 20-25%
Risk: Children, new-onset, severe acidosis, rapid correction, bicarbonate
Early signs: Headache, irritability, vomiting, bradycardia
Late: ↓GCS, Cushing's triad, fixed pupils, posturing
Treatment: Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg OR 3% saline 2.5-5mL/kg
Prevention: Slow glucose fall, isotonic fluids, no bicarbonate
Hypokalaemia
Initial K+ NORMAL/HIGH (acidosis)
Insulin + pH correction → K+ shift IN
Protocol: >5.5 hold; 3.5-5.5: 40mmol/L; <3.5 hold insulin + 40mmol/h
Monitor: q1-2h first 6h
Complications: Arrhythmias, ileus, rhabdo, cardiac arrest
Other Complications
AKI (hypovolaemia, rhabdo)
Rhabdomyolysis
Thrombosis (DVT/PE, cerebral venous)
Hypophosphataemia, hypomagnesaemia
Aspiration pneumonia
Line sepsis
Bicarbonate
Not routine
Only pH <6.9
Risks: CSF acidosis, cerebral oedema, hypokalaemia

11. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
Cerebral OedemaIncidence 0.5–1% (children); mortality 20–25%; risk: children, severe acidosis, rapid correction
Early SignsHeadache, irritability, vomiting, inappropriate bradycardia
Late Signs↓ GCS, Cushing's triad, fixed pupils, posturing, respiratory arrest
TreatmentMannitol 0.5–1g/kg IV OR 3% saline 2.5–5mL/kg; restrict fluids; elevate head 30°; ICU
HypokalaemiaTotal body K+ depleted; initial serum K+ normal/high → rapid drop with insulin
K+ Protocol>5.5 hold; 3.5–5.5 → 40mmol/L IVF; <3.5 hold insulin + 40mmol/h K+
Monitor K+Every 1–2h first 6h; then 2–4h; continuous cardiac monitoring
BicarbonateNOT routine; only if pH <6.9; ↑ cerebral oedema risk
Other ComplicationsAKI, rhabdo, thrombosis (LMWH prophylaxis), hypoglycaemia, aspiration, line sepsis
Key ScoresGCS hourly (children); q2h (adults); pH <7.0 = high risk

12. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

13. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt
Cerebral Oedema (Epidemiology, Risk, Signs)
Cerebral Oedema (Treatment & Prevention)
Hypokalaemia (Physiology & Protocol)
Other Complications
Bicarbonate Controversy

Mnemonics / Algorithms

topics.title: Diabetes In The Elderly
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify common causes of in-hospital hypoglycaemia (insulin errors, NPO, renal failure)
  • [ ] Implement insulin safety protocols (insulin passport, double-check, prescribing standards)
  • [ ] Manage NPO patients on glucose-lowering therapy
  • [ ] Apply CGM in hospital for high-risk patients

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionBlood glucose <3.9 mmol/L in hospitalised patient with diabetes
Incidence10–25% of inpatients with diabetes; severe (<3.0) 3–8%
Mortality associationHospital hypoglycaemia → ↑30-day mortality (independent predictor)
Common settingsICU, general wards, pre/post-op, renal units, elderly care

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

ScenarioTypical Presentation
Insulin prescribing errorWrong dose (10x), wrong insulin (rapid vs basal), wrong patient
NPO without adjustmentPre-op fasting, procedure delays, unrecognised NPO status
Renal failure↓Insulin/SU clearance; eGFR <30 on glargine/SU/glibenclamide
Sliding scale over-relianceReactive correction without basal coverage → erratic glucose
Corticosteroid taperInsulin dose not reduced as steroid tapered

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SeverityGlucoseHospital Action
Level 1 (Alert)<3.9 mmol/LOral carbs if able; review insulin
Level 2 (Clinical)<3.0 mmol/LOral/IV carbs; mandatory insulin review
Level 3 (Severe)Requires assistanceGlucagon/IV dextrose; incident report; root cause analysis

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRole
CGM (real-time)Detects nocturnal/unrecognised hypo; alerts staff
Capillary glucosePoint-of-care confirmation; q4h if on insulin
Medication reviewIdentify culprit (insulin, SU, GLP-1 RA + insulin)
Renal functioneGFR for dose adjustment

6. Differential Diagnosis

CauseDistinguishing Features
Insulin errorSudden hypo after dose; check prescription vs administration
NPO-relatedHypo during fasting; insulin not held/reduced
Renal failureProgressive hypo over days; eGFR <30 on renal-cleared drugs
FactitiousSelf-administered insulin (staff/patient); inconsistent stories

7. Management

Prevention Protocols

ProtocolKey Elements
Insulin prescribingInsulin passport; double-check by 2 nurses; standard concentrations; separate rapid/basal syringes; no abbreviations (U for units)
NPO protocolBasal insulin continued (50–80% dose); rapid insulin held; glucose 5–10% infusion if NPO >12h; pre-procedure checklist
Renal dosingAuto-alerts if eGFR<30 on insulin/SU; pharmacist review; dose reduction 25–50%
Corticosteroid taperInsulin reduction protocol parallel to steroid taper
Sliding scaleAvoid standalone — use basal-bolus with correction doses; if sliding scale → add basal

Acute Management in Hospital

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Hospital Hypo <3.9
↓
Patient conscious?
↓
15-20g fast carbs (oral/NG)
IV access?
↓
Recheck 15min
75-80ml 20% dextrose IV → 10% infusion
Glucagon 1mg IM/SC
↓
Glucose >3.9?
↓
Repeat carbs/dextrose
Complex carbs + insulin review
↓
Root cause analysis within 24h

Root Cause Analysis (Required for Level 2/3)

StepAction
1. IdentifyDrug, dose, timing, prescriber, administrator
2. ClassifyPrescribing / dispensing / administration / monitoring error
3. System fixProtocol update, education, IT alert, formulary change
4. FeedbackTo team, patient, governance

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Incidence10–25% inpatients with diabetes; severe 3–8%
Top causesInsulin errors (wrong dose/type/patient), NPO without adjustment, renal failure, sliding scale overuse
Insulin safetyPassport, double-check, no abbreviations, standard concentrations
NPO protocolContinue basal (50–80%), hold rapid, glucose infusion if >12h NPO
Renal failureeGFR<30 → ↓insulin/SU 50%; pharmacist alert
CGM in hospitalReduces severe hypo 50%; real-time alerts
MortalityHospital hypo → ↑30-day mortality (independent)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the common causes of in-hospital hypoglycaemia?Insulin prescribing errors (dose, type, patient), NPO without insulin adjustment, renal failure with unchanged insulin/SU dose, sliding scale over-reliance, corticosteroid taper without insulin reduction
What is the NPO protocol for patients on insulin?Continue basal insulin at 50–80% dose; hold rapid insulin; start 5–10% glucose infusion if NPO >12h; pre-procedure checklist
How do you adjust insulin in renal failure?eGFR <30: reduce insulin/SU dose by 25–50%; monitor glucose closely; avoid glibenclamide; consider GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i/DPP-4i
What is an insulin passport?Patient-held record of insulin type, dose, device, recent changes; double-check by 2 nurses at each administration; reduces errors
What is the role of CGM in hospital?Real-time glucose alerts; detects nocturnal/unrecognised hypo; reduces severe hypoglycaemia by 50%
Why is sliding scale insulin alone dangerous?Reactive only; no basal coverage → erratic glucose; ↑hypo and hyperglycaemia; use basal-bolus with correction

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Hold all insulin if NPO?NO — continue basal at 50–80%; only hold rapid/bolus
Glucagon always available in hospital?Should be; but IV dextrose faster if access; glucagon for no IV access
Hospital hypo = outpatient hypo?Different causes (system errors, NPO, renal); requires system fixes not just patient education

Mnemonic: HOSPITAL-HYPO

  • Hypoglycaemia in hospital: 10-25% incidence
  • Over-reliance on sliding scale → dangerous
  • System errors: insulin prescribing (dose/type/patient)
  • Prescribing: Insulin passport, double-check, no abbreviations
  • Insulin NPO: basal 50-80%, hold rapid, glucose infusion
  • Three causes: errors, NPO, renal failure
  • Alert: CGM reduces severe hypo 50%
  • Level 2/3: mandatory root cause analysis
  • Hold basal in NPO? NO — 50-80% continued
  • Yearning for safety: protocolise everything
  • Pharmacist review: eGFR<30 on insulin/SU
  • Outcome: hypo → ↑30-day mortality

Local Navigation

  • Parent Heading: [[Diabetic Emergencies/Severe hypoglycaemia|Diabetic Emergencies/Severe hypoglycaemia]]
  • Chapter Map: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
  • Chapter MOC: [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]
  • **Drug Reference": [[../../../Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing|Drugs]]
  • **Related": [[]]
