topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, elderly-diabetes, frailty, individualised-targets, deintensification, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Frailty assessment; individualised HbA1c targets (category-based); deintensification/deprescribing; hypoglycaemia risk; care home management"

see_also: ["Hypoglycaemia risk in elderly", "Deintensification and deprescribing", "Care home diabetes management", "HbA1c targets (individualised)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Frailty and Individualised HbA1c Targets in the Elderly

[!info]
Age alone ≠ target — Use frailty category (fit / vulnerable / frail) to set HbA1c targets; avoid "one size fits all"; deintensify to prevent hypoglycaemia.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify older adults by frailty (CFS / Rockwood)
  • [ ] Apply individualised HbA1c targets per category
  • [ ] Identify drugs to deintensify/deprescribe
  • [ ] Recognise hypoglycaemia risk factors in elderly
  • [ ] Apply care home diabetes management principles

2. Frailty Classification (Clinical Frailty Scale — CFS)

CategoryCFS ScoreDescriptionHbA1c Target
Fit1–3Active, independent, comorbidities controlled<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)
Vulnerable / Mild frailty4"Slowed up", needs help with heavy tasks<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)
Frail / Moderate-severe5–7Needs help with ADLs, housebound<64 mmol/mol (8.0%)
Severely frail / End of life8–9Completely dependent, terminal<75 mmol/mol (9.0%) — symptom control only

CFS Assessment: "How have you been in last 2 weeks?" — mobility, energy, activities, illnesses.

3. Individualised HbA1c Targets (ADA/NICE/IDF Consensus)

Health StatusHbA1c TargetRationale
Healthy / Fit (few comorbidities, intact cognition)<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)Long life expectancy; microvascular benefit
Complex / Vulnerable (multiple comorbidities, mild cognitive impairment)<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)Balance benefit vs hypoglycaemia risk
Frail / Very complex (ADL dependence, moderate-severe dementia)<64 mmol/mol (8.0%)Hypoglycaemia risk > microvascular benefit
End-of-life / Palliative<75 mmol/mol (9.0%)Symptom control only; avoid osmotic symptoms

Key: Avoid HbA1c <48 (6.5%) in frail elderly — ↑ mortality (ACCORD legacy), ↑ falls, ↑ cognitive decline.

4. Deintensification / Deprescribing Algorithm

When to Deintensify

TriggerAction
HbA1c well below target (e.g., <48 in frail)Reduce/stop glucose-lowering drugs
Recurrent hypoglycaemia (Level 2/3)Immediate deintensification
Frailty progression (CFS ↑)Reassess targets, reduce therapy
New dementia / cognitive declineSimplify regimen, avoid sulphonylureas/insulin if possible
CKD progression (eGFR <30)Review metformin, sulfonylureas, insulin doses
Polypharmacy / fallsReduce insulin/sulfonylurea burden

Drug-Specific Deintensification

DrugDeintensification Approach
SulfonylureasFirst to stop — high hypo risk, no CV benefit; taper over 2–4 weeks
Insulin (basal)Reduce by 10–20% weekly; switch to once-daily if BD; consider stopping if HbA1c <58
Insulin (bolus)Stop first; retain basal if needed
MetforminContinue if eGFR ≥30; reduce dose if eGFR 30–45; stop if <30
DPP-4 inhibitorsLow hypo risk — can continue; reduce dose if eGFR <50
SGLT2 inhibitorsContinue if eGFR ≥20 (CV/renal benefit); monitor volume status
GLP-1 RAContinue if tolerated; reduce if weight loss excessive

5. Hypoglycaemia Risk Factors in Elderly

FactorMechanism
Renal impairment (eGFR <45)↓ Insulin/glucose-lowering clearance
Cognitive impairment / dementiaMissed meals, erratic dosing, unable to recognise/treat hypo
PolypharmacyDrug interactions, ↓ appetite, ↓ renal/hepatic clearance
Malnutrition / sarcopenia↓ Glycogen stores, ↓ gluconeogenesis
AlcoholImpairs gluconeogenesis
Beta-blockersMasks adrenergic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia)
Sulfonylureas / insulinDirect hypo risk
Autonomic neuropathyImpaired counter-regulation

Hypoglycaemia unawareness: Common in elderly; CGM with alarms essential if on insulin/sulfonylurea.

6. Cognitive Impairment & Diabetes

IssueManagement
Missed dosesSimplify to once-daily; use dosette box; carer administration
Erratic eatingReduce/stop bolus insulin; basal only; regular carers for meals
Unable to recognise hypoCGM with carer alerts; avoid sulfonylureas/insulin if possible
Executive dysfunctionStructured routines; visual aids; telemonitoring
Advanced dementiaComfort-focused targets; stop glucose-lowering if HbA1c <64

7. Care Home Diabetes Management

PrincipleAction
Individualised targetsPer frailty category (not blanket targets)
Avoid sliding scaleUse basal insulin + fixed bolus or basal only
Hypoglycaemia protocolWritten plan; carer training; glucagon available
Medication review6-monthly (pharmacist-led); deprescribe aggressively
NutritionRegular meals/snacks; avoid "diabetic diet" (restrictive)
Foot careDaily inspection; podiatry 3–6 monthly
ScreeningAnnual: HbA1c, renal, feet, eyes (if life expectancy >2y)

8. End-of-Life Diabetes Care

SituationApproach
Last weeks of lifeStop all glucose-lowering except insulin for symptom control
InsulinBasal only once-daily; target 8–15 mmol/L (avoid osmotic symptoms)
Steroids (dexamethasone)Expect hyperglycaemia; basal insulin + QDS correction
Nausea/vomitingReduce/stop insulin; treat symptoms
Dying phaseStop insulin if not needed for comfort; capillary glucose only if symptomatic

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Frailty categorisationCFS 1–3 fit, 4 vulnerable, 5–7 frail, 8–9 severe/end-of-life
HbA1c targets by frailtyFit <53; Vulnerable <58; Frail <64; End-of-life <75 mmol/mol
Avoid HbA1c <48 in frail↑ Mortality, falls, cognitive decline
Deintensify firstSulfonylureas (highest hypo risk, no CV benefit)
Insulin reduction10–20% weekly; stop bolus first
Cognitive impairmentSimplify regimen; avoid bolus insulin; CGM with carer alerts
Care homesIndividual targets; avoid sliding scale; 6-monthly med review
End-of-lifeComfort targets 8–15 mmol/L; basal insulin only if needed
Steroids + diabetesBasal insulin + QDS correction; anticipate hyperglycaemia
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA in elderlyContinue if eGFR allows — CV/renal benefit outweighs risk

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

see_also: ["GDM screening (IADPSG, NICE, ADA)", "Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy (type 1, type 2)", "Postpartum follow-up and future risk", "Metformin in pregnancy"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

GDM Management (Diet, Metformin, Insulin)

[!info]
Stepwise management: Medical nutrition therapy (MNT) → Metformin (if fasting/pre-prandial not at target) → Insulin (if post-prandial not at target or metformin contraindicated/declined).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State glycaemic targets for GDM (NICE/ADA)
  • [ ] Apply stepwise management: MNT → Metformin → Insulin
  • [ ] Dose metformin in pregnancy (max dose, renal adjustment)
  • [ ] Choose insulin regimen (basal, bolus, basal-bolus)
  • [ ] Manage delivery timing and postpartum transition

2. Glycaemic Targets in GDM (NICE NG3 / ADA)

TargetNICEADANotes
Fasting<5.3 mmol/L<5.3 mmol/L (95 mg/dL)Key driver of macrosomia
1-hour post-prandial<7.8 mmol/L<7.8 mmol/L (140 mg/dL)Preferred by ADA
2-hour post-prandial<6.4 mmol/L<6.7 mmol/L (120 mg/dL)NICE stricter
HbA1c<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)If measured (2nd/3rd trimester)
Pre-prandial—<5.3 mmol/LADA

Continuous glucose monitoring: TIR 3.5–7.8 mmol/L >70% (ATTD pregnancy targets).

3. Step 1: Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) — First Line

ComponentRecommendation
Calories30 kcal/kg ideal body weight (not actual); ~1800–2200 kcal/day
Carbohydrate175g/day minimum (for fetal brain); 40–45% total calories
Distribution3 meals + 2–3 snacks (prevent nocturnal ketosis)
TypeLow GI, high fibre; avoid SSBs, refined carbs
MonitoringSMBG 4–7x/day (fasting + 1h or 2h post each meal)
Exercise30 min moderate daily (walking post-meal ↓ glucose 1–2 mmol/L)

Failure criteria (NICE): >2 fasting ≥5.3 OR >2 post-prandial ≥7.8 (1h) or ≥6.4 (2h) in 1 week → escalate.

4. Step 2: Metformin — First-Line Pharmacotherapy

AspectDetails
IndicationFasting ≥5.3 mmol/L OR pre-prandial ≥ target despite MNT (NICE)
ContraindicationseGFR <30, acute illness (hypoxia risk), patient decline
Starting dose500 mg OD with evening meal
Titration500 mg weekly to 500 mg BD → 1g BD (max 2g/day in pregnancy; 3g non-pregnant)
Renal monitoringeGFR q4–6 weeks; stop if <30
Folic acidContinue 5mg daily (metformin ↓ folate absorption)
B12Monitor annually if long-term

Evidence (MiG trial, 2008; follow-up):

  • Metformin vs insulin: Similar neonatal outcomes (LGA, hypoglycaemia, NICU)
  • Less maternal weight gain (−2kg), less hypoglycaemia, higher treatment satisfaction
  • 20–45% need supplemental insulin (metformin alone insufficient)

NICE preference: Metformin before insulin for fasting hyperglycaemia.

5. Step 3: Insulin — When Metformin Fails/Contraindicated

Indications for Insulin

  • Post-prandial glucose not at target on metformin + MNT
  • Fasting not at target on max metformin
  • Metformin contraindicated/declined
  • GDM diagnosed >34 weeks (insulin faster onset)
  • Multiple gestation, macrosomia on scan, polyhydramnios

Regimens

RegimenIndicationDosing
Basal only (NPH or detemir/glargine)Isolated fasting hyperglycaemia0.2 u/kg at bedtime; titrate to fasting <5.3
Bolus only (rapid analogue)Isolated post-prandial hyperglycaemiaPer ICR (start 1u/10g carb); pre-bolus 10–15 min
Basal-bolus (MDI)Both fasting + post-prandial highBasal 0.2–0.3 u/kg + bolus per ICR
CSII (pump)Intensive needs, pre-existing pumpStandard pump settings; tighter targets

Insulins safe in pregnancy:

  • Rapid: Aspart, Lispro, Faster aspart (Fiasp), Lispro-aabc (Lyumjev) — Category B
  • Basal: Detemir, Glargine U100 — Category B (extensive data); Degludec limited data but used
  • Avoid: NPH (less predictable), Glargine U300 (limited pregnancy data)

Titration Protocol (Basal)

  1. Start 0.2 u/kg (or 4–6u) at bedtime
  2. Adjust by 10–20% every 2–3 days
  3. Target fasting <5.3 mmol/L
  4. If nocturnal hypo suspected → CGM or 3 AM check

Titration Protocol (Bolus)

  1. ICR start: 1 unit per 10–15g carb
  2. Pre-bolus 10–15 min before meal
  3. Target 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4
  4. Adjust by 1 unit per 5g carb error

6. Delivery Timing (NICE/RCOG)

ScenarioTimingMode
GDM diet/metformin controlled, no complications38–40 weeksInduction if not spontaneous
GDM on insulin37–38+6 weeksInduction
Poor control / macrosomia / polyhydramnios36–37 weeksConsider C-section if EFW >4.5kg
Previous stillbirth / severe complicationsIndividualisedEarlier delivery

Intrapartum management:

  • Stop SC insulin; IV insulin sliding scale (or continue basal insulin pump at 80% rate)
  • Target 4–7 mmol/L (NICE) / 70–110 mg/dL (ADA)
  • Dextrose 5% + insulin infusion per protocol

7. Postpartum Management

TimingAction
ImmediateStop all glucose-lowering therapy (metformin/insulin)
Before dischargeCheck fasting glucose (exclude persistent diabetes)
6–12 weeks75g OGTT (not HbA1c — unreliable <3 months postpartum)
AnnualHbA1c or FPG (lifelong T2DM risk 50% at 10y, 70% at 20y)
BreastfeedingEncouraged — improves maternal glucose, infant outcomes
ContraceptionDiscuss pre-pregnancy; avoid oestrogen if VTE risk

8. Metformin in Breastfeeding

  • Safe — minimal transfer to milk (<0.5% maternal dose)
  • Continue if needed for postpartum glucose / PCOS
  • Monitor infant for GI symptoms (rare)

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
GDM targets (NICE)Fasting <5.3; 1h post-prandial <7.8; 2h <6.4 mmol/L
Stepwise managementMNT → Metformin → Insulin
Metformin max dose pregnancy2g/day (vs 3g non-pregnant)
Metformin advantageLess weight gain, less hypo, higher satisfaction; 20–45% need add-on insulin
Insulin for fastingBasal only (NPH/detemir/glargine) at bedtime
Insulin for post-prandialRapid analogue (aspart/lispro) pre-meal
Delivery timingDiet/metformin: 38–40w; Insulin: 37–38+6w; Complications: earlier
Postpartum testOGTT at 6–12 weeks (not HbA1c)
T2DM risk50% at 10y, 70% at 20y — lifelong annual screen
Metformin breastfeedingSafe — minimal milk transfer

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - IADPSG one-step vs NICE two-step screening; diagnostic thresholds; timing; risk factors; universal vs selective screening debate"

see_also: ["GDM management (diet, metformin, insulin)", "Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy (type 1, type 2)", "Postpartum follow-up and future risk", "Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) epidemiology"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

GDM Screening (IADPSG, NICE, ADA)

[!info]
Two major approaches: IADPSG/ADA/WHO = Universal one-step 75g OGTT; NICE = Selective two-step (risk factors → OGTT). Different thresholds → different prevalence.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Compare IADPSG (one-step) vs NICE (two-step) screening
  • [ ] State diagnostic OGTT thresholds for each guideline
  • [ ] Apply screening timing (booking vs 24–28 weeks)
  • [ ] Identify high-risk groups for early screening
  • [ ] Explain rationale for universal vs selective screening

2. IADPSG / ADA / WHO — Universal One-Step (75g OGTT)

Strategy

  • All pregnant women without known diabetes
  • Timing: 24–28 weeks gestation
  • Test: 75g OGTT (fasting, 1h, 2h) after 8h fast
  • Diagnosis: Any ONE value meets threshold

Diagnostic Thresholds (HAPO-Based)

Time PointThresholdRationale
Fasting≥5.1 mmol/L (92 mg/dL)OR 1.75 for birth weight >90th %ile, C-section, neonatal hypoglycaemia
1-hour≥10.0 mmol/L (180 mg/dL)Unique to IADPSG; strong predictor
2-hour≥8.5 mmol/L (153 mg/dL)

Early Pregnancy (<24 weeks) — Overt Diabetes

If tested early (high risk), use non-pregnant criteria:

  • FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L
  • HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
  • Random ≥11.1 mmol/L + symptoms

→ Diagnose as overt/pre-existing diabetes, NOT GDM

3. NICE NG3 — Selective Two-Step

Step 1: Risk Assessment at Booking (10–12 weeks)

Risk factors for GDM:

  • BMI ≥30 kg/m²
  • Previous macrosomic baby (≥4.5kg or >90th %ile)
  • Previous GDM
  • Family history T2DM (1st degree relative)
  • Ethnicity: South Asian, Black Caribbean, Middle Eastern (high prevalence)
  • Age ≥40 years
  • PCOS
  • Antipsychotic medication

Step 2: OGTT for Those with Risk Factors

  • Timing: 24–28 weeks (if previous GDM: also at booking)
  • Test: 75g OGTT (fasting + 2h only — no 1-hour)

NICE Diagnostic Thresholds

Time PointThreshold
Fasting≥5.6 mmol/L
2-hour≥7.8 mmol/L

Note: No 1-hour threshold in NICE.

4. Comparison Table

FeatureIADPSG / ADA / WHONICE
ApproachUniversal one-stepSelective two-step
Screen ALLYes (24–28w)No — only if risk factors
OGTT Load75g75g
Time PointsFasting, 1h, 2hFasting, 2h (no 1h)
Fasting Threshold5.1 mmol/L5.6 mmol/L
1-hour Threshold10.0 mmol/LNot used
2-hour Threshold8.5 mmol/L7.8 mmol/L
Diagnosis RuleAny ONE abnormalEither fasting OR 2h
GDM Prevalence15–25% (high)5–10% (lower)
Evidence BaseHAPO (continuous risk)Health economic modelling
CostHigher (more tests, more GDM)Lower

5. Risk Factors for Early Screening (Both Guidelines)

Risk FactorNICE: Test at BookingIADPSG/ADA: Test Early (<24w)
Previous GDMYes (OGTT at booking + 24–28w)Yes (overt diabetes criteria)
Glycosuria (≥2+ on 2 occasions)YesYes
BMI ≥30Yes (at 24–28w)Consider early
PCOSYesConsider early
FHx T2DM (1st degree)YesConsider early
High-risk ethnicityYesConsider early
Age ≥40YesConsider early
Previous macrosomiaYesConsider early

6. ADA 2024 Specifics

PopulationScreening
All pregnant75g OGTT 24–28w (IADPSG thresholds)
High risk (BMI ≥25 + 1 risk factor)Test at first prenatal visit
Previous GDMEarly OGTT + repeat 24–28w if normal
Diagnosis early (<24w)Use non-pregnant criteria → overt diabetes

ADA also endorses HbA1c ≥39 mmol/mol (5.7%) at first visit as high-risk flag (but not diagnostic).

7. Controversies & Debate

IssueIADPSG/ADA ViewNICE View
Universal vs selectiveUniversal — misses fewer cases; HAPO shows continuous riskSelective — cost-effective; treats those most likely to benefit
1-hour valueAdds predictive power; identifies moreNo evidence it improves outcomes beyond fasting+2h
Lower thresholdsCaptures milder hyperglycaemia with riskMay over-medicalise; resource implications
Cost-effectivenessLong-term offspring benefits (epigenetic)Short-term NHS cost per QALY favourable for selective

UK Practice: NICE is mandated; IADPSG used in some academic centres, research, and for comparison with international literature.

8. Practical Algorithm (NICE-Based)

Booking (10-12w)
  ├─ Risk factors? → OGTT now
  │    ├─ GDM → Manage
  │    └─ Normal → Repeat OGTT 24-28w
  ├─ Previous GDM? → OGTT now + repeat 24-28w
  └─ No risk factors → No routine OGTT
       (but check glycosuria at each visit)

Glycosuria: ≥2+ on 2 occasions → OGTT (renal threshold lowered in pregnancy).

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
IADPSG fasting threshold5.1 mmol/L (lower than NICE 5.6)
IADPSG 1-hour threshold10.0 mmol/L (unique to IADPSG)
IADPSG 2-hour threshold8.5 mmol/L (lower than NICE 7.8)
NICE screeningRisk-factor based — not universal
NICE OGTTFasting + 2h only (no 1h)
Previous GDMOGTT at booking + repeat 24–28w
Overt diabetes in pregnancyFPG ≥7.0 / HbA1c ≥48 at booking → pre-existing, not GDM
HAPO studyBasis for IADPSG — continuous risk, no clear threshold
Prevalence differenceIADPSG 15–25%; NICE 5–10%
Cost-effectivenessNICE selective approach favoured in UK health economic models

topics.title: Dka Diagnosis Criteria
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State DKA diagnostic criteria (glucose, ketones, pH, bicarbonate)
  • [ ] Grade DKA severity (mild/moderate/severe)
  • [ ] Differentiate DKA from HHS and euglycaemic DKA
  • [ ] Identify precipitating factors
  • [ ] Apply initial assessment scoring

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionAcute diabetic emergency: hyperglycaemia + ketonaemia + acidosis
Diagnostic Triad1) Glucose >11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) 2) Ketonaemia >3 mmol/L (or significant ketonuria) 3) pH <7.3 and/or bicarbonate <15 mmol/L
Incidence4–8 per 1000 patient-years in T1DM; rising with SGLT2i use
Mortality<1% (mild/moderate); 5–10% (severe); higher in elderly/comorbidities
PrecipitantsInfection (pneumonia, UTI), insulin omission, MI, stroke, trauma, surgery, drugs (SGLT2i, steroids), pregnancy

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
Polyuria/polydipsiaUniversalPrecedes by days-weeks
Nausea/vomiting/abdominal pain75%Abdominal pain mimics surgical abdomen
Kussmaul breathingModerate/severeDeep, rapid respirations (compensatory)
DehydrationUniversalDry mucosa, poor skin turgor, tachycardia, hypotension
Ketotic breathCommonPear-drop/acetone smell
Altered consciousnessSevereConfusion, drowsiness, coma (pH<7.0)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SeveritypHBicarbonate (mmol/L)Ketonaemia (mmol/L)Mental StatusK+ (mmol/L)
Mild7.25–7.3015–18>3Alert>3.5
Moderate7.00–7.2410–15>3Alert/drowsy3.0–3.5
Severe<7.00<10>3Drowsy/coma<3.0
Euglycaemic DKA: Glucose <13.9 mmol/L (250 mg/dL) + same ketone/pH criteria. Associated with SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy, starvation, alcohol.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
Blood glucoseConfirm hyperglycaemiaPortal/venous >11.1 mmol/L
Blood ketones (β-hydroxybutyrate)Confirm ketonaemia>3 mmol/L diagnostic; superior to urine ketones (nitroprusside detects acetoacetate only)
Venous blood gas (VBG)Assess acidosispH, bicarbonate, pCO2, base excess. VBG ≈ ABG for pH/HCO3
U&E, CreatinineRenal function, K+K+ critical for insulin safety
FBC, CRPInfection screenLeucocytosis common (stress + infection)
ECGK+ effects, IschaemiaPeaked T-waves (hyperK+), flat T/U-waves (hypoK+); silent MI
CXR / Urine culturePrecipitant identificationPneumonia, UTI common
Pregnancy testIn women of childbearing ageEuglycaemic DKA risk

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HHSGlucose >30 mmol/L, osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, pH >7.3, bicarbonate >18, minimal ketones
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9 mmol/L, same ketone/pH criteria; SGLT2i, pregnancy, starvation
Alcoholic ketoacidosisNormal/mildly elevated glucose, high ketones, metabolic acidosis; high anion gap; history alcohol binge
Lactic acidosisNormal glucose/ketones, high lactate; metformin, sepsis, shock
Renal tubular acidosisNormal glucose, non-anion gap acidosis, normal ketones

7. Management (Immediate)

StepInterventionDose / Details
1Fluids: 0.9% NaCl1L in 1st hour, then 500ml/hr ×2h, then 250ml/hr; adjust to correct deficit over 24–48h
2Insulin: Fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII)0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U in 50ml = 1 U/ml); continue until ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, bicarbonate >18
3PotassiumK+ <3.5: 60mmol/L in fluids; 3.5–5.5: 40mmol/L; >5.5: hold; target 4.0–5.0
4BicarbonateONLY if pH <7.0 (severe): 100mmol in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
5MonitoringGlucose q1h, ketones q2–4h, VBG q2–4h, K+ q1–2h, fluid balance, neurology (cerebral oedema in children)
⬇ Clinical Pathway
DKA Suspected
↓
IV Access, Labs: Glucose, Ketones, VBG, U&E, FBC
↓
pH <7.0?
↓
Severe: ICU, Bicarb, Cardiac monitor
FRII 0.1U/kg/hr + 0.9% NaCl
↓
K+ replacement per level
↓
Monitor: glucose q1h, ketones q2-4h, VBG q4h
↓
Ketones <0.6 AND pH >7.3?
↓
Switch to SC insulin, stop FRII 30-60min after 1st SC dose

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Diagnostic criteriaGlucose >11.1 + Ketones >3 mmol/L + pH <7.3 / HCO3 <15
Severity gradingMild: pH 7.25–7.30; Mod: 7.0–7.24; Severe: <7.0
Fluid deficit~5–7L (100ml/kg); replace 50% in 12h, rest over 24–48h
InsulinFRII 0.1 U/kg/hr; DO NOT BOLUS; continue till ketones clear
K+ replacement<3.5: 60mmol/L; 3.5–5.5: 40mmol/L; >5.5: hold; cardiac monitor
BicarbonateOnly if pH <7.0
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9; SGLT2i-associated; same management; STOP SGLT2i
Cerebral oedemaChildren <20y; rapid osmolar drop; headache, bradycardia, hypertension, ↓GCS; mannitol 0.5–1g/kg

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the diagnostic criteria for DKA?Glucose >11.1 mmol/L + blood ketones >3 mmol/L (or significant ketonuria) + venous pH <7.3 or bicarbonate <15 mmol/L
How do you grade DKA severity?Mild: pH 7.25–7.30, K+ >3.5; Moderate: pH 7.00–7.24, K+ 3.0–3.5; Severe: pH <7.00, K+ <3.0
What is the initial fluid regimen?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500ml/hr ×2h, then 250ml/hr; total deficit ~5–7L over 24–48h
What insulin regimen is used?Fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII) 0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U Actrapid in 50ml 0.9% NaCl = 1U/ml); NO bolus
How do you manage potassium?Replace in fluids: K+ <3.5 → 60mmol/L; 3.5–5.5 → 40mmol/L; >5.5 → hold. Target 4.0–5.0. Cardiac monitoring essential.
When do you give bicarbonate?ONLY if pH <7.0 (severe DKA): 100mmol in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
What is euglycaemic DKA?DKA with glucose <13.9 mmol/L (250 mg/dL); associated with SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy, starvation; same ketone/pH criteria
What are the complications of DKA management?Hypokalaemia (insulin drives K+ in), cerebral oedema (children, rapid osmolar drop), hypoglycaemia (if insulin not reduced when glucose <14), hypophosphataemia

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
DKA vs HHSDKA: ketosis + acidosis; HHS: severe hyperosmolality, minimal ketosis, no acidosis. Mixed DKA-HHS exists.
Euglycaemic DKA vs DKASame ketone/pH criteria; glucose <13.9; SGLT2i is major cause; STOP SGLT2i
Insulin bolus vs infusionNEVER bolus in DKA; FRII 0.1U/kg/hr only. Suboptimal bolus → cerebral oedema risk

Mnemonic: DKA-KUSS

  • Diagnosis: Glucose >11.1 + Ketones >3 + pH <7.3/HCO3 <15
  • Ketones: β-hydroxybutyrate >3 mmol/L (blood preferred over urine)
  • Acidosis: pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15
  • K+: replace aggressively (40–60mmol/L in fluids)
  • Urine ketones: nitroprusside = acetoacetate only (misses β-OHB)
  • Severity: Mild 7.25–7.30 / Mod 7.0–7.24 / Sev <7.0
  • Stop insulin only after ketones <0.6 + pH >7.3 (overlap 30–60min)

topics.title: Monogenic Diabetes In Children
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, monogenic-children, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Type 1 diabetes in children", "Type 2 diabetes in youth", "Transition to adult care"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Monogenic diabetes in children

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate MODY from T1DM/T2DM in children
  • [ ] Recognise neonatal diabetes (<6 months) and management
  • [ ] Apply genetic testing criteria (probability > 6% cost-effective)
  • [ ] Manage GCK-MODY (no treatment) vs HNF1A-MODY (sulfonylurea responsive)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
MODY1-2% of diabetes; autosomal dominant; onset < 25y
Neonatal DM< 6 months; 1:100,000-500,000; KCNJ11/ABCC8 most common
Prevalence~100,000 MODY in UK; underdiagnosed

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

MODY Red Flags in Children

FeatureMODYT1DMT2DM
Age< 25y (often < 15)< 25y> 10y
BMINon-obeseNormal/lowObese
FHAutosomal dominant (3 generations)VariableVariable
AutoantibodiesNegativePositive (2+)Negative
C-peptidePreservedLow/absentPreserved/high
Insulin requirementOften not needed (GCK) or SU responsive (HNF1A)ImmediateVariable

Neonatal Diabetes

FeatureTransient (TNDM)Permanent (PNDM)
RemissionBy 18 monthsNever
RelapseAdolescence/adulthoodNever
Genetics6q24 imprinting (70%)KCNJ11, ABCC8, INS
SulfonylureaVariableKCNJ11/ABCC8: 90% responsive

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Common MODY Types

TypeGeneFeaturesTreatment
MODY 2 (GCK)GCKMild fasting hyperglycaemia (5.5-8.0); stableNo treatment needed
MODY 3 (HNF1A)HNF1AProgressive; low renal threshold; low CRPSulfonylurea (excellent response)
MODY 1 (HNF4A)HNF4AMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemiaSulfonylurea responsive
MODY 5 (HNF1B)HNF1BRenal cysts, GU malformationsOften insulin needed

Genetic Testing Indications

IndicationDetail
Age < 25y at diagnosis
Non-obese (BMI < 25)
Strong FH (3 generations, autosomal dominant)
Negative autoantibodies
Non-insulin requiring at 3 years
Probability calculator > 6%Cost-effective

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestIndication
AutoantibodiesNegative in MODY/neonatal
C-peptidePreserved
Genetic panelIf probability > 6%
Targeted testingIf FH suggests specific gene

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide, DKA common
T2DMObese, older, no FH of autosomal dominant
Transient hyperglycaemiaStress, steroids; resolves

7. Management

MODY-Specific

TypeManagement
GCK-MODY (MODY2)No treatment; monitor HbA1c annually
HNF1A-MODY (MODY3)Sulfonylurea (gliclazide MR); excellent response
HNF4A-MODY (MODY1)Sulfonylurea responsive
HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)Often insulin; monitor renal/GU

Neonatal Diabetes

GeneManagement
KCNJ11/ABCC8Switch to sulfonylurea (tolbutamide/gliclazide 0.5-1mg/kg/day)
6q24 (TNDM)Insulin until remission; monitor for relapse
INSInsulin
ECF2AK3 (Wolcott-Rallison)Insulin; multi-system

Genetic Counselling

AspectDetail
InheritanceAutosomal dominant (MODY); mostly de novo (neonatal)
PrenatalAvailable if familial mutation known
Family screeningOffer to relatives

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MODY red flags< 25y, non-obese, 3-gen FH, negative Abs, C-peptide preserved
GCK-MODYMild fasting hyperglycaemia; no treatment
HNF1A-MODYSulfonylurea responsive (excellent)
Neonatal DM< 6 months; KCNJ11/ABCC8 -> sulfonylurea responsive (90%)
Genetic testingProbability > 6% cost-effective

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the red flags for MODY?Age < 25, non-obese, strong FH (3 generations), negative autoantibodies, preserved C-peptide
What is the treatment for GCK-MODY?No treatment needed; mild stable fasting hyperglycaemia
What is the treatment for HNF1A-MODY?Sulfonylurea (gliclazide MR) - excellent response
What is neonatal diabetes?Diabetes diagnosed < 6 months of age
How do you treat KCNJ11/ABCC8 neonatal diabetes?Switch to sulfonylurea (tolbutamide/gliclazide 0.5-1mg/kg/day); 90% responsive
When do you do genetic testing for MODY?Probability > 6% (calculator); age < 25, non-obese, 3-gen FH, neg Abs

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All MODY = insulin?NO - GCK = no treatment; HNF1A/4A = SU responsive; HNF1B = often insulin
Neonatal DM = T1DM?NO - < 6 months; KCNJ11/ABCC8 -> sulfonylurea responsive
All neonatal DM permanent?NO - transient (TNDM) remits by 18mo, relapses later

Mnemonic: MONOGENIC-KIDS

  • MODY: 1-2% DM; autosomal dominant; < 25y
  • One gene: HNF1A (3), GCK (2), HNF4A (1), HNF1B (5)
  • Neonatal DM: < 6 months
  • One gene: KCNJ11, ABCC8 -> sulfonylurea 90% responsive
  • GCK-MODY: no treatment (mild fasting hyperglycaemia)
  • Excellent SU response: HNF1A (> 90%)
  • Neonatal KCNJ11/ABCC8: 90% SU switch
  • Early onset: < 6mo = neonatal; < 25y = MODY
  • Negative Abs: MODY/neonatal
  • Insulin: GCK = no; HNF1A = SU; neonatal KCNJ11 = SU
  • Cost-effective testing: probability > 6%
  • Inhritance: MODY autosomal dominant; neonatal mostly de novo
  • Sulfonylurea: HNF1A, HNF4A, KCNJ11, ABCC8 responsive

topics.title: Postpartum Follow Up And Future Risk
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, postpartum, follow-up, future-risk, type2-diabetes, gdm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Postpartum OGTT at 6-12 weeks (not HbA1c); 7x lifetime T2DM risk; annual screening; breastfeeding benefits; contraception"

see_also: ["GDM management (diet, metformin, insulin)", "Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy (type 1, type 2)", "Type 2 diabetes prevention", "Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) epidemiology"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Postpartum Follow-Up and Future Risk After GDM

[!info]
GDM = 7× lifetime T2DM risk — 50% at 10 years, 70% at 20 years. Postpartum OGTT at 6–12 weeks essential (HbA1c unreliable <3 months).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State timing and test for postpartum glucose assessment
  • [ ] Interpret postpartum OGTT results (classify)
  • [ ] Quantify future T2DM and CVD risk
  • [ ] Apply annual screening strategy
  • [ ] Counsel on breastfeeding, contraception, lifestyle

2. Immediate Postpartum (Delivery to 6 Weeks)

ActionTimingDetails
Stop glucose-lowering therapyAt deliveryInsulin/metformin — usually not needed
Glucose monitoringFirst 24–48hFasting + 2h post-prandial × 1–2 days; exclude persistent diabetes
Breastfeeding initiationImmediatelyEncouraged — improves maternal glucose, infant outcomes
Contraception discussionBefore dischargeLARC preferred; avoid oestrogen if VTE risk
Folic acidContinue5mg daily if planning future pregnancy

3. Definitive Postpartum Testing — 6–12 Weeks

Test: 75g OGTT (NOT HbA1c)

  • HbA1c unreliable <3 months postpartum (↑ RBC turnover, blood loss, iron deficiency)
  • OGTT same as non-pregnant criteria

OGTT Interpretation (WHO/ADA Non-Pregnant Criteria)

ResultFasting2-HourClassification
Normal<6.1 mmol/L<7.8 mmol/LNormoglycaemia
IFG6.1–6.9 mmol/L<7.8 mmol/LPrediabetes
IGT<6.1 mmol/L7.8–11.0 mmol/LPrediabetes
IFG+IGT6.1–6.9 mmol/L7.8–11.0 mmol/LHigh-risk prediabetes
Diabetes≥7.0 mmol/L≥11.1 mmol/LT2DM (overt)

4. Future Risk After GDM

OutcomeRiskTimeline
T2DM7× general population50% at 10 years, 70% at 20 years
Progression to T2DM2.5–5%/yearHighest first 5 years
Recurrent GDM30–50%Next pregnancy
CVD2× (independent of T2DM)Early onset
Metabolic syndrome3–4×Within 5 years

Risk modifiers:

  • Higher fasting glucose in pregnancy → higher T2DM risk
  • Insulin-treated GDM → higher risk than diet-controlled
  • Early diagnosis (<24w) → higher risk
  • Postpartum weight retention → higher risk
  • Breastfeeding >3 months → ↓ T2DM risk 30–50%

5. Annual Screening Strategy

TestFrequencyThreshold for Action
HbA1cAnnual (after first OGTT)≥42 mmol/mol (6.0%) → repeat OGTT
FPGAnnual (if HbA1c not available)≥6.1 mmol/L → OGTT
OGTTEvery 1–3 years (if prediabetes)Per diagnostic criteria
Lipids, BP, weightAnnualPer CVD prevention guidelines

If planning pregnancy: Test every 1–2 years; pre-conception HbA1c <48 mmol/mol.

6. Lifestyle Intervention for Prevention

Evidence: DPP, Da Qing, DPS all included women with prior GDM — lifestyle reduces progression ~50%.

TargetIntervention
WeightReturn to pre-pregnancy weight; then 5–7% loss if BMI ≥25
DietMediterranean/DASH; high fibre; low GI; limit SSBs
Activity≥150 min/week moderate + resistance 2×/week
BreastfeedingExclusive ≥3 months → ↓ T2DM risk 30–50%
Sleep7–9h; treat sleep apnoea
MetforminConsider if BMI ≥30, HbA1c ≥42, prior GDM (ADA) — not NICE

7. Breastfeeding Benefits

For MotherFor Infant
↑ Insulin sensitivity↓ Obesity risk
↑ Weight loss (500 kcal/day)↓ T2DM risk later
↓ T2DM risk 30–50% (≥3mo exclusive)↓ Asthma, infections
↓ CVD riskOptimal nutrition
↓ Ovarian/breast cancerBonding

8. Contraception Considerations

MethodSuitability
LARC (IUD/IUS, implant)First-line — highly effective, no oestrogen
Progestogen-only pillGood alternative
Combined OCPAvoid if BMI ≥30, smoker >35, VTE risk, hypertension
Depot injectionWeight gain concern; ↓ bone density
BarrierLow effectiveness

Timing: Start before discharge; ensure effective before ovulation resumes (can be <6 weeks if not breastfeeding).

9. CVD Risk Reduction

  • BP target: <130/80 (NICE) / <120/80 (ADA)
  • Lipids: Statin if CVD risk ≥10% (QRISK3) — avoid in pregnancy planning
  • Aspirin: 75mg if CVD risk high (postpartum, not pregnancy contraindicated)
  • Lifestyle: As above — foundation of prevention

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Postpartum test75g OGTT at 6–12 weeks (NOT HbA1c)
HbA1c postpartumUnreliable <3 months (↑ RBC turnover, iron deficiency)
T2DM risk after GDM7× lifetime; 50% at 10y, 70% at 20y
Recurrent GDM30–50% next pregnancy
Breastfeeding≥3 months exclusive → ↓ T2DM risk 30–50%
Annual screenHbA1c or FPG; OGTT if prediabetes
Metformin preventionADA: yes (BMI ≥30, prior GDM); NICE: lifestyle only
ContraceptionLARC first-line; avoid COCP if VTE risk
CVD risk2× independent of T2DM
Weight retentionMajor modifiable risk factor for progression

11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Diabetes in pregnancy|Diabetes in pregnancy]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[GDM management (diet, metformin, insulin)]], [[Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy (type 1, type 2)]], [[Type 2 diabetes prevention]], [[Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) epidemiology]]

topics.title: Transition To Adult Care
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, transition-care, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Type 1 diabetes in children", "Type 2 diabetes in youth", "Monogenic diabetes in children"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Transition to adult care

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply structured transition programme (age 16-18)
  • [ ] Identify key components: joint clinics, DAFNE-Youth, psychologist
  • [ ] Address adherence, eating disorders, contraception, alcohol, driving
  • [ ] Apply NICE/SIGN transition guidelines

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionPlanned transfer from paediatric to adult diabetes services
Age16-18 years (individualised)
Gap Risk30-60% loss to follow-up during transition
OutcomeDeterioration in HbA1c, increased DKA, complications

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - process/transition)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Transition Readiness Checklist

DomainCompetency
Self-managementSMBG/CGM, carb counting, insulin adjustment, sick day rules
KnowledgeHypoglycaemia, DKA, complications, targets
PsychosocialAdherence, mental health, eating disorders, substance use
IndependenceAppointments, prescriptions, insurance, driving
EmergencyGlucagon, sick day rules, when to seek help

NICE NG18 / SIGN 154 Recommendations

RecommendationDetail
AgeStart preparation at 14-16; transfer 16-18
Joint clinicsMinimum 2-3 joint paediatric-adult appointments
Named workerTransition coordinator/nurse
DAFNE-YouthStructured education for young adults
PsychologyScreen for eating disorders, depression, distress

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A - process)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Transition Pathway

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Age 14-15
↓
Start transition planning
↓
Assess readiness checklist
↓
Named transition coordinator
↓
Joint paediatric-adult clinics (2-3 min)
↓
DAFNE-Youth / structured education
↓
Psychology screening: eating disorders, depression
↓
Discuss: driving, alcohol, contraception, alcohol
↓
Transfer at 16-18y (individualised)
↓
Adult service: named consultant, 3mo follow-up

Key Discussion Topics

TopicKey Points
AdherenceCGM/SMBG, insulin omission, bolus skipping
Eating disordersDiabulimia (insulin omission for weight loss); screen DEPS-R
ContraceptionPre-conception counselling; folic acid 5mg if T1DM/T2DM
AlcoholHypoglycaemia risk; carb content; glucagon ineffective if drunk
DrivingDVLA rules: hypo awareness, 3 monthly reviews if on insulin
EmploymentShift work, safety-critical jobs, rights (Equality Act)

Psychological Support

IssueScreening/Management
Diabetes distressPAID/DDS; education, shared decision-making
DepressionPHQ-9; SSRIs 1st line; collaborative care
Eating disordersDEPS-R >=20; diabulimia; MDT (psych, diabetologist, dietitian)
AnxietyGAD-7; CBT; mindfulness

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Age16-18 years (individualised); prep starts 14-16
Joint clinics2-3 shared paediatric-adult appointments
DAFNE-YouthStructured education for young adults
PsychologyScreen: eating disorders (DEPS-R), depression (PHQ-9), distress (PAID)
Key topicsDriving, alcohol, contraception, employment, eating disorders
Loss to follow-up30-60% during transition; named coordinator reduces

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the recommended age for transition to adult diabetes services?16-18 years (individualised); preparation starts 14-16 years
What are the key components of a transition programme?Joint clinics (2-3), named coordinator, DAFNE-Youth, psychology screening
What psychological issues should be screened during transition?Eating disorders (DEPS-R >=20), depression (PHQ-9), diabetes distress (PAID)
What are the DVLA rules for driving with T1DM?Must have hypo awareness; no severe hypo in last 12 months; 3-monthly reviews if on insulin
What is diabulimia?Insulin omission for weight loss; recurrent DKA; high mortality; MDT management
What is the role of a transition coordinator?Ensure continuity; coordinate joint clinics; track attendance; support young person

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Transition = discharge?NO - transfer of care with continuity; not discharge
All 16-year-olds transfer?NO - individualised; some may stay paediatric until 18
Adult services = paediatric services?NO - different model; young person must self-advocate

Mnemonic: TRANSITION-DM

  • Transition: 16-18y (prep 14-16)
  • Readiness: checklist (self-mgmt, knowledge, psychosocial)
  • Adolescent: joint clinics (2-3 paeds/adult)
  • Named coordinator: tracks, supports, prevents loss to follow-up
  • Structured education: DAFNE-Youth
  • Independence: driving, alcohol, contraception, employment
  • Therapy: psychology screen (eating disorders, depression, distress)
  • Insulin: self-management (CGM, pump, sick day rules)
  • Outreach: named coordinator prevents loss to follow-up
  • Next: adult service 3mo follow-up
  • Need: MDT (diabetologist, nurse, dietitian, psychologist)

topics.title: Type 1 Diabetes In Children
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, t1dm-children, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Type 2 diabetes in youth", "Monogenic diabetes in children", "Transition to adult care"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Type 1 diabetes in children

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply ISPAD guidelines for T1DM in children
  • [ ] Manage DKA in children (cerebral oedema risk)
  • [ ] Apply HbA1c targets by age
  • [ ] Manage school, exercise, sick days in children

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
IncidenceRising 3-5%/year; peak 10-14y; bimodal 4-7 and 10-14y
Presentation25-30% in DKA at diagnosis (<5y highest risk)
Glycaemic TargetsHbA1c <53mmol/mol (7.0%) if achievable without severe hypo

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Age GroupPresentation
<5 yearsOften DKA at diagnosis; non-specific symptoms (lethargy, polyuria)
5-12 yearsClassic osmotic symptoms; weight loss
AdolescentsSimilar to adults; adherence challenges; eating disorder risk

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

HbA1c Targets by Age (ISPAD)

AgeTarget HbA1c
<6 years<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)
6-12 years<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)
>12 years<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)
AllIndividualised; avoid severe hypo

DKA in Children - Cerebral Oedema Risk

Risk FactorSignificance
<5 yearsHighest risk
New-onset T1DM0.5-1% incidence
Rapid fluid/insulinAvoid rapid osmolar drop
Low pCO2Hyperventilation risk

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
HbA1c3-monthly
SMBG/CGM4-8x/day; CGM recommended (NICE: all T1DM)
Thyroid (TFT)Annual (autoimmune thyroiditis 15-20%)
Coeliac (TTG)Annual (screening)
LipidsAnnual from age 12

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
MODY<25y, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies
Stress hyperglycaemiaResolves; no autoantibodies
Cystic fibrosis-related DMExocrine insufficiency, respiratory symptoms

7. Management

School & Daily Life

AspectManagement
Individual Healthcare PlanRequired (school, parents, diabetes team)
InsulinBasal-bolus or CSII; CSII preferred (NICE)
CGMAll T1DM (NICE); predictive alerts for hypoglycaemia
ExercisePre-exercise carb if glucose <7; reduce basal if CSII
Sick day rulesCheck ketones if ill/glucose>14; supplemental insulin

Transition to Adult Care

ComponentDetail
Age16-18 years (planned transition)
ProcessJoint paediatric-adult clinics; DAFNE-Youth; psychologist
Key IssuesAdherence, eating disorders, contraception, alcohol, driving

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
DKA at diagnosis25-30% children; <5y highest risk
HbA1c targets<6y: <58; >6y: <53 mmol/mol (avoid severe hypo)
CGMNICE: all T1DM; predictive alerts for hypo
CSII preferredNICE: CSII if HbA1c>58 on MDI or severe hypo
ScreeningAnnual thyroid, coeliac, lipids (age>12)
Transition16-18y; joint clinics; psychologist; DAFNE-Youth
Cerebral oedemaKids DKA: mannitol 0.5-1g/kg; avoid rapid osmolar drop

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the HbA1c target for a 4-year-old with T1DM?<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)
What is the HbA1c target for a 14-year-old with T1DM?<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)
What is the DKA cerebral oedema risk in children?<5y highest; 0.5-1% incidence; mannitol 0.5-1g/kg; avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr
When do you transition to adult care?16-18 years; planned joint paediatric-adult clinics
What screening is needed for T1DM children?Annual: TFT, coeliac (TTG), lipids (age>12), retinal (age>12)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Children = same HbA1c as adults?NO - <6y: <58 mmol/mol (allows slightly higher to avoid hypo)
CSII for all children?NICE: CSII if HbA1c>58 on MDI or severe hypo; not all
Cerebral oedema only in DKA?Mostly DKA; can occur with rapid glucose correction in HHS

Mnemonic: T1DM-KIDS-CARE

  • T1DM kids: peak 10-14y, bimodal 4-7, 10-14
  • 1 DKA at dx: 25-30% (highest <5y)
  • DKA cerebral oedema: mannitol 0.5-1g/kg, avoid rapid osmolar drop
  • Merge HbA1c targets: <6y <58, >6y <53
  • KIDS: COE (cerebral oedema) risk highest <5y
  • Insulin: MDI or CSII (NICE prefers CSII if HbA1c>58)
  • DAILY: CGM recommended (NICE all T1DM)
  • Screening: thyroid, coeliac, lipids (age>12)
  • Cgm: predictive alerts for hypo
  • Adherence: teen challenge; psychologist help
  • Retinal screen: age>12y
  • Eating disorders: screen teens (diabulimia)
  • Transition: 16-18y, joint clinics, DAFNE-Youth
  • School plan: Individual Healthcare Plan mandatory

Local Navigation

  • **Chapter Map": [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
  • **Chapter MOC": [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]
  • **Drug Reference": [[../../../Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing|Drugs]]

topics.title: Type 2 Diabetes In Youth
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, t2dm-youth, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Type 1 diabetes in children", "Monogenic diabetes in youth", "Transition to adult care"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Type 2 diabetes in youth

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise rising T2DM in youth (obesity-driven)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria and screening (ADA: BMI >= 85th percentile + 2 RF; age >= 10 or puberty)
  • [ ] Apply management: metformin 1st line; GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i if HbA1c > 75
  • [ ] Manage comorbidities (PCOS, NAFLD, sleep apnoea, hypertension)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
TrendRising with childhood obesity; 10-19y peak; T2DM > T1DM in some ethnic groups
Risk FactorsObesity (BMI >= 95th percentile), FH T2DM, ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean), acanthosis nigricans, PCOS, maternal GDM
ScreeningADA: age >= 10 or puberty + BMI >= 85th percentile + 2 risk factors

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureT2DM YouthT1DM Youth
ObesityUsually present (BMI >= 95th percentile)Absent/normal
Acanthosis nigricansCommon (insulin resistance marker)Rare
FH T2DMStrong (multiple relatives)Variable
AutoantibodiesNegativePositive (2+)
C-peptidePreserved/highLow/absent
DKA at dxRare25-30%

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Screening Criteria (ADA)

CriteriaThreshold
Age>= 10 years or onset of puberty
BMI>= 85th percentile
Risk Factors>= 2 of: FH T2DM, high-risk ethnicity, acanthosis nigricans, PCOS, maternal GDM, hypertension, dyslipidaemia

HbA1c Targets

AgeTarget
All<53 mmol/mol (7.0%) if achievable without hypo
If high risk<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
HbA1c / FPG / OGTTDiagnosis (ADA criteria)
AutoantibodiesExclude T1DM (negative in T2DM)
C-peptidePreserved in T2DM
Lipids, LFT, UACRBaseline comorbidities

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide, DKA common
MODY<25y, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies, preserved C-peptide
Secondary DMSteroid, Cushing, acromegaly, etc.

7. Management

Stepwise Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
T2DM Youth
↓
Lifestyle + Metformin
↓
HbA1c > 75mmol/mol (9%)?
↓
Add GLP-1 RA (liraglutide 1.8mg) or SGLT2i (dapagliflozin 10mg)
Optimise metformin; lifestyle
↓
HbA1c target met?
Continue + monitor
↓
Add insulin; basal-bolus

Lifestyle Intervention

ComponentRecommendation
DietReduced calorie; low GI; dietitian-led
Exercise60min/day moderate-vigorous
Screen time<2 hours/day
Family involvementEssential; whole-family approach

Comorbidity Screening (Annual)

ConditionScreen
DyslipidaemiaFasting lipid profile
HypertensionBP at each visit
NAFLDALT, USS liver
PCOSIrregular menses, hyperandrogenism
Sleep apnoeaSnoring, daytime sleepiness
DepressionPHQ-9

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
ScreeningAge >= 10 or puberty + BMI >= 85th percentile + 2 RF (ADA)
PresentationObesity, acanthosis nigricans, FH T2DM, negative Abs
1st lineLifestyle + Metformin
2nd lineGLP-1 RA (liraglutide) or SGLT2i (dapagliflozin) if HbA1c > 75
ComorbiditiesPCOS, NAFLD, HTN, dyslipidaemia, sleep apnoea
HbA1c target<53 (7.0%) if achievable without hypo

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the screening criteria for T2DM in youth?Age >= 10 or puberty + BMI >= 85th percentile + 2 risk factors (FH, ethnicity, acanthosis, PCOS, GDM, HTN, dyslipidaemia)
How does T2DM youth differ from T1DM?Obesity, acanthosis nigricans, negative Abs, preserved C-peptide, strong FH, no DKA
What is 1st line therapy for T2DM in youth?Lifestyle + Metformin
When do you add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i in youth?HbA1c > 75mmol/mol (9%) on metformin
What comorbidities to screen for?PCOS, NAFLD, HTN, dyslipidaemia, sleep apnoea, depression

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
T2DM youth = T1DM?NO - obesity, acanthosis, negative Abs, preserved C-peptide
Metformin in children?Safe >10y; extend-release preferred for GI tolerance
Insulin in T2DM youth?If HbA1c > 75 on metformin + GLP-1/SGLT2i or symptomatic
Bariatric surgery in youth?Consider if BMI > 35 + comorbidity or BMI > 40; specialist centre

Mnemonic: T2DM-YOUTH-RISE

  • T2DM youth: rising with obesity
  • 2 screening: age >= 10 or puberty + BMI > 85th percentile + 2 RF
  • Diagnosis: obesity, acanthosis, FH, negative Abs
  • Metformin 1st line
  • Youth obesity: BMI > 95th percentile
  • Obesity: acanthosis nigricans marker
  • US screening: ADA age >= 10 or puberty + BMI > 85th percentile + 2RF
  • Type 2 vs 1: obesity, acanthosis, neg Abs, preserved C-peptide
  • HbA1c target: <53 (7.0%)
  • Rising trend: obesity epidemic
  • Insulin if HbA1c > 75 on metformin + GLP-1/SGLT2i
  • Screen comorbidities: PCOS, NAFLD, HTN, dyslipidaemia, apnoea
  • Ethnicity: South Asian, African-Caribbean higher risk

topics.title: Dka Management Protocol
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Execute DKA management protocol per JBDS/ADA guidelines
  • [ ] Calculate fluid deficit and replacement rates
  • [ ] Manage fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII)
  • [ ] Replace potassium safely with cardiac monitoring
  • [ ] Monitor for and manage complications (cerebral oedema, hypoglycaemia, hypophosphataemia)
  • [ ] Transition to subcutaneous insulin

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GuidelinesJBDS (UK, 2023) / ADA (US, 2024) — broadly aligned
Core Principles1) Fluid resuscitation 2) Insulin infusion 3) K+ replacement 4) Bicarbonate only if pH<7.0 5) Monitoring 6) Precipitant treatment 7) Transition to SC insulin

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(See DKA diagnosis criteria — management applies after diagnosis)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

(See DKA severity grading — protocol adjusts by severity)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationTimingTarget/Action
Glucoseq1h↓3–5 mmol/L/hr; if <14 → add 5% dextrose
Blood ketonesq2–4h↓0.5 mmol/L/hr; target clearance <0.6
VBG (pH, HCO3, pCO2)q2–4hpH ↑, HCO3 ↑; severe: arterial line q1–2h
K+q1–2hMaintain 4.0–5.0; replace per level
Fluid balanceHourlyInput/output, CVP if severe
Neurology (GCS)q1–4h by severityCerebral oedema watch in children

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A — management of confirmed DKA)

7. Management

Fluid Resuscitation

PhaseFluidRateDuration
1st hour0.9% NaCl1L60 min
Hours 2–30.9% NaCl500ml/hr2 hr
Hours 4–240.9% NaCl250ml/hrAdjust to replace deficit over 24–48h
If Na+ ↑ >5 mmol/LSwitch to 0.45% NaClPer protocolHypernatraemia prevention
If glucose <14 mmol/L5% Dextrose + 0.45%/0.9% NaClMatch insulin infusionPrevent hypoglycaemia
Total deficit: ~5–7L (100ml/kg). Replace 50% in 12h, rest over 24–48h.

Insulin Therapy

ParameterDetail
RegimenFixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII)
Dose0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U Actrapid/Humulin S in 50ml 0.9% NaCl = 1U/ml)
Bolus?NO IV bolus — increases cerebral oedema risk
DurationUntil ketones <0.6 mmol/L AND pH >7.3 AND bicarbonate >18
Glucose <14Add 5% dextrose; DO NOT STOP INSULIN until ketone clearance
Transition to SCGive 1st SC dose (basal/bolus) → continue FRII 30–60min → stop FRII

Potassium Replacement

Serum K+ (mmol/L)K+ in Fluids (mmol/L)Notes
<3.060 (or 80 in severe)Hold insulin until K+ >3.3 — cardiac arrest risk
3.0–3.560Cardiac monitor; replace aggressively
3.5–5.540Standard replacement
>5.5Hold (0)Recheck q1h; usually falls with insulin
Target: 4.0–5.0 mmol/L. Total K+ requirement often 500–1000mmol over 24h.

Bicarbonate

IndicationDose
pH <7.0 (severe)100mmol NaHCO3 in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
pH 7.0–7.3Do not give — no outcome benefit, paradoxical CNS acidosis risk

Monitoring & Complications

ComplicationPrevention / Management
Cerebral oedemaChildren <20y: avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr; mannitol 0.5–1g/kg if suspected; hyperventilation; ICU
HypokalaemiaK+ replacement per protocol; cardiac monitor; hold insulin if K+ <3.3
HypoglycaemiaAdd 5% dextrose when glucose <14; don't stop insulin until ketones clear
HypophosphataemiaIf <0.3 mmol/L: IV phosphate (risk of hypocalcaemia)
Hyperchloraemic acidosisFrom 0.9% NaCl; switch to 0.45% NaCl if Na+ rising rapidly
⬇ Clinical Pathway
DKA Confirmed
↓
Severity?
↓
Ward: FRII 0.1U/kg/hr + 0.9% NaCl protocol
HDU: FRII + closer monitoring
ICU: FRII + arterial line + CVP + inotropes if shock
↓
K+ replacement per level
↓
Glucose <14?
↓
Add 5% dextrose
Continue 0.9% NaCl
↓
Ketones <0.6 AND pH >7.3?
↓
1st SC insulin dose → wait 30-60min → stop FRII

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Fluids1L 0.9% NaCl/hr ×1, then 500ml/hr ×2, then 250ml/hr; total 5–7L over 24–48h
InsulinFRII 0.1U/kg/hr; NO bolus; continue till ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18
K+<3.0: 60–80mmol/L (hold insulin till >3.3); 3.0–3.5: 60; 3.5–5.5: 40; >5.5: hold
BicarbONLY pH <7.0: 100mmol/2h
DextroseStart 5% dextrose when glucose <14; DO NOT stop insulin
Transition1st SC dose → overlap FRII 30–60min → stop FRII
Cerebral oedemaChildren: mannitol 0.5–1g/kg; ICU; avoid rapid osmolar drop
PrecipitantTreat infection (antibiotics), stop SGLT2i, etc.

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the initial fluid regimen for DKA?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500ml/hr for 2h, then 250ml/hr; total deficit ~5–7L over 24–48h
What insulin regimen is used?Fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII) 0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U Actrapid in 50ml 0.9% NaCl); NO IV bolus
How do you manage potassium in DKA?Replace in fluids: K+ <3.0 → 60–80mmol/L (hold insulin till K+>3.3); 3.0–3.5 → 60mmol/L; 3.5–5.5 → 40mmol/L; >5.5 → hold. Cardiac monitoring essential.
When do you add dextrose?When glucose <14 mmol/L; switch to 5% dextrose + 0.45%/0.9% NaCl; continue insulin until ketones clear
When do you give bicarbonate?ONLY if pH <7.0: 100mmol NaHCO3 in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
How do you transition to subcutaneous insulin?Give 1st SC dose (basal + bolus if eating) → continue FRII for 30–60min → stop FRII
What are the complications of DKA treatment?Hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia (if dextrose not added), cerebral oedema (children), hyperchloraemic acidosis (0.9% NaCl), hypophosphataemia
What is the cereal oedema management?Mannitol 0.5–1g/kg IV stat; hyperventilation; ICU; avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Stop insulin when glucose normal?NO — continue FRII until ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18; glucose controlled with dextrose
Bicarbonate for all acidosis?NO — only pH <7.0; paradoxical CNS acidosis, hypokalaemia risk
Insulin bolus to start?NO — never bolus; FRII 0.1U/kg/hr only; bolus → cerebral oedema

Mnemonic: DKA-FLUIDS

  • Fluids: 1L → 500 → 250 ml/hr (0.9% NaCl)
  • Low glucose <14 → 5% dextrose
  • Units insulin: 0.1U/kg/hr FRII (NO bolus)
  • Insulin continued until ketones <0.6 + pH >7.3
  • Dextrose + insulin overlap
  • Stop FRII 30-60min after 1st SC dose
  • K+: <3.0→60-80, 3-3.5→60, 3.5-5.5→40, >5.5→hold
  • Bicarb: ONLY pH<7.0, 100mmol/2h
  • Cerebral oedema: kids, mannitol 0.5-1g/kg
  • Transition: SC dose → wait → stop FRII

topics.title: Diabetes In Children And Adolescents
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State HHS diagnostic criteria (glucose, osmolality, pH, ketones)
  • [ ] Differentiate HHS from DKA and mixed DKA-HHS
  • [ ] Execute HHS management protocol (fluids priority, low-dose insulin)
  • [ ] Monitor osmolality decline rate (<3 mOsm/kg/hr)
  • [ ] Recognise mortality predictors

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionHyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State: severe hyperglycaemia + hyperosmolality + minimal ketosis/acidosis
Diagnostic CriteriaGlucose >30 mmol/L (540 mg/dL) + Osmolality >320 mOsm/kg + pH >7.30 + Bicarbonate >18 mmol/L + Minimal ketonaemia
Calculated Osmolality2×Na + Glucose + Urea (mmol/L) — normal 285–295
Incidence<1% of diabetic admissions; 10x less common than DKA
Mortality10–20% (vs 1–5% DKA) — higher in elderly, comorbidities
Typical PatientElderly T2DM, nursing home, infection, steroid use, non-compliance

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
Altered consciousnessUniversalConfusion, lethargy, coma (correlates with osmolality)
Severe dehydrationUniversal>10L deficit; dry mucosa, poor turgor, hypotension
Polyuria → oliguriaProgressivePre-renal AKI
Focal neurological signs25–50%Hemiparesis, seizures, nystagmus (resolve with treatment)
No Kussmaul breathingAbsentNo acidosis → no respiratory compensation
PrecipitantsInfection (pneumonia, UTI), MI, stroke, drugs (steroids, diuretics, antipsychotics), non-compliance

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureHHSDKAMixed DKA-HHS
Glucose>30 mmol/L>11.1 mmol/LVariable
Osmolality>320 mOsm/kg<320>320
pH>7.30<7.30<7.30
Bicarbonate>18<15<15
KetonesMinimal>3 mmol/L>3 mmol/L
Age>60y<60y (often)Variable
Mortality10–20%1–5%Intermediate

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
GlucoseConfirm hyperglycaemia>30 mmol/L
Calculated osmolalityConfirm hyperosmolality2Na + Glucose + Urea >320 mOsm/kg
VBGExclude acidosispH >7.3, HCO3 >18
Blood ketonesExclude significant ketosis<3 mmol/L (but may be mildly elevated)
U&E, CreatinineRenal function, Na+, K+AKI common; corrected Na+ if glucose high
Corrected Na+Account for hyperglycaemiaMeasured Na+ + 1.6×(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
ECGK+ effects, ischaemiaSilent MI common
Infection screenPrecipitantBlood/urine culture, CXR, CRP

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
DKAAcidosis (pH<7.3), ketosis (>3 mmol/L), lower glucose
Mixed DKA-HHSBoth acidosis AND hyperosmolality present
Severe dehydration aloneNormal glucose, normal osmolality
StrokeFocal signs without hyperglycaemia/osmolality
Septic encephalopathyInfection + confusion, but glucose/osmolality normal

7. Management

Fluid Resuscitation (PRIORITY)

PhaseFluidRateKey Points
1st hour0.9% NaCl1LRapid restoration of perfusion
Hours 2–60.9% NaCl500–1000ml/hrAdjust to correct deficit; HHS deficit 8–12L (150–200ml/kg)
If corrected Na+ rising >100.45% NaClPer protocolPrevent rapid osmolar fall
If glucose <145% Dextrose + 0.45% NaClMatch insulinPrevent hypoglycaemia
Goal: Correct deficit over 24–48h; osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr

Insulin Therapy

ParameterHHS (vs DKA)
TimingDelay 1–2h after fluids started — fluids alone drop glucose
Dose0.05 U/kg/hr (HALF of DKA dose) — lower insulin sensitivity
Bolus?NO
TargetGlucose decline 2–4 mmol/L/hr; stop when glucose 10–15 mmol/L
PotassiumSame as DKA — replace per level

Monitoring

ParameterFrequencyTarget
Osmolality (calculated)HourlyDecline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
GlucoseHourlyDecline 2–4 mmol/L/hr
Na+ (corrected)2-hourlyAvoid rapid correction
K+1–2 hourly4.0–5.0 mmol/L
Urine outputHourly>0.5 ml/kg/hr
Neurology (GCS)HourlyImprovement expected
Fluid balanceHourlyPositive then neutral
⬇ Clinical Pathway
HHS Suspected
↓
Labs: Glucose, Osmolality, VBG, Ketones, U&E
↓
pH >7.3 AND Osmolality >320?
↓
HHS Diagnosis
DKA or Mixed
↓
0.9% NaCl 1L/hr ×1-2h
↓
Delay insulin 1-2h
↓
FRII 0.05U/kg/hr
↓
Monitor osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
↓
K+ management per DKA protocol
↓
Glucose 10-15 → switch to SC insulin

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Diagnostic triadGlucose >30 + Osmolality >320 + pH >7.3 + minimal ketones
HHS vs DKAHHS: older, T2DM, higher glucose/osmolality, NO acidosis, higher mortality (10–20%)
Fluids FIRST1L 0.9% NaCl/hr before insulin; deficit 8–12L
Insulin HALF dose0.05 U/kg/hr (vs 0.1 in DKA); delay 1–2h
Osmolality decline<3 mOsm/kg/hr — prevent cerebral oedema
Mortality predictorsAge >70, coma, hypotension, osmolality >350, comorbidities, delayed presentation
Neurological signsHemiparesis, seizures common — resolve with treatment

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the diagnostic criteria for HHS?Glucose >30 mmol/L, calculated osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, pH >7.30, bicarbonate >18 mmol/L, minimal ketonaemia
How does HHS differ from DKA?HHS: older, T2DM, glucose >30, osmolality >320, pH >7.3, NO significant ketosis, mortality 10–20%. DKA: younger, T1DM, glucose >11.1, pH <7.3, ketones >3, mortality 1–5%.
What is the initial fluid management in HHS?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500–1000ml/hr; total deficit 8–12L over 24–48h. FLUIDS BEFORE INSULIN.
What insulin dose in HHS?0.05 U/kg/hr (half DKA dose); start AFTER 1–2h of fluid resuscitation.
What is the target osmolality decline rate?<3 mOsm/kg/hr — rapid correction causes cerebral oedema.
How do you calculate osmolality?2×Na+ + Glucose + Urea (all in mmol/L). Corrected Na+ = Measured Na+ + 1.6×(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
What are mortality predictors in HHS?Age >70, coma/GCS<12, systolic BP <90, osmolality >350, significant comorbidities, delayed presentation >24h.

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Insulin before fluids in HHS?NO — fluids alone drop glucose via dilution and improved renal perfusion; insulin delayed 1–2h
Same insulin dose as DKA?NO — half dose (0.05 vs 0.1 U/kg/hr); HHS more insulin sensitive
Cerebral oedema only in DKA?Also in HHS if osmolality corrected too fast — same <3 mOsm/kg/hr rule

Mnemonic: HHS-FLUIDS-FIRST

  • Hyperglycaemia >30 mmol/L
  • Hyperosmolality >320 mOsm/kg
  • Serum pH >7.3 (NO acidosis)
  • Fluids FIRST (1L/hr, then 500–1000)
  • Low insulin (0.05 U/kg/hr, delayed)
  • Urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr
  • Insulin after 1-2h fluids
  • Decline osmolality <3 mOsm/kg/hr
  • Stop insulin at glucose 10-15
  • Fatal if missed (10-20% mortality)
  • Infection screen/treat
  • Resolve neuro signs with treatment
  • Switch to SC when stable

topics.title: Diabetes In Ckd And Dialysis
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Execute HHS management protocol (fluids priority, low-dose insulin)
  • [ ] Monitor osmolality decline rate (<3 mOsm/kg/hr)
  • [ ] Manage electrolytes (K+, Na+) and fluid balance
  • [ ] Recognise and manage complications (cerebral oedema, rhabdomyolysis)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DiagnosisGlucose >30 mmol/L + Osmolality >320 mOsm/kg + pH >7.3 + Bicarbonate >18 + Minimal ketones
Mortality10-20% (vs 1-5% DKA) - higher in elderly, comorbidities
Key PrincipleFLUIDS FIRST - insulin delayed 1-2h after fluid resuscitation

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(See HHS diagnosis criteria)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

(See HHS diagnosis criteria)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationFrequencyKey Details
GlucoseHourlyDecline 2-4 mmol/L/hr
Calculated osmolalityHourlyDecline <3 mOsm/kg/hr (2Na+Glucose+Urea)
Corrected Na+2-hourlyMeasured Na+ + 1.6(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
K+1-2 hourly4.0-5.0 mmol/L target
Urine outputHourly>0.5 ml/kg/hr
Neurology (GCS)HourlyImprovement expected

6. Differential Diagnosis

(See HHS vs DKA differentiation)

7. Management

Fluid Resuscitation (PRIORITY)

PhaseFluidRateKey Points
1st hour0.9% NaCl1LRapid restoration of perfusion
Hours 2-60.9% NaCl500-1000ml/hrAdjust to correct deficit; HHS deficit 8-12L (150-200ml/kg)
If corrected Na+ rising >100.45% NaClPer protocolPrevent rapid osmolar fall
If glucose <145% Dextrose + 0.45% NaClMatch insulinPrevent hypoglycaemia
Goal: Correct deficit over 24-48h; osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr

Insulin Therapy

ParameterHHS (vs DKA)
TimingDelay 1-2h after fluids started -- fluids alone drop glucose
Dose0.05 U/kg/hr (HALF of DKA dose) -- lower insulin sensitivity
Bolus?NO
TargetGlucose decline 2-4 mmol/L/hr; stop when glucose 10-15 mmol/L
PotassiumSame as DKA -- replace per level

Monitoring

ParameterFrequencyTarget
Osmolality (calculated)HourlyDecline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
GlucoseHourlyDecline 2-4 mmol/L/hr
Na+ (corrected)2-hourlyAvoid rapid correction
K+1-2 hourly4.0-5.0 mmol/L
Urine outputHourly>0.5 ml/kg/hr
Neurology (GCS)HourlyImprovement expected
Fluid balanceHourlyPositive then neutral
⬇ Clinical Pathway
HHS Suspected
↓
Labs: Glucose, Osmolality, VBG, Ketones, U&E
↓
pH >7.3 AND Osmolality >320?
↓
HHS Diagnosis
DKA or Mixed
↓
0.9% NaCl 1L/hr x1-2h
↓
Delay insulin 1-2h
↓
FRII 0.05U/kg/hr
↓
Monitor osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
↓
K+ management per DKA protocol
↓
Glucose 10-15 -> switch to SC insulin

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Fluids FIRST1L 0.9% NaCl/hr before insulin; deficit 8-12L over 24-48h
Insulin HALF dose0.05 U/kg/hr (vs 0.1 in DKA); delay 1-2h
Osmolality decline<3 mOsm/kg/hr -- prevent cerebral oedema
Mortality predictorsAge >70, coma, hypotension, osmolality >350, comorbidities, delayed presentation
Neurological signsHemiparesis, seizures common -- resolve with treatment

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the initial fluid management in HHS?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500-1000ml/hr; total deficit 8-12L over 24-48h. FLUIDS BEFORE INSULIN.
What insulin dose in HHS?0.05 U/kg/hr (half DKA dose); start AFTER 1-2h of fluid resuscitation.
What is the target osmolality decline rate?<3 mOsm/kg/hr -- rapid correction causes cerebral oedema.
How do you calculate osmolality?2xNa+ + Glucose + Urea (all in mmol/L). Corrected Na+ = Measured Na+ + 1.6(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
What are mortality predictors in HHS?Age >70, coma/GCS<12, systolic BP <90, osmolality >350, significant comorbidities, delayed presentation >24h.
How does HHS differ from DKA management?Fluids priority (insulin delayed 1-2h); half insulin dose (0.05 vs 0.1 U/kg/hr); osmolality monitoring; higher mortality.

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Insulin before fluids in HHS?NO - fluids alone drop glucose via dilution and improved renal perfusion; insulin delayed 1-2h
Same insulin dose as DKA?NO - half dose (0.05 vs 0.1 U/kg/hr); HHS more insulin sensitive
Cerebral oedema only in DKA?Also in HHS if osmolality corrected too fast -- same <3 mOsm/kg/hr rule

Mnemonic: HHS-FLUIDS-FIRST

  • Hyperglycaemia >30 mmol/L
  • Hyperosmolality >320 mOsm/kg
  • Serum pH >7.3 (NO acidosis)
  • Fluids FIRST (1L/hr, then 500-1000)
  • Low insulin (0.05 U/kg/hr, delayed)
  • Urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr
  • Insulin after 1-2h fluids
  • Decline osmolality <3 mOsm/kg/hr
  • Stop insulin at glucose 10-15
  • Fatal if missed (10-20% mortality)
  • Infection screen/treat
  • Resolve neuro signs with treatment
  • Switch to SC when stable

11. Mind Map

HHS Management
Fluids
1L/hr x1-2h
500-1000ml/hr
Deficit 8-12L
Correct over 24-48h
Insulin
HALF dose: 0.05 U/kg/hr
Delayed 1-2h after fluids
Stop at glucose 10-15

Glucose 2-4 mmol/L/hr decline
K+ 1-2 hourly
Neuro hourly
Mortality
10-20%
Age>70, coma, hypotension

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionGlucose >30, Osmolality >320, pH >7.3, min ketones; fluid deficit 8-12L
**Key Test"Calculated osmolality; glucose hourly; corrected Na+
**Classification"HHS vs DKA: pH>7.3, no acidosis, osmolality>320
**Acute Mgmt"FLUIDS FIRST (1L/hr x1-2h); insulin 0.05U/kg/hr delayed 1-2h
**Chronic Mgmt"Switch to SC insulin at glucose 10-15; treat precipitant
**Key Score"Osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
**Complications"Cerebral oedema, rhabdomyolysis, AKI, DVT, mortality 10-20%
**Prognosis"Resolves with fluids; neuro signs reversible; address precipitant

topics.title: Hhs Management Protocol
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Execute HHS management protocol (fluids priority, low-dose insulin)
  • [ ] Monitor osmolality decline rate (<3 mOsm/kg/hr)
  • [ ] Manage electrolytes (K+, Na+) and fluid balance
  • [ ] Recognise and manage complications (cerebral oedema, rhabdomyolysis)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DiagnosisGlucose >30 mmol/L + Osmolality >320 mOsm/kg + pH >7.3 + Bicarbonate >18 + Minimal ketones
Mortality10-20% (vs 1-5% DKA) - higher in elderly, comorbidities
Key PrincipleFLUIDS FIRST - insulin delayed 1-2h after fluid resuscitation

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(See HHS diagnosis criteria)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

(See HHS diagnosis criteria)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationFrequencyKey Details
GlucoseHourlyDecline 2-4 mmol/L/hr
Calculated osmolalityHourlyDecline <3 mOsm/kg/hr (2Na+Glucose+Urea)
Corrected Na+2-hourlyMeasured Na+ + 1.6(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
K+1-2 hourly4.0-5.0 mmol/L target
Urine outputHourly>0.5 ml/kg/hr
Neurology (GCS)HourlyImprovement expected

6. Differential Diagnosis

(See HHS vs DKA differentiation)

7. Management

Fluid Resuscitation (PRIORITY)

PhaseFluidRateKey Points
1st hour0.9% NaCl1LRapid restoration of perfusion
Hours 2-60.9% NaCl500-1000ml/hrAdjust to correct deficit; HHS deficit 8-12L (150-200ml/kg)
If corrected Na+ rising >100.45% NaClPer protocolPrevent rapid osmolar fall
If glucose <145% Dextrose + 0.45% NaClMatch insulinPrevent hypoglycaemia
Goal: Correct deficit over 24-48h; osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr

Insulin Therapy

ParameterHHS (vs DKA)
TimingDelay 1-2h after fluids started -- fluids alone drop glucose
Dose0.05 U/kg/hr (HALF of DKA dose) -- lower insulin sensitivity
Bolus?NO
TargetGlucose decline 2-4 mmol/L/hr; stop when glucose 10-15 mmol/L
PotassiumSame as DKA -- replace per level

Monitoring

ParameterFrequencyTarget
Osmolality (calculated)HourlyDecline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
GlucoseHourlyDecline 2-4 mmol/L/hr
Na+ (corrected)2-hourlyAvoid rapid correction
K+1-2 hourly4.0-5.0 mmol/L
Urine outputHourly>0.5 ml/kg/hr
Neurology (GCS)HourlyImprovement expected
Fluid balanceHourlyPositive then neutral
⬇ Clinical Pathway
HHS Suspected
↓
Labs: Glucose, Osmolality, VBG, Ketones, U&E
↓
pH >7.3 AND Osmolality >320?
↓
HHS Diagnosis
DKA or Mixed
↓
0.9% NaCl 1L/hr x1-2h
↓
Delay insulin 1-2h
↓
FRII 0.05U/kg/hr
↓
Monitor osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
↓
K+ management per DKA protocol
↓
Glucose 10-15 -> switch to SC insulin

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Fluids FIRST1L 0.9% NaCl/hr before insulin; deficit 8-12L over 24-48h
Insulin HALF dose0.05 U/kg/hr (vs 0.1 in DKA); delay 1-2h
Osmolality decline<3 mOsm/kg/hr -- prevent cerebral oedema
Mortality predictorsAge >70, coma, hypotension, osmolality >350, comorbidities, delayed presentation
Neurological signsHemiparesis, seizures common -- resolve with treatment

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the initial fluid management in HHS?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500-1000ml/hr; total deficit 8-12L over 24-48h. FLUIDS BEFORE INSULIN.
What insulin dose in HHS?0.05 U/kg/hr (half DKA dose); start AFTER 1-2h of fluid resuscitation.
What is the target osmolality decline rate?<3 mOsm/kg/hr -- rapid correction causes cerebral oedema.
How do you calculate osmolality?2xNa+ + Glucose + Urea (all in mmol/L). Corrected Na+ = Measured Na+ + 1.6(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
What are mortality predictors in HHS?Age >70, coma/GCS<12, systolic BP <90, osmolality >350, significant comorbidities, delayed presentation >24h.
How does HHS differ from DKA management?Fluids priority (insulin delayed 1-2h); half insulin dose (0.05 vs 0.1 U/kg/hr); osmolality monitoring; higher mortality.

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Insulin before fluids in HHS?NO - fluids alone drop glucose via dilution and improved renal perfusion; insulin delayed 1-2h

Cerebral oedema only in DKA?Also in HHS if osmolality corrected too fast -- same <3 mOsm/kg/hr rule

Mnemonic: HHS-FLUIDS-FIRST

  • Hyperglycaemia >30 mmol/L

topics.title: Glucagon Im In Auto Injector
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State glucagon indications, dosing, and routes
  • [ ] Choose appropriate formulation (IM, IN, auto-injector)
  • [ ] Recognise contraindications (starvation, alcohol, adrenal insufficiency)
  • [ ] Manage glucagon failure

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
IndicationSevere hypoglycaemia (unconscious/seizure/unable to swallow) when IV access unavailable
MechanismStimulates hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis -> increases blood glucose
RequirementRequires hepatic glycogen stores -- ineffective if depleted

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Glucagon Formulations

FormulationDoseRouteOnsetDurationKey Features
GlucaGen HypoKit1mg (adult), 0.5mg (<25kg)IM/SC10-15min60-90minReconstitute powder; standard UK
Baqsimi3mgIntranasal10-15min60-90minNeedle-free; device-based; single use
Gvoke HypoPen/Pre-filled syringe0.5mg (paed), 1mg (adult)SC (auto-injector/syringe)10-15min60-90minReady-to-use liquid; stable at room temp
Zegalogue (dasiglucagon)0.6mgSC (auto-injector/syringe)10min60-90minReady-to-use; stable; no reconstitution

Dosing by Age/Weight

PopulationIM/SC (GlucaGen)Intranasal (Baqsimi)Auto-injector (Gvoke)
Adult1mg3mg1mg
Child <25kg / <6-8y0.5mg3mg (age>=4)0.5mg

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRole
Blood glucoseConfirm hypo before/after; recheck 15min post-glucagon
KetonesIf DKA suspected -- glucagon contraindicated (requires insulin)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ScenarioGlucagon Effective?
Insulin/SU hypoglycaemiaYES (glycogen present)
Starvation/prolonged fastingNO - glycogen depleted
Alcohol-induced hypoglycaemiaNO - glycogen depleted, blocks gluconeogenesis
Adrenal insufficiencyNO - impaired counter-regulation
Post-bariatric hypoglycaemiaVariable (may have glycogen)

7. Management

Acute Administration Protocol

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Severe Hypoglycaemia
↓
IV Access?
↓
IV Dextrose 75-80ml 20% (or 150ml 10%) bolus -> 10% infusion
Conscious?
↓
Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult) or Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi)
↓
Recheck glucose 15min
↓
Glucose >3.9?
↓
IV access? -> dextrose; or 2nd glucagon NOT recommended
Complex carb snack if meal >1h

Key Administration Points

StepAction
1Check glucose (if possible) -- but DON'T delay if clinical hypo
2Choose formulation: IM/SC (GlucaGen), IN (Baqsimi), Auto (Gvoke)
3Administer per device instructions
4Position recovery position (aspiration risk if vomiting)
5Recheck glucose at 15min
6If no response in 15min -> IV dextrose (do NOT repeat glucagon)
7Once glucose >3.9 -> complex carbs if next meal >1h

Post-Glucagon Care

ActionDetail
Complex carbsOnce conscious and glucose >3.9; snack if meal >1h away
MonitoringGlucose q15-30min for 1-2h
Investigate causeReview insulin/SU doses, meal timing, alcohol, exercise
EducationPatient/carer training on glucagon use; expiry dates

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
IndicationSevere hypo (unconscious/seizure/unable to swallow) + NO IV access
Dose adult1mg IM/SC (GlucaGen); 3mg IN (Baqsimi); 1mg auto-injector (Gvoke)
Dose child0.5mg <25kg/age<8y; Baqsimi 3mg (age>=4)
Onset/Duration10-15min onset; 60-90min duration
ContraindicationsStarvation, alcohol, adrenal insufficiency -- NO glycogen -> NO effect
FailureIf no response 15min -> IV dextrose; do NOT repeat glucagon (glycogen depleted)
Post-glucagonRecheck 15min; complex carbs if glucose>3.9; monitor 1-2h

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the dose of glucagon for severe hypoglycaemia in an adult?1mg IM/SC (GlucaGen); 3mg intranasal (Baqsimi); 1mg SC auto-injector (Gvoke)
What is the dose for a child <25kg?0.5mg IM/SC (GlucaGen); 3mg IN (Baqsimi, age>=4); 0.5mg auto-injector (Gvoke)
When is glucagon CONTRAINDICATED?Starvation/prolonged fasting, alcohol-induced hypoglycaemia, adrenal insufficiency -- NO hepatic glycogen stores
What if glucagon fails to raise glucose in 15 minutes?Give IV dextrose (75-80ml 20% or 150ml 10%); do NOT repeat glucagon
How long does glucagon effect last?60-90 minutes -- complex carbs needed if next meal >1h away
What are the available glucagon formulations?GlucaGen HypoKit (IM/SC, 1mg), Baqsimi (intranasal, 3mg), Gvoke (auto-injector/syringe, 0.5/1mg), Zegalogue (dasiglucagon, 0.6mg)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Glucagon works in starvation?NO -- requires hepatic glycogen; use IV dextrose instead
Repeat glucagon if no response?NO -- depletes glycogen; if no response 15min -> IV dextrose
Glucagon in DKA?NO -- DKA has hyperglycaemia; glucagon would worsen it; insulin needed

Mnemonic: GLUCAGON-1MG

  • Glucagon: 1mg IM/SC adult
  • Licenced: IM, IN, Auto-injector
  • Under 25kg: 0.5mg
  • Contraindicated: starvation, alcohol, adrenal insufficiency
  • Administration: IM/SC/IN/Auto; 10-15min onset
  • Glucose check: 15min post-dose
  • Again? NO repeat -- if fail -> IV dextrose
  • Once glucose>3.9: complex carbs if meal>1h
  • Nasal Baqsimi 3mg (age>=4)
  • 1mg adult / 0.5mg <25kg
  • Must have glycogen (fails in starvation/alcohol)
  • Gvoke auto-injector ready-to-use

11. Mind Map

Glucagon
Indications
Severe hypo
No IV access
Unconscious/seizure
Formulations
GlucaGen 1mg IM/SC
Baqsimi 3mg IN
Gvoke 1mg Auto
Zegalogue 0.6mg
Dosing
Adult: 1mg
<25kg: 0.5mg
Contraindicated
Starvation
Alcohol
Adrenal insufficiency
Failure
No response 15min
-> IV dextrose
NO repeat glucagon

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionHepatic glycogenolysis stimulator for severe hypoglycaemia without IV access
**Key Test"Glucose at 15min post-dose; must have glycogen stores
**Classification"IM/SC (GlucaGen), IN (Baqsimi), Auto (Gvoke/Zegalogue)
**Acute Mgmt"1mg IM/SC adult; 3mg IN; 0.5mg <25kg; recheck 15min
**Chronic Mgmt"Education on use; expiry dates; carer training
**Key Score"1mg adult / 0.5mg child; onset 10-15min; duration 60-90min
**Complications"Nausea, vomiting; inefficacy if glycogen depleted
**Prognosis"Effective if glycogen present; life-saving in severe hypo

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

Topic-Group hub for Diabetes in HIV — covers 2 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
ART Metabolic SyndromeProtease inhibitors (lopinavir, atazanavir) → insulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, lipodystrophy · Integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir, bictegravir) → weight gain, ↑diabetes risk
Insulin ResistanceChronic inflammation, mitochondrial toxicity, visceral adiposity, direct β-cell toxicity (older NRTIs)
ScreeningADA: Annual FPG/HbA1c in HIV on ART · Higher risk with PI, age >40, BMI >25, FH, HCV co-infection
ManagementPrefer integrase inhibitors (less metabolic) · Metformin 1st line · SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for CV/renal benefit · Monitor weight, lipids, glucose
