topics.title: Insulin Resistance
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define insulin resistance and its molecular mechanisms
  • [ ] Explain tissue-specific insulin resistance (liver, muscle, adipose)
  • [ ] Identify mediators: FFA, inflammation, adipokines, ceramides
  • [ ] Apply HOMA-IR and other IR indices

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionImpaired biological response to physiological insulin concentrations
Prevalence~50% adults have some degree; central to T2DM, MetS, CVD
Tissue SpecificityLiver (HGP), Muscle (glucose disposal), Adipose (lipolysis)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Molecular Mechanisms of Insulin Resistance

LevelMechanismKey Mediators
ReceptorDecreased insulin receptor tyrosine kinase activitySerine phosphorylation (IRS-1)
Post-receptorImpaired PI3K-Akt signallingPKC-theta, JNK, IKK-beta
MetabolicLipotoxicity, glucotoxicityCeramides, DAG, ROS, ER stress
InflammatoryAdipose tissue inflammationTNF-alpha, IL-6, MCP-1, leptin/down adiponectin
Ectopic fatLiver, muscle, pancreas fat depositionHepatic steatosis, myosteatosis

Tissue-Specific Effects

TissueNormal Insulin ActionInsulin Resistance Effect
LiverDecreased HGP (glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis)Increased HGP (fasting hyperglycaemia)
MuscleIncreased glucose uptake (GLUT4)Decreased glucose disposal (post-prandial hyperglycaemia)
AdiposeDecreased lipolysis, increased lipogenesisIncreased FFA, decreased adiponectin, increased leptin

Surrogate Indices

IndexFormulaCut-off (IR)
HOMA-IR(Fasting Insulin µU/mL x Fasting Glucose mmol/L) / 22.5>2.5 (varies by population)
QUICKI1 / (log(Insulin) + log(Glucose))<0.33
Matsuda IndexOGTT-derived<4.0

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
Fasting insulin + glucoseHOMA-IR calculation
OGTT with insulinMatsuda index, beta-cell function
Hyperinsulinaemic-euglycaemic clampGold standard (research only)
Adiponectin/leptinAdipokine profile

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Insulin receptor mutationsSevere IR, acanthosis nigricans, hyperinsulinaemia (Donohue syndrome)
LipodystrophyGeneralised/partial fat loss, severe IR, high triglycerides
Cushing/AcromegalyHormone excess causing secondary IR

7. Management

StrategyMechanism
Weight lossDecreased ectopic fat, decreased inflammation, increased adiponectin
ExerciseIncreased GLUT4 translocation (AMPK), increased muscle insulin sensitivity
MetforminIncreased AMPK, decreased HGP
TZDs (pioglitazone)PPAR-gamma agonist -> increased adiponectin, decreased inflammation
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RAWeight loss + direct metabolic effects

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
DefinitionImpaired response to physiological insulin; central to T2DM
Key mediatorSerine phosphorylation of IRS-1 -> blocks PI3K-Akt
LipotoxicityCeramides, DAG -> PKC/JNK activation -> IRS-1 serine phosphorylation
Adipose inflammationTNF-alpha, IL-6, MCP-1; decreased adiponectin, increased leptin
Tissue effectsLiver: increased HGP; Muscle: decreased glucose disposal; Adipose: increased FFA
HOMA-IR(Insulin x Glucose)/22.5; >2.5 suggests IR

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the molecular mechanism of insulin resistance?Serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 -> blocks PI3K-Akt signalling; mediated by PKC, JNK, IKK-beta, ceramides, inflammation
How does lipotoxicity cause insulin resistance?Ectopic fat -> ceramides/DAG -> PKC/JNK activation -> IRS-1 serine phosphorylation -> blocks insulin signalling
What is the role of adipose tissue inflammation?TNF-alpha, IL-6, MCP-1 from adipose macrophages -> systemic inflammation -> serine phosphorylation of IRS-1; decreased adiponectin
How do you measure insulin resistance clinically?HOMA-IR = (Fasting insulin µU/mL x Fasting glucose mmol/L) / 22.5; >2.5 suggests IR

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
IR = T2DM?IR necessary but not sufficient; beta-cell failure also required
All obese = IR?Metabolically healthy obesity exists; visceral fat more important than BMI

Mnemonic: IR-INSULIN

  • Insulin resistance: impaired response to physiological insulin
  • Receptor: normal binding, post-receptor defect (PI3K-Akt)
  • IRS-1: serine phosphorylation blocks signalling
  • NF-kappaB: inflammation pathway (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
  • Serine phosphorylation: IRS-1 inactivation
  • Untoward lipids: ceramides, DAG -> PKC/JNK
  • Lipotoxicity: ectopic fat in liver/muscle/pancreas
  • Inflammation: TNF-alpha, IL-6, decreased adiponectin
  • Non-receptor: post-receptor defect predominates

11. Mind Map

Insulin Resistance
Mechanisms
Receptor: normal
Post-receptor: PI3K-Akt blocked
IRS-1 serine phosphorylation
Mediators
Ceramides/DAG -> PKC/JNK
TNF-alpha, IL-6, MCP-1
Adiponectin down
Tissues
Liver: HGP up
Muscle: glucose disposal down
Adipose: FFA up, lipolysis up
Indices
HOMA-IR = (Ins x Glc)/22.5
QUICKI, Matsuda
Treatment
Weight loss, exercise
Metformin, TZDs
SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionImpaired response to physiological insulin; central to T2DM
**Key Test"HOMA-IR = (Fasting Insulin x Fasting Glucose)/22.5; >2.5 = IR
**Classification"Liver/Muscle/Adipose specific; PI3K-Akt blockade
**Acute Mgmt"N/A (chronic)
**Chronic Mgmt"Weight loss, exercise, metformin, TZDs, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA
**Key Score"HOMA-IR >2.5; QUICKI <0.33
**Complications"T2DM, CVD, NAFLD, PCOS, Metabolic Syndrome
**Prognosis"Reversible with weight loss/exercise; progresses to T2DM if beta-cell fails

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
ScreeningABI <0.9 diagnostic · TBI if calcified vessels (ABI >1.4) · Screen if >50 or symptoms (claudication, rest pain)
ClassificationAsymptomatic · Claudication (Rutherford 1-3) · Critical limb ischaemia (Rutherford 4-6)
Critical Limb IschaemiaRest pain >2wk, tissue loss (ulcer/gangrene), ABI <0.4, TcPO₂ <30mmHg · Urgent vascular referral
ManagementAntiplatelet, statin, exercise therapy · Endovascular 1st → Surgical bypass · Amputation if unreconstructible

topics.title: Sglt2 Inhibitors In Hf Dapa Hf Emperor Deliver
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, sgl2i, hf, dapa-hf, emperor, deliver, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - SGLT2i = cornerstone HF therapy across EF spectrum; DAPA-HF/EMPEROR-Reduced (HFrEF); DELIVER/EMPEROR-Preserved (EF >40%); eGFR ≥20; CV death/HF hosp ↓ 20–30%; renal benefit; mechanism: metabolic, haemodynamic, anti-inflammatory"

see_also: ["HFrEF and HFpEF in diabetes", "GLP-1 RA in HF (FIGHT, STEP-HFpEF)", "DKD management (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, finerenone, GLP-1 RA)", "Diabetes in CKD and dialysis"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

SGLT2 Inhibitors in Heart Failure (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR, DELIVER)

[!info]
SGLT2i = disease-modifying across EF spectrum (HFrEF → HFmrEF → HFpEF); dapagliflozin/empagliflozin licensed for HF (eGFR ≥20); CV death/HF hosp ↓ 20–30%; renal protection additive; mechanism: metabolic (ketones), haemodynamic (preload/afterload), anti-inflammatory.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Summarise key HF outcome trials (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced, DELIVER, EMPEROR-Preserved)
  • [ ] State indications, dosing, and eGFR thresholds
  • [ ] Explain proposed mechanisms of benefit
  • [ ] Apply SGLT2i in HF management algorithms
  • [ ] Monitor for adverse effects (euglycaemic DKA, volume depletion, genital infections)

2. Key HF Outcome Trials

TrialPopulationDrugPrimary OutcomeKey Result
DAPA-HF (2019)HFrEF (EF ≤40%), ±T2DMDapagliflozin 10mgCV death / HF hosp / urgent visitHR 0.74 (26% ↓); consistent ±T2DM
EMPEROR-Reduced (2020)HFrEF (EF ≤40%), ±T2DMEmpagliflozin 10mgCV death / HF hospHR 0.75 (25% ↓); consistent ±T2DM
DELIVER (2022)HFmrEF/pEF (EF >40%), ±T2DMDapagliflozin 10mgCV death / HF hospHR 0.82 (18% ↓); benefit down to EF ~60%
EMPEROR-Preserved (2021)HFpEF (EF >40%), ±T2DMEmpagliflozin 10mgCV death / HF hospHR 0.79 (21% ↓); consistent ±T2DM

Meta-analysis (5 trials, 11,000+ patients): CV death/HF hosp ↓ 23% across EF spectrum.

3. Indications & Dosing

IndicationDrugDoseeGFR Threshold
HFrEF (EF ≤40%)Dapagliflozin / Empagliflozin10mg OD≥20 mL/min
HFmrEF (EF 41–49%)Dapagliflozin / Empagliflozin10mg OD≥20 mL/min
HFpEF (EF ≥50%)Dapagliflozin / Empagliflozin10mg OD≥20 mL/min
T2DM + CKDDapagliflozin / Empagliflozin10mg OD≥20 (dapa/empa)

Note: Canagliflozin not licensed for HF; HF license = dapagliflozin & empagliflozin only.

4. Mechanism of Benefit (Multifactorial)

MechanismDescription
Metabolic↑ Ketone bodies (β-hydroxybutyrate) → efficient cardiac fuel; ↓ glucose oxidation
HaemodynamicOsmotic diuresis → ↓ preload; natriuresis → ↓ afterload; ↓ ventricular filling pressures
Anti-inflammatory/anti-fibrotic↓ NLRP3 inflammasome, ↓ IL-6, TNF-α, ↓ cardiac fibrosis
Ion transport↑ Na+/H+ exchange inhibition; ↓ intracellular Na+/Ca2+ overload
Renal↑ Tubuloglomerular feedback → afferent constriction → ↓ intraglomerular pressure

Not dependent on glycaemic effect — benefit identical in T2DM and non-diabetic HF.

5. EF Spectrum — Consistent Benefit

EF CategoryTrialsBenefit
HFrEF (≤40%)DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-ReducedRRR 25–26%
HFmrEF (41–49%)DELIVER, EMPEROR-PreservedRRR ~20%
HFpEF (≥50%)DELIVER, EMPEROR-PreservedRRR 18–21%
Continuous EFDELIVER (EF up to 65%)Benefit persists across EF range

No lower EF threshold for benefit — works even in advanced HFrEF.

6. Adverse Effects & Monitoring

Adverse EffectIncidenceManagement
Genital mycotic infections~5–10% (women > men)Topical antifungal; hygiene; usually continue
Volume depletion / hypotension~2–3%Reduce loop diuretic; monitor weight/BP; counsel on sick days
Euglycaemic DKARare (~0.1%)Stop SGLT2i if acute illness/surgery/fasting; check ketones if unwell
AKIRare (initial eGFR dip expected)Initial 3–5 mL drop = haemodynamic — do not stop; stabilises
AmputationCANVAS (cana) signal; not seen with dapa/empaFoot care; avoid in active ulcer/ischaemia
Fournier's gangreneVery rareUrgent surgical debridement; stop SGLT2i

7. Dosing by eGFR (HF Indication)

eGFRDapagliflozinEmpagliflozin
≥2010mg OD (full dose)10mg OD (full dose)
15–20Consider 10mg OD (off-label)Consider 10mg OD (off-label)
<15Not recommendedNot recommended

Initiation: No need to stop diuretics; monitor weight/BP 1–2w; check eGFR/K+ 4w.

8. HF Guideline Position (ESC 2021 / ACC/AHA 2022 / NICE)

GuidelineHFrEFHFmrEF/pEF
ESC 2021Class I, Level A (with ARNI, BB, MRA)Class I, Level A (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin)
ACC/AHA 2022Class 1, Level AClass 2a, Level A
NICE TATA679 (dapa), TA774 (empa)TA959 (dapa), TA960 (empa)

Four pillars of HFrEF: ARNI (or ACEi/ARB) + Beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i.

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
SGLT2i HF licenseDapagliflozin & Empagliflozin only (not canagliflozin)
DAPA-HF / EMPEROR-ReducedHFrEF: 25–26% ↓ CV death/HF hosp
DELIVER / EMPEROR-PreservedHFpEF: 18–21% ↓; benefit across EF spectrum
MechanismMetabolic (ketones), haemodynamic, anti-inflammatory
eGFR threshold≥20 mL/min for HF indication
Dose10mg OD (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin)
Not dependent on diabetesBenefit identical in non-diabetic HF
Genital infectionsCommon (5–10%); treat, usually continue
Euglycaemic DKARare; stop if ill/surgery/fasting; check ketones
Four pillars HFrEFARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

topics.title: Statin Therapy In Diabetes Intensity Targets
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, statin, intensity, targets, ldl, non-hdl, cvot, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - NICE: atorvastatin 20mg primary prevention (QRISK3 ≥10%); high-intensity 80mg secondary; ADA/ACC: high-intensity for diabetes >40y or risk factors; LDL targets; non-HDL; ezetimibe/PCSK9i add-on"

see_also: ["ASCVD risk calculators (QRISK3, ASCVD Pooled Cohort)", "Aspirin primary prevention", "BP targets in diabetes (NICE, ADA, ESC)", "Diabetes as cardiovascular risk equivalent"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Statin Therapy in Diabetes (Intensity, Targets)

[!info]
Diabetes = high CVD risk — NICE: atorvastatin 20mg primary prevention (QRISK3 ≥10%); high-intensity 80mg secondary; ADA/ACC: high-intensity for most diabetes; LDL target <1.8 (secondary) or <2.6 (primary); non-HDL <2.6/3.4; ezetimibe/PCSK9i if not at target.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State statin intensity categories and diabetes indications
  • [ ] Apply NICE vs ADA/ACC guidelines
  • [ ] Define LDL and non-HDL targets
  • [ ] Apply add-on therapy (ezetimibe, PCSK9i, bempedoic acid)
  • [ ] Manage statin intolerance and monitoring

2. Statin Intensity Classification

IntensityStatins & DosesLDL Reduction
HighAtorvastatin 40–80mg, Rosuvastatin 20–40mg≥50%
ModerateAtorvastatin 10–20mg, Rosuvastatin 10mg, Simvastatin 20–40mg, Pravastatin 40–80mg30–50%
LowSimvastatin 10mg, Pravastatin 10–20mg, Fluvastatin 20–40mg<30%

3. Guideline Recommendations

NICE CG181 / NG28 (UK)

PopulationStatinDoseTarget
Primary prevention: T2DM, QRISK3 ≥10%Atorvastatin20mg (moderate)Non-HDL <2.6 or >40% reduction
Primary prevention: T1DM >40y OR >10y duration OR microvascular complicationsAtorvastatin20mgNon-HDL <2.6
Secondary prevention (established CVD)Atorvastatin80mg (high)LDL <1.8 or Non-HDL <2.2 or >50% reduction
CKD (eGFR <60)Atorvastatin20mg (primary) / 80mg (secondary)Per above

Review: 3 months → check lipids, LFTs → adjust if <40% non-HDL reduction.

ADA 2024 / ACC/AHA 2018

PopulationStatin IntensityTarget
Diabetes, age 40–75High-intensity (atorva 40–80 / rosuva 20–40)LDL <1.8 if ASCVD; <2.6 if primary
Diabetes, age <40, risk factorsModerate/highLDL <2.6
Diabetes, age >75Moderate (clinical judgement)Individualised
Established ASCVDHigh-intensityLDL <1.4 (very high risk) / <1.8
CKD (eGFR 15–59)Moderate/highPer ASCVD status

ADA adds: If LDL >2.6 on max tolerated statin → add ezetimibe → if still >1.8 (ASCVD) or >2.6 (primary) → PCSK9i.

ESC/EAS 2019

Risk CategoryLDL TargetNon-HDL TargetStatin Intensity
Very high (diabetes + CVD or diabetes + target organ damage or diabetes >20y)<1.4 (55 mg/dL)<1.8High + ezetimibe if needed
High (diabetes no other risk factors, age >40)<1.8<2.2High
Moderate (diabetes age <40, no complications)<2.6<2.6Moderate

4. Lipid Targets Summary

ScenarioLDL-C TargetNon-HDL-C TargetApoB Target
Secondary prevention / Very high risk<1.4 (ESC) / <1.8 (NICE/ADA)<2.2 (NICE) / <1.8 (ESC)<65 mg/dL
Primary prevention / High risk<1.8 / <2.6<2.6<80 mg/dL
CKDPer abovePer abovePer above

Non-HDL = Total Cholesterol − HDL; better predictor than LDL in diabetes (hypertriglyceridaemia); preferred by NICE.

5. Add-On Therapy (If Not at Target on Max Tolerated Statin)

AgentDoseLDL ReductionIndicationKey Trials
Ezetimibe10mg OD15–20%First add-on; cheap, safeIMPROVE-IT (↓ events on simva+ezetimibe)
PCSK9i (alirocumab, evolocumab)75–150mg SC 2w / 300–420mg SC monthly50–60%Very high risk, LDL not at targetFOURIER, ODYSSEY
Bempedoic acid180mg OD15–20%Statin intolerant; add to ezetimibeCLEAR Outcomes
Inclisiran (siRNA)284mg SC day 1, 90, then 6-monthly50%Very high risk; adherence advantageORION-10, -11

6. Statin Intolerance — Management Algorithm

Statin intolerance (muscle symptoms, LFT rise)
    │
    ├─► Confirm: CK if myalgia; LFT if hepatotoxicity
    │
    ├─► Try alternate statin (rosuvastatin often better tolerated)
    │
    ├─► Reduce dose / alternate day dosing
    │
    ├─► Switch to non-statin:
    │     ├─► Ezetimibe 10mg (first)
    │     ├─► Bempedoic acid 180mg (if ezetimibe insufficient)
    │     └─► PCSK9i (if very high risk, LDL not at target)
    │
    └─► Refer lipid clinic if complex

CoQ10: Not recommended routinely (no RCT evidence for statin myalgia).

7. Monitoring

TestBaseline3 MonthsAnnually
Lipid profile (fasting preferred)✓✓ (check % reduction)✓
LFTs (ALT)✓✓✓ (if symptomatic)
CKOnly if myalgiaOnly if myalgiaOnly if myalgia
HbA1c✓—✓ (statins ↑ HbA1c ~2–3 mmol/mol; new T2DM risk ~0.1%/yr)

New-onset diabetes on statin: Small absolute risk; CVD benefit outweighs; do not stop statin.

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
NICE primary preventionAtorvastatin 20mg if T2DM + QRISK3 ≥10%
NICE T1DMAtorvastatin 20mg if >40y OR >10y duration OR microvascular complications
NICE secondary preventionAtorvastatin 80mg (high-intensity)
ADA/ACC age 40–75High-intensity statin (atorva 40–80 / rosuva 20–40) for all diabetes
ESC very high riskLDL <1.4; non-HDL <1.8
NICE targetsPrimary: Non-HDL <2.6 or >40% reduction; Secondary: LDL <1.8 or Non-HDL <2.2
First add-onEzetimibe 10mg (15–20% further LDL reduction)
PCSK9iIf LDL not at target on max statin + ezetimibe; very high risk
Statin intoleranceSwitch statin → reduce dose → ezetimibe → bempedoic acid → PCSK9i
Statin + diabetes riskSmall ↑ HbA1c/new T2DM; do not stop for this — CVD benefit >> risk

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

topics.title: Aspirin Primary Prevention
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, aspirin, primary-prevention, antiplatelet, bleeding-risk, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - ASCEND, ASPREE, ARRIVE trials; aspirin primary prevention in diabetes: net benefit marginal; NICE/ADA/ESC differ; bleeding risk (HAS-BLED); shared decision-making"

see_also: ["ASCVD risk calculators (QRISK3, ASCVD Pooled Cohort)", "Statin therapy in diabetes (intensity, targets)", "BP targets in diabetes (NICE, ADA, ESC)", "Diabetes as cardiovascular risk equivalent"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Aspirin Primary Prevention in Diabetes

[!info]
ASCEND trial (2018): Aspirin 100mg ↓ serious vascular events 12% (HR 0.88) but ↑ major bleeding 29% (HR 1.29) → net clinical benefit marginal; NICE: do not offer routinely; ADA: consider if high CVD risk + low bleeding risk.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Summarise key trial evidence (ASCEND, ASPREE, ARRIVE, ACCEPT-D)
  • [ ] Compare NICE, ADA, ESC guidelines
  • [ ] Assess bleeding risk (HAS-BLED)
  • [ ] Apply shared decision-making framework
  • [ ] Distinguish primary vs secondary prevention

2. Key Trial Evidence

TrialPopulationAspirin DoseCV OutcomeBleeding OutcomeConclusion
ASCEND (2018)15,480 T2DM, no CVD100mg↓ 12% serious vascular events (8.5% vs 9.6%; HR 0.88)↑ 29% major bleeding (4.1% vs 3.2%; HR 1.29)Net benefit marginal
ASPREE (2018)19,114 >70y (no CVD/dementia)100mgNo benefit (HR 0.95)↑ 38% major bleedingHarm > benefit in elderly
ARRIVE (2018)12,546 moderate risk (no diabetes)100mgNo benefit (HR 0.96)↑ bleedingNo role in moderate risk
ACCEPT-D (2020)5,183 T1DM/T2DM, no CVD100mg↓ CV events (HR 0.82)↑ major bleeding (HR 1.45)Similar to ASCEND

Meta-analyses: ~10% CV reduction, ~30% major bleeding increase → NNT ~100–200 for CV benefit, NNH ~100–200 for major bleed.

3. Guideline Recommendations

NICE NG28 / CG181 (UK) — Do Not Offer Routinely

  • Do not offer aspirin for primary prevention in T2DM
  • Consider only if high CVD risk (QRISK3 ≥10%) AND low bleeding risk — shared decision
  • Review annually — stop if bleeding risk increases

ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Consider Selectively

  • Consider aspirin (75–162mg) for primary prevention in:
  • High CVD risk (≥10% 10-year ASCVD risk) AND
  • Low bleeding risk (no GI bleed, no anticoagulant, age <70)
  • Not recommended for:
  • Low CVD risk (<10%)
  • Age >70 (ASPREE harm)
  • High bleeding risk

ESC 2021 CVD Prevention — Individualised

  • Class IIb, Level B: May be considered in very high risk diabetes (target organ damage, diabetes >20y, CKD)
  • Bleeding risk assessment mandatory (HAS-BLED)
  • Contraindicated if HAS-BLED ≥3

4. Bleeding Risk Assessment — HAS-BLED

LetterRisk FactorPoints
HHypertension (uncontrolled, SBP >160)1
AAbnormal renal/liver function (eGFR <60, cirrhosis)1 each
SStroke (prior)1
BBleeding history or predisposition (anaemia, GI bleed)1
LLabile INRs (if on warfarin)1
EElderly (age ≥65)1
DDrugs (NSAIDs, antiplatelets, anticoagulants) / Alcohol (≥8 units/week)1 each

Score Interpretation:

  • 0–1: Low bleeding risk → aspirin reasonable if CV benefit
  • 2: Moderate → caution, shared decision
  • ≥3: High → avoid aspirin for primary prevention

5. Primary vs Secondary Prevention — Critical Distinction

AspectPrimary PreventionSecondary Prevention
DefinitionNo prior CVD eventPrior MI, stroke, PAD, revascularisation, ACS
Aspirin benefitMarginal (NNT 100–200)Clear (NNT ~50)
Guideline consensusControversial / SelectiveMandatory (unless contraindicated)
Dose75–100mg75–100mg (75mg UK)
DurationReview annuallyLifelong

In diabetes: Prior CVD = secondary prevention → aspirin lifelong (unless high bleed risk).

6. Practical Decision Framework

Patient with diabetes, NO prior CVD
    │
    ├─► Age >70? → NO aspirin (ASPREE harm)
    │
    ├─► Calculate 10-year risk (QRISK3 / ASCVD)
    │     ├─► <10% / <7.5% → NO aspirin
    │     │
    │     └─► ≥10% / ≥7.5% → Assess bleeding (HAS-BLED)
    │           ├─► HAS-BLED ≥3 → NO aspirin
    │           │
    │           └─► HAS-BLED 0–2 → Shared decision:
    │                 • CV benefit: ~1 event prevented per 100–200 treated/year
    │                 • Bleed harm: ~1 major bleed per 100–200 treated/year
    │                 • Patient values, preferences
    │                 • Document discussion
    │
    └─► If started: Review annually — stop if bleed, age >70, risk changes

7. Special Situations

SituationRecommendation
T1DM, age <40, no CVD, no risk factorsNo aspirin
T1DM, age >40 OR >10y durationConsider per primary prevention framework
CKD G3–G5↑ Bleeding risk (uraemic platelet dysfunction) → caution
On anticoagulant (AF, VTE)No aspirin (dual therapy ↑ bleed without CV benefit)
Post-bariatric surgeryNo specific data — follow primary prevention
PregnancyLow-dose aspirin 75–150mg from 12w for pre-eclampsia prevention (not CVD)

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ASCEND trialT2DM primary prevention: 12% CV reduction, 29% major bleed increase
NICE primary preventionDo not offer aspirin routinely in diabetes
ADA primary preventionConsider if ≥10% 10-year risk + low bleeding risk
ASPREE trial>70y: harm > benefit — no aspirin primary prevention
HAS-BLED ≥3High bleeding risk — avoid aspirin
Primary vs secondaryPrior CVD = secondary = aspirin mandatory
T1DMNo specific trial data; extrapolate from T2DM
CKD↑ Bleeding risk (platelet dysfunction) — HAS-BLED captures
Anticoagulant + aspirinAvoid dual therapy for primary prevention
Shared decision-makingDocument: benefit ~1/100–200, harm ~1/100–200, patient preference

topics.title: Bp Targets In Diabetes Nice Ada Esc
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, bp-targets, hypertension, nice, ada, esc, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - NICE <140/90 (<130/80 if CKD/diabetes); ADA <130/80; ESC <130/80; ACCORD BP trial; SPRINT; first-line agents (ACEi/ARB, CCB, thiazide); avoid beta-blockers first-line"

see_also: ["Statin therapy in diabetes (intensity, targets)", "Aspirin primary prevention", "ASCVD risk calculators (QRISK3, ASCVD Pooled Cohort)", "Diabetic kidney disease staging (KDIGO)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

BP Targets in Diabetes (NICE, ADA, ESC)

[!info]
NICE: <140/90 (<130/80 if CKD/diabetes with albuminuria); ADA/ESC: <130/80 for all diabetes; ACCORD BP: intensive <120 systolic ↓ stroke but ↑ adverse events; SPRINT: non-diabetes intensive benefit.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State BP targets per NICE, ADA, ESC guidelines
  • [ ] Apply ACCORD BP and SPRINT trial evidence
  • [ ] Choose first-line and add-on antihypertensives
  • [ ] Manage resistant hypertension in diabetes
  • [ ] Monitor for adverse effects (AKI, hyperkalaemia, hypotension)

2. Guideline Targets Comparison

GuidelineGeneral Diabetes TargetCKD / Albuminuria / High RiskAge/Specific
NICE NG28 / NG136<140/90<130/80 (if ACR ≥30 or eGFR <60)<150/90 if >80y frail
ADA 2024<130/80<130/80 (same)Individualised if >65/frail
ESC 2023<130/80<130/80<140/90 if >80/frail
KDIGO 2021—<120/80 (CKD)—
ACC/AHA 2017—<130/80—

Key difference: NICE allows <140/90 for uncomplicated diabetes; ADA/ESC/KDIGO uniformly target <130/80.

3. Key Trial Evidence

ACCORD BP (2010) — T2DM, High CVD Risk

ArmSBP TargetPrimary Outcome (MI, stroke, CV death)Adverse Events
Intensive<120 mmHgNo significant difference (1.87%/yr vs 2.09%/yr; HR 0.88, p=0.20)↑ Hypotension, syncope, AKI, electrolyte imbalance
Standard<140 mmHgReferenceLower

Subgroup: Stroke significantly reduced (0.32% vs 0.53%/yr; HR 0.59) with intensive.

SPRINT (2015) — Non-Diabetes, High CVD Risk

ArmSBP TargetPrimary OutcomeAdverse Events
Intensive<120 mmHg↓ 25% (HR 0.75)↑ Hypotension, syncope, AKI, electrolyte
Standard<140 mmHgReferenceLower

SPRINT NOT in diabetes — but informs intensive targets.

UKPDS (1998) — T2DM, Newly Diagnosed

  • Tight control (<150/85) vs less tight (<180/105)
  • ↓ 24% diabetes-related endpoints; ↓ 32% stroke; ↓ 37% microvascular

4. First-Line Agents in Diabetes

Drug ClassAgent ExamplesPositionRationale
ACE InhibitorRamipril, lisinopril, perindopril1st line (all guidelines)Renal protection (↓ albuminuria), CV benefit, ↓ new-onset diabetes
ARBLosartan, candesartan, valsartan, olmesartan1st line (ACEi intolerant)Same as ACEi; less cough
CCB (Dihydropyridine)Amlodipine, felodipine, lercanidipine1st line add-on / alternativePotent BP lowering; neutral metabolic; avoid verapamil/diltiazem with beta-blockers
Thiazide/Thiazide-likeIndapamide, chlorthalidone, hydrochlorothiazide2nd/3rd lineCheap; ↑ glucose mildly (↓ insulin secretion); chlorthalidone > HCTZ

Beta-blockers: Not 1st line in diabetes (mask hypoglycaemia, ↑ insulin resistance, weight gain) — use if CAD, HF, post-MI.

5. Treatment Algorithm (NICE/ADA Consensus)

Step 1: ACEi or ARB (titrate to max tolerated)
    │
    ├─► If Black African/Caribbean: ACEi/ARB + CCB (less ACEi response)
    │
Step 2: Add CCB (amlodipine) OR Thiazide-like (indapamide/chlorthalidone)
    │
    ├─► If CKD/albuminuria: prefer CCB (less metabolic)
    │
Step 3: Triple therapy: ACEi/ARB + CCB + Thiazide
    │
Step 4: Add 4th agent:
    ├─► Spironolactone 25mg (if K+ ≤4.5, eGFR >45) — RESISTANT HTN evidence
    ├─► Beta-blocker (if CAD/HF)
    ├─► Alpha-blocker (doxazosin)
    ├─► Central agent (clonidine, moxonidine)
    │
Step 5: Referral to hypertension specialist

6. Special Considerations

SituationManagement
CKD (eGFR <60, ACR ≥30)Target <130/80 (NICE) / <120/80 (KDIGO); ACEi/ARB mandatory; monitor K+/creatinine 1–2w after start/change
AlbuminuriaACEi/ARB max dose; add SGLT2i (↓ albuminuria); finerenone
Postural hypotensionCheck standing BP; reduce/stop alpha-blockers, diuretics; review timing
Resistant HTN (≥4 drugs including diuretic)Exclude adherence, white coat, secondary causes (hyperaldosteronism, RAS); spironolactone 25mg
PregnancyTarget <135/85; avoid ACEi/ARB (teratogenic); use labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa
Elderly/frailTarget <140/90 (NICE) / <140/90 (ESC >80); avoid overtreatment; standing BP check

7. Monitoring & Safety

ParameterFrequency
BP (clinic)Every visit until controlled; then 3–6 monthly
Home BP (HBPM)7-day averages (discard day 1); target <135/85
ABPMIf white coat suspected
Renal function (U&E, eGFR)1–2w post ACEi/ARB/diuretic change; then 6–12 monthly
PotassiumSame as renal; check if on spironolactone/ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i
Orthostatic BPAnnual (elderly, autonomic neuropathy)

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
NICE target<140/90 general; <130/80 if CKD (ACR≥30 or eGFR<60)
ADA/ESC target<130/80 for all diabetes
ACCORD BPIntensive <120: ↓ stroke but no CV death/MI benefit, ↑ AKI/hypotension
SPRINTNon-diabetes: intensive <120 ↓ CV events; not directly applicable to diabetes
First-lineACEi or ARB (renal protection, CV benefit, ↓ new diabetes)
Add-onCCB (amlodipine) or thiazide-like (indapamide/chlorthalidone)
Beta-blockersNot 1st line in diabetes (mask hypo, weight gain, insulin resistance)
Resistant HTNSpironolactone 25mg (if K+ ≤4.5, eGFR >45) — best evidence
CKD + diabetesACEi/ARB mandatory; max dose; monitor K+/creat 1–2w
PregnancyLabetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa; avoid ACEi/ARB

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

AspectDetails
Stroke RiskDiabetes → 2-4x ischaemic stroke risk · Lacunar infarcts common · Haemorrhagic stroke also increased
Risk FactorsHTN (major), dyslipidaemia, AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc: DM = 1 point), carotid stenosis
PreventionBP control <130/80 · High-intensity statin · Anticoagulation if AF (DOAC preferred) · Antiplatelet if no AF (aspirin/clopidogrel)
Acute StrokeThrombolysis (alteplase) if <4.5h · Thrombectomy if LVO <24h · Glucose management (target 6-10 mmol/L)
Post-StrokeIntensive risk factor modification · Statin, BP, antiplatelet/anticoagulant · Rehabilitation

topics.title: A3
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State DKA diagnostic criteria (glucose, ketones, pH, bicarbonate)
  • [ ] Grade DKA severity (mild/moderate/severe)
  • [ ] Execute DKA management protocol per JBDS/ADA
  • [ ] Manage potassium replacement and bicarbonate indications
  • [ ] Recognise and manage complications (cerebral oedema, hypokalaemia)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionAcute diabetic emergency: hyperglycaemia + ketonaemia + acidosis
Diagnostic Triad1) Glucose >11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) 2) Ketonaemia >3 mmol/L (or significant ketonuria) 3) pH <7.3 and/or bicarbonate <15 mmol/L
Incidence4-8 per 1000 patient-years in T1DM; rising with SGLT2i use
Mortality<1% (mild/moderate); 5-10% (severe); higher in elderly/comorbidities
PrecipitantsInfection (pneumonia, UTI), insulin omission, MI, stroke, trauma, surgery, drugs (SGLT2i, steroids), pregnancy

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
Polyuria/polydipsiaUniversalPrecedes by days-weeks
Nausea/vomiting/abdominal pain75%Abdominal pain mimics surgical abdomen
Kussmaul breathingModerate/severeDeep, rapid respirations (compensatory)
DehydrationUniversalDry mucosa, poor skin turgor, tachycardia, hypotension
Ketotic breathCommonPear-drop/acetone smell
Altered consciousnessSevereConfusion, drowsiness, coma (pH<7.0)
Red Flags: Ketosis at presentation -> think Type 1 / LADA; rapid weight loss + osmotic symptoms in adult -> LADA; pancreatic cancer (new-onset DM >50y + weight loss).

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SeveritypHBicarbonate (mmol/L)Ketonaemia (mmol/L)Mental StatusK+ (mmol/L)
Mild7.25-7.3015-18>3Alert>3.5
Moderate7.00-7.2410-15>3Alert/drowsy3.0-3.5
Severe<7.00<10>3Drowsy/coma<3.0
Euglycaemic DKA: Glucose <13.9 mmol/L (250 mg/dL) + same ketone/pH criteria. Associated with SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy, starvation, alcohol. STOP SGLT2i, same management.

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
Blood glucoseConfirm hyperglycaemiaPortal/venous >11.1 mmol/L
Blood ketones (β-hydroxybutyrate)Confirm ketonaemia>3 mmol/L diagnostic; superior to urine ketones (nitroprusside detects acetoacetate only)
Venous blood gas (VBG)Assess acidosispH, bicarbonate, pCO2, base excess. VBG ≈ ABG for pH/HCO3
U&E, CreatinineRenal function, K+K+ critical for insulin safety
FBC, CRPInfection screenLeucocytosis common (stress + infection)
ECGK+ effects, ischaemiaPeaked T (hyperK+), flat T/U (hypoK+); silent MI
CXR / Urine culturePrecipitant identificationPneumonia, UTI common
Pregnancy testWomen of childbearing ageEuglycaemic DKA risk
⬇ Clinical Pathway
DKA Suspected
↓
IV Access, Labs: Glucose, Ketones, VBG, U&E, FBC
↓
pH <7.0?
↓
Severe: ICU, Bicarb, Cardiac monitor
FRII 0.1U/kg/hr + 0.9% NaCl
↓
K+ replacement per level
↓
Monitor: glucose q1h, ketones q2-4h, VBG q2-4h
↓
Ketones <0.6 AND pH >7.3?
↓
Switch to SC insulin, stop FRII 30-60min after 1st SC dose

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HHSGlucose >30 mmol/L, osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, pH >7.3, bicarbonate >18, minimal ketones
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9 mmol/L, same ketone/pH criteria; SGLT2i, pregnancy, starvation
Alcoholic ketoacidosisNormal/mildly elevated glucose, high ketones, metabolic acidosis; history alcohol binge
Lactic acidosisNormal glucose/ketones, high lactate; metformin, sepsis, shock
Renal tubular acidosisNormal glucose, non-anion gap acidosis, normal ketones

7. Management

Fluid Resuscitation

PhaseFluidRateDuration
1st hour0.9% NaCl1L60 min
Hours 2-30.9% NaCl500ml/hr2 hr
Hours 4-240.9% NaCl250ml/hrAdjust to replace deficit over 24-48h
If Na+ rising >50.45% NaClPer protocolPrevent hypernatraemia
If glucose <14 mmol/L5% Dextrose + 0.45%/0.9% NaClMatch insulinPrevent hypoglycaemia
Total deficit: ~5-7L (100ml/kg). Replace 50% in 12h, rest over 24-48h.

Insulin Therapy

ParameterDetail
RegimenFixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII)
Dose0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U Actrapid/Humulin S in 50ml 0.9% NaCl = 1U/ml)
Bolus?NO IV bolus - increases cerebral oedema risk
DurationUntil ketones <0.6 mmol/L AND pH >7.3 AND bicarbonate >18
Glucose <14Add 5% dextrose; DO NOT STOP INSULIN until ketone clearance

Potassium Replacement

Serum K+ (mmol/L)K+ in Fluids (mmol/L)Notes
<3.060-80Hold insulin until K+ >3.3 - cardiac arrest risk
3.0-3.560Cardiac monitor; replace aggressively
3.5-5.540Standard replacement
>5.5Hold (0)Recheck q1h; usually falls with insulin
Target: 4.0-5.0 mmol/L. Total K+ requirement often 500-1000mmol over 24h.

Bicarbonate

IndicationDose
pH <7.0 (severe)100mmol NaHCO3 in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
pH 7.0-7.3Do not give - no outcome benefit, paradoxical CNS acidosis risk

Monitoring & Complications

ComplicationPrevention / Management
Cerebral oedemaChildren <20y; rapid osmolar drop; headache, bradycardia, hypertension, ↓GCS; mannitol 0.5-1g/kg; hyperventilation; ICU; avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr
HypokalaemiaK+ replacement per protocol; cardiac monitor; hold insulin if K+<3.3
HypoglycaemiaAdd 5% dextrose when glucose <14; don't stop insulin until ketones clear
HypophosphataemiaIf <0.3 mmol/L: IV phosphate (risk of hypocalcaemia)
Hyperchloraemic acidosisFrom 0.9% NaCl; switch to 0.45% if Na+ rising rapidly

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Diagnostic criteriaGlucose >11.1 + Ketones >3 + pH <7.3 / HCO3 <15
Severity gradingMild: pH 7.25-7.30; Mod: 7.0-7.24; Sev: <7.0
Fluids1L/hr ×1, 500ml/hr ×2, 250ml/hr → total 5-7L over 24-48h
InsulinFRII 0.1 U/kg/hr; NO bolus; continue till ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18
K+<3.0: 60-80mmol/L (hold insulin till >3.3); 3.0-3.5: 60; 3.5-5.5: 40; >5.5: hold
BicarbONLY pH <7.0: 100mmol/2h
DextroseStart 5% when glucose <14; DON'T stop insulin
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9; SGLT2i-associated; STOP SGLT2i; same Rx
Cerebral oedemaChildren: mannitol 0.5-1g/kg; ICU; avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr
PrecipitantTreat infection, stop SGLT2i, restart insulin

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the diagnostic criteria for DKA?Glucose >11.1 mmol/L + blood ketones >3 mmol/L (or significant ketonuria) + venous pH <7.3 or bicarbonate <15 mmol/L
How do you grade DKA severity?Mild: pH 7.25-7.30, bicarb 15-18, alert, K+ >3.5; Moderate: pH 7.00-7.24, bicarb 10-15, drowsy, K+ 3.0-3.5; Severe: pH <7.00, bicarb <10, coma/GCS<12, K+ <3.0
What is the initial fluid regimen for DKA?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500ml/hr ×2h, then 250ml/hr; total deficit ~5-7L over 24-48h
What insulin regimen is used?Fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII) 0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U Actrapid in 50ml 0.9% NaCl = 1U/ml); NO IV bolus
How do you manage potassium in DKA?Replace in fluids: K+ <3.0 → 60-80mmol/L (hold insulin till K+>3.3); 3.0-3.5 → 60mmol/L; 3.5-5.5 → 40mmol/L; >5.5 → hold. Target 4.0-5.0. Cardiac monitoring essential.
When do you give bicarbonate in DKA?ONLY if pH <7.0: 100mmol NaHCO3 in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
When do you add dextrose?When glucose <14 mmol/L; switch to 5% dextrose + 0.45%/0.9% NaCl; DO NOT stop insulin until ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18
What is euglycaemic DKA?DKA with glucose <13.9 mmol/L; associated with SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy, starvation; same ketone/pH criteria; STOP SGLT2i
What are the complications of DKA treatment?Hypokalaemia (insulin drives K+ in), hypoglycaemia (if dextrose not added), cerebral oedema (children, rapid osmolar drop), hyperchloraemic acidosis (0.9% NaCl)
How do you transition to subcutaneous insulin?Give 1st SC dose (basal + bolus if eating) → continue FRII 30-60min → stop FRII

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
DKA vs HHSDKA: ketosis + acidosis; HHS: severe hyperosmolality, minimal ketosis, no acidosis. Mixed DKA-HHS exists.
Euglycaemic DKA vs DKASame ketone/pH criteria; glucose <13.9; SGLT2i major cause; STOP SGLT2i
Insulin bolus vs infusionNEVER bolus in DKA; FRII 0.1U/kg/hr only; bolus → cerebral oedema risk
Stop insulin when glucose normal?NO - continue FRII until ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18; glucose controlled with dextrose
Bicarbonate for all acidosis?NO - only pH <7.0; paradoxical CNS acidosis, hypokalaemia risk

Mnemonic: DKA-KUSS

  • Diagnosis: Glucose >11.1 + Ketones >3 + pH <7.3/HCO3 <15
  • Ketones: β-hydroxybutyrate >3 mmol/L (blood preferred over urine)
  • Acidosis: pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15
  • K+: replace aggressively (40-60mmol/L in fluids)
  • Urine ketones: nitroprusside = acetoacetate only (misses β-OHB)
  • Severity: Mild 7.25-7.30 / Mod 7.0-7.24 / Sev <7.0
  • Stop insulin only after ketones <0.6 + pH >7.3

11. Mind Map

DKA
Definition
Glucose >11.1
Ketones >3
pH <7.3
Severity
Mild 7.25-7.30
Mod 7.0-7.24
Sev <7.0
Fluids
1L -> 500 -> 250 ml/hr
0.9% NaCl
5-7L total
Insulin
FRII 0.1U/kg/hr
NO bolus
Continue til ketones<0.6
K+
<3.0: 60-80, hold insulin
3-3.5: 60
3.5-5.5: 40
>5.5: hold
Bicarb
ONLY pH<7.0
100mmol/2h
Complications
Cerebral oedema (kids)
Hypokalaemia
Hypoglycaemia
Euglycaemic
Glucose <13.9
SGLT2i
STOP SGLT2i

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionGlucose >11.1 + Ketones >3 + pH <7.3/HCO3 <15
Key TestBlood β-hydroxybutyrate >3 mmol/L; VBG pH/HCO3
ClassificationMild 7.25-7.30; Mod 7.0-7.24; Sev <7.0
Acute Mgmt1L NS/hr → 500 → 250 ml/hr; FRII 0.1U/kg/hr; K+ per level; Bicarb ONLY if pH<7.0
Chronic MgmtStop insulin only after ketones<0.6 + pH>7.3 + HCO3>18; transition SC with 30-60min overlap
Key ScoreJBDS/ADA severity grading; K+ replacement protocol
ComplicationsCerebral oedema (kids), hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia, hyperchloraemic acidosis
PrognosisMortality <1% mild/mod; 5-10% severe; higher elderly/comorbid

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt
Definition & Epidemiology
Classification & Staging
Clinical Features
Diagnosis & Investigations
Management (Acute)

Complications
Viva Questions
DDx Distinctions
Mnemonics/Algorithms

3.0-3.560Cardiac monitor; replace aggressively
3.5-5.540Standard replacement
>5.5Hold (0)Recheck q1h; usually falls with insulin
Target: 4.0-5.0 mmol/L. Total K+ requirement often 500-1000mmol over 24h.

Bicarbonate

IndicationDose
pH <7.0 (severe)100mmol NaHCO3 in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
pH 7.0-7.3Do not give - no outcome benefit, paradoxical CNS acidosis risk

Monitoring & Complications

ComplicationPrevention / Management
Cerebral oedemaChildren <20y; rapid osmolar drop; headache, bradycardia, hypertension, ↓GCS; mannitol 0.5-1g/kg; hyperventilation; ICU; avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr
HypokalaemiaK+ replacement per protocol; cardiac monitor; hold insulin if K+<3.3
HypoglycaemiaAdd 5% dextrose when glucose <14; don't stop insulin until ketones clear
HypophosphataemiaIf <0.3 mmol/L: IV phosphate (risk of hypocalcaemia)
Hyperchloraemic acidosisFrom 0.9% NaCl; switch to 0.45% if Na+ rising rapidly

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Diagnostic criteriaGlucose >11.1 + Ketones >3 + pH <7.3 / HCO3 <15
Severity gradingMild: pH 7.25-7.30; Mod: 7.0-7.24; Sev: <7.0
Fluids1L/hr ×1, 500ml/hr ×2, 250ml/hr → total 5-7L over 24-48h
InsulinFRII 0.1 U/kg/hr; NO bolus; continue till ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18
K+<3.0: 60-80mmol/L (hold insulin till >3.3); 3.0-3.5: 60; 3.5-5.5: 40; >5.5: hold
BicarbONLY pH <7.0: 100mmol/2h
DextroseStart 5% when glucose <14; DON'T stop insulin
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9; SGLT2i-associated; STOP SGLT2i; same Rx
Cerebral oedemaChildren: mannitol 0.5-1g/kg; ICU; avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr
PrecipitantTreat infection, stop SGLT2i, restart insulin

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the diagnostic criteria for DKA?Glucose >11.1 mmol/L + blood ketones >3 mmol/L (or significant ketonuria) + venous pH <7.3 or bicarbonate <15 mmol/L
How do you grade DKA severity?Mild: pH 7.25-7.30, bicarb 15-18, alert, K+ >3.5; Moderate: pH 7.00-7.24, bicarb 10-15, drowsy, K+ 3.0-3.5; Severe: pH <7.00, bicarb <10, coma/GCS<12, K+ <3.0
What is the initial fluid regimen for DKA?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500ml/hr ×2h, then 250ml/hr; total deficit ~5-7L over 24-48h
What insulin regimen is used?Fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII) 0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U Actrapid in 50ml 0.9% NaCl = 1U/ml); NO IV bolus
How do you manage potassium in DKA?Replace in fluids: K+ <3.0 → 60-80mmol/L (hold insulin till K+>3.3); 3.0-3.5 → 60mmol/L; 3.5-5.5 → 40mmol/L; >5.5 → hold. Target 4.0-5.0. Cardiac monitoring essential.
When do you give bicarbonate in DKA?ONLY if pH <7.0: 100mmol NaHCO3 in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
When do you add dextrose?When glucose <14 mmol/L; switch to 5% dextrose + 0.45%/0.9% NaCl; DO NOT stop insulin until ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18
What is euglycaemic DKA?DKA with glucose <13.9 mmol/L; associated with SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy, starvation; same ketone/pH criteria; STOP SGLT2i
What are the complications of DKA treatment?Hypokalaemia (insulin drives K+ in), hypoglycaemia (if dextrose not added), cerebral oedema (children, rapid osmolar drop), hyperchloraemic acidosis (0.9% NaCl)
How do you transition to subcutaneous insulin?Give 1st SC dose (basal + bolus if eating) → continue FRII 30-60min → stop FRII

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
DKA vs HHSDKA: ketosis + acidosis; HHS: severe hyperosmolality, minimal ketosis, no acidosis. Mixed DKA-HHS exists.
Euglycaemic DKA vs DKASame ketone/pH criteria; glucose <13.9; SGLT2i major cause; STOP SGLT2i
Insulin bolus vs infusionNEVER bolus in DKA; FRII 0.1U/kg/hr only; bolus → cerebral oedema risk
Stop insulin when glucose normal?NO - continue FRII until ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18; glucose controlled with dextrose
Bicarbonate for all acidosis?NO - only pH <7.0; paradoxical CNS acidosis, hypokalaemia risk

Mnemonic: DKA-KUSS

  • Diagnosis: Glucose >11.1 + Ketones >3 + pH <7.3/HCO3 <15
  • Ketones: β-hydroxybutyrate >3 mmol/L (blood preferred over urine)
  • Acidosis: pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15
  • K+: replace aggressively (40-60mmol/L in fluids)
  • Urine ketones: nitroprusside = acetoacetate only (misses β-OHB)
  • Severity: Mild 7.25-7.30 / Mod 7.0-7.24 / Sev <7.0
  • Stop insulin only after ketones <0.6 + pH >7.3

11. Mind Map

DKA
Definition
Glucose >11.1
Ketones >3
pH <7.3
Severity
Mild 7.25-7.30
Mod 7.0-7.24
Sev <7.0
Fluids
1L -> 500 -> 250 ml/hr
0.9% NaCl
5-7L total
Insulin
FRII 0.1U/kg/hr
NO bolus
Continue til ketones<0.6
K+
<3.0: 60-80, hold insulin
3-3.5: 60
3.5-5.5: 40
>5.5: hold
Bicarb
ONLY pH<7.0
100mmol/2h
Complications
Cerebral oedema (kids)
Hypokalaemia
Hypoglycaemia
Euglycaemic
Glucose <13.9
SGLT2i
STOP SGLT2i

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionGlucose >11.1 + Ketones >3 + pH <7.3/HCO3 <15
Key TestBlood β-hydroxybutyrate >3 mmol/L; VBG pH/HCO3
ClassificationMild 7.25-7.30; Mod 7.0-7.24; Sev <7.0
Acute Mgmt1L NS/hr → 500 → 250 ml/hr; FRII 0.1U/kg/hr; K+ per level; Bicarb ONLY if pH<7.0
Chronic MgmtStop insulin only after ketones<0.6 + pH>7.3 + HCO3>18; transition SC with 30-60min overlap
Key ScoreJBDS/ADA severity grading; K+ replacement protocol
ComplicationsCerebral oedema (kids), hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia, hyperchloraemic acidosis
PrognosisMortality <1% mild/mod; 5-10% severe; higher elderly/comorbid

topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, dfu-classification, wagner, university-of-texas, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Wagner (depth only) vs University of Texas (depth + ischaemia + infection); UT Classification superior for prognosis; guides management and amputation prediction"

see_also: ["Diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) management", "Foot risk classification (IWGDF/Scottish)", "Charcot neuroarthropathy", "Peripheral arterial disease (PAD)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Diabetic Foot Ulcer (DFU) Classification (Wagner, University of Texas)

[!info]
Wagner = depth-based only (Grades 0–5); University of Texas (UT) = depth + ischaemia + infection (Grades 0–3, Stages A–D) — UT superior for prognosis; both guide management and amputation risk.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify DFU using Wagner and UT systems
  • [ ] Explain why UT classification is prognostically superior
  • [ ] Apply classification to guide management and referral
  • [ ] Predict amputation risk by grade/stage

2. Wagner Classification (Depth-Based)

GradeDescriptionFeatures
0Pre-ulcerativeIntact skin; high risk (callus, deformity, healed ulcer)
1Superficial ulcerInvolves skin/subcutaneous tissue only; no tendon/bone/joint
2Deep ulcerPenetrates to tendon, joint capsule, or deep fascia; no bone
3Ulcer with osteomyelitisDeep ulcer + bone involvement (probe-to-bone test +ve, X-ray/MRI changes)
4Partial foot gangreneForefoot/midfoot gangrene
5Whole foot gangreneExtensive gangrene; limb-threatening

Limitations: Does not account for ischaemia or infection severity — two major determinants of outcome.

3. University of Texas (UT) Classification — Preferred

Grade (Depth) — Same as Wagner 0–3

GradeDescription
0Pre-ulcerative / healed ulcer
1Superficial (no tendon/bone/joint)
2Deep (tendon/joint capsule)
3Deep + bone (osteomyelitis)

Stage (Ischaemia + Infection) — Key Prognostic Factor

StageIschaemiaInfectionClinical Findings
ANoNoClean, non-ischaemic
BNoYesInfected (purulence, erythema >2cm, warmth)
CYesNoIschaemic (absent pulses, low ABI/TcPO2)
DYesYesInfected + ischaemic (highest amputation risk)

UT Notation: e.g., Grade 2, Stage D = deep ulcer with ischaemia AND infection.

4. Why UT Superior to Wagner

AspectWagnerUT
IschaemiaNot assessedStage C/D = ischaemia
InfectionNot assessedStage B/D = infection
PrognosisLimitedStage predicts healing/amputation
Healing rateNot differentiatedStage A: ~80%; B: ~60%; C: ~40%; D: ~20%
Amputation riskGrade-based onlyStage D → 50%+ major amputation
Management guidanceVagueStage-based pathways

Evidence: Multiple studies (Oyibo et al., 2001; Armstrong et al.) show UT stage independently predicts healing time, amputation, and resource use.

5. Combined Clinical Application

Classification Determines Urgency

UT StageHealing ProbabilityAmputation RiskReferral Urgency
1A, 2AHigh (>80%)LowRoutine podiatry (1–2w)
1B, 2BModerate (60–70%)ModerateMDT within 1w (infection control)
1C, 2CLow (40–50%)HighUrgent vascular (1–3 days) + MDT
1D, 2D, 3DVery low (<30%)Very high (>50%)Emergency MDT + vascular same day
3A, 3B, 3CLowHighMDT 1w; osteomyelitis workup

Probe-to-Bone Test

  • Positive: Hard, gritty bone palpable at ulcer base
  • Specificity >85%, sensitivity ~60% for osteomyelitis
  • Grade 3 (Wagner) / Grade 3 (UT) if positive

6. Other Classification Systems (Brief)

SystemFocusUse
SINBADSite, Ischaemia, Neuropathy, Bacterial infection, Area, DepthSimple scoring (0–6); audit/research
PEDIS (IWGDF)Perfusion, Extent, Depth, Infection, SensationIWGDF guideline standard; 4 grades per domain
DEPADepth, Extent, Phase (infection), Associated aetiologyLess common

PEDIS is the IWGDF recommended system for international communication.

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Wagner grades0=pre-ulcer, 1=superficial, 2=deep to tendon/joint, 3=osteomyelitis, 4=forefoot gangrene, 5=whole foot gangrene
UT gradesSame depth 0–3
UT stagesA=clean, B=infected, C=ischaemic, D=infected + ischaemic
UT superiorityIncludes ischaemia + infection → predicts healing/amputation
Stage DHighest amputation risk (>50% major amputation)
Probe-to-bone+ve → osteomyelitis likely → Grade 3
IWGDF standardPEDIS classification
Referral urgencyStage D = emergency MDT + vascular same day

topics.title: Albuminuria Categories A1 A2 A3
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify hypoglycaemia levels (1/2/3) and manage each
  • [ ] Apply glucagon dosing (IM, IN, auto-injector)
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia unawareness
  • [ ] Prevent recurrent hypoglycaemia

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Level 1 (Alert)Glucose <3.9 mmol/L (70 mg/dL) - threshold for neuroendocrine response
Level 2 (Clinical)Glucose <3.0 mmol/L (54 mg/dL) - clinically significant
Level 3 (Severe)Severe - requires assistance (unconscious/seizure)
Incidence T1DM2-3 severe episodes/patient-year
Incidence T2DM (insulin)1-2 severe episodes/patient-year
Mortality~4-10% of T1DM deaths; "dead in bed" syndrome

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

LevelAutonomic SymptomsNeuroglycopenic Symptoms
Level 1 (<3.9)Sweating, tremor, palpitations, hunger, anxiety-
Level 2 (<3.0)Intense autonomicConfusion, drowsiness, speech difficulty, ataxia
Level 3 (Severe)May be absent (unawareness)Seizure, coma, focal signs, death

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

ClassificationGlucoseClinical
Level 1 (Alert)<3.9 mmol/LSelf-treat
Level 2 (Clinical)<3.0 mmol/LSelf-treat, impaired judgement
Level 3 (Severe)Any (needs help)Unconscious, seizure, needs 3rd party
Counter-regulationStatus
NormalGlucagon ↑, epinephrine ↑, cortisol/GH ↑
Impaired (T1DM)Glucagon response lost first, then epinephrine blunted → unawareness

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
Capillary/venous glucoseConfirm hypo<3.9 = Level 1; <3.0 = Level 2
CGMDetect nocturnal/unrecognisedTIR/TBR metrics; alerts for <3.9
Whipple's triadDiagnosticSymptoms + low glucose + relief with glucose
Insulin/C-peptideFactitious hypoglycaemiaExogenous insulin: high insulin, low C-peptide; Insulinoma: high both

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Factitious hypoglycaemiaSurreptitious insulin/SU use; high insulin, low C-peptide; urine SU screen
InsulinomaFasting hypo; high insulin + high C-peptide + high proinsulin; CT/MRI pancreas
Adrenal insufficiencyHypo + hypotension + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia; cortisol low
Post-bariatric hypoglycaemiaLate dumping (1-3h post-meal); nesidioblastosis; GLP-1 mediated
Non-islet cell tumour (NICTH)Large tumours; IGF-2 mediated; low insulin, low C-peptide, low IGF-1

7. Management

Acute Management

ScenarioTreatment
Conscious, able to swallow15-20g fast carbs (glucose tabs, 150ml juice, 5-6 jelly babies); recheck 15min; repeat if <3.9; complex carb if meal >1h
Conscious, unable to swallowGlucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult); 0.5mg <25kg; Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi); Auto-injector 0.5/1mg (Gvoke)
Unconscious + IV access75-80ml 20% dextrose (or 150ml 10%) IV bolus → 10% dextrose infusion
Unconscious, no IVGlucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult); 0.5mg <25kg; Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi)
Hospital insulin errorSame + root cause analysis; insulin passport; double-check protocol; CGM in hospital

Glucagon Formulations

FormDoseRouteOnsetDuration
GlucaGen HypoKit1mg (0.5mg <25kg)IM/SC10-15min60-90min
Baqsimi3mgIntranasal10-15min60-90min
Gvoke (auto-injector)0.5mg/1mgSC10-15min60-90min
Zegalogue (dasiglucagon)0.6mgSC/auto10minReady-to-use, stable at room temp

Prevention of Recurrent Hypoglycaemia

StrategyDetail
Hypoglycaemia unawareness reversalAvoid <3.9 for 2-3 weeks → restores autonomic symptoms; higher targets (HbA1c 58-64); CGM alerts; CSII/AID
Medication adjustmentReduce/stop SU; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; insulin dose reduction; basal-bolus → CSII/AID
EducationCarb counting, sick day rules, alcohol + food, exercise adjustments
TechnologyCGM with predictive alerts; AID systems (reduce hypo by 30-50%)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
ClassificationLevel 1: <3.9; Level 2: <3.0; Level 3: severe (needs help)
Conscious treatment15-20g fast carbs → recheck 15min → repeat if <3.9 → complex carb if meal >1h
Unconscious + IV75-80ml 20% dextrose (or 150ml 10%) IV bolus → 10% dextrose infusion
Unconscious, no IVGlucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi)
Glucagon typesIM/SC (GlucaGen), IN (Baqsimi 3mg), Auto (Gvoke 0.5/1mg), Dasiglucagon (Zegalogue 0.6mg)
Unawareness reversalAvoid <3.9 for 2-3 weeks → restores autonomic symptoms; CGM alarms; higher targets
Hospital preventionInsulin passport; double-check protocol; CGM in hospital; reduce sliding scale

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How do you classify hypoglycaemia?Level 1: <3.9 mmol/L (alert); Level 2: <3.0 mmol/L (clinically significant); Level 3: Severe - requires assistance (unconscious/seizure)
Management of conscious hypoglycaemia?15-20g fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, 150ml juice, jelly babies) → recheck 15min → repeat if <3.9 → complex carb snack if meal >1h away
Management of unconscious hypoglycaemia?IV access: 75-80ml 20% dextrose (or 150ml 10%) IV bolus → 10% dextrose infusion. No IV: Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; or Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi)
Glucagon dose and routes?1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi); Auto-injector 0.5/1mg (Gvoke); Dasiglucagon 0.6mg SC (Zegalogue)
What is hypoglycaemia unawareness?Absent autonomic symptoms due to blunted epinephrine response (glucagon already lost in T1DM); sudden neuroglycopenia; 6x risk severe hypo
How do you reverse hypoglycaemia unawareness?Strict avoidance of glucose <3.9 mmol/L for 2-3 weeks → restores autonomic symptoms; use higher targets, CGM alerts, CSII/AID
What is the "dead in bed" syndrome?Nocturnal hypoglycaemia → cardiac arrhythmia (QT prolongation) → sudden death in young T1DM; prevention: CGM nocturnal alerts, avoid tight control at bedtime

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Glucagon in starvation/alcoholINEFFECTIVE - requires hepatic glycogen stores; use IV dextrose
Glucagon repeat doseNOT recommended - depletes glycogen; if no response in 15min → IV dextrose
Level 3 definition"Requires assistance" - NOT defined by specific glucose value

Mnemonic: HYPO-15

  • Hypoglycaemia: <3.9 mmol/L
  • You check glucose
  • Patient conscious? → 15-20g carbs
  • Oral carbs: glucose tabs, juice, jelly babies
  • 15min recheck
  • 5 → if still low, repeat 15g
  • Unconscious: No IV → Glucagon 1mg IM/IN; IV → 20% dextrose 75ml
  • Unawareness: avoid <3.9 ×2-3 weeks → restores symptoms
  • Glucagon: needs glycogen (fails in starvation/alcohol) |

11. Mind Map

Hypoglycaemia
Classification
Level 1 <3.9
Level 2 <3.0
Level 3 severe
Clinical
Autonomic: sweat, tremor, palpitations
Neuroglycopenic: confusion, seizure
Treatment
Conscious: 15-20g carbs
Unconscious + IV: 20% dextrose 75ml
Unconscious no IV: Glucagon 1mg IM/IN
Glucagon
1mg IM/SC adult
0.5mg <25kg
Nasal 3mg
Fails if no glycogen
Unawareness
Avoid <3.9 x2-3 weeks
CGM alerts
Higher targets

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionGlucose <3.9 mmol/L; Level 1<3.9, Level 2<3.0, Level 3=severe (needs help)
Key TestCapillary/venous glucose; CGM for nocturnal/unrecognised
ClassificationLevel 1: <3.9 (alert); Level 2: <3.0 (clinical); Level 3: severe (needs help)
Acute MgmtConscious: 15-20g carbs → recheck 15min; Unconscious: IV 20% dextrose 75ml or Glucagon 1mg IM/IN
Chronic MgmtUnawareness: avoid <3.9 ×2-3 weeks; CGM alerts; higher targets; CSII/AID
Key ScoreADA/IHSG classification Level 1/2/3
ComplicationsSeizure, coma, "dead in bed" (nocturnal hypo + arrhythmia), falls, cognitive decline
Prognosis4-10% T1DM deaths; recurrent hypo → unawareness → vicious cycle

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

TopicKey Points
ClassificationLevel 1: <3.9; Level 2: <3.0; Level 3: severe (needs help)
Conscious treatment15-20g fast carbs → recheck 15min → repeat if <3.9 → complex carb if meal >1h
Unconscious + IV75-80ml 20% dextrose (or 150ml 10%) IV bolus → 10% dextrose infusion
Unconscious, no IVGlucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi)
Glucagon typesIM/SC (GlucaGen), IN (Baqsimi 3mg), Auto (Gvoke 0.5/1mg), Dasiglucagon (Zegalogue 0.6mg)
Unawareness reversalAvoid <3.9 for 2-3 weeks → restores autonomic symptoms; CGM alarms; higher targets
Hospital preventionInsulin passport; double-check protocol; CGM in hospital; reduce sliding scale

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How do you classify hypoglycaemia?Level 1: <3.9 mmol/L (alert); Level 2: <3.0 mmol/L (clinically significant); Level 3: Severe - requires assistance (unconscious/seizure)
Management of conscious hypoglycaemia?15-20g fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, 150ml juice, jelly babies) → recheck 15min → repeat if <3.9 → complex carb snack if meal >1h away
Management of unconscious hypoglycaemia?IV access: 75-80ml 20% dextrose (or 150ml 10%) IV bolus → 10% dextrose infusion. No IV: Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; or Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi)
Glucagon dose and routes?1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi); Auto-injector 0.5/1mg (Gvoke); Dasiglucagon 0.6mg SC (Zegalogue)
What is hypoglycaemia unawareness?Absent autonomic symptoms due to blunted epinephrine response (glucagon already lost in T1DM); sudden neuroglycopenia; 6x risk severe hypo
How do you reverse hypoglycaemia unawareness?Strict avoidance of glucose <3.9 mmol/L for 2-3 weeks → restores autonomic symptoms; use higher targets, CGM alerts, CSII/AID
What is the "dead in bed" syndrome?Nocturnal hypoglycaemia → cardiac arrhythmia (QT prolongation) → sudden death in young T1DM; prevention: CGM nocturnal alerts, avoid tight control at bedtime

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Glucagon in starvation/alcoholINEFFECTIVE - requires hepatic glycogen stores; use IV dextrose
Glucagon repeat doseNOT recommended - depletes glycogen; if no response in 15min → IV dextrose
Level 3 definition"Requires assistance" - NOT defined by specific glucose value

Mnemonic: HYPO-15

  • Hypoglycaemia: <3.9 mmol/L
  • You check glucose
  • Patient conscious? → 15-20g carbs
  • Oral carbs: glucose tabs, juice, jelly babies
  • 15min recheck
  • 5 → if still low, repeat 15g
  • Unconscious: No IV → Glucagon 1mg IM/IN; IV → 20% dextrose 75ml
  • Unawareness: avoid <3.9 ×2-3 weeks → restores symptoms
  • Glucagon: needs glycogen (fails in starvation/alcohol) |

11. Mind Map

Hypoglycaemia
Classification
Level 1 <3.9
Level 2 <3.0
Level 3 severe
Clinical
Autonomic: sweat, tremor, palpitations
Neuroglycopenic: confusion, seizure
Treatment
Conscious: 15-20g carbs
Unconscious + IV: 20% dextrose 75ml
Unconscious no IV: Glucagon 1mg IM/IN
Glucagon

Nasal 3mg

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionGlucose <3.9 mmol/L; Level 1<3.9, Level 2<3.0, Level 3=severe (needs help)
Key TestCapillary/venous glucose; CGM for nocturnal/unrecognised
ClassificationLevel 1: <3.9 (alert); Level 2: <3.0 (clinical); Level 3: severe (needs help)
Acute MgmtConscious: 15-20g carbs → recheck 15min; Unconscious: IV 20% dextrose 75ml or Glucagon 1mg IM/IN
Chronic MgmtUnawareness: avoid <3.9 ×2-3 weeks; CGM alerts; higher targets; CSII/AID
Key ScoreADA/IHSG classification Level 1/2/3
ComplicationsSeizure, coma, "dead in bed" (nocturnal hypo + arrhythmia), falls, cognitive decline
Prognosis4-10% T1DM deaths; recurrent hypo → unawareness → vicious cycle

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - ACEi/ARB max dose first; SGLT2i (eGFR ≥20) + ACEi/ARB; Finerenone (eGFR ≥25, A2/A3); GLP-1 RA (eGFR ≥15); additive benefits; BP <130/80; avoid NSAIDs; monitor K+/eGFR"

see_also: [["Diabetic kidney disease staging (KDIGO)", "GFR categories (G1-G5)", "Albuminuria categories (A1/A2/A3)", "Diabetic nephropathy", "Diabetes in CKD and dialysis"]]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

DKD Management (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, Finerenone, GLP-1 RA)

[!info]
DKD management = "Big Four" — ACEi/ARB (foundation) + SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA + Finerenone; additive, complementary mechanisms; start early (A2), continue through G3–4; individualise by eGFR/K+.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Sequentially build DKD treatment regimen
  • [ ] Dose each agent by eGFR category
  • [ ] Monitor for hyperkalaemia, AKI, volume depletion
  • [ ] Apply KDIGO 2024 treatment algorithm
  • [ ] Manage drug combinations safely

2. Treatment Algorithm — Sequential Add-On

Step 1: ACEi OR ARB (Foundation)

AspectDetail
IndicationAll DKD A2/A3 (even normotensive)
Target doseMaximum tolerated (e.g., ramipril 10mg, losartan 100mg)
MechanismEfferent arteriolar dilatation → ↓ intraglomerular pressure
MonitorK+, creatinine at 1–2 weeks, then 3–6 monthly
Acceptable riseCreatinine <30% (eGFR drop <30%) = expected; >30% → halve dose
HyperkalaemiaK+ >5.5 → reduce/hold; >6.0 → stop; treat K+, restart low dose
ContraindicationBilateral renal artery stenosis; K+ >5.5 at baseline

ACEi vs ARB: Equivalent renal benefit; ARB if ACEi cough/angioedema; do not combine (↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI without benefit).

Step 2: Add SGLT2 Inhibitor (If eGFR ≥20)

AgenteGFR RangeDoseKey Trials
Dapagliflozin≥2010mg ODDAPA-CKD, CREDENCE
Empagliflozin≥2010mg ODEMPA-KIDNEY, EMPEROR-Reduced
Canagliflozin≥30100mg ODCREDENCE

Mechanism: Afferent arteriolar constriction (via tubuloglomerular feedback) → ↓ intraglomerular pressure; ↓ proximal glucose/Na reabsorption → ↓ workload.

eGFR dip: Initial 3–5 mL drop expected (haemodynamic) — do not stop; stabilises at new baseline.

Contraindications: T1DM (euglycaemic DKA risk), recurrent GU infections, eGFR <20 (most).

Step 3: Add GLP-1 RA (If T2DM, eGFR ≥15)

AgenteGFR RangeDoseKey Trials
Semaglutide SC≥150.5→1.0mg weeklyFLOW, SUSTAIN-6
Dulaglutide≥150.75–1.5mg weeklyAWARD-7, REWIND
Liraglutide≥151.2–1.8mg dailyLEADER

Mechanism: ↓ Inflammation, ↓ oxidative stress, weight loss, BP reduction, direct renal effects.

No dose adjustment for renal impairment — safe down to dialysis.

Step 4: Add Finerenone (If T2DM, A2/A3, eGFR ≥25)

AspectDetail
Dose10mg OD (if eGFR 25–60); 20mg OD (if eGFR ≥60)
Key TrialsFIDELIO-DKD, FIGARO-DKD
MechanismNon-steroidal MRA → anti-inflammatory, anti-fibrotic (no BP effect at low dose)
Monitor K+At 4 weeks, then monthly ×3, then quarterly
Hold ifK+ >5.5; restart at lower dose when K+ <5.0
ContraindicationeGFR <25, K+ >5.0 at baseline, Addison's

3. Treatment by KDIGO Stage

StageRegimen
G1–2, A2ACEi/ARB max + SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA (if T2DM)
G1–2, A3Above + Finerenone (eGFR ≥25)
G3a, A2Above (metformin max 1g BD)
G3a, A3Above + Finerenone
G3b, A2/A3Above (metformin contraindicated if eGFR <30 NICE)
G4, any AACEi/ARB max tolerated + SGLT2i (dapa/empa ≥20) + GLP-1 RA + Finerenone (eGFR ≥25); nephrology co-care
G5 / DialysisContinue ACEi/ARB + GLP-1 RA; stop SGLT2i/finerenone/metformin

4. Blood Pressure Target

GuidelineTarget
KDIGO 2024<120/80 (office)
NICE<130/80 (if A2/A3)
ADA/ESC<130/80

Preferred agents: ACEi/ARB → CCB (amlodipine) → Thiazide-like (indapamide/chlorthalidone, if eGFR >30) → Spironolactone (if resistant, K+ ≤4.5).

5. Monitoring Schedule

ParameterBaseline1–2 Weeks3 Months6 MonthlyAnnually
eGFR✓✓✓✓✓
K+✓✓✓✓✓
UACR✓—✓✓✓
BP✓✓✓✓✓
HbA1c✓—✓✓✓
Phosphate, Ca, PTH————✓ (if G3b+)

6. Common Pitfalls & Solutions

ProblemSolution
Creatinine rise >30% on ACEi/ARBExpected if <30% (haemodynamic); if >30% → halve dose, recheck 1w
Hyperkalaemia on ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + FinerenoneReview K+ binders (patiromer, SZC); reduce finerenone first; loop diuretic if volume overloaded
Volume depletion on SGLT2iReduce diuretic dose; ensure euvolaemia; counsel on sick day rules
Euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2i)Stop SGLT2i if acute illness/surgery/starvation; check ketones if unwell
Non-adherenceSimplify regimen; once-daily dosing; fixed-dose combinations

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
First-line DKDACEi/ARB max dose (even if normotensive)
SGLT2i in DKDeGFR ≥20 (dapa/empa); cana ≥30; add to ACEi/ARB
FinerenoneeGFR ≥25 + A2/A3; monitor K+ at 4w; hold if >5.5
GLP-1 RA renalNo dose adjust down to eGFR 15; FLOW trial benefit
ACEi + ARB comboContraindicated — ↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI, no extra benefit
Creatinine rise ACEi/ARB<30% acceptable (haemodynamic); >30% → reduce dose
BP target KDIGO<120/80; NICE/ADA <130/80
Metformin in DKDG3a: max 1g BD; G3b/G4: contraindicated (NICE)
SGLT2i eGFR dipInitial 3–5 mL drop expected — do not stop
Patiromer/SZCK+ binders enable RAASi/SGLT2i/finerenone continuation

8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, foot-risk-classification-iwgdf-scottish, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Foot risk classification (IWGDF/Scottish)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Foot risk classification (IWGDF/Scottish)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply IWGDF/Scottish foot risk classification (0-3) for screening and referral
  • [ ] Identify LOPS (loss of protective sensation) and PAD as risk multipliers
  • [ ] Set screening intervals by risk category
  • [ ] Coordinate referral to podiatry/MDT foot clinic

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PurposeStratify diabetic patients by foot ulcer/amputation risk
Tools10g monofilament (LOPS), vibration (128Hz), pedal pulses, ABI, deformity inspection
OutcomeRisk 0-3 drives screening interval and referral pathway
EvidenceRisk stratification reduces amputations 50-80% with MDT care

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - risk classification based on examination findings)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

IWGDF / Scottish Foot Risk Classification

RiskCriteriaScreening IntervalReferral
0 (Low)No LOPS, no PAD, no deformityAnnualNone (routine care)
1 (Moderate)LOPS only (cannot feel 10g monofilament at >=1 site)6-monthlyPodiatry
2 (High)LOPS + PAD and/or deformity3-6 monthlyPodiatry + Vascular if PAD
3 (Very High/Active)Prior ulcer or amputation1-3 monthlyMDT Foot Clinic
LOPS = Loss of Protective Sensation: cannot feel 10g monofilament at >=1 of 6 sites
PAD = Peripheral Arterial Disease: absent pedal pulses OR ABI <0.9
Deformity = Prominent metatarsal heads, claw/hammer toes, Charcot, limited joint mobility, bony prominences

Risk Factor Definitions

FactorDefinition
LOPSMiss >=1 site with 10g monofilament (6 sites/foot)
PADAbsent DP/PT pulses OR ABI <0.9 OR TBI <0.7
DeformityClaw/hammer toes, prominent met heads, Charcot, limited joint mobility, bony prominences
Prior ulcerHistory of full-thickness skin break below ankle
Prior amputationAny level below knee

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

ExaminationMethodRisk Contribution
10g monofilament6 sites/footLOPS = miss >=1 site
128Hz tuning forkHallux IP, medial malleolusConfirms neuropathy
Pedal pulsesDP + PT (both feet)PAD if absent
ABI / TBIABI <0.9 diagnostic; TBI if ABI >1.4Confirms PAD
Deformity inspectionVisual + palpationClaw toes, Charcot, limited mobility

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A - risk classification based on exam)

7. Management

Screening Pathway by Risk

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Diabetic Foot Screen
↓
LOPS?
↓
PAD or Deformity?
PAD or Deformity?
↓
Risk 0: Annual
Risk 2: 3-6mo + Podiatry + Vascular
Risk 1: 6mo + Podiatry
↓
Annual rescreen
3-6 monthly + Podiatry + Vascular
6-monthly + Podiatry
↓
Prior ulcer/amp?
↓
Risk 3: 1-3mo + MDT

Referral Criteria

RiskReferralTimeline
0NoneAnnual review
1Podiatry6-monthly
2Podiatry + Vascular (if PAD)3-6 monthly
3MDT Foot Clinic1-3 monthly

Patient Education by Risk

RiskEducation Focus
0Daily foot check, footwear, annual screen
1Daily check, emollients, footwear, podiatry 6mo
2Daily check, professional nail/skin care, offloading insoles, vascular review
3MDT coordination, custom footwear, home monitoring, urgent access

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Risk 0No LOPS, no PAD, no deformity -> Annual
Risk 1LOPS only -> 6-monthly + Podiatry
Risk 2LOPS + PAD/deformity -> 3-6mo + Podiatry + Vascular
Risk 3Prior ulcer/amputation -> 1-3mo + MDT Foot Clinic
LOPS10g monofilament miss >=1/6 sites
PADAbsent pulses OR ABI <0.9
MDTDiabetologist, podiatrist, vascular, orthotist, microbiology, orthopaedics -> amputations reduced 50-80%

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the IWGDF foot risk classification?0: no LOPS/PAD/deformity; 1: LOPS; 2: LOPS+PAD/deformity; 3: prior ulcer/amputation
How do you define LOPS?Inability to feel 10g monofilament at >=1 of 6 sites (hallux, 1st/3rd/5th met heads, medial/lateral heel)
What screening interval for Risk 2?3-6 monthly + podiatry + vascular referral
What defines Risk 3 (very high)?History of prior foot ulcer or amputation
When do you refer to MDT Foot Clinic?Risk 3 (prior ulcer/amputation); also Risk 2 with active concerns
What is the evidence for risk stratification?Reduces amputations 50-80% with MDT care

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Risk 1 = PAD?NO - Risk 1 = LOPS only; PAD + LOPS = Risk 2
All prior ulcers = Risk 3?YES - any history of full-thickness ulcer below ankle = Risk 3
ABI vs pulses for PAD?Both used; ABI <0.9 or absent DP/PT pulses = PAD; TBI if calcified vessels

Mnemonic: FOOT-RISK-0123

  • Foot risk: IWGDF/Scottish 0-3
  • Overall goal: amputations reduced 50-80% with MDT
  • One LOPS = Risk 1 (6mo screen + podiatry)
  • Two = LOPS + PAD/deformity = Risk 2 (3-6mo + podiatry + vascular)
  • Risk 0 = none of above = annual
  • Ischemia: ABI<0.9 or absent pulses = PAD
  • Screening intervals: 0=annual, 1=6mo, 2=3-6mo, 3=1-3mo
  • Know: prior ulcer/amp = Risk 3 = MDT 1-3mo
  • 0 = Low risk
  • 1 = LOPS only
  • 2 = LOPS + PAD/deformity
  • 3 = Prior ulcer/amp = Risk 3 = MDT

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - G1–G5 categories; CKD-EPI 2021 equation (race-free); chronicity >3 months; drug dosing adjustments by eGFR; nephrology referral <30"

see_also: ["Diabetic kidney disease staging (KDIGO)", "Albuminuria categories (A1/A2/A3)", "Diabetic nephropathy", "DKD management"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

GFR Categories (G1–G5)

[!info]
KDIGO GFR categories (G1–G5) based on CKD-EPI 2021 creatinine equation (race-free); requires chronicity >3 months; determines drug dosing, monitoring, referral.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Name and define all 5 GFR categories
  • [ ] Apply CKD-EPI equation and interpret eGFR
  • [ ] Adjust diabetes medications by eGFR
  • [ ] Identify nephrology referral thresholds

2. KDIGO GFR Categories

CategoryeGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)DescriptionClinical Implications
G1≥90Normal / HighMonitor if risk factors (diabetes, HTN); drug dosing normal
G260–89Mildly decreasedMonitor annually; screen for albuminuria; drug dosing normal
G3a45–59Mild-moderate decreased6-monthly monitoring; adjust renally-cleared drugs; avoid NSAIDs
G3b30–44Moderate-severe decreased6-monthly; metformin max 1g BD (NICE) / contraindicated (some); nephrology referral
G415–29Severely decreased3–6 monthly; metformin contraindicated; SGLT2i OK (dapa/empa ≥20); RRT planning; mandatory nephrology
G5<15Kidney failureNephrology mandatory; RRT (dialysis/transplant) or conservative; adjust all drugs

3. CKD-EPI 2021 Equation (Race-Free)

Variables: Serum creatinine (µmol/L), age, sex

  • No race coefficient (removed 2021)
  • Preferred over MDRD (more accurate at higher GFR)
  • Cystatin C equation available (eGFRcys, eGFRcr-cys) — use if creatinine unreliable (amputees, muscle wasting, high meat diet)

Chronicity: eGFR <60 on ≥2 occasions ≥90 days apart = CKD.

4. Drug Dosing Adjustments by GFR Category (Key Diabetes Drugs)

DrugG1 (≥90)G2 (60–89)G3a (45–59)G3b (30–44)G4 (15–29)G5 (<15)
MetforminFullFullMax 1g BDContraindicated (NICE)ContraindicatedContraindicated
SulfonylureasFullGliclazide preferredAvoid glibenclamide/glimepirideAvoidAvoidAvoid
DPP-4iFullFullDose reduce (saxa/alo/vilda); Linagliptin no adjustDose reduceDose reduceDose reduce
SGLT2iFullFullDapa/Empa OK (≥20); Cana avoid <45Dapa/Empa OK (≥20)Dapa/Empa OK (≥20)Avoid
GLP-1 RAFullFullFullFullFullFull
FinerenoneFullFullFulleGFR ≥25eGFR ≥25Avoid
ACEi/ARBFullFullMonitor K+/Cr 1–2wMonitor K+/Cr 1–2wMonitor K+/Cr 1–2wSpecialist
InsulinStandard↓ 10–20%↓ 20–30%↓ 30–50%↓ 50%+↓ 50%+

5. Monitoring by GFR Category

CategoryUACR + eGFRBPHbA1cOther
G1–2Annual<130/803–6 monthlyLipids annual
G3a6-monthly<130/803–6 monthlyK+, bicarbonate, phosphate, Ca, PTH annual
G3b6-monthly<130/803–6 monthlyAs above + vitamin D, bone profile
G43–6 monthly<130/803–6 monthlyAs above + RRT planning, access
G5Monthly (dialysis)<130/803-monthlyFull dialysis bloods

6. Special Considerations

SituationeGFR Interpretation
Acute kidney injury (AKI)eGFR not valid — use KDIGO AKI criteria (Cr rise, UO)
Rapidly progressiveeGFR decline >5 mL/min/yr → urgent nephrology
Muscle wasting/amputeeCreatinine-based eGFR overestimates — use cystatin C
PregnancyeGFR ↑ physiologically — use pregnancy-specific ranges
Elderly/frailG3a may be "normal ageing" — assess trajectory, not single value

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
GFR categoriesG1 ≥90, G2 60–89, G3a 45–59, G3b 30–44, G4 15–29, G5 <15
CKD-EPI 2021Race-free; preferred over MDRD
ChronicityeGFR <60 on ≥2 occasions ≥90 days apart
Metformin cutoffG3a: max 1g BD; G3b (<45): contraindicated (NICE)
SGLT2i in CKDDapagliflozin/Empagliflozin ≥20; Canagliflozin ≥45
LinagliptinNo dose adjustment at any eGFR
FinerenoneeGFR ≥25 + A2/A3
ACEi/ARB monitoringK+/Cr at 1–2w after start/change, then 6m
Nephrology referraleGFR <30 (G4) or ACR >70 or rapid decline
Cystatin CUse if creatinine unreliable (low muscle mass, amputees)

topics.title: Amputation Prevention And Multidisciplinary Care
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe classic symptomatic presentation of T1DM
  • [ ] Recognise DKA at diagnosis (25-30%)
  • [ ] Differentiate from T2DM, LADA, stress hyperglycaemia
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria in symptomatic patients

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PresentationAcute/subacute onset (days-weeks)
DKA at diagnosis25-30% of T1DM children; higher in <5 years
Peak age10-14 years (bimodal: 4-7, 10-14)
Seasonal variationWinter peak (viral triggers)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

SymptomFrequencyMechanism
Polyuria95%Osmotic diuresis (glucose > renal threshold)
Polydipsia95%Thirst from dehydration/hyperosmolality
Weight loss80%Catabolism (lipolysis/proteolysis) despite polyphagia
Fatigue/lethargy70%Insulin deficiency -> impaired glucose utilisation
Blurred vision30%Osmotic lens swelling
Abdominal pain40% (if DKA)Ketosis, gastric stasis
Kussmaul breathingDKA onlyRespiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis
Ketotic breathDKAAcetone excretion
Red Flags: Ketosis at presentation -> T1DM/LADA; rapid weight loss + osmotic symptoms in adult -> LADA; pancreatic cancer (new-onset DM >50y + weight loss).

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureT1DM ClassicT2DMLADA
OnsetAcute (days-weeks)Insidious (months-years)Subacute
AgePeak 10-14y>40y (usually)>30y
BMINormal/lowOverweight/obeseNormal/low
KetosisCommon (25-30% DKA)RareUncommon
Autoantibodies2+ (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)NegativeGAD65+ (often single)
C-peptideLow/absentNormal/highLow-normal

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationT1DMT2DMLADA
Autoantibodies2+ positiveNegativeGAD65+ (single often)
C-peptideLow/absentNormal/highLow-normal
HbA1c>48 usuallyVariableVariable
KetonesPositive if DKANegativeUsually negative

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMInsidious, obese, no ketosis, negative autoantibodies
LADAAdult, slow progression, GAD65+, initial non-insulin response
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness, resolves, no prior dysglycaemia
MODY<25y, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies
Pancreatic cancer>50y, weight loss, new-onset DM, no autoantibodies

7. Management

Acute (DKA at presentation)

StepAction
1ABCDE assessment; IV access; labs (glucose, ketones, VBG, U&E, FBC)
2DKA protocol: fluids, FRII 0.1U/kg/hr, K+ replacement, monitor
3Once stable: transition to SC insulin (basal-bolus)

Chronic (after DKA resolution)

ComponentDetail
InsulinBasal-bolus MDI or CSII (see Insulin therapy note)
EducationCarb counting, sick day rules, hypoglycaemia management
CGMStrongly recommended (NICE: all T1DM)
Follow-upMDT (diabetologist, educator, dietitian, psychologist)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Classic triadPolyuria, polydipsia, weight loss (despite polyphagia)
DKA at diagnosis25-30% children; <5y highest risk; medical emergency
Symptom durationDays to weeks before presentation
Age peak4-7y and 10-14y (bimodal)
vs T2DMAcute, lean, ketosis, autoantibodies 2+
vs LADAAcute vs subacute; child/adolescent vs adult

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the classic presentation of T1DM?Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss despite polyphagia, fatigue; duration days-weeks
What percentage present in DKA?25-30% of children; higher in <5 years
How do you differentiate T1DM from T2DM at presentation?Acute onset, lean, ketosis/DKA, autoantibodies 2+, low C-peptide
What is the peak age of onset?Bimodal: 4-7 years and 10-14 years
What are the osmotic symptoms?Polyuria (glucose > renal threshold), polydipsia (dehydration), polyphagia (catabolism)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Weight loss = T1DM only?NO - can occur in uncontrolled T2DM, pancreatic cancer, hyperthyroidism
DKA = T1DM only?NO - T2DM can present in DKA (esp. Afro-Caribbean, ketosis-prone T2DM)

Mnemonic: T1DM-CLASSIC

  • T1DM: acute onset days-weeks
  • 1 Osmotic: polyuria, polydipsia
  • DKA: 25-30% at diagnosis
  • Mass loss: weight loss despite polyphagia
  • Classic: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue
  • Leading to DKA if missed
  • Age peak: 4-7, 10-14 bimodal
  • Symptoms: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue
  • Seasonal: winter peak (viral)
  • Insulin required immediately
  • Child/adolescent peak

11. Mind Map

T1DM Classic Presentation
Symptoms
Polyuria
Polydipsia
Weight loss (polyphagia)
Fatigue
DKA at Dx
25-30% children
<5y highest

Bimodal: 4-7, 10-14
vs T2DM
