topics.title: Neonatal Diabetes
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify sulfonylureas by generation and duration
  • [ ] Apply renal/hepatic dosing adjustments
  • [ ] Recognise hypoglycaemia risk factors and management
  • [ ] Apply CAROLINA trial implications (CV neutrality but ↑hypo vs DPP-4i)
  • [ ] Choose preferred agent in CKD/elderly (gliclazide MR)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Drug ClassSulfonylureas (insulin secretagogues)
MechanismBind SUR1 subunit of KATP channel → channel closure → membrane depolarisation → Ca²⁺ influx → insulin exocytosis (glucose-independent at high doses)
Generations1st gen: tolbutamide, chlorpropamide (long duration, more side effects); 2nd gen: glibenclamide (glyburide), glipizide, gliclazide, glimepiride (more potent, shorter duration); 3rd gen: glimepiride
Agents & DosesGliclazide MR: 30-120mg OD (max 120mg); Gliclazide: 40-320mg BD; Glimepiride: 1-6mg OD; Glipizide: 2.5-20mg BD; Glibenclamide: 2.5-15mg BD (AVOID in elderly/CKD)
HbA1c Reduction1.0-1.5% (11-16 mmol/mol) monotherapy
Weight Change+2-4kg (insulin anabolic effect)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A — drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Agent Comparison

AgentGenerationDurationDose RangeRenal AdjustmentKey Features
Gliclazide MR2nd24h30-120mg ODSafe in CKD (no active metabolites)Preferred in elderly/CKD; antioxidant; ↓platelet aggregation
Gliclazide2nd12-24h40-320mg BDCaution CKDStandard
Glimepiride3rd24h1-6mg ODUse caution (active metabolites)Once daily; ↑hypo risk in elderly
Glipizide2nd12-24h2.5-20mg BDSafe in CKD (inactive metabolites)Short-acting option
Glibenclamide2nd24h+2.5-15mg BDAVOID in CKD/elderlyProlonged active metabolites → severe/prolonged hypo

CAROLINA Trial (Glimepiride vs Linagliptin)

OutcomeGlimepirideLinagliptinHR (95% CI)
MACE (CV death/MI/stroke)12.4%12.0%1.03 (0.90-1.18) — non-inferior
Severe hypoglycaemia2.3%0.6%3.8x higher
Weight gain+1.2kg–1.0kg—
ConclusionCV neutral vs DPP-4iBut significantly more hypoglycaemia

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
Glucose (SMBG/CGM)Hypoglycaemia detectionEducate on symptoms; CGM if high risk
Renal function (eGFR)Dose adjustmentBefore initiation, then 6-12 monthly
WeightWeight gain trackingEvery visit

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycaemiaCan occur hours after dose; prolonged (esp glibenclamide, glimepiride); elderly, CKD, missed meals, alcohol, β-blockers mask symptoms
Factitious hypoglycaemiaSurreptitious SU use; ↑insulin + ↓C-peptide (exogenous insulin) — but SU → ↑insulin + ↑C-peptide; screen urine for SU metabolites
InsulinomaFasting hypo; ↑insulin + ↑C-peptide + ↑proinsulin; CT/MRI pancreas
Post-bariatric hypoglycaemiaLate dumping (1-3h post-meal); nesidioblastosis; GLP-1 mediated

7. Management

Initiation & Dosing

StepAction
1. Select agentGliclazide MR 1st choice (CKD safe, once daily, ↓hypo); Glipizide if short-acting needed; AVOID glibenclamide
2. Start lowGliclazide MR 30mg OD; Glimepiride 1mg OD; Glipizide 2.5mg BD
3. TitrateIncrease every 2-4 weeks to max dose or till HbA1c target
4. CombineAdd to metformin; avoid triple SU + insulin + DPP-4i (hypo risk)

Hypoglycaemia Prevention

Risk FactorMitigation
Elderly (>75)Use gliclazide MR; start 30mg; target HbA1c 58-64; avoid glimepiride/glibenclamide
CKD (eGFR<45)Gliclazide MR or glipizide only; dose reduce; monitor glucose
Erratic mealsEducate on regular meals; consider DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA instead
AlcoholWarn on fasting alcohol + SU = severe hypo
β-blockersMask autonomic symptoms; educate on neuroglycopenic signs

Switching/Deintensification

ScenarioAction
Recurrent hypoglycaemia↓Dose or stop SU; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Starting insulinSTOP SU (↓hypo risk); continue metformin ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Frailty/elderlyDeintensify: stop SU, relax HbA1c target, simplify regimens
⬇ Clinical Pathway
Sulfonylurea Indicated
↓
Agent Selection
↓
Gliclazide MR 30mg OD
Glipizide 2.5mg BD
Glimepiride 1mg OD - caution elderly/CKD
Glibenclamide - prolonged hypo
↓
Titrate q2-4wk to max
↓
Recurrent hypo?
Starting insulin?
↓
↓Dose or STOP; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Continue + monitor HbA1c 6-monthly
STOP SU; continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismBind SUR1 → close KATP channel → depolarisation → Ca²⁺ influx → insulin secretion (glucose-independent at high doses)
Preferred agentGliclazide MR: once daily, CKD safe, ↓hypo vs glimepiride, antioxidant
AVOIDGlibenclamide (prolonged active metabolites → severe/prolonged hypo in elderly/CKD)
Hypoglycaemia riskElderly, CKD, erratic meals, alcohol, β-blockers, high dose, glimepiride/glibenclamide
Weight gain+2-4kg (insulin anabolic effect)
CAROLINA trialGlimepiride ≈ linagliptin for MACE (non-inferior) but 3.8x more severe hypo; weight gain vs loss
Renal dosingGliclazide MR/glipizide safe; glimepiride caution; glibenclamide CONTRAINDICATED
With insulinSTOP SU when insulin started (↓hypo risk); continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
DeintensificationStop SU if recurrent hypo, elderly/frail; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of sulfonylureas?Bind SUR1 subunit of KATP channel on pancreatic β-cells → channel closure → membrane depolarisation → voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channel opening → Ca²⁺ influx → insulin exocytosis
Which sulfonylurea is preferred in CKD/elderly?Gliclazide MR — no active metabolites, once daily, ↓hypoglycaemia risk vs glimepiride, antioxidant properties
Why is glibenclamide avoided in elderly/CKD?Prolonged active metabolites excreted renally → accumulation → severe/prolonged hypoglycaemia; higher CV mortality in some studies
What did the CAROLINA trial show?Glimepiride non-inferior to linagliptin for MACE (HR 1.03) but severe hypoglycaemia 3.8x higher (2.3% vs 0.6%); weight gain +1.2kg vs -1.0kg
How do you manage sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycaemia?Acute: 15-20g fast carbs; chronic: ↓dose or stop SU; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; educate on meal regularity, alcohol avoidance
What happens when you start insulin on a sulfonylurea?STOP the sulfonylurea — combination ↑hypoglycaemia risk; continue metformin ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Difference between gliclazide MR and standard gliclazide?MR: modified release, once daily (30-120mg), smoother profile, ↓hypo; standard: BD dosing (40-320mg BD)
Can sulfonylureas be used in T1DM?NO — require functional β-cells; ineffective in absolute insulin deficiency

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Sulfonylureas cause weight loss?NO — cause weight gain 2-4kg (insulin anabolic)
All sulfonylureas same hypo risk?NO — gliclazide MR < glipizide < glimepiride < glibenclamide (highest)
Sulfonylurea + DPP-4i combo?Possible but ↑hypo risk vs DPP-4i alone; prefer SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA as add-on
Sulfonylurea + GLP-1 RA?Possible but ↑hypo risk; GLP-1 RA preferred over SU as 2nd line after metformin

Mnemonic: SU-GLIC-MR

  • Secretagogue: close KATP (SUR1) → insulin release
  • Up weight: +2-4kg
  • Gliclazide MR: 1st choice (CKD safe, once daily, ↓hypo)
  • Low dose start: Glic MR 30mg, Glime 1mg
  • Insulin combo: STOP SU when insulin started
  • CAROLINA: Glimepiride = Lina MACE but 3.8x severe hypo
  • MR preferred: once daily, smoother profile
  • Renal: Glic MR/Glipizide safe; Glime caution; Glibenclamide AVOID

11. Mind Map

Sulfonylureas
Mechanism
SUR1 binding
KATP closure
Ca2+ influx
Insulin secretion
Agents
Gliclazide MR (1st choice)
Gliclazide
Glimepiride
Glipizide
Glibenclamide (AVOID)
Effects
HbA1c ↓1-1.5%
Weight ↑2-4kg
Hypoglycaemia risk
CAROLINA
Glimepiride vs Linagliptin
MACE non-inferior
Hypo 3.8x higher
Renal
Glic MR safe
Glipizide safe
Glimepiride caution
Glibenclamide CONTRAINDICATED
With Insulin
STOP SU
Continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionSulfonylureas: gliclazide MR, glimepiride, glipizide, glibenclamide — KATP channel blockers
**Key Test"SMBG for hypoglycaemia; HbA1c 6-monthly; eGFR before initiation
**Classification"1st gen: tolbutamide; 2nd gen: gliclazide, glipizide, glibenclamide; 3rd gen: glimepiride
**Acute Mgmt"Hypoglycaemia: 15-20g carbs; severe: glucagon/IV dextrose
**Chronic Mgmt"Glic MR 30mg OD → titrate; stop if hypo; STOP when insulin started
**Key Score"CAROLINA: MACE non-inferior but 3.8x severe hypo vs DPP-4i
**Complications"Hypoglycaemia (elderly, CKD, erratic meals), weight gain
**Prognosis"Effective glucose lowering; hypoglycaemia limits use in high-risk

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours

90 days

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify sulfonylureas by generation and duration
  • [ ] Apply renal/hepatic dosing adjustments
  • [ ] Recognise hypoglycaemia risk factors and management
  • [ ] Apply CAROLINA trial implications (CV neutrality but ↑hypo vs DPP-4i)
  • [ ] Choose preferred agent in CKD/elderly (gliclazide MR)

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3)", "GCK-MODY (MODY 2)", "HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1)", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Other MODY subtypes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise less common MODY subtypes (HNF1B, PDX1, NEUROD1, KCNJ11, BLK, APPL1)
  • [ ] Identify distinguishing clinical features
  • [ ] Apply appropriate management per subtype

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Collective prevalence~5-10% of MODY
Key subtypesHNF1B (MODY5), PDX1 (MODY4), NEUROD1 (MODY6), KCNJ11 (MODY13), BLK (MODY11), APPL1 (MODY14)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)

FeatureDetail
GeneHNF1B (17cen-q21.3)
Key featuresRenal cysts, renal dysplasia, GU malformations, hyperuricaemia
DiabetesVariable; often insulin-requiring
Extra-pancreaticLiver dysfunction, genital tract malformations, hypomagnesaemia
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptideVariable

NEUROD1-MODY (MODY6)

FeatureDetail
GeneNEUROD1 (2q32)
FeaturesPancreatic/brain development; neurological features possible
DiabetesYoung onset; insulin often required
NeurologicalLearning difficulties, cerebellar hypoplasia

PDX1-MODY (MODY4)

FeatureDetail
GenePDX1 (13q12.1)
FeaturesPancreatic duodenal homeobox 1; pancreatic agenesis if homozygous
DiabetesVariable; may need insulin

KCNJ11-MODY (MODY13)

FeatureDetail
GeneKCNJ11 (11p15.1) - Kir6.2 subunit
FeaturesOverlap with neonatal diabetes
SulfonylureaResponsive

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestIndication
Genetic panelAll MODY genes if probability >6%
Renal ultrasoundHNF1B-MODY (renal cysts)
MagnesiumHNF1B-MODY (hypomagnesaemia)
Liver functionHNF1B-MODY (liver dysfunction)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HNF1A-MODYLow CRP, excellent SU response
GCK-MODYMild fasting hyperglycaemia, no treatment
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide
T2DMObese, older, insulin resistance

7. Management

HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)

IssueManagement
DiabetesOften insulin-requiring; SU variable
RenalNephrology follow-up; monitor eGFR, magnesium
GUUrology review; prenatal counselling
MagnesiumSupplement if hypomagnesaemia

NEUROD1/PDX1

AspectManagement
DiabetesOften insulin-requiring
NeurologicalPaediatric neurology follow-up (NEUROD1)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HNF1B-MODYRenal cysts, GU malformations, hypomagnesaemia; insulin often needed
NEUROD1Neurological features; insulin often needed
KCNJ11Sulfonylurea responsive
Rare MODYPDX1, NEUROD1, BLK, APPL1 - insulin often needed

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What distinguishes HNF1B-MODY?Renal cysts, GU malformations, hypomagnesaemia; insulin often needed
What is the key feature of NEUROD1-MODY?Neurological involvement (learning difficulties, cerebellar hypoplasia)
How do you manage HNF1B-MODY diabetes?Often insulin-requiring; monitor renal function, magnesium

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All MODY = sulfonylurea responsive?NO - HNF1B/PDX1/NEUROD1 often insulin-requiring
HNF1B = renal only?No - GU malformations, liver, magnesium, pancreas

Mnemonic: OTHER-MODY-SUBTYPES

  • Other MODY: 5-10%
  • Types: HNF1B(5), PDX1(4), NEUROD1(6), KCNJ11(13), BLK(11), APPL1(14)
  • HNF1B: Renal cysts, GU, hypoMg, insulin
  • ENUROD1: Neuro + diabetes, insulin
  • Rare: PDX1, BLK, APPL1
  • MODY5 = HNF1B = renal/GU
  • Overlap: KCNJ11 = neonatal DM spectrum
  • Diabetes: insulin often needed
  • Subtypes: each distinct
  • Unique features per gene
  • Beyond HNF1A/GCK/HNF4A
  • You need genetic panel
  • Test if probability >6%
  • You refer: nephrology, urology, genetics
  • Prenatal: if familial
  • Exome if panel negative
  • Sequencing: confirm VUS**

Topic Overview

Other MODY subtypes

HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)
NEUROD1-MODY (MODY6)
PDX1-MODY (MODY4)
KCNJ11-MODY (MODY13)

see_also: ["HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3)", "GCK-MODY (MODY 2)", "HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1)", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Other MODY subtypes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise less common MODY subtypes (HNF1B, PDX1, NEUROD1, KCNJ11, BLK, APPL1)
  • [ ] Identify distinguishing clinical features
  • [ ] Apply appropriate management per subtype

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Collective prevalence~5-10% of MODY
Key subtypesHNF1B (MODY5), PDX1 (MODY4), NEUROD1 (MODY6), KCNJ11 (MODY13), BLK (MODY11), APPL1 (MODY14)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)

FeatureDetail
GeneHNF1B (17cen-q21.3)
Key featuresRenal cysts, renal dysplasia, GU malformations, hyperuricaemia
DiabetesVariable; often insulin-requiring
Extra-pancreaticLiver dysfunction, genital tract malformations, hypomagnesaemia
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptideVariable

NEUROD1-MODY (MODY6)

FeatureDetail
GeneNEUROD1 (2q32)
FeaturesPancreatic/brain development; neurological features possible
DiabetesYoung onset; insulin often required
NeurologicalLearning difficulties, cerebellar hypoplasia

PDX1-MODY (MODY4)

FeatureDetail
GenePDX1 (13q12.1)
FeaturesPancreatic duodenal homeobox 1; pancreatic agenesis if homozygous
DiabetesVariable; may need insulin

KCNJ11-MODY (MODY13)

FeatureDetail
GeneKCNJ11 (11p15.1) - Kir6.2 subunit
FeaturesOverlap with neonatal diabetes
SulfonylureaResponsive

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestIndication
Genetic panelAll MODY genes if probability >6%
Renal ultrasoundHNF1B-MODY (renal cysts)
MagnesiumHNF1B-MODY (hypomagnesaemia)
Liver functionHNF1B-MODY (liver dysfunction)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HNF1A-MODYLow CRP, excellent SU response
GCK-MODYMild fasting hyperglycaemia, no treatment
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide
T2DMObese, older, insulin resistance

7. Management

HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)

IssueManagement
DiabetesOften insulin-requiring; SU variable
RenalNephrology follow-up; monitor eGFR, magnesium
GUUrology review; prenatal counselling
MagnesiumSupplement if hypomagnesaemia

NEUROD1/PDX1

AspectManagement
DiabetesOften insulin-requiring
NeurologicalPaediatric neurology follow-up (NEUROD1)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HNF1B-MODYRenal cysts, GU malformations, hypomagnesaemia; insulin often needed
NEUROD1Neurological features; insulin often needed
KCNJ11Sulfonylurea responsive
Rare MODYPDX1, NEUROD1, BLK, APPL1 - insulin often needed

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What distinguishes HNF1B-MODY?Renal cysts, GU malformations, hypomagnesaemia; insulin often needed
What is the key feature of NEUROD1-MODY?Neurological involvement (learning difficulties, cerebellar hypoplasia)
How do you manage HNF1B-MODY diabetes?Often insulin-requiring; monitor renal function, magnesium

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All MODY = sulfonylurea responsive?NO - HNF1B/PDX1/NEUROD1 often insulin-requiring
HNF1B = renal only?No - GU malformations, liver, magnesium, pancreas

Mnemonic: OTHER-MODY-SUBTYPES

  • Other MODY: 5-10%
  • Types: HNF1B(5), PDX1(4), NEUROD1(6), KCNJ11(13), BLK(11), APPL1(14)
  • HNF1B: Renal cysts, GU, hypoMg, insulin
  • ENUROD1: Neuro + diabetes, insulin
  • Rare: PDX1, BLK, APPL1
  • MODY5 = HNF1B = renal/GU
  • Overlap: KCNJ11 = neonatal DM spectrum
  • Diabetes: insulin often needed
  • Subtypes: each distinct
  • Unique features per gene
  • Beyond HNF1A/GCK/HNF4A
  • You need genetic panel
  • Test if probability >6%
  • You refer: nephrology, urology, genetics
  • Prenatal: if familial
  • Exome if panel negative
  • Sequencing: confirm VUS**

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Sulfonylurea responsiveness

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain mechanism of sulfonylurea responsiveness in KCNJ11/ABCC8 neonatal diabetes
  • [ ] Apply switch protocol from insulin to sulfonylurea
  • [ ] Monitor for dose adjustments and adverse effects

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
MechanismKATP channel mutation prevents closure -> sulfonylurea binds SUR1 -> closes channel -> insulin secretion
Responsiveness~90% of KCNJ11/ABCC8 PNDM respond to sulfonylureas
DoseHigher than MODY (0.5-3 mg/kg/day)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
MechanismKATP channel mutation prevents closure -> sulfonylurea binds SUR1 -> closes channel -> insulin secretion
Responsiveness~90% of KCNJ11/ABCC8 PNDM respond to sulfonylureas
DoseHigher than MODY (0.5-3 mg/kg/day)

3. Classification / Staging / Grading

Sulfonylurea Response by Gene

GeneResponsivenessTypical Dose
KCNJ1190-95%0.5-3 mg/kg/day
ABCC885-90%0.5-3 mg/kg/day
INS0%Insulin only
GCK0%No treatment needed

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Switch Protocol

StepAction
1Confirm KCNJ11/ABCC8 mutation
2Start gliclazide MR 0.5 mg/kg/day (max 30mg if <10kg)
3Reduce insulin by 50% day 1; stop insulin by day 3-5 if glucose controlled
4Titrate SU weekly by 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day to max 3 mg/kg/day
5Monitor glucose 4-6x/day for first 2 weeks

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Switch Protocol

StepAction
1Confirm KCNJ11/ABCC8 mutation
2Start gliclazide MR 0.5 mg/kg/day (max 30mg if <10kg)
3Reduce insulin by 50% day 1; stop insulin by day 3-5 if glucose controlled
4Titrate SU weekly by 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day to max 3 mg/kg/day
5Monitor glucose 4-6x/day for first 2 weeks

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestPurpose
Genetic confirmationKCNJ11/ABCC8 activating mutation
Glucose monitoring4-6x/day during switch
HbA1c3-monthly after stable

6. Differential Diagnosis

GeneSU Response
KCNJ1195%
ABCC890%
INS0%
GCK0%

7. Management

Dose Titration

WeekDose (mg/kg/day)Monitoring
10.54-6x/day glucose; reduce insulin 50%
21.0Stop insulin if glucose <10 mmol/L
41.5-2.0Monitor for hypoglycaemia
8Up to 3.0Max dose; monitor neuro development

Long-term Follow-up

ParameterFrequency
HbA1c3-monthly
Growth6-monthly (height, weight)
NeurologicalAnnual (DEND/iDEND)
Dose adjustmentAs needed with growth

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Response rateKCNJ11 95%, ABCC8 90%
MechanismSU binds SUR1 -> closes KATP -> insulin release
DoseHigher than MODY (0.5-3 mg/kg/day)
SwitchGliclazide MR 0.5 -> 3 mg/kg/day over weeks
DEND/iDENDHigher doses needed; neuro monitoring

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the sulfonylurea response rate in KCNJ11 PNDM?95%
What is the mechanism of sulfonylurea in KCNJ11 diabetes?SU binds SUR1 -> closes KATP channel -> insulin secretion
What dose of sulfonylurea for neonatal diabetes?0.5-3 mg/kg/day gliclazide MR (higher than MODY)
What is the switch protocol?Start 0.5 mg/kg/day gliclazide MR; reduce insulin 50% day 1; stop by day 3-5

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Same dose as HNF1A-MODY?NO - neonatal DM needs higher doses (0.5-3 mg/kg vs 30-120mg fixed)
All neonatal DM = SU responsive?NO - only KCNJ11/ABCC8; INS/GCK = no
Sulfonylurea = cure?Life-long treatment; dose increases with age

Mnemonic: SU-NEONATAL-RESPONSE

  • Sulfonylurea: closes KATP -> insulin
  • Unique: KCNJ11 95%, ABCC8 90%
  • Response: 90% overall
  • Excellent: switch from insulin
  • Switch protocol: 0.5 -> 3 mg/kg over weeks
  • Protocol: reduce insulin 50% day 1
  • Once daily: gliclazide MR
  • Neonatal: higher dose than MODY
  • Sulfonylurea: bind SUR1 -> close KATP
  • Efficacy: 90-95%
  • Rate increase: weekly
  • Patient: monitor 4-6x/day glucose
  • Over time: dose increases with growth
  • Sustain: life-long treatment
  • Expert: neurological monitoring for DEND/iDEND
  • Alternative: insulin if SU fails
  • Titration: weekly increments
  • Infant: tolbutamide if <10kg
  • Variable: DEND needs higher dose
  • End: life-long; no cure**

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Transient vs permanent neonatal diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate transient (TNDM) vs permanent (PNDM) neonatal diabetes
  • [ ] Recognise 6q24 imprinting defect in TNDM
  • [ ] Apply management differences
  • [ ] Counsel on relapse risk

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureTransient (TNDM)Permanent (PNDM)
DefinitionRemission by 18 monthsLifelong
Proportion~50% of neonatal DM~50% of neonatal DM
Genetics6q24 imprinting (70%); ZFP57, HYMAIKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A
RemissionMedian 12 weeks (range 2w-18mo)Never
RelapseAdolescence/early adulthood (50%)N/A

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureTNDMPNDM
Age at dx<6 months<6 months
RemissionBy 18 monthsNever
RelapseAdolescence/young adulthoodNever
Genetics6q24 (70%); ZFP57, HYMAIKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A
Birth weightLow (IUGR)Low (IUGR)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureTNDMPNDM
Age at dx<6 months<6 months
RemissionBy 18 monthsNever
RelapseAdolescence/young adulthoodNever
Genetics6q24 (70%); ZFP57, HYMAIKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A
Birth weightLow (IUGR)Low (IUGR)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

TNDM Genetic Subtypes

SubtypeGene/RegionMechanismProportion
TNDM16q24 (PLAGL1/HYMAI)Imprinting defect (paternal UPD/duplication)70%
TNDM26q24 (ZFP57)ZFP57 mutation -> imprinting lossRare
TNDM3KCNJ11/ABCC8Activating mutations (milder)10-20%

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureTNDMPNDM
Age at dx<6 months<6 months
RemissionBy 18 monthsNever
RelapseAdolescence/young adulthoodNever
Genetics6q24 (70%); ZFP57, HYMAIKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A
Birth weightLow (IUGR)Low (IUGR)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestTNDMPNDM
Genetic testing6q24 methylation analysisKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A, EIF2AK3
Methylation assayHypomethylation at 6q24Normal
C-peptideLow at dx; recovers at remissionPersistently low

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
PNDMNever remits; KCNJ11/ABCC8/INS
SyndromicWolcott-Rallison (EIF2AK3), IPEX (FOXP3), Wolfram

7. Management

TNDM

PhaseManagement
AcuteInsulin (usually low dose; ~0.3 U/kg/day)
RemissionStop insulin; monitor glucose weekly x 3mo
SurveillanceAnnual OGTT/HbA1c from age 10; relapse risk 50%

PNDM

GeneManagement
KCNJ11/ABCC8Sulfonylurea 90% switch
INS/GCK/PDX1/PTF1AInsulin lifelong
EIF2AK3 (Wolcott-Rallison)Insulin; multi-system management

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
TNDM6q24 imprinting (70%); remission by 18mo; relapse 50% in adulthood
PNDMKCNJ11/ABCC8 (90% SU switch); INS/GCK/PDX1 = insulin
6q24Paternal UPD/duplication; HYMAI/PLAGL1 overexpression
RelapseTNDM relapse ~50% in adolescence/young adulthood
Birth weightLow in both (IUGR)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the main genetic cause of TNDM?6q24 imprinting defect (70%) - paternal UPD or duplication at 6q24
What is the remission age for TNDM?Median 12 weeks; by 18 months
What is the relapse rate for TNDM?~50% relapse in adolescence/young adulthood
How does TNDM differ from PNDM?TNDM remits by 18mo (6q24); PNDM lifelong (KCNJ11/ABCC8/INS)
What is the 6q24 mechanism?Paternal UPD or duplication -> HYMAI/PLAGL1 overexpression -> ↓insulin

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All TNDM = 6q24?70%; KCNJ11/ABCC8 also cause TNDM
Relapse = new diabetes?Same genetic defect; beta-cell failure recurs
TNDM = always transient?50% relapse later; not "cured"

Mnemonic: TNDM-TRANSIENT

  • TNDM: 6q24 imprinting (70%)
  • Neonatal diabetes: <6 months
  • Defect: paternal UPD/duplication at 6q24
  • Methylation: hypomethylation at 6q24
  • Transient: remission by 18 months
  • Remission: median 12 weeks
  • Adolescence relapse: 50%
  • Neonatal: <6 months
  • Syndromic: ZFP57 (TNDM2)
  • Interval: remission by 18mo
  • Expect relapse: 50% in adulthood
  • Next: PNDM if no remission
  • Test: 6q24 methylation assay
  • Temporary: not cured, just remission

What is the relapse rate for TNDM?~50% relapse in adolescence/young adulthood
How does TNDM differ from PNDM?TNDM remits by 18mo (6q24); PNDM lifelong (KCNJ11/ABCC8/INS)
What is the 6q24 mechanism?Paternal UPD or duplication -> HYMAI/PLAGL1 overexpression -> ↓insulin

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All TNDM = 6q24?70%; KCNJ11/ABCC8 also cause TNDM
Relapse = new diabetes?Same genetic defect; beta-cell failure recurs
TNDM = always transient?50% relapse later; not "cured"

Mnemonic: TNDM-TRANSIENT

  • TNDM: 6q24 imprinting (70%)
  • Neonatal diabetes: <6 months
  • Defect: paternal UPD/duplication at 6q24
  • Methylation: hypomethylation at 6q24
  • Transient: remission by 18 months
  • Remission: median 12 weeks
  • Adolescence relapse: 50%
  • Neonatal: <6 months
  • Syndromic: ZFP57 (TNDM2)
  • Interval: remission by 18mo
  • Expect relapse: 50% in adulthood
  • Next: PNDM if no remission
  • Test: 6q24 methylation assay
  • Temporary: not cured, just remission

topics.html:
tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, wolfram, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Mitochondrial diabetes", "MODY", "Lipodystrophy syndromes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Wolfram syndrome

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise Wolfram syndrome (DIDMOAD)
  • [ ] Identify WFS1 gene and inheritance
  • [ ] Apply multidisciplinary management
  • [ ] Counsel on prognosis and genetic counselling

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
AcronymDIDMOAD: Diabetes Insipidus, Diabetes Mellitus, Optic Atrophy, Deafness
GeneWFS1 (wolframin) - 4p16.1; also WFS2 (CISD2)
InheritanceAutosomal recessive
Prevalence1:500,000 - 1:1,000,000

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
Diabetes MellitusOnset 4-10y; insulin-requiring; non-autoimmune
Diabetes InsipidusCentral (pituitary); polyuria/polydipsia; onset ~14y
Optic AtrophyProgressive; onset ~11y; colour vision loss; legal blindness by adulthood
DeafnessSensorineural; high-frequency; onset ~13y
NeurologicalAtaxia, peripheral neuropathy, autonomic dysfunction, psychiatric
UrinaryNeurogenic bladder; recurrent UTIs
EndocrineHypogonadism, growth hormone deficiency, hypothyroidism

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

WFS1 vs WFS2

GeneFeatures
WFS1 (Wolframin)Classic DIDMOAD; ER calcium handling
WFS2 (CISD2)Similar; also peptic ulcer disease, bleeding diathesis

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Progression Timeline

AgeOnset
4-10yDiabetes Mellitus
~11yOptic Atrophy
~13yDeafness
~14yDiabetes Insipidus
AdolescenceNeurological decline

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingWFS1 mutation (biallelic); WFS2 (CISD2)
OphthalmologyOptic atrophy (temporal pallor)
AudiometrySensorineural deafness
Water deprivation testCentral DI
MRI brainCerebellar/brainstem atrophy (late)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingWFS1 mutation (biallelic); WFS2 (CISD2)
OphthalmologyOptic atrophy (temporal pallor)
AudiometrySensorineural deafness
Water deprivation testCentral DI
MRI brainCerebellar/brainstem atrophy (late)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
MIDD (m.3243A>G)Maternal inheritance; diabetes + deafness; no DI/OA
MODYAutosomal dominant; no DI/OA/deafness
Isolated DINo DM/OA/deafness
Alström syndromeALMS1; obesity, cardiomyopathy, hepatic steatosis

7. Management

SystemManagement
Diabetes MellitusInsulin (basal-bolus); tight control slows complications
Diabetes InsipidusDesmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal; monitor electrolytes
Optic AtrophyLow vision aids; regular ophthalmology
DeafnessHearing aids; cochlear implant if severe
NeurologicalPhysiotherapy; orthotics for ataxia
HypogonadismHormone replacement

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
MIDD (m.3243A>G)Maternal inheritance; no DI/OA
MODYAutosomal dominant; no DI/OA/deafness
Alström syndromeALMS1; obesity, cardiomyopathy, hepatic steatosis, no DI
Isolated DI + DMNo optic atrophy/deafness

7. Management

SystemManagement
Diabetes MellitusInsulin (basal-bolus); tight control slows complications
Diabetes InsipidusDesmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal; monitor electrolytes
Optic AtrophyLow vision aids; regular ophthalmology
DeafnessHearing aids; cochlear implant if severe
NeurologicalPhysiotherapy; orthotics for ataxia
HypogonadismHormone replacement

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
DIDMOADDiabetes Insipidus, Diabetes Mellitus, Optic Atrophy, Deafness
GeneWFS1 (autosomal recessive); WFS2/CISD2
ProgressionDM (4-10y) -> OA (11y) -> Deafness (13y) -> DI (14y) -> Neuro
Life expectancyReduced (median ~30y); respiratory failure, neurogenic bladder
InheritanceAutosomal recessive (WFS1, CISD2)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What does DIDMOAD stand for?Diabetes Insipidus, Diabetes Mellitus, Optic Atrophy, Deafness
What is the inheritance of Wolfram syndrome?Autosomal recessive (WFS1/WFS2)
What is the typical progression of Wolfram syndrome?DM -> Optic Atrophy -> Deafness -> DI -> Neurological decline
How do you manage diabetes insipidus in Wolfram?Desmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal; monitor sodium
What is the inheritance pattern?Autosomal recessive (WFS1/CISD2)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Wolfram = MIDD?NO - Wolfram = AR, DIDMOAD; MIDD = maternal, no DI/OA
All DI + DM = Wolfram?NO - need optic atrophy + deafness
WFS1 = WFS2?WFS1 = classic; WFS2 = also peptic ulcer, bleeding

Mnemonic: WOLFRAM-DIDMOAD

  • Wolfram: WFS1 gene (4p16)
  • Optic atrophy: ~11y; progressive blindness
  • Life expectancy: ~30y (median)
  • Features: DIDMOAD
  • Recessive: autosomal recessive
  • Autosomal recessive
  • Mellitus: DM 4-10y
  • Insipidus: central DI ~14y
  • Deafness: sensorineural ~13y
  • Optic atrophy: ~11y
  • Median survival: ~30y
  • Diabetes: DM + DI
  • Insulin: required for DM
  • Desmopressin: for DI
  • Mortality: respiratory failure, neurogenic bladder
  • Ophthalmology: annual optic nerve assessment
  • Ataxia: cerebellar atrophy late
  • Diabetes: both mellitus and insipidus**

topics.title: Hnf1A Mody Mody 3
topics.html:
1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe classic symptomatic presentation of T2DM
  • [ ] Differentiate from T1DM, LADA, stress hyperglycaemia
  • [ ] Recognise hyperglycaemic emergencies (DKA, HHS) at presentation
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria in symptomatic patients

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PresentationInsidious onset (months-years); often asymptomatic at diagnosis
Asymptomatic at Dx~50% detected on screening/incidental finding
SymptomaticOsmotic symptoms, weight loss, infections, fatigue
Age at diagnosis>40 years (increasing in younger due to obesity)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

SymptomFrequencyMechanism
PolyuriaCommon if marked hyperglycaemiaOsmotic diuresis
PolydipsiaCommon if marked hyperglycaemiaDehydration from osmotic diuresis
Weight lossVariableCatabolism if significant insulin deficiency
Fatigue/lethargyCommonImpaired glucose utilisation
Blurred vision20-30%Osmotic lens swelling
Recurrent infectionsCommonCandidiasis, UTIs, skin infections
Acanthosis nigricans30-50%Insulin resistance marker
Tingling/numbnessIf neuropathy presentPeripheral neuropathy at diagnosis
Red Flags: Ketosis at presentation -> T1DM/LADA/ketosis-prone T2DM; rapid weight loss + osmotic symptoms in adult -> LADA; new-onset DM >50y + weight loss -> pancreatic cancer.

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureT2DM ClassicT1DMLADA
OnsetInsidious (months-years)Acute (days-weeks)Subacute
Age>40y (usually)Peak 10-14y>30y
BMIOverweight/obeseNormal/lowNormal/low
KetosisRare (unless ketosis-prone)Common (25-30% DKA)Uncommon
AutoantibodiesNegative2+ (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)GAD65+ (often single)
C-peptideNormal/highLow/absentLow-normal

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationT2DMT1DMLADA
AutoantibodiesNegative2+ positiveGAD65+ (often single)
C-peptideNormal/highLow/absentLow-normal
HbA1cVariable>48 usuallyVariable
KetonesNegative (usually)Positive if DKAUsually negative

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAcute, lean, DKA common, 2+ autoantibodies
LADAAdult, slow, GAD65+, initial oral response
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness, resolves, no prior dysglycaemia
MODY<25y, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies
Pancreatic cancer>50y, weight loss, new-onset DM, no autoantibodies

7. Management

Acute (if DKA/HHS)

EmergencyProtocol
DKADKA protocol: fluids, FRII 0.1U/kg/hr, K+, monitor
HHSHHS protocol: fluids, lower insulin 0.05U/kg/hr, osmolality monitoring

Chronic (after stabilisation)

ComponentDetail
LifestyleMNT, 150min/week exercise, weight loss 5-10%
Metformin1st line unless contraindicated (eGFR<30)
Add-onPer ADA/EASD algorithm (SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for ASCVD/HF/CKD/obesity)
EducationSelf-monitoring, sick day rules, hypoglycaemia awareness
CGMConsider if on insulin, hypoglycaemia unawareness

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
OnsetInsidious (months-years); 50% asymptomatic at diagnosis
Classic symptomsPolyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, recurrent infections
Weight lossOccurs if significant insulin deficiency/catabolism
Acanthosis nigricans30-50%; insulin resistance marker
Asymptomatic diagnosis~50% on screening/incidental
vs T1DMInsidious, obese, no ketosis, negative autoantibodies
vs LADAObese, negative autoantibodies, insulin resistance primary

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the typical presentation of T2DM?Insidious onset; often asymptomatic; if symptomatic: polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, recurrent infections
What percentage are asymptomatic at diagnosis?~50% detected on screening/incidental finding
How does T2DM differ from T1DM at presentation?Insidious vs acute; obese vs lean; no ketosis vs DKA common; negative vs 2+ autoantibodies
What is acanthosis nigricans and its significance?Velvety hyperpigmentation (axilla, neck); marker of insulin resistance
Can T2DM present in DKA?Rare; ketosis-prone T2DM (Afro-Caribbean); usually HHS if emergency

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
T2DM always asymptomatic?NO - 50% present with symptoms if hyperglycaemia marked
Weight loss = T1DM?NO - can occur in uncontrolled T2DM with significant insulin deficiency

Mnemonic: T2DM-INSIDIOUS

  • T2DM: insidious onset months-years
  • 2 presentations: asymptomatic (50%) or symptomatic
  • Dysglycaemia: slow progression
  • Metabolic: insulin resistance + beta-cell failure
  • Insidious: no acute crisis usually
  • Diagnosis: often screening/incidental
  • Infections: recurrent (candidiasis, UTI)
  • Obesity: overweight/obese usual
  • Unrecognized: 50% asymptomatic
  • Screening: ADA age>=35 + 1 RF

11. Mind Map

T2DM Symptomatic Presentation
Asymptomatic
50% at diagnosis
Screening/incidental
Symptoms
Polyuria, polydipsia
Fatigue
Recurrent infections
Weight loss (if severe)
Blurred vision
Signs
Acanthosis nigricans (30-50%)
Peripheral neuropathy
vs T1DM
Insidious vs acute
Obese vs lean
Negative Abs vs 2+
vs LADA
Obese vs normal BMI
Negative Abs vs GAD65+
IR vs autoimmunity

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionInsidious onset T2DM; 50% asymptomatic; osmotic symptoms if hyperglycaemic
**Key Test"HbA1c/FPG (screening); autoantibodies (negative)
**Classification"Insidious onset; obese; negative autoantibodies
**Acute Mgmt"HHS if severe; then lifestyle + metformin
**Chronic Mgmt"ADA/EASD algorithm: Met -> SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA per comorbidities
**Key Score"ADA screening age>=35/BMI>=25+1RF
**Complications"Early micro/macrovascular if undiagnosed
**Prognosis"Progressive; early intervention preserves beta-cell function

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise HNF4A-MODY clinical features (macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia)
  • [ ] Explain sulfonylurea responsiveness
  • [ ] Differentiate from HNF1A-MODY and other MODY types

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GeneHNF4A (hepatocyte nuclear factor 4-alpha)
Chromosome20q13.1
Prevalence~5-10% of MODY
InheritanceAutosomal dominant

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
OnsetChildhood/adolescence
Birth weightMacrosomia (>4kg)
NeonatalTransient neonatal hypoglycaemia (due to hyperinsulinaemia)
ProgressionProgressive beta-cell dysfunction (similar to HNF1A)
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved
LipidsLow HDL, raised ApoAII

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureHNF4A-MODYHNF1A-MODYGCK-MODY
BirthMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemiaNormalNormal
SU responseExcellentExcellentNone
LipidsLow HDL, high ApoAIILow HDLNormal
AutoantibodiesNegativeNegativeNegative

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingHNF4A mutation
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved
LipidsLow HDL, raised ApoAII

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HNF1A-MODYNo macrosomia/neonatal hypoglycaemia; low CRP; low renal threshold
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide
T2DMObese, older, insulin resistance
Beckwith-WiedemannMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, macrosomia, hemihypertrophy

7. Management

AspectDetail
SulfonylureaExcellent response (gliclazide MR 1st line)
MonitoringHbA1c 6-monthly; lipids annual
Genetic counsellingAutosomal dominant; 50% offspring risk

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Key featuresMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, SU responsive
LipidsLow HDL, raised ApoAII
SulfonylureaExcellent response (like HNF1A)
GeneticsHNF4A (20q13); autosomal dominant

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What distinguishes HNF4A-MODY from HNF1A-MODY?Macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, low HDL, raised ApoAII
What is the sulfonylurea response in HNF4A-MODY?Excellent (similar to HNF1A)
What is the inheritance pattern?Autosomal dominant (HNF4A, 20q13)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HNF4A = HNF1A?Both SU responsive but HNF4A has macrosomia/neonatal hypoglycaemia
All MODY need insulin?NO - HNF1A/4A = SU; GCK = none; HNF1B = insulin

Mnemonic: HNF4A-MODY-MACRO

  • HNF4A: hepatocyte nuclear factor 4-alpha
  • Neonatal hypoglycaemia (transient)
  • Fetal macrosomia (>4kg)
  • 4 (MODY 1)
  • Autosomal dominant (20q13)
  • Macrosomia + neonatal hypoglycaemia
  • Organic: low HDL, raised ApoAII
  • Diagnosis: macrosomia + neonatal hypo + SU responsive
  • You: SU excellent response
  • Macrosomia = key differentiator from HNF1A
  • Organic lipids: low HDL
  • DNA: HNF4A gene (20q13)

Genetic testingHNF4A mutation
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved
LipidsLow HDL, raised ApoAII

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HNF1A-MODYNo macrosomia/neonatal hypoglycaemia; low CRP; low renal threshold
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide
T2DMObese, older, insulin resistance
Beckwith-WiedemannMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, macrosomia, hemihypertrophy

7. Management

AspectDetail
SulfonylureaExcellent response (gliclazide MR 1st line)
MonitoringHbA1c 6-monthly; lipids annual
Genetic counsellingAutosomal dominant; 50% offspring risk

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Key featuresMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, SU responsive
LipidsLow HDL, raised ApoAII
SulfonylureaExcellent response (like HNF1A)
GeneticsHNF4A (20q13); autosomal dominant

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What distinguishes HNF4A-MODY from HNF1A-MODY?Macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, low HDL, raised ApoAII
What is the sulfonylurea response in HNF4A-MODY?Excellent (similar to HNF1A)
What is the inheritance pattern?Autosomal dominant (HNF4A, 20q13)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HNF4A = HNF1A?Both SU responsive but HNF4A has macrosomia/neonatal hypoglycaemia
All MODY need insulin?NO - HNF1A/4A = SU; GCK = none; HNF1B = insulin

Mnemonic: HNF4A-MODY-MACRO

  • HNF4A: hepatocyte nuclear factor 4-alpha
  • Neonatal hypoglycaemia (transient)
  • Fetal macrosomia (>4kg)
  • 4 (MODY 1)
  • Autosomal dominant (20q13)
  • Macrosomia + neonatal hypoglycaemia
  • Organic: low HDL, raised ApoAII
  • Diagnosis: macrosomia + neonatal hypo + SU responsive
  • You: SU excellent response
  • Macrosomia = key differentiator from HNF1A
  • Organic lipids: low HDL
  • DNA: HNF4A gene (20q13)

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

KCNJ11 and ABCC8 neonatal diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise neonatal diabetes (<6 months) due to KCNJ11/ABCC8
  • [ ] Explain sulfonylurea responsiveness mechanism
  • [ ] Apply genetic testing and management
  • [ ] Recognise DEND/iDEND syndromes

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionDiabetes diagnosed <6 months age
GeneticsActivating mutations in KCNJ11 (Kir6.2) or ABCC8 (SUR1)
Incidence~1:100,000-500,000 live births; 40-50% of neonatal DM
MechanismMutated KATP channel fails to close -> ↓insulin secretion

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
Age<6 months (often <1 month)
PresentationHyperglycaemia, dehydration, ketoacidosis
Birth weightOften low (reduced fetal insulin -> ↓growth)
NeurologicalDEND (developmental delay, epilepsy, neonatal diabetes)
GrowthFailure to thrive

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Neurological Spectrum

SyndromeFeaturesMutation Severity
Permanent Neonatal Diabetes (PNDM)Diabetes onlyMild
iDENDDiabetes + developmental delayModerate
DENDDevelopmental delay, epilepsy, neonatal diabetesSevere

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingKCNJ11 or ABCC8 activating mutation
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptideLow/undetectable
CT/MRI brainNormal or subtle changes (DEND)
EEGEpileptiform activity (DEND/iDEND)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
6q24 TNDMRemission by 18mo; imprinting defect
INS mutationNo SU response; insulin only
GCK-MODYMild fasting hyperglycaemia; no treatment
Transient neonatal DM6q24 imprinting; remission by 18mo

7. Management

Sulfonylurea Switch (90% Success)

AspectDetail
DrugGliclazide MR (tolbutamide if <10kg)
Starting dose0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day
TitrationIncrease weekly to max 2-3 mg/kg/day
MonitoringPre-prandial glucose; HbA1c 3-monthly; neuro assessment
Insulin taperGradual over 2-4 weeks as SU established

DEND/iDEND Specific

AspectManagement
EpilepsyAnticonvulsants (sodium channel blockers may help)
DevelopmentalEarly intervention; physiotherapy, speech therapy

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
GenesKCNJ11 (Kir6.2), ABCC8 (SUR1) - KATP channel subunits
PNDMDiabetes <6mo; no remission
TNDM6q24 imprinting; remission by 18mo, relapse later
Sulfonylurea90% switch successfully (KCNJ11/ABCC8)
DEND/iDENDDevelopmental delay, epilepsy; higher SU doses needed
MechanismMutated KATP stays open -> ↓insulin; SU closes channel

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What genes cause neonatal diabetes?KCNJ11 (Kir6.2), ABCC8 (SUR1) - KATP channel subunits
What is the sulfonylurea response rate?~90%
What is DEND syndrome?Developmental delay, Epilepsy, Neonatal Diabetes
How do you switch from insulin to sulfonylurea?Gradual taper over 2-4 weeks; SU gliclazide 0.5-1mg/kg/day

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All neonatal DM = KCNJ11/ABCC8?NO - 6q24 TNDM, INS, GCK, PDX1 also
All KCNJ11 = PNDM?Some TNDM also have KCNJ11 mutations
Sulfonylurea = cure?No - life-long treatment; dose may need increase with age

Mnemonic: NEONATAL-KCNJ11

  • Neonatal DM: <6 months
  • Early onset: <1 month common
  • One gene: KCNJ11 (Kir6.2) or ABCC8 (SUR1)
  • Neonatal: <6 months
  • ACTivating KATP mutation
  • Treatment: sulfonylurea 90%
  • A: ABCC8 (SUR1) or KCNJ11 (Kir6.2)
  • L: Low birth weight
  • KATP channel: stays open -> no insulin
  • Close mutations: severe = DEND
  • Neurological: DEND / iDEND
  • Juvenile: remission? no (PNDM)
  • 1 (KCNJ11) + 1 (ABCC8) = 2 genes
  • 1 in 100,000-500,000

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

see_also: ["Wolfram syndrome", "Mitochondrial diabetes", "Secondary Diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Lipodystrophy syndromes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify lipodystrophy syndromes (generalised vs partial)
  • [ ] Recognise genetic vs acquired forms
  • [ ] Apply management: metreleptin, insulin, metabolic control
  • [ ] Recognise associated complications

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionSelective loss of adipose tissue -> metabolic complications
ClassificationGeneralised (CGL) vs Partial (FPLD); Genetic vs Acquired
PrevalenceRare: CGL 1:10M; FPLD 1:1M
Metabolic consequenceSevere insulin resistance, diabetes, hypertriglyceridaemia, hepatic steatosis

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Congenital Generalised Lipodystrophy (CGL)

TypeGeneFeatures
CGL1AGPAT2Severe; early onset; acanthosis nigricans; hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
CGL2BSCL2Severe; intellectual disability; cardiomyopathy risk
CGL3CAV1Mild; vitamin D resistance
CGL4PTRF (CAVIN1)Myopathy; pyloric stenosis

Familial Partial Lipodystrophy (FPLD)

TypeGeneFeatures
FPLD1UnknownWomen; gluteofemoral fat loss
FPLD2 (Dunnigan)LMNAPuberty onset; limb/trunk fat loss; face/neck fat gain; high CVD risk
FPLD3PPARGEarly onset; severe insulin resistance
FPLD4PLIN1Adult onset; mild
FPLD5CIDECChildhood onset; severe

Acquired Lipodystrophy

TypeFeatures
Generalised (Lawrence)Autoimmune; post-infectious; panniculitis
Partial (Barraquer-Simons)Centripetal fat loss; complement C3 nephritic factor; MPGN
Drug-inducedProtease inhibitors (HIV); insulin injections (local)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Generalised vs Partial

FeatureGeneralised (CGL, Lawrence)Partial (FPLD, Barraquer-Simons)
Fat lossNear-totalPartial (limbs, trunk)
Metabolic severityExtremeVariable
OnsetInfancy/childhoodPuberty/adulthood
GeneticCGL1-4 (AGPAT2, BSCL2, CAV1, PTRF)FPLD1-6 (LMNA, PPARG, etc.)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestPurpose
Genetic testingAGPAT2, BSCL2, LMNA, PPARG, others
Body compositionDEXA, MRI (adipose tissue quantification)
MetabolicGlucose, HbA1c, lipids (TG >> cholesterol), insulin
LeptinLow (hallmark)
LiverUltrasound/MRI (steatosis); ALT/AST
HeartEcho (cardiomyopathy in CGL)
KidneyProteinuria (CGL2)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DM with obesityIncreased fat mass (vs loss)
Cushing syndromeCentral obesity + fat redistribution
Anorexia nervosaVoluntary weight loss; psychopathology
AgeingGradual fat redistribution

7. Management

Metabolic Management

TargetStrategy
DiabetesHigh-dose insulin (often >2 U/kg/day); U-500 insulin; CSII
HypertriglyceridaemiaFibrates; omega-3; avoid alcohol; strict glucose control
Leptin replacementMetreleptin (FDA approved for GL; off-label for PL)
Hepatic steatosisWeight loss (if partial); pioglitazone? (caution)

Metreleptin Therapy

AspectDetail
IndicationFDA approved for GL; off-label for PL with severe metabolic complications
Dose0.06 mg/kg/day SC (adjust by response)
Effects↓HbA1c 2-3%; ↓TG 50-60%; ↓hepatic fat; ↑satiety
MonitoringLeptin levels; HbA1c, TG, LFT; TSH (thyroid autoimmunity risk)
Side effectsAnti-leptin antibodies (neutralising); hypoglycaemia; T-cell lymphoma (theoretical)

Complication Management

ComplicationManagement
DiabetesHigh-dose insulin; CSII; metreleptin
HypertriglyceridaemiaFibrates + omega-3; avoid alcohol/estrogen
CardiomyopathyEcho surveillance; standard HF therapy
AutoimmunityScreen (CGL2); treat if present

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
LeptinLOW (hallmark); metreleptin replacement
CGLAGPAT2, BSCL2; severe IR; infancy onset
FPLD2LMNA; puberty onset; limb fat loss; high CVD
MetreleptinLeptin replacement; ↓HbA1c, ↓TG, ↓hepatic fat
ComplicationsSevere IR, diabetes, TG >20, hepatic steatosis, pancreatitis

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the hallmark of lipodystrophy?Low leptin; severe insulin resistance; hypertriglyceridaemia
How do you classify lipodystrophy?Generalised (CGL1-4) vs Partial (FPLD1-6); Genetic vs Acquired
What is metreleptin?Recombinant leptin; FDA approved for GL; ↓HbA1c, ↓TG, ↓hepatic fat
What is FPLD2 (Dunnigan)?LMNA mutation; puberty onset; limb fat loss; face/neck fat gain; high CVD
How do you manage diabetes in lipodystrophy?High-dose insulin (often >2 U/kg); CSII; metreleptin; U-500 insulin

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Lipodystrophy = anabolic?NO - CATABOLIC state; fat loss -> metabolic chaos
All lipodystrophy = genetic?NO - acquired forms exist (Lawrence, Barraquer-Simons)
Leptin = weight loss drug?In lipodystrophy = REPLACEMENT therapy for deficiency

Mnemonic: LIPO-DYSTROPHY-SYNDROMES

  • Lipodystrophy: fat loss -> metabolic chaos
  • Insulin resistance: severe
  • Partial (FPLD) vs Generalised (CGL)
  • Obesity: NO - fat LOSS
  • Diabetes: severe IR; high-dose insulin
  • Yleptin: LOW (hallmark); metreleptin replacement
  • Syndromes: CGL1-4, FPLD1-6, Acquired
  • Triglycerides: very high (>20 mmol/L)
  • Risk: pancreatitis, hepatic steatosis, CVD
  • Organomegaly: hepatomegaly (fatty liver)
  • Partial: FPLD2 (LMNA) - Dunnigan
  • Hypertriglyceridaemia: >20 mmol/L
  • Yleptin replacement: metreleptin (FDA for GL)
  • Extreme IR: >2 U/kg/day insulin
  • Syndromes: CGL1-4, FPLD1-6, Acquired
  • You monitor: leptin, TG, HbA1c, LFT
  • Nephropathy: CGL2 -> proteinuria
  • Dunnigan: LMNA, FPLD2
  • Receptor: none (leptin resistance not the issue)
  • Organ: liver (steatosis), heart (cardiomyopathy)
  • Metreleptin: ↓HbA1c 2-3%, ↓TG 50-60%
  • Echo: cardiomyopathy (CGL)
  • Severe: CGL1 (AGPAT2) - infantile**

Topic Overview

Lipodystrophy syndromes

3. Clinical Features / Presentation
Congenital Generalised Lipodystrophy (CGL)
Familial Partial Lipodystrophy (FPLD)
Acquired Lipodystrophy
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Generalised vs Partial
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Metabolic Management
Metreleptin Therapy

topics.title: Post Surgery Diabetes Management
topics.html:
[!info]
Post-bariatric glucose management: IV insulin → SC 50–70% dose reduction within days; rapid remission (incretin effect); dumping syndrome (early/late); nutritional deficiencies (B12, iron, Ca, D, thiamine); lifelong supplementation + monitoring; contraception 12–18m; pregnancy planning.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Manage glucose in immediate postoperative period (Days 0–30)
  • [ ] Recognise and treat dumping syndrome (early vs late)
  • [ ] Prevent and monitor nutritional deficiencies
  • [ ] Apply long-term follow-up schedule
  • [ ] Advise on contraception and pregnancy planning

2. Immediate Postoperative Glucose Management (Days 0–30)

Day 0–1 (ICU/HDU)

SituationManagement
NPO / IV fluidsIV insulin infusion (sliding scale) target 6–10 mmol/L
Regular insulin drip0.05–0.1u/kg/h; adjust q1–2h
Existing basal insulinSTOP — IV insulin replaces

Day 1–2 (Ward, Starting Oral Intake)

StepAction
1. Stop IV insulinWhen tolerating oral fluids/protein shakes
2. Start SC insulin50–70% of pre-op total daily dose (incretin effect + caloric restriction)
3. RegimenBasal (glargine/detemir/degludec) + minimal/rapid correction only
4. Stop oral glucose medsSulfonylurea, SGLT2i, DPP-4i, GLP-1 RA, TZD → all STOP
5. MetforminContinue if eGFR ≥30 (adjust for weight loss)

Day 3–7

Glucose PatternAction
Normoglycaemia (4–7)Stop ALL glucose meds (assess remission)
Mild hyperglycaemia (7–10)Low-dose basal only (e.g., 5–10u); recheck daily
Hypoglycaemia (<4)Reduce/stop insulin; carb snack; reassess dose

Key: Rapid remission common — 50–70% off all meds by discharge.

3. Dumping Syndrome — Early vs Late

FeatureEarly Dumping (15–30min post-meal)Late Dumping (1–3h post-meal)
MechanismRapid fluid shift → hypovolaemiaReactive hypoglycaemia (hyperinsulinaemia)
SymptomsNausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, cramps, flushing, tachycardia, dizziness, syncopeSweating, tremor, palpitations, hunger, confusion, neuroglycopenia
TriggerHyperosmolar foods (sugar, simple carbs), large volumesHigh glycaemic carbs → rapid absorption → insulin surge
DiagnosisClinical (Symptom score: Sigstad >7)CGM/glucose <3.5 at 1–3h post-meal
ManagementDiet: small frequent meals, low sugar, low GI, fluid separate from food (30min), protein/fat with carbs, lie down post-mealDiet: complex carbs, protein/fat each meal, avoid simple sugars; Acarbose 50mg TDS (delays absorption); Octreotide (refractory)

4. Nutritional Deficiencies — Lifelong Monitoring

NutrientDeficiency RiskMonitoringSupplementation
Vitamin B12High (ileal bypass, ↓ intrinsic factor + acid)Annual (B12, MMA, homocysteine)IM 1mg 3-monthly OR oral 1mg daily
IronHigh (duodenal bypass, ↓ acid, menstrual loss)6-monthly (ferritin, Tsat, Hb)Oral 100–200mg elemental ± IV if severe
Calcium / Vit DHigh (bypass, ↓ absorption, bone loss)Annual (Ca, PO4, ALP, PTH, 25-OH-D, DEXA q2y)Ca 1200–1500mg + Vit D 2000–4000 IU daily
Thiamine (B1)Critical (Wernicke's risk — rapid weight loss, vomiting)Annual (clinical; erythrocyte transketolase if indicated)Prophylactic 100mg daily post-op; IV 500mg TDS if suspected Wernicke's
FolateModerateAnnual1mg daily (in multivitamin)
Zinc / Copper / SeleniumModerate (malabsorptive procedures)AnnualIn bariatric multivitamin
Vitamin A / KLow (DS/BPD)If DS/BPDAs per protocol

5. Long-Term Follow-Up Schedule

TimepointAssessments
6 weeksWound, diet advancement, glucose, B12, iron, folate, D, Ca, PTH, thiamine
3 monthsFull bloods, HbA1c, weight, diet adherence, medication review, supplementation check
6 monthsFull nutritional panel, DEXA (baseline), weight trajectory
Annual (lifelong)CBC, U&E, LFT, iron studies, B12, folate, Vit D, Ca, PTH, Mg, Zn, Cu, Se, HbA1c, weight, DEXA q2y, endoscopy if symptoms

Supplementation (Lifelong, all procedures):

  • Bariatric multivitamin (2/day)
  • B12: IM 1mg 3-monthly OR oral 1mg daily
  • Iron: 100–200mg elemental daily (adjust by ferritin)
  • Calcium citrate 1200–1500mg + Vit D3 2000–4000 IU daily
  • Thiamine 100mg daily

6. Specific Post-Surgical Issues

Hypoglycaemia (Late Dumping / Reactive)

  • CGM essential for detection
  • Diet: complex carbs, protein/fat each meal, avoid simple sugars
  • Acarbose 50–100mg TDS with meals
  • Diazoxide / Octreotide (refractory, specialist)

Bone Health

  • DEXA at baseline, 2y, then q2–3y
  • High fracture risk (↓ weight-bearing, ↓ Ca/VitD, ↑ bone turnover)
  • Bisphosphonate if T-score <−2.5 or fracture

Anaemia

  • Multifactorial: Iron, B12, folate, copper, chronic inflammation
  • Treat specific deficiency; IV iron if oral fails/intolerant

Nephrolithiasis

  • ↑ Oxalate absorption (fat malabsorption) → calcium oxalate stones
  • High fluid intake (2–3L/day); citrate (potassium citrate); low oxalate diet

7. Contraception & Pregnancy

RecommendationDetail
ContraceptionEffective required 12–18 months post-op (rapid fertility return)
PreferredLARC (IUD, implant); avoid oral (malabsorption risk)
Pregnancy planningWait 12–18 months (weight stabilisation, nutrition optimisation)
Pre-conceptionNutritional optimisation (B12, iron, folate 5mg, Ca, D); HbA1c <48; stop teratogens
During pregnancySpecialist obstetric + bariatric team; increased nutritional monitoring; adjusted supplementation

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Post-op insulin reduction50–70% dose reduction (incretin effect)
Rapid remissionOften within days off all meds
Dumping syndromeEarly (osmotic, 15–30min) vs Late (reactive hypo, 1–3h)
Early dumping managementSmall meals, fluid separate, lie down post-meal, low sugar
Late dumping managementComplex carbs, protein/fat, acarbose, octreotide
B12 deficiencyIM 1mg 3-monthly lifelong (ileal bypass)
Iron deficiency6-monthly ferritin; oral/IV replacement
ThiamineProphylactic 100mg daily; Wernicke's risk with vomiting
DEXABaseline, 2y, q2–3y (high fracture risk)
ContraceptionLARC; avoid oral (malabsorption); wait 12–18m for pregnancy

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Metabolic surgery|Metabolic surgery]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Bariatric surgery indications]], [[Weight management and remission]], [[Type 2 diabetes remission]], [[Diabetes in pregnancy]]

modified: 2026-06-13

m.3243A>G (MELAS/MIDD)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise mitochondrial diabetes due to m.3243A>G mutation
  • [ ] Identify MELAS and MIDD syndromes
  • [ ] Apply maternal inheritance counselling
  • [ ] Manage diabetes and extra-pancreatic features

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Mutationm.3243A>G in tRNA-Leu (UUR)
SyndromesMELAS (mitochondrial encephalomyopathy, lactic acidosis, stroke-like episodes) / MIDD (maternally inherited diabetes and deafness)
InheritanceMaternal (mtDNA); heteroplasmy determines severity
Prevalence~1% of diabetes; most common mtDNA diabetes

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureMIDDMELAS
DiabetesYes (progressive)Yes
DeafnessSensorineural (high frequency)Yes
Stroke-like episodesNoYes (>90%)
Lactic acidosisRareCommon
MyopathyMildSevere
CardiomyopathyHypertrophicCommon
RenalProteinuria (FSGS)Rare
Short statureCommonCommon

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureMIDDMELAS
DiabetesYes (progressive)Yes
DeafnessSensorineural (high frequency)Yes
Stroke-like episodesNoYes (>90%)
Lactic acidosisRareCommon
MyopathyMildSevere
CardiomyopathyHypertrophicCommon
RenalProteinuria (FSGS)Rare
Short statureCommonCommon

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Heteroplasmy & Severity

Heteroplasmy LevelPhenotype
<10%Asymptomatic carrier
10-30%MIDD (diabetes + deafness)
30-90%MIDD + cardiomyopathy + myopathy
>90%MELAS (severe)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingm.3243A>G mutation (blood, urine, hair follicles)
Heteroplasmy levelQuantify in blood/urine
AudiometryHigh-frequency sensorineural hearing loss
ECG/EchoHypertrophic cardiomyopathy
MRI brainStroke-like lesions (MELAS)
LactateElevated (esp. CSF in MELAS)
Urine proteinProteinuria (FSGS pattern in MIDD)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DM + deafnessNo maternal inheritance; no heteroplasmy
Wolfram syndromeWFS1 gene; DI, DM, OA, D (DIDMOAD)
Alport syndromeDeafness + renal; COL4A5
Diabetic cardiomyopathyNo deafness; no maternal inheritance

7. Management

Diabetes Management

AspectDetail
TreatmentInsulin often needed (progressive beta-cell failure)
AvoidMetformin (lactic acidosis risk); glitazones (fluid retention)
SGLT2iCaution (renal impairment, low BMI)
GLP-1 RACaution (weight loss may worsen myopathy)
InsulinOften required; variable doses

Extra-pancreatic Management

SystemManagement
CardiomyopathyEcho surveillance; ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker
DeafnessHearing aids; cochlear implant if severe
Renal (FSGS)ACEi/ARB; nephrology referral
Lactic acidosisAvoid metformin; IV fluids, bicarbonate if acute
Stroke-like episodes (MELAS)Arginine IV; avoid triggers

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Mutationm.3243A>G (tRNA-Leu UUR)
InheritanceMaternal; heteroplasmy determines severity
MIDDDiabetes + sensorineural deafness
MELASStroke-like episodes, lactic acidosis, myopathy
HeteroplasmyLow = MIDD; High = MELAS
TreatmentInsulin often needed; avoid metformin; monitor heart/kidney

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the m.3243A>G mutation?tRNA-Leu(UUR) point mutation; causes MIDD/MELAS
What is the inheritance pattern?Maternal (mtDNA); heteroplasmy
What is MIDD?Maternally Inherited Diabetes and Deafness
What is MELAS?Mitochondrial Encephalomyopathy, Lactic Acidosis, Stroke-like episodes
How does heteroplasmy affect phenotype?Low = MIDD; High = MELAS
Why avoid metformin?Lactic acidosis risk (impaired mitochondrial oxidation)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All diabetes + deafness = MIDD?NO - Alport (COL4A5), Wolfram (WFS1), maternal inheritance key
MELAS = just stroke?No - stroke-like episodes + lactic acidosis + myopathy + diabetes
Heteroplasmy = always tested?Yes - blood, urine, hair; correlates with severity

Mnemonic: MELAS-MIDD-MUTATION

  • MELAS: Mitochondrial Encephalomyopathy, Lactic Acidosis, Stroke-like
  • Encephalomyopathy: brain + muscle
  • Lactic acidosis: elevated lactate (blood/CSF)
  • Astroke-like: not true strokes; cortical
  • Streptomycin: aminoglycosides worsen (avoid)
  • MIDD: Maternally Inherited Diabetes & Deafness
  • Inheritance: Maternal (mtDNA)
  • Deafness: sensorineural, high-frequency
  • Diabetes: progressive, insulin-requiring
  • Mutation: m.3243A>G (tRNA-Leu)
  • UUR: tRNA-Leu(UUR)
  • Teroplasmy: low=MIDD; high=MELAS
  • Avoid metformin: lactic acidosis risk
  • Insulin often needed
  • Ophthalmoplegia: chronic progressive (MELAS)
  • Nephropathy: FSGS (MIDD); rare in MELAS

Topic Overview

m.3243A>G (MELAS/MIDD

topics.title: Mody Genetic Testing Indications
topics.html:
[!info]
SGLT2 inhibitors: empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin, ertugliflozin — ↓renal glucose reabsorption → glucosuria; proven CV death↓, HF hosp↓, CKD progression↓; euglycaemic DKA risk; genital mycotic infections.

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State SGLT2i mechanism, pharmacokinetics, and dosing
  • [ ] Apply eGFR-based initiation/continuation thresholds
  • [ ] Recognise indications: ASCVD, HF (HFrEF/HFpEF), CKD, T2DM
  • [ ] Manage key adverse effects: euglycaemic DKA, genital infections, amputation (canagliflozin), Fournier's gangrene
  • [ ] Counsel on sick-day rules (STOP during acute illness)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Drug ClassSodium-glucose co-transporter 2 inhibitors
MechanismInhibit SGLT2 in proximal tubule (S1 segment) → ↓glucose reabsorption → ↑urinary glucose excretion (~70–90g/day) → ↓plasma glucose, ↓weight, ↓BP, ↓intraglomerular pressure
Agents & DosesEmpagliflozin 10/25mg OD; Dapagliflozin 10mg OD; Canagliflozin 100/300mg OD; Ertugliflozin 5/15mg OD
HbA1c Reduction0.5–1.0% (5–11 mmol/mol) as add-on; weight loss 2–3kg; SBP ↓3–5 mmHg
PharmacokineticsOral, ~80% bioavailability; hepatic glucuronidation (UGT1A9); renal excretion <2%; t½ 12–17h

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A — drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

eGFR Thresholds — CRITICAL FOR EXAMS

AgentInitiate if eGFR ≥Continue if eGFR ≥Stop if eGFR <
Empagliflozin20 (HF/CKD) / 45 (T2DM)2020
Dapagliflozin25 (HF/CKD) / 45 (T2DM)1515
Canagliflozin303030
Ertugliflozin454545
Key: HF/CKD indications allow lower eGFR initiation than T2DM-only use. DAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEY: continue to dialysis.

Indications by Comorbidity

IndicationPreferred Agent(s)Key Trials
T2DM + ASCVDEmpagliflozin, Canagliflozin, DapagliflozinEMPA-REG, CANVAS, DECLARE
HFrEF (EF≤40%)Dapagliflozin, EmpagliflozinDAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced
HFpEF (EF≥50%)Empagliflozin, DapagliflozinEMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER
CKD (eGFR≥20)Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin, CanagliflozinDAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY, CREDENCE
T2DM without high-risk comorbidityAny (consider cost/access)—

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
eGFR (CKD-EPI)Dosing/eligibilityBefore initiation, 2–4 weeks post, then 3–6 monthly
Ketones (blood)Euglycaemic DKA suspicionIf unwell, nausea, abdominal pain — check ketones even if glucose <13.9
Genital examMycotic infectionsClinical review if symptomatic
Foot examAmputation risk (canagliflozin)Baseline + annual; avoid if active ulcer/PAD
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9 mmol/L + ketonaemia >3 + pH <7.3; on SGLT2i, illness, low carb, surgery, alcohol; STOP SGLT2i, DKA protocol
Genital mycotic infectionVulvovaginitis, balanitis; common (10–15%); treat with topical antifungals; consider recurrent = stop
Fournier's gangreneRare (<0.1%); necrotising fasciitis of perineum; STOP SGLT2i, urgent surgical debridement, IV antibiotics
Volume depletionHypotension, AKI; elderly, diuretics, low SBP; monitor renal function
Amputation (canagliflozin)↑Toe/midfoot amputation (CANVAS); avoid if active DFU/PAD

7. Management

Initiation & Sick-Day Rules

StepAction
1. Check eligibilityeGFR per table above; no active DKA, not T1DM, not pregnant
2. Counsel sick-day rulesSTOP SGLT2i if: acute illness, fasting, surgery (3 days pre-op), low carb/keto diet, alcohol excess, dehydration
3. Start doseStandard dose (no titration needed); take any time with/without food
4. MonitoreGFR 2–4 weeks, then 3–6 monthly; ketones if unwell

Adverse Effect Management

Adverse EffectManagement
Genital mycotic infectionTopical clotrimazole/fluconazole; hygiene; if recurrent → consider stop
UTIStandard antibiotics; ↑fluid intake
Volume depletion/hypotension↓diuretic dose; ensure euvolaemia; monitor renal function
Euglycaemic DKASTOP SGLT2i immediately; standard DKA protocol (fluids, insulin, K+); glucose may be normal — don't delay insulin
Fournier's gangreneSTOP SGLT2i; emergency surgical debridement; broad-spectrum IV antibiotics; ICU

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismInhibit SGLT2 in proximal tubule (S1) → glucosuria ~70–90g/day → ↓glucose, ↓weight, ↓BP, ↓intraglomerular pressure
eGFR thresholdsEmpa: initiate ≥45 (T2DM) / ≥20 (HF/CKD); Dapa: ≥45/≥25; Cana: ≥30; Ertu: ≥45
CV benefitEmpa: EMPA-REG (CV death↓); Cana: CANVAS (MACE↓); Dapa: DECLARE (HF hosp↓)
HF benefitAll HFrEF/HFpEF: Dapa (DAPA-HF, DELIVER), Empa (EMPEROR-Red/Pres) — 1st line for HF in DM
CKD benefitCana (CREDENCE), Dapa (DAPA-CKD), Empa (EMPA-KIDNEY) — continue to dialysis
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9 + ketones >3 + pH <7.3; STOP SGLT2i, standard DKA Rx; check ketones if unwell
Genital infections10–15% (women > men); topical antifungals; recurrent → stop
AmputationCanagliflozin: ↑toe/midfoot amputation (CANVAS); avoid if active DFU/PAD
Fournier's gangreneRare (<0.1%); necrotising perineal infection; STOP SGLT2i, surgical emergency
Sick-day rulesSTOP if: illness, fasting, surgery (3d pre), low carb, alcohol, dehydration

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of SGLT2 inhibitors?Inhibit SGLT2 in proximal tubule (S1) → ↓glucose reabsorption → ↑urinary glucose excretion (~70–90g/day) → ↓plasma glucose, ↓weight, ↓BP, ↓intraglomerular pressure
What are the eGFR thresholds for SGLT2i initiation?Empagliflozin: ≥45 (T2DM), ≥20 (HF/CKD); Dapagliflozin: ≥45 (T2DM), ≥25 (HF/CKD); Canagliflozin: ≥30; Ertugliflozin: ≥45
Which SGLT2i have proven HF benefit?All HFrEF/HFpEF: Dapagliflozin (DAPA-HF, DELIVER), Empagliflozin (EMPEROR-Reduced, EMPEROR-Preserved) — 1st line for HF in diabetes
Which SGLT2i have proven CKD benefit?Canagliflozin (CREDENCE), Dapagliflozin (DAPA-CKD), Empagliflozin (EMPA-KIDNEY) — continue to dialysis
What is euglycaemic DKA and how do you manage it?DKA with glucose <13.9 mmol/L; on SGLT2i + precipitant (illness, fasting, surgery, low carb); STOP SGLT2i, standard DKA protocol — check ketones even if glucose normal
What are the sick-day rules for SGLT2i?STOP if: acute illness, fasting, surgery (3 days pre-op), low carbohydrate/ketogenic diet, alcohol excess, dehydration; restart when eating/drinking normally and well
What is the amputation risk with canagliflozin?CANVAS trial: ↑toe/midfoot amputation (6.3 vs 3.4/1000 pt-yrs); avoid in active DFU/PAD; monitor feet
How does SGLT2i reduce intraglomerular pressure?↑Tubuloglomerular feedback: ↑distal NaCl delivery → afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction → ↓intraglomerular pressure → ↓hyperfiltration → renal protection

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
SGLT2i in T1DM?Contraindicated — euglycaemic DKA risk high; only in clinical trials with strict monitoring
SGLT2i + insulin?Safe; ↓insulin dose often needed (20–30%) to avoid hypoglycaemia
Euglycaemic DKA vs DKA?Same ketone/pH criteria; glucose <13.9; STOP SGLT2i; don't delay insulin because glucose "not high"

Mnemonic: SGLT2-EMPA-CAN-DAPA

  • SGLT2 → proximal tubule S1 segment
  • Glucosuria ~70-90g/day
  • Lowers glucose, weight, BP, intraglomerular pressure
  • Thresholds: Empa≥45/20, Dapa≥45/25, Cana≥30
  • 2 (SGLT2) not SGLT1 (gut)
  • EMPA-REG: CV death↓
  • MP: EMPEROR-HF (Red/Pres)
  • AKI: EMPA-KIDNEY
  • CREDENCE: Cana renal
  • Amputation: Cana↑
  • Nephroprotection: all
  • DAPA-HF: HFrEF
  • ADAPA-CKD: CKD
  • Preserved: DELIVER HFpEF
  • All HF types benefit**

topics.title: Sglt2 Inhibitors
topics.html:
[!info]
SGLT2 inhibitors: empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin, ertugliflozin — ↓renal glucose reabsorption → glucosuria; proven CV death↓, HF hosp↓, CKD progression↓; euglycaemic DKA risk; genital mycotic infections.

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State SGLT2i mechanism, pharmacokinetics, and dosing
  • [ ] Apply eGFR-based initiation/continuation thresholds
  • [ ] Recognise indications: ASCVD, HF (HFrEF/HFpEF), CKD, T2DM
  • [ ] Manage key adverse effects: euglycaemic DKA, genital infections, amputation (canagliflozin), Fournier's gangrene
  • [ ] Counsel on sick-day rules (STOP during acute illness)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Drug ClassSodium-glucose co-transporter 2 inhibitors
MechanismInhibit SGLT2 in proximal tubule (S1 segment) → ↓glucose reabsorption → ↑urinary glucose excretion (~70–90g/day) → ↓plasma glucose, ↓weight, ↓BP, ↓intraglomerular pressure
Agents & DosesEmpagliflozin 10/25mg OD; Dapagliflozin 10mg OD; Canagliflozin 100/300mg OD; Ertugliflozin 5/15mg OD
HbA1c Reduction0.5–1.0% (5–11 mmol/mol) as add-on; weight loss 2–3kg; SBP ↓3–5 mmHg
PharmacokineticsOral, ~80% bioavailability; hepatic glucuronidation (UGT1A9); renal excretion <2%; t½ 12–17h

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A — drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

eGFR Thresholds — CRITICAL FOR EXAMS

AgentInitiate if eGFR ≥Continue if eGFR ≥Stop if eGFR <
Empagliflozin20 (HF/CKD) / 45 (T2DM)2020
Dapagliflozin25 (HF/CKD) / 45 (T2DM)1515
Canagliflozin303030
Ertugliflozin454545
Key: HF/CKD indications allow lower eGFR initiation than T2DM-only use. DAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEY: continue to dialysis.

Indications by Comorbidity

IndicationPreferred Agent(s)Key Trials
T2DM + ASCVDEmpagliflozin, Canagliflozin, DapagliflozinEMPA-REG, CANVAS, DECLARE
HFrEF (EF≤40%)Dapagliflozin, EmpagliflozinDAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced
HFpEF (EF≥50%)Empagliflozin, DapagliflozinEMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER
CKD (eGFR≥20)Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin, CanagliflozinDAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY, CREDENCE
T2DM without high-risk comorbidityAny (consider cost/access)—

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
eGFR (CKD-EPI)Dosing/eligibilityBefore initiation, 2–4 weeks post, then 3–6 monthly
Ketones (blood)Euglycaemic DKA suspicionIf unwell, nausea, abdominal pain — check ketones even if glucose <13.9
Genital examMycotic infectionsClinical review if symptomatic
Foot examAmputation risk (canagliflozin)Baseline + annual; avoid if active ulcer/PAD
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Euglycaemic DKAGlucose <13.9 mmol/L + ketonaemia >3 + pH <7.3; on SGLT2i, illness, low carb, surgery, alcohol; STOP SGLT2i, DKA protocol
Genital mycotic infectionVulvovaginitis, balanitis; common (10–15%); treat with topical antifungals; consider recurrent = stop
Fournier's gangreneRare (<0.1%); necrotising fasciitis of perineum; STOP SGLT2i, urgent surgical debridement, IV antibiotics
Volume depletionHypotension, AKI; elderly, diuretics, low SBP; monitor renal function
Amputation (canagliflozin)↑Toe/midfoot amputation (CANVAS); avoid if active DFU/PAD

7. Management

Initiation & Sick-Day Rules

StepAction
1. Check eligibilityeGFR per table above; no active DKA, not T1DM, not pregnant
2. Counsel sick-day rulesSTOP SGLT2i if: acute illness, fasting, surgery (3 days pre-op), low carb/keto diet, alcohol excess, dehydration
3. Start doseStandard dose (no titration needed); take any time with/without food
4. MonitoreGFR 2–4 weeks, then 3–6 monthly; ketones if unwell

Adverse Effect Management

Adverse EffectManagement

Euglycaemic DKASTOP SGLT2i immediately; standard DKA protocol (fluids, insulin, K+); glucose may be normal — don't delay insulin
Fournier's gangreneSTOP SGLT2i; emergency surgical debridement; broad-spectrum IV antibiotics; ICU
